crumbs: The Data-Science Workflow: The Whole Game

title: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text: An analysis is finished only when someone can act on it — and trust it. Quarto is the tool R for Data Science uses to close that loop: a “unified authoring framework” that weaves prose, code, and results into one source file, so the report regenerates every figure and number from the data each time it’s rendered. (Wickham et al. 2023) The narrative can never silently fall out of sync with the computation, because the computation is the document.

title: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
Why it matters
This is the communicate station of the workflow — “absolutely critical,” the authors say, because an insight no one can follow changes nothing. (Wickham et al. 2023) Quarto is also the practical face of open and reproducible science: a copy-pasted screenshot of a result is a claim; a Quarto document that rebuilds the result from data and code is evidence. (This very encyclopedia is built in Quarto.)

title: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
Literate programming
Quarto descends from Donald Knuth’s literate programming: the document’s spine is a narrative that explains the what and why of an analysis, with the code that performs it woven alongside. (Wickham et al. 2023) It “integrates your work into a document with commentary.” When you render (the old word is knit), a tool (knitr for R) executes the code into a markdown file of results, which pandoc then turns into the final output. (Wickham et al. 2023) You write thinking and computing together; the machine assembles the artefact.

title: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
Anatomy of a .qmd file
A Quarto document has three parts: (Wickham et al. 2023)

YAML header — between triple-dash --- fences at the very top; controls document-wide settings (title, date, output format):

---
title: "Penguin report"
format: html
---

Markdown narrative — the prose spine, written in “a lightweight set of conventions for formatting plain-text files”: # for headings, **bold**, _italics_. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Executable code chunks — a fenced block opening with the language in braces, {r} (or {python}), carrying #| options, then code, and closing with a bare fence. When rendered, the code runs and its output is inserted into the document. A chunk that draws a captioned boxplot would set a label, an echo option, and a fig-cap, then call ggplot(...) + geom_boxplot() — and the report shows the plot, regenerated from the data, not a pasted image. (Wickham et al. 2023)


Chunk options
Each chunk’s behaviour is tuned by options (in the #| header): (Wickham et al. 2023)







Option
Effect




echo
show the source code? (false → show the result but hide the code)


eval
run the code? (false → display it, don’t execute)


include
false → run the code but show neither code nor output


warning / message
suppress warnings/messages from the rendered output


fig-cap
caption a figure (and float it in some formats)


label
a unique name — used for graphic filenames and easy navigation/cross-referencing

title: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
One source, many formats
The payoff of separating content from format: a single .qmd renders to a “vast array” of outputs without rewriting. (Wickham et al. 2023)

Documents — HTML (the default), PDF (via LaTeX), MS Word.
Presentations — revealjs / Beamer slides.
Bigger structures — dashboards, whole websites, and books (the bookdown lineage).

Write once; deliver as a web page for a colleague, a Word doc for a journal, slides for a meeting.

title: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
Why reproducibility is the point
The authors call this “the right way to communicate data analysis” for concrete reasons: (Wickham et al. 2023)

In sync. Because every number and figure regenerates from the data on each render, the narrative can’t drift from the computation — no stale copy-pasted table.
A definitive record. The document is the analysis; it’s auditable and re-runnable, sparing you the “long-term agony” of finding a result in your workspace but having forgotten the code that made it.
Efficient. Caching (cache: true) saves long-running chunks and recomputes only when their code changes.
For future-you. It lets you “record your thoughts as you go,” so a collaborator — or you in six months — can reconstruct why each choice was made.

title: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Pasting results instead of computing them. A static screenshot breaks the moment the data updates; let the chunk regenerate it. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Relying on a hidden workspace. If a number depends on objects not created in the document, the report isn’t reproducible. Keep the analysis self-contained. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Showing code your audience doesn’t want. Use echo: false for an executive report; keep it visible for a methods appendix. Match the output to the reader. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Forgetting cache invalidation. Caching speeds renders but can serve stale results if you change data the chunk reads without changing its code. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — Quarto as a “unified authoring framework” weaving prose + code + results; literate programming (Knuth) and the render/knit pipeline (knitr → markdown → pandoc); the three components (YAML header, markdown narrative, executable {r} chunks) and chunk options (echo, eval, include, warning/message, fig-cap, label); the many output formats (HTML/PDF/Word, slides, dashboards, websites, books) from one source; and the reproducibility case — narrative stays in sync, a definitive, re-runnable record, caching, and documenting rationale for “future-you.”

title: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
Connections

Builds on: the data-science workflow (this is the communicate station) and everything upstream — the report renders your transformed and visualized data.
Pairs with: open & reproducible science (the why) and communicating quantitative findings (honest presentation).
Realises: the whole track — tidy, transform, visualize, and now communicate, reproducibly.

title: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“What is Quarto, and how does literate programming (Knuth) shape the way it combines prose, code, and results?”
“What are the three components of a .qmd file, and what do chunk options like echo, eval, include, and label control?”
“What output formats can one Quarto source produce, and why is that separation of content and format useful?”
“Why do Wickham et al. argue reproducible documents are the right way to communicate analysis — what does ‘the narrative stays in sync with the computation’ buy you?”

title: Missing Values
text: Real data has holes. How you handle them quietly decides your results — drop the wrong rows and you bias the sample; ignore an NA and a whole summary collapses to NA. R is deliberately strict here: a missing value is unknown, and the unknown is contagious. This entry covers what NA means, the crucial split between explicit and implicit missingness, and the tools to handle each.

title: Missing Values
text:
Why it matters
Missingness is not a formatting nuisance; it is a substantive feature of the data with its own statistics. Where the holes are, and why, can bias every estimate downstream — the same selection logic that breaks censored survival data or an experiment with attrition. R forces you to confront it rather than letting it pass silently, which is a feature, not an annoyance. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Missing Values
text:
NA is contagious
In R, NA represents an unknown value. (Wickham et al. 2023) And because it’s unknown, almost any operation touching it is also unknown — “NA is contagious.” (Wickham et al. 2023)
NA > 5      # NA  — is an unknown number bigger than 5? unknown.
10 == NA    # NA  — does 10 equal an unknown? unknown.
NA == NA    # NA  — are two unknowns equal? STILL unknown (the confusing one)
That last line is the trap: NA == NA is NA, not TRUE. So you can never test for missingness with == NA; you must use is.na(). (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Missing Values
text:
Explicit vs implicit missingness
A subtle and important distinction: (Wickham et al. 2023)

An explicit missing value is “the presence of an absence” — a cell flagged NA. You can see it.
An implicit missing value is “the absence of a presence” — a row that simply isn’t in the table. A respondent-quarter with no record; a product-month with no sales row. You can’t see it, and it’s the more dangerous kind because a naive count treats “no row” as if the case didn’t exist.

Much of careful data work is making implicit missings explicit so you can decide what to do with them.

title: Missing Values
text:
Tools
Summaries propagate NA by default — mean(), sum(), median() return NA if any input is missing, which is R insisting you make a choice. Pass na.rm = TRUE to drop missings before computing. (Wickham et al. 2023) For wrangling:







Tool
What it does




is.na(x)
the only correct test for missingness (logical vector)


coalesce(x, y, …)
take the first non-missing value across several vectors — fill from a backup


tidyr::complete()
generate all combinations of given columns, inserting explicit NA rows where they were implicitly missing


tidyr::fill()
last observation carried forward — fill a gap with the most recent non-missing value


str_replace_na()
turn NA into the literal string "NA" for display



# Make implicit missings explicit, then carry the last value forward
stocks |>
  complete(year, quarter) |>     # every year x quarter now has a row (NA where absent)
  fill(price)                    # fill the new gaps with the prior price






Warningna.rm = TRUE is a decision, not a default



Silently dropping missings changes which units you’re averaging over. If the missingness is related to the outcome (the unhappiest customers don’t respond), na.rm = TRUE gives a biased answer that looks clean. The honest move is to know why values are missing before you remove them — exactly the selection concern at the heart of causal inference. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Missing Values
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Testing x == NA. Always NA; use is.na(). (Wickham et al. 2023)
A summary that mysteriously returns NA. One missing input poisoned it; add na.rm = TRUE — but only after deciding that dropping is appropriate. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Ignoring implicit missingness. “No row” is invisible to is.na(); use complete() to surface the gaps before they bias a count. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Filling blindly with fill(). Last-observation-carried-forward assumes the value persisted; that’s a modelling assumption, not a fact. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Missing Values
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — NA as an unknown value and “NA is contagious” (NA > 5, 10 == NA, and the trap that NA == NA is NA, so use is.na()); explicit (“presence of an absence”) vs implicit (“absence of a presence”) missingness; summaries propagating NA and the na.rm argument; and the wrangling tools coalesce, complete (implicit → explicit), and fill (last observation carried forward).

title: Missing Values
text:
Connections

Builds on: logical vectors & summaries (is.na() is a logical test; na.rm governs summaries) and tidy data (implicit missing = a row that should exist).
Leads to: the statistical treatment of missingness — messy real data and the selection logic of survival/censoring and attrition.
Underwrites: every honest analysis — why data is missing is a substantive question, not a cleaning detail.

title: Missing Values
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Why is NA ‘contagious,’ and why is NA == NA equal to NA rather than TRUE? How do you test for missingness?”
“Explain the difference between explicit and implicit missing values, and why implicit ones are more dangerous.”
“How do summaries handle NA by default, and when is na.rm = TRUE appropriate versus misleading?”
“What do coalesce(), complete(), and fill() each do for missing data?”

title: Iteration & Functional Programming
text: The companion to writing functions is iteration: applying that function to many inputs — every column, every file, every group. The tidyverse’s answer is functional: across() to do the same thing to many columns, and the purrr map() family to apply a function to each element of a list and get a typed result back. The win over a for-loop is readability — you say what to do, not how to store the results.

title: Iteration & Functional Programming
text:
Why it matters
Iteration “reduces duplication when you need to perform the same operation on multiple inputs.” (Wickham et al. 2023) It’s the difference between thirty near-identical pasted lines (one per column) and a single expression that can’t drift out of sync. Combined with functions, it’s how an analysis stays short, correct, and reproducible as it scales.

title: Iteration & Functional Programming
section: across(): the same thing to many columns
text:
across(): the same thing to many columns
Inside a mutate() or summarize(), across() applies a function to many columns at once — “do the same thing to many columns.” (Wickham et al. 2023) It replaces the older mutate_each()/summarize_each() precursors.
# Summarise the mean of EVERY numeric column, missing values removed — one line, any width
df |>
  summarize(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)))

# Rescale several named columns with our own function, in place
df |>
  mutate(across(c(height, weight, age), rescale01))

title: Iteration & Functional Programming
text:
The purrr map() family
map() applies a function to each element of a vector or list and returns a result — the functional replacement for a for-loop. (Wickham et al. 2023) Its variants differ only in the type they return, which keeps your output predictable: (Wickham et al. 2023)



Function
Returns




map()
a list


map_dbl()
a double vector


map_int()
an integer vector


map_chr()
a character vector



map_dbl(mtcars, mean)          # mean of each column -> a named numeric vector
A canonical use is reading many files: store the paths in a vector and map(paths, read_csv) to apply read_csv() to each, then bind. (Wickham et al. 2023)
files <- list.files("data/", pattern = "\\.csv$", full.names = TRUE)
data  <- map(files, read_csv) |> list_rbind()

title: Iteration & Functional Programming
text:
Why functional iteration beats loops
For-loops are explicit but verbose: they demand “bookkeeping code” — pre-allocating the output vector, tracking the index — that must be duplicated for every loop. (Wickham et al. 2023) map() hides that machinery, with three consequences the book highlights: (Wickham et al. 2023)

Readability — map functions focus on the operation; loops focus on the mechanics of storing results.
Composability — they break a list-manipulation problem into independent “bite-sized pieces” that chain with the pipe.
Predictable output — the _dbl/_chr/_int suffix guarantees the return type, so a wrong-typed result fails loudly instead of silently.







NoteFunctional, not anti-loop



The point isn’t that loops are forbidden — it’s that the repetitive bookkeeping a loop forces you to re-write is exactly the part that hides bugs. A map() says “this operation, over these inputs” and lets the package handle the rest. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Iteration & Functional Programming
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Pasting a block once per column. That’s the iteration trigger; use across() so a change happens once. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Using map() when you want a vector. map() returns a list; reach for map_dbl/map_chr/map_int to get a typed vector. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Re-deriving for-loop bookkeeping. Pre-allocation and indexing are error-prone and repetitive; a functional hides them. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Silent type drift. A loop building a result can quietly change type; the typed map_* variants catch it. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Iteration & Functional Programming
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — iteration to reduce duplication; across() (“do the same thing to many columns”) inside mutate/summarize; the purrr map() family (map → list, map_dbl/map_int/map_chr → typed vectors) applying a function to each element; reading many files with map(paths, read_csv); and why functional iteration beats for-loops (loops need duplicated “bookkeeping code”; maps are more readable, composable, and type-safe).

title: Iteration & Functional Programming
text:
Connections

Builds on: writing functions (you iterate a function) and the grammar of manipulation (across() lives inside the verbs).
Pairs with: importing data (read a whole folder with map) and beyond flat files (process many pages/tables).
Enables: reproducible pipelines — the same operation over every input, expressed once.

title: Iteration & Functional Programming
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“What problem does iteration solve, and what does across() do inside mutate()/summarize()?”
“What does map() do, and how do map_dbl, map_int, and map_chr differ?”
“How would you read a directory of CSVs into one data frame with map()?”
“Why do Wickham et al. argue functional iteration is more readable than a for-loop — what ‘bookkeeping’ does a loop force you to repeat?”

title: Importing Data: Getting It Into R
text: Nothing happens until the data is in R — “if you can’t get your data into R, you can’t do data science on it!” (Wickham et al. 2023) Import is “routine and boring” 80% of the time and “weird and frustrating” the other 20%, and that 20% is where analyses silently go wrong: a column guessed as text, a decimal comma mistaken for a thousands separator. This entry is the readr toolkit for reading flat files correctly, and the one habit — pinning column types — that makes import reproducible.
crumbs: Importing Data: Getting It Into R

title: Importing Data: Getting It Into R
text:
Why it matters
Import is the first station of the workflow and a real technical hurdle to clear before the creative work begins. (Wickham et al. 2023) Getting it wrong corrupts everything downstream; the goal is to read the file once, correctly, reproducibly.
crumbs: Importing Data: Getting It Into R

title: Importing Data: Getting It Into R
text:
readr and the tibble
The readr package gives a consistent family for turning flat files into data frames: read_csv() (comma-delimited), read_csv2() (semicolon-delimited — common where the comma is the decimal mark), read_tsv() (tab-delimited). (Wickham et al. 2023) They produce a tibble — a “slightly tweaked data frame that works better in the tidyverse,” with a refined print method showing the first 10 rows and each column’s type (<dbl>, <int>, <chr>) under its name. (Wickham et al. 2023)
library(tidyverse)
students <- read_csv("students.csv")
#> Rows: 6 Columns: 5
#> ── Column specification ──────────────
#> chr (3): name, favourite.food, mealPlan
#> dbl (2): id, age          <- readr GUESSED these types
By default the first line supplies the column names; tweak with skip = n (drop leading metadata) or comment = "#" (drop comment lines). With no header, col_names = FALSE auto-names X1…Xn, or pass a character vector to set them. (Wickham et al. 2023)






WarningNever trust the type-guesser blindly



readr guesses each column’s type from the first 1000 rows. (Wickham et al. 2023) That heuristic fails when the early rows are unrepresentative — all integers at the top, doubles later; blanks before the real values. The fix the authors insist on for reproducible code: always supply col_types explicitly with helpers like col_double(), col_date(), col_factor(). When parsing does fail, problems() returns a tibble pinpointing exactly which cells and why. (Wickham et al. 2023)


read_csv("data.csv", col_types = cols(
  id   = col_integer(),
  date = col_date(format = "%Y-%m-%d"),
  rate = col_double()
))
crumbs: Importing Data: Getting It Into R

title: Importing Data: Getting It Into R
text:
readr vs base R’s read.csv
The readr functions are “~10× faster,” show a progress bar on big files, and avoid base R’s frustrations — they don’t silently convert strings to factors and don’t mangle column names. (Wickham et al. 2023) Prefer them.
crumbs: Importing Data: Getting It Into R

title: Importing Data: Getting It Into R
text:
Writing data back
write_csv() / write_tsv() save to disk — but CSV is plain text and loses type metadata, so a factor or date round-trips as a string. To preserve types exactly, write_rds() uses R’s binary format. (Wickham et al. 2023)
crumbs: Importing Data: Getting It Into R

title: Importing Data: Getting It Into R
text:
Many files at once
To read a folder of CSVs into one tibble, collect the paths into a vector and apply read_csv() to each with a map() (or a for loop), then bind the results — the natural job for iteration. (Wickham et al. 2023)
files <- list.files("data/", pattern = "\\.csv$", full.names = TRUE)
all   <- map(files, read_csv) |> list_rbind()
crumbs: Importing Data: Getting It Into R

title: Importing Data: Getting It Into R
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Accepting guessed column types. The 1000-row heuristic misreads unrepresentative headers; pin col_types for anything you’ll rerun. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Wrong delimiter / decimal mark. European CSVs use ; and decimal commas — read_csv2(), not read_csv(). (Wickham et al. 2023)
Round-tripping types through CSV. Factors and dates degrade to text; use write_rds() to keep them. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Reaching for base read.csv. Slower, factor-coercing, name-munging; read_csv() avoids all three. (Wickham et al. 2023)
crumbs: Importing Data: Getting It Into R

title: Importing Data: Getting It Into R
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — import as the essential first step (“if you can’t get your data into R…”); readr (read_csv/read_csv2/read_tsv), the tibble and its print method; header handling (skip, comment, col_names); type guessing from the first 1000 rows and the rule to set col_types (with problems() to diagnose); read_csv vs read.csv (≈10× faster, no factor coercion / name munging); write_csv vs write_rds (type preservation); and reading many files with map().
crumbs: Importing Data: Getting It Into R

title: Importing Data: Getting It Into R
text:
Connections

Leads to: beyond flat files (spreadsheets, databases, JSON, the web), tidy data (the next station), and iteration (reading many files).
Pairs with: strings & regex (parsing is where text becomes typed columns) and the workflow (the import box).
crumbs: Importing Data: Getting It Into R

title: Importing Data: Getting It Into R
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“How does readr guess column types, why can it fail, and why should you set col_types explicitly?”

“How would you read a whole directory of CSV files into a single data frame?”

title: Factors: Categorical Data
text: Social-science data is overwhelmingly categorical: party ID, religion, Likert responses, “Refused.” Stored as plain strings, categories sort alphabetically (so “Apr” comes before “Jan”) and silently absorb typos. A factor fixes both — it pins a variable to a known set of levels in an order you control. The forcats package then lets you reorder and recode those levels, which is most of what makes a categorical plot or table readable.

title: Factors: Categorical Data
text:
Why factors exist
A factor is R’s structure for a variable with “a fixed and known set of possible values.” (Wickham et al. 2023) Recording categories as raw strings has two failure modes the book names directly: strings “don’t sort in a useful way” (month names land alphabetically — Apr, Aug, Dec…), and there is “no protection against typos” — "Jam" for "Jan" slips through unnoticed. (Wickham et al. 2023) A factor solves both by declaring the levels up front.
library(tidyverse)

month_levels <- c("Jan","Feb","Mar","Apr","May","Jun",
                  "Jul","Aug","Sep","Oct","Nov","Dec")
x <- c("Dec","Apr","Jam","Mar")
factor(x, levels = month_levels)
#> [1] Dec  Apr  <NA> Mar      <- "Jam" wasn't a valid level, so it became NA (the typo is caught)
#> Levels: Jan Feb Mar ... Dec  <- and they now sort in calendar order, not alphabetically
factor() maps the data onto the declared levels; any value not in the set becomes NA (so typos surface as missing rather than passing silently). (Wickham et al. 2023) Omit the levels and R falls back to alphabetical order — rarely what you want. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Factors: Categorical Data
text:
The forcats verbs
The point of forcats (an anagram of factors) is to change the order or the values of levels, which is where most categorical-data work actually lives: (Wickham et al. 2023)







Verb
What it does




fct_reorder()
reorder levels by another (numeric) variable — sort religions by mean TV hours to reveal the pattern


fct_relevel()
hand-move specific levels to the front (keep a principled order, but pull "Not applicable" first)


fct_infreq() / fct_rev()
order by frequency (and reverse it) — the standard tidy-up for bar charts


fct_recode()
rename levels by hand for clearer, consistent labels


fct_collapse()
bundle many old levels into a few new ones (various party labels → rep, dem, ind)


fct_lump()
automatically lump the smallest groups into "Other" to de-clutter a plot or table



The highest-value one is fct_reorder(): a categorical axis sorted by the value you’re plotting turns a jumbled bar chart into an instantly readable ranking. (Wickham et al. 2023)
# Reorder 'relig' by the mean of tvhours so the chart reads top-to-bottom by magnitude
gss_cat |>
  group_by(relig) |>
  summarize(tvhours = mean(tvhours, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = tvhours, y = fct_reorder(relig, tvhours))) +
  geom_point()






NoteThe gss_cat example



The book uses the General Social Survey sample (gss_cat) — real survey data, with the real headaches: thousands of categories, and non-substantive levels like “Refused” and “No answer” that you must decide how to handle. (Wickham et al. 2023) This is exactly the categorical mess a quantitative social scientist faces, which is why factors are a survey-research staple, not a programming nicety.

title: Factors: Categorical Data
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Letting categories sort alphabetically. Months, Likert scales, and income brackets have a meaningful order; declare it in the levels or your tables and plots mislead. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Not catching invalid levels. Values outside the declared set become NA — useful, but check, or a miscoded category vanishes silently. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Plotting unordered categories. A bar chart in alphabetical order hides the pattern; fct_reorder() / fct_infreq() make it visible. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Carrying dozens of tiny categories. They clutter and overfit visuals; fct_lump() into “Other” keeps the signal. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Factors: Categorical Data
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — factors for variables with a fixed known set of values; why strings fail (no useful sort, no typo protection); factor() and levels (invalid values → NA, default alphabetical order); the forcats verbs fct_reorder, fct_relevel, fct_infreq/fct_rev, fct_recode, fct_collapse, fct_lump; and the gss_cat (General Social Survey) examples with “Refused”/“No answer” levels.

title: Factors: Categorical Data
text:
Connections

Builds on: strings & regex (clean the text, then make it a factor) and tidy data.
Leads to: the grammar of graphics (fct_reorder is how a categorical axis becomes a readable ranking) and regression (factor levels become the dummy variables of a model).
Reappears in: all survey analysis — party, religion, education bands, Likert items are factors.

title: Factors: Categorical Data
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Why do factors exist — what two problems with storing categories as strings do they solve?”
“How does factor() use levels, what happens to values outside the set, and what’s the default order?”
“What does each forcats verb do (fct_reorder, fct_relevel, fct_infreq, fct_recode, fct_collapse, fct_lump), and why is fct_reorder so useful for plots?”
“Using the gss_cat survey data, how would you handle non-substantive levels like ‘Refused’ and lump rare categories?”

title: Dates & Times
text: Dates look easy and are not. They have to reconcile two physical facts — the Earth spinning and orbiting — with “a whole raft of geopolitical phenomena”: months of unequal length, time zones, leap years, daylight saving. (Wickham et al. 2023) Get them wrong and your “events per week” is silently off by an hour or a day. The lubridate package gives dates a sane, consistent toolkit: parse them, pull them apart, round them, and do arithmetic that respects the calendar’s weirdness.

title: Dates & Times
text:
Why dates are hard
The book is unusually candid: dates and times are difficult because they must square the Earth’s rotation and orbit with human inventions — months, time zones, and daylight saving time. (Wickham et al. 2023) Every hard case (a “day” that is 23 or 25 hours long under DST, a February that is 28 or 29) comes from that collision. Treat a date as “just a number” and the calendar will eventually betray you.

title: Dates & Times
text:
Three types
R distinguishes three things, by precision: (Wickham et al. 2023)

Date — a calendar day, printed in a tibble as <date>.
Time — a time within a day (handled by the hms package).
Date-time — a date and a time that pins down a single instant (POSIXct), printed as <dttm>.

Reach for the simplest type that captures what you need; a date-time carries time-zone baggage a plain date does not.

title: Dates & Times
text:
Parsing: from text to dates
Real dates arrive as strings in a dozen formats. lubridate’s helpers parse them once you name the order of the components — ymd(), mdy(), dmy() — and you append _hms/_hm for date-times: (Wickham et al. 2023)
library(tidyverse)

ymd("2024-01-31")        # year-month-day
mdy("January 31, 2024")  # month-day-year  (same instant, messier string)
dmy("31/1/24")           # day-month-year
ymd_hms("2024-01-31 14:05:00")   # a full date-time
When the pieces live in separate columns (year, month, day, hour…), make_datetime() assembles them — the book’s flights example builds a departure date-time from five columns. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Dates & Times
Dates & Times
text:
Time spans: durations, periods, intervals
Arithmetic on dates needs three different notions of “how long,” and choosing wrong is a classic bug: (Wickham et al. 2023)

Durations — an exact number of seconds. ddays(1) is always 86,400 seconds, even across a DST boundary. Use them for physical elapsed time.
Periods — human units (weeks, months) with no fixed length in seconds; they “just work” across DST and leap years. Adding months(1) lands you on the same day next month, however many days that took.
Intervals — a duration with a start point, so it is precise even for human units (how many days in this specific month).

The DST trap: add a duration of one day to a date crossing the spring-forward boundary and you land an hour off; add a period of one day and you land on the right calendar day. Match the type to the question. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Dates & Times
text:
Time zones
R uses the IANA names of the form "<continent>/<city>" (e.g. "America/New_York") — not the ambiguous “EST”. (Wickham et al. 2023) Two operations you must not confuse: with_tz() changes how an instant is displayed (same moment, different clock), while force_tz() relabels an instant whose zone was recorded wrong (different moment). (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Dates & Times
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Durations vs periods across DST. ddays(1) (exactly 86,400s) and days(1) (one calendar day) differ on DST days. Use periods for human “a day later,” durations for elapsed physical time. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Parsing with the wrong component order. 01/02/03 is three different dates depending on locale; pick ymd/mdy/dmy deliberately. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Confusing with_tz() and force_tz(). One re-displays the same instant; the other changes which instant you mean. Mixing them corrupts timestamps. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Using a date-time when a date will do. Time-zone handling adds bugs you don’t need if the time of day is irrelevant. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Dates & Times
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — why dates are hard (rotation + orbit vs “geopolitical phenomena”, DST, leap years); the three types (<date>, time/hms, date-time/POSIXct <dttm>); lubridate parsing (ymd/mdy/dmy, _hms, make_datetime from columns); accessors (year, month, mday, yday, wday, hour), update(), and rounding (floor_date/round_date/ceiling_date); durations (fixed seconds, ddays), periods (human units), intervals; and IANA time zones with with_tz vs force_tz.

title: Dates & Times
text:
Connections

Builds on: strings & regex (dates usually arrive as text to be parsed) and the grammar of manipulation (mutate the parsed date, group_by a rounded one).
Leads to: time-series fundamentals — once observations are time-stamped and regularly bucketed, they become a series you can model and forecast.
Reappears in: panel & longitudinal data, event studies, and any analysis with a when.

title: Dates & Times
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Why are dates and times genuinely hard, and what are the three temporal types in R?”
“How do lubridate’s ymd/mdy/dmy and make_datetime parse dates, and how do accessors like wday and rounding like floor_date help?”
“Explain durations, periods, and intervals — why does the difference matter across daylight saving time?”
“What’s the difference between with_tz() and force_tz(), and why are IANA ‘continent/city’ time zones used?”

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text: Everything before this taught you to measure well. This entry is the warning that measuring is never neutral. The instant a number carries stakes — pay, promotion, ranking, funding — the people being measured become self-conscious actors who optimise the number, not the goal beneath it. Test scores rise while learning stagnates; survival rates climb because surgeons turn away the sick. The metric improves; the world it represented does not. Knowing when not to trust a number is the final skill of the quantitative researcher.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
Why it matters
The whole manual has been a machine for producing trustworthy numbers. Muller’s The Tyranny of Metrics supplies the conscience: a number that drives behaviour changes the behaviour it measures, often perversely. (Muller 2018) This is not a niche caution — it is the central risk of applied quantitative work, where your estimate doesn’t sit in a journal but becomes a target someone is paid to hit. The researcher who can build a metric and foresee how it will be gamed is the one who can be trusted with a real decision.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
Metric fixation
Muller names the disease metric fixation: “the seemingly irresistible pressure to measure performance, to publicize it, and to reward it.” (Muller 2018) It rests on three beliefs: (Muller 2018)

Replace judgment with numbers — that standardised, comparable metrics can and should substitute for the judgment of experienced practitioners.
Accountability = transparency — that making the numbers public guarantees institutions serve their purpose.
Motivate by extrinsic reward — that people work best when pay and penalties are tied to measured performance (pay-for-performance, league tables).

Each belief is plausible; together they license replacing professional judgment with a dashboard. The rest of the entry is what goes wrong when you do.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
Goodhart’s law and Campbell’s law
Two “laws” name the mechanism: (Muller 2018)

Goodhart’s law — “when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.”
Campbell’s law — “the more any quantitative social indicator is used for social decision-making, the more subject it will be to corruption pressures and the more apt it will be to distort and corrupt the social processes it is intended to monitor.”

The engine under both: people are self-conscious actors. Attach stakes to a measurement and they respond by skewing the data to maximise the score, whether or not the underlying reality improves. (Muller 2018) A measure passively observed and the same measure optimised against are different objects — the act of targeting corrupts the signal. The figure shows the signature: a proxy that faithfully tracks the goal until the day it becomes a target, then peels away.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
t = np.arange(0, 24)
target_start = 12
# Before targeting, proxy and true goal move together; after, proxy is gamed up, goal drifts down.
true_goal = 50 + 1.2 * t + rng.normal(0, 1.0, t.size)
true_goal[target_start:] = true_goal[target_start-1] - 0.8 * (t[target_start:] - t[target_start-1]) \
                           + rng.normal(0, 1.0, t.size - target_start)
proxy = true_goal.copy()
proxy[target_start:] = true_goal[target_start-1] + 3.2 * (t[target_start:] - t[target_start-1]) \
                       + rng.normal(0, 1.0, t.size - target_start)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
ax.plot(t, proxy, "o-", color="#c0392b", lw=2.2, ms=3, label="measured proxy (the metric)")
ax.plot(t, true_goal, "o-", color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.2, ms=3, label="true goal (what we care about)")
ax.axvline(target_start - 0.5, color="#9aa0a6", ls="--", lw=1.4)
ax.text(target_start - 0.3, ax.get_ylim()[1]*0.96, "metric becomes a target", fontsize=8, color="#555")
ax.fill_between(t[target_start:], true_goal[target_start:], proxy[target_start:],
                color="#c0392b", alpha=0.10)
ax.text(18, 70, "the gap =\nGoodhart corruption", fontsize=8.5, color="#b03a2e", ha="center")
ax.set_xlabel("time"); ax.set_ylabel("performance")
ax.set_title("A measure tracks the goal — until it becomes a target")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Goodhart’s law in action. Before the metric carries stakes (left of the dashed line), the measured proxy tracks the true goal it was meant to capture. Once it becomes a target — tied to pay, ranking, or funding — effort flows into the proxy itself: it keeps climbing (gamed) while the true outcome stalls or declines. The gap that opens after targeting is the corruption Goodhart and Campbell describe.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
How metrics get gamed
When judgement is reduced to a narrow score, practitioners “juke the stats.” Muller catalogues the recurring tactics: (Muller 2018)

Teaching to the test. Effort shifts from the institution’s true purpose to whatever lifts the metric — narrowing a curriculum to the tested subjects and test-taking strategy.
Creaming / cherry-picking. Avoid the hard cases that might dent your success rate. Cardiac surgeons facing public scorecards refer the sickest patients elsewhere — improving their mortality numbers by not treating the people who most need treatment.
Lowering standards. Hit the target by making it easier — inflating graduation rates by making courses easier to pass.
Outright gaming and fraud. Misclassify or falsify: police downgrading felonies to misdemeanours (or thefts to “lost property”) so they don’t count against headline crime.
Short-termism. Sacrifice long-term investment (R&D, training) for an immediate numerical bump.
Goal displacement. The deepest pathology — the metric means replace the organisational ends they were meant to serve. Hitting the number becomes the mission.







NoteMuller’s case files




Education (No Child Left Behind). High-stakes testing narrowed curricula to math and English and, in several cities, produced systematic cheating — teachers altering answer sheets. (Muller 2018)
Healthcare (surgical scorecards). Public mortality rankings drove case-selection bias: surgeons declined high-risk patients to protect their stats. (Muller 2018)
Policing (Compstat / FBI reports). Pressure to “reduce crime” led to reclassifying offences downward and chasing easy street arrests over hard long-term cases. (Muller 2018)
The military (Vietnam body counts). McNamara’s reliance on body counts as a success metric produced fabricated numbers — and soldiers risking their lives to find corpses to hit the target. (Muller 2018)
Universities (rankings). League tables push schools toward glossy marketing and revenue-maximising programs over their educational mission. (Muller 2018)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
section: Surrogate outcomes: measuring the easy thing
text:
Surrogate outcomes: measuring the easy thing
A quieter failure: metric fixation pushes you to measure what is easy to count, not what matters. Organisations track inputs (money spent) or processes (workshops held) because the real outcomes are complex or delayed. (Muller 2018) Muller’s barb is worth memorising: “the ease of measuring may be inversely proportional to the significance of what is measured.” (Muller 2018) This is the field cousin of the surrogate-outcome and proxy-variable problems — only here the substitution is driven by managerial convenience, not statistical necessity.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
The costs
Metric fixation is not free, even when it isn’t gamed: (Muller 2018)

A data arms race. Effort migrates from frontline work to documenting it — more managers and analysts, producers spending their hours on reports instead of the job.
Discouraged innovation. Real innovation needs experiments that might fail; metrics punish failure, so people retreat to safe, established, measurable goals.
Demoralisation. Professionals resent the loss of autonomy and a scoreboard that conflicts with their vocational ethos — driving the most creative people out.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
The balanced verdict
Muller is not anti-measurement — he is anti-fixation. (Muller 2018) Metrics are a valuable complement to judgment, never a replacement for it. They work best when they are: (Muller 2018)

Internal and diagnostic — used by practitioners themselves to spot outliers and refine practice, not as a public cudgel.
Low-stakes — not the sole basis of pay or promotion, so the incentive to game is weak.
Bottom-up — designed with the people measured, who then trust the metric’s validity.

The mature stance the whole book has built toward: a number is an input to judgment, not a substitute for it. The skill is holding both — rigorous measurement and the wisdom to know its limits.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Attaching high stakes to a single number. Goodhart guarantees it will be gamed; the tighter the incentive, the faster the corruption. (Muller 2018)
Measuring the proxy and forgetting the goal. Optimise a surrogate and you can improve it while the real outcome decays — verify the proxy still tracks the thing you care about. (Muller 2018)
Counting inputs/processes as if they were outcomes. Workshops held is not learning gained; spend follows ease of measurement, not importance. (Muller 2018)
Ignoring gaming when designing a metric. If you can’t say how people will game it, you haven’t finished designing it. (Muller 2018)
Replacing professional judgment wholesale. Metrics inform judgment; they don’t substitute for the experience that knows which cases the number misreads. (Muller 2018)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Muller, The Tyranny of Metrics — metric fixation (replace judgment / accountability-as- transparency / extrinsic reward); Goodhart’s and Campbell’s laws and the self-conscious-actor mechanism; the gaming taxonomy (teaching to the test, creaming, lowering standards, fraud, short-termism, goal displacement); surrogate/proxy outcomes and “ease of measuring inversely proportional to significance”; the costs (data arms race, discouraged innovation, demoralisation); the balanced verdict (metrics as complement — internal, low-stakes, bottom-up); and the case files: NCLB, surgical scorecards, Compstat policing, Vietnam body counts, university rankings.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
Appendix · the mechanism

Goodhart formalised. Let the true goal be G and a proxy M=G+\eta with measurement slack \eta an agent can influence at cost. Passively, \eta\approx0 and \operatorname{Corr}(M,G)\approx1 — the proxy is informative. Introduce a reward R(M) for high M. A rational agent now invests effort to raise \eta (gaming) up to where marginal reward equals marginal cost, independently of G. The observed M rises, but \operatorname{Corr}(M,G) collapses — the very act of using M for control destroys its correlation with G. The proxy was a good measure because no one was optimising it; the optimisation is what breaks it.
Why surrogates drift. A surrogate S validated to satisfy \mathbb E[G\mid S]=f(S) under observational conditions need not preserve that relation under intervention on S — exactly the \operatorname{do} vs conditioning distinction. Optimising S intervenes on it, and the conditional relation that licensed S as a stand-in can fail — the managerial twin of reward hacking.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Tyranny of Metrics
text:
Connections

Builds on: concepts & measurement (validity and operationalisation — now under incentive pressure), the replication crisis (incentives distorting science), and proxy/surrogate variables.
Leads to: questionable research practices (Goodhart applied to researchers chasing significance) and from evidence to decision (using numbers without being ruled by them).
Reappears in: the limits of quantification, the book’s closing case for judgment.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
text: The Overture’s ethics covered protecting subjects — consent, the IRB, do-no-harm. This entry covers protecting the science and the public: don’t fabricate or falsify, disclose who funded you, claim only the authorship you earned — and, now that data is scraped from billions of people and models are deployed on millions, honour privacy and check for the biases your model will otherwise amplify. Integrity is not a compliance form; it is the precondition for the whole enterprise being worth anything.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
text:
Why it matters
Every technical safeguard in this Part — preregistration, honest reporting — assumes a researcher acting in good faith. This entry is about the good faith itself, and its institutional pressures. (Reinhart 2015; Bryman 2016) It matters because the failures here are not subtle statistical slips but betrayals of trust — faked data that kills clinical trials, hidden funding that warps public health, a deployed model that quietly discriminates. The quantitative researcher wields real power; integrity is what makes that power legitimate.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
text:
Scientific misconduct: FFP
The gravest breaches are FFP — Fabrication (making up data), Falsification (manipulating data or materials), and Plagiarism (appropriating others’ work). (Reinhart 2015) These are not honest QRPs; they are fraud.






NoteThe Anil Potti case



A Duke researcher whose cancer-genomics work promised personalised treatment — until it emerged that Potti had falsified both his data and his résumé. (Reinhart 2015) The fallout: papers retracted, clinical trials already under way cancelled, and a company built on the technology closed. Uncovering it took biostatisticians Baggerly and Coombes an estimated 2,000 hours of forensic reconstruction — possible only because they fought for the data and code. (Reinhart 2015) The case is why open science is an integrity tool, not just a convenience: fabrication hides where no one can re-run the analysis.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
section: Conflicts of interest and funding bias
text:
Conflicts of interest and funding bias
Money bends findings — sometimes subtly, sometimes by contract — so disclosure of funding and conflicts is a baseline duty. (Bryman 2016) The corpus’s cases: (Bryman 2016; Reinhart 2015)

Food industry. Agreements between researchers and Coca-Cola were found to let the company review and comment on research before publication and included early-termination clauses that could kill a study producing unfavourable results — pre-loading the published record toward favourable findings. (Bryman 2016)
Pharma. The Reboxetine case: published trials suggested Pfizer’s antidepressant worked, while three times as much unpublished data showed it didn’t — selective publication as a tool of funding bias. (Reinhart 2015)
Tobacco (and a sobering aside that statistics itself grew up entangled with the eugenics movement) — a long history of industry shaping which questions get asked and which answers get out. (Reinhart 2015)

The figure shows the aggregate footprint: when a funder controls design, review, and what gets published, the literature tilts even if no single study is fraudulent.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(4)
n = 600
# True effect is mildly unfavourable to the funder (centered slightly negative).
truth = -0.1
independent = rng.normal(truth, 1.0, n)
# Industry: favourable-design shift + selective publication (drop the most unfavourable).
industry_raw = rng.normal(truth + 0.6, 1.0, n)
industry = industry_raw[industry_raw > np.quantile(industry_raw, 0.35)]  # file-drawer the bad ones

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.0))
bins = np.linspace(-4, 4, 40)
ax.hist(independent, bins=bins, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.7, density=True, label="independently funded")
ax.hist(industry, bins=bins, color="#c0392b", alpha=0.55, density=True, label="industry funded (published)")
ax.axvline(truth, color="#1f8a4c", ls="--", lw=1.8)
ax.text(truth - 0.1, ax.get_ylim()[1]*0.92, "truth", color="#1f8a4c", fontsize=9, ha="right")
ax.axvline(np.mean(industry), color="#c0392b", ls=":", lw=1.4)
ax.set_xlabel("finding:  unfavourable  ←→  favourable to funder")
ax.set_ylabel("density"); ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_title("Who pays can tilt what gets published")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Funding bias in the published record. Each study’s finding lands on a scale from ‘unfavourable’ to ‘favourable’ to the funder’s interest. Independent studies (grey) scatter around the truth. But when an industry sponsor can shape the design, review results pre-publication, and halt unfavourable trials (red), the published distribution shifts toward favourable — not necessarily through fraud in any one study, but through selection at every stage. The reader sees only the published tilt.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
text:
Perverse incentives and authorship
Misconduct doesn’t happen in a vacuum — it grows in an incentive structure. The “frenzied quest” for publications, where tenure, grants, and prestige hang on a long list of top-journal papers, creates relentless pressure to cut corners or torture the data into significance. (Reinhart 2015) The same novelty premium that starves replication rewards the splashy and the fast.
Authorship is where credit and responsibility meet, and it has its own ethics: (Bryman 2016)

Authorship should reflect a substantial contribution. A statistician who shapes the analysis is a research partner deserving co-authorship, not unpaid technical support.
Honorary authorship (a senior name added for clout, having done little) and ghost authorship (the real authors omitted — common in industry-funded trials, to hide the sponsor’s hand) are both violations. (Bryman 2016)
The fear of being scooped pushes researchers to hoard data until they’ve wrung every paper from it — in tension with the openness science needs.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
section: Data ethics in the age of harvested data
text:
Data ethics in the age of harvested data
Classic research ethics assumed you collected the data from consenting subjects. Big data breaks that assumption: much research now uses harvested data — social media, administrative records — generated with no research in mind. (Bryman 2016) New duties follow: (Bryman 2016)

The right to erasure. If someone deletes a post, immortalising it in a paper bypasses their right to withdraw and can expose them to real harm. Harvested ≠ free to use forever.
Consent friction. Users technically “agree” via Terms of Service they never read and don’t understand — a thin substitute for informed consent.
Anonymisation and re-identification. Truly anonymising data is hard and gets harder as outside datasets grow (re-identification risk rises with time); proper scrubbing causes real delays — up to 20 months for some Facebook data. (Bryman 2016) “It’s public” is not an ethics argument.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
text:
Algorithmic fairness before deployment
The book’s causal and predictive ML tools don’t sit in a notebook — they get deployed, and a model is only as fair as the data that trained it. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) Models learn the historical biases in their training data — the documented gender and racial bias in word embeddings is the textbook case — and then amplify them at scale. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) Acting on a biased model in a clinical or policy setting produces systemic injustice, efficiently. The obligation is concrete: fairness checks before deployment — verify the model performs (and estimates effects) accurately across subgroups, not just on average. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) A model that is right on average and wrong for a protected group is not “mostly right”; it is discriminatory. This is the tyranny of metrics with the stakes of real lives — and the reason interpretability and fairness auditing are ethical, not optional.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Treating QRPs and fraud as a spectrum you can drift along. Honest flexibility and FFP differ in kind; never falsify, full stop. (Reinhart 2015)
Non-disclosure of funding/conflicts. Even good-faith work needs the funding source on the table; contracts that gate publication are a red flag. (Bryman 2016)
Honorary or ghost authorship. Credit must track contribution; hidden authors hide hidden interests. (Bryman 2016)
“It’s public data, so consent isn’t needed.” Harvested data carries privacy duties — the right to erasure, re-identification risk, and meaningful (not ToS) consent. (Bryman 2016)
Deploying a model without a fairness audit. Average accuracy can mask severe subgroup harm; check performance across protected groups before go-live. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — FFP and the Anil Potti misconduct case (falsified data/résumé; retractions; cancelled trials; Baggerly–Coombes ~2,000 hours); funding bias and selective publication (Reboxetine); the publish-or-perish incentive driving corner-cutting.
Bryman, Social Research Methods — conflicts of interest / funding bias (Coca-Cola review/termination clauses); authorship ethics (substantial contribution; statisticians as partners; honorary and ghost authorship); big-data ethics — harvested data, the right to erasure, ToS-consent friction, anonymisation/re-identification (Facebook ~20 months).
Chernozhukov / Jacobucci — algorithmic fairness: ML encoding/amplifying gender and racial bias (word embeddings), the harm of deploying biased models, and fairness checks across subgroups before deployment.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
text:
Appendix · two formal notes

Funding bias as selection. Let a study’s finding be \theta=\theta_0+\varepsilon around a truth \theta_0. A sponsor’s influence acts as (i) a design shift \theta_0\to\theta_0+\delta (favourable framing) and (ii) selective publication \Pr(\text{publish})=\pi(\theta) increasing in favourability. The published mean is \mathbb E[\theta\mid\text{published}] = \theta_0+\delta+\operatorname{Cov}(\theta,\pi)/\mathbb E[\pi] > \theta_0 — biased upward even if every individual study is internally valid. The distortion lives in the selection, which is why registered reports (publication independent of result) are the structural fix.
Fairness across subgroups. A model can satisfy small overall error while badly failing a group g: \mathbb E[L]=\sum_g \Pr(g)\,\mathbb E[L\mid g] is dominated by large groups, so a small \Pr(g) lets \mathbb E[L\mid g] be large undetected. Fairness auditing reports the per-group losses (or per-group treatment-effect accuracy) rather than the aggregate — the same lesson as the rare-class accuracy paradox, now an ethical mandate.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Research Integrity & Data Ethics
text:
Connections

Builds on: ethics & human subjects (consent/IRB, here extended past it), questionable research practices (the honest-vs-fraud line; funding-driven selective publication), open science (transparency as an integrity tool), and causal ML (fairness in deployment).
Leads to: from evidence to decision (whose biases and incentives shape how numbers are used) and the limits of quantification.
Underwrites: the trust that makes every other entry’s rigour worth having.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text: Questionable research practices flourish in the dark — in analytic choices no one sees and null results no one publishes. Open science is the architecture that turns the lights on: preregister the plan before the data arrive, share the data and code so anyone can re-run them, and replicate to see if the effect was ever real. None of it requires trusting the researcher’s virtue; it changes the system so honesty is the path of least resistance.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text:
Why it matters
Every fix in the last entry was something an individual must remember to do. Open science makes integrity structural — baked into how studies are registered, reviewed, and published — so it survives ordinary human incentives. (Reinhart 2015) It is the infrastructure of trust, and the reason this very book is built as a reproducible document. It does not make weak studies strong, but it makes all studies checkable — the precondition for science to self-correct.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text:
Two kinds of “it holds up”
The vocabulary matters because the two are routinely confused: (Reinhart 2015)

Reproducibility (computational). An independent analyst, given your same data and same code, gets the same numbers. This is a floor, not a ceiling — and shockingly often unmet. If code and data were posted, a colleague could simply read the code instead of spending thousands of hours reconstructing your steps. (Reinhart 2015)
Replicability (empirical). A new study, with new data and subjects but the same design, finds the same effect. Replication is called the heart of science — yet is rarely done, because it is laborious and brings little professional reward. (Reinhart 2015)

A finding can be perfectly reproducible (the code runs) and utterly un-replicable (the effect was noise). You need both.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
section: Preregistration: closing the garden of forking paths
text:
Preregistration: closing the garden of forking paths
The most direct antidote to researcher degrees of freedom: preregistration — committing to the hypothesis and the full analysis plan before seeing the data. (Reinhart 2015) It works by drawing a bright line: (Reinhart 2015)

Confirmatory research tests a priori hypotheses with the pre-specified analysis — these p-values mean what they claim.
Exploratory research searches the data for patterns — legitimate and valuable, but its findings are hypotheses to test later, not confirmed results.

By fixing the outliers, covariates, and outcome in advance, preregistration closes the garden of forking paths: you can no longer (even unconsciously) pick the analysis most flattering to your hypothesis. (Reinhart 2015) It doesn’t forbid exploration — it forbids disguising exploration as confirmation.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text:
Registered reports: defeating publication bias
Preregistration fixes the analysis; registered reports fix the publication incentive. (Reinhart 2015) Here the journal peer-reviews the study design before any data are collected and, if the design is sound, grants in-principle acceptance — a promise to publish regardless of whether the results are significant. (Reinhart 2015) This strikes directly at publication bias / the file drawer: when null results are guaranteed a home, the literature stops being a biased sample of only the “eye-catching” findings. (Reinhart 2015)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text:
Replication: direct and conceptual
Replication is the system’s error-correction, in two forms: (Reinhart 2015)

Direct replication recreates the original conditions as precisely as possible — did this specific finding hold?
Conceptual replication tests the same idea with different settings, subjects, or measures — does the underlying effect generalise?

A failure to replicate suggests the original was a fluke (noise — the winner’s curse made visible) or depended on unmeasured context. (Reinhart 2015) The figure shows the pattern the Reproducibility Project found across psychology: replication effects are systematically smaller than the originals, and many vanish — exactly what truth inflation predicts.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(12)
k = 100
# True effects are mostly small; originals are significance-selected (inflated upward).
true = np.abs(rng.normal(0.2, 0.15, k))
sig_thresh = 0.25
orig = true + np.abs(rng.normal(0.25, 0.12, k))   # winner's curse: originals overshoot
repl = true + rng.normal(0, 0.10, k)              # replications scatter around the truth

failed = repl < sig_thresh
frac_fail = failed.mean()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.8, 4.4))
lim = 0, max(orig.max(), repl.max()) * 1.05
ax.axhspan(lim[0], sig_thresh, color="#c0392b", alpha=0.07)
ax.plot(lim, lim, color="#9aa0a6", ls="--", lw=1.4, label="equal effect (45°)")
ax.scatter(orig[~failed], repl[~failed], s=22, color="#1f8a4c", alpha=0.7, label="replicated")
ax.scatter(orig[failed], repl[failed], s=22, color="#c0392b", alpha=0.7,
           label=f"failed to replicate ({frac_fail*100:.0f}%)")
ax.axhline(sig_thresh, color="#c0392b", lw=1.0, ls=":")
ax.text(lim[1]*0.6, sig_thresh*0.4, "replication not significant", color="#b03a2e", fontsize=8)
ax.set_xlabel("original published effect size")
ax.set_ylabel("replication effect size")
ax.set_title("Replications shrink toward the truth")
ax.set_xlim(lim); ax.set_ylim(lim)
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Original vs replication effect sizes (Reproducibility-Project style). Each point is a study: its originally published effect (x) against the effect found when independently repeated (y). Almost all points fall below the 45° line — replications are smaller — and many land below the significance threshold (shaded), i.e. fail to replicate. This is truth inflation at the scale of a literature: published effects are systematically exaggerated, and replication is what reveals it.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text:
Data, code, and reproducible workflows
The practical machinery of openness: (Reinhart 2015; Bryman 2016)

Open data, code, and materials — post everything, so verification is reading the code, not archaeology. A study by Wicherts found that, despite a requirement to share, nearly three- quarters of authors refused when asked — and refusers were more likely to have made statistical errors. (Reinhart 2015) Reluctance to share is itself a signal.
Literate programming. Tools like Sweave and Quarto embed the analysis code inside the document, so every table and figure is regenerated from raw data — no copy-paste. (This encyclopedia is built that way: each figure is executable code, not a pasted image.) (Reinhart 2015)
Version control. Recording every change to the analysis means you can always reconstruct how a specific number was produced. (Reinhart 2015)
Permanent repositories — GenBank, Dryad, Figshare — give data and code citable, lasting homes. (Reinhart 2015)







WarningWhen the code is the bug



Openness catches errors that peer review never could. Biostatisticians Baggerly and Coombes spent roughly 2,000 hours trying to reproduce a Duke cancer-genomics study (Potti) and uncovered a mess — mislabelled data, duplicated samples, columns shifted by one — that invalidated research already guiding clinical trials. (Reinhart 2015) And Reinhart’s plain warning: manually copying data into spreadsheets is “tremendously error-prone,” and Excel’s regression is often numerically ill-behaved. A result no one can re-run is a result no one has truly checked. (Reinhart 2015)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text:
The incentives, and the limits
Why isn’t all science already open? Because the incentives fight it: (Reinhart 2015)

The novelty premium. Careers reward novel, groundbreaking results in prestige journals; there is little glory in replicating others or cleaning code for public release. (Reinhart 2015)
Fear of being scooped. Researchers worry that sharing hard-won data lets competitors beat them to the next finding. (Reinhart 2015)

And the honest caveat: open science is necessary but not sufficient. Transparency cannot rescue a study that is underpowered or built on a flawed premise — it just lets everyone see that it is. (Reinhart 2015) It makes science checkable; it doesn’t make it correct.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Confusing reproducibility with replicability. Code that runs is not an effect that’s real; you need both. (Reinhart 2015)
Presenting exploratory findings as confirmatory. Without preregistration the distinction blurs and p-values lose their meaning; label which is which. (Reinhart 2015)
“Data available on request.” Wicherts shows requests are routinely refused; deposit data and code in a real repository. (Reinhart 2015)
Spreadsheet pipelines. Copy-paste and Excel formulas are error-prone and unauditable; use scripted, version-controlled, literate workflows. (Reinhart 2015)
Expecting openness to fix bad design. It reveals problems; it doesn’t cure underpowered or ill-conceived studies. (Reinhart 2015)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — reproducibility (computational) vs replicability; preregistration (confirmatory vs exploratory; closing the garden of forking paths); registered reports and in-principle acceptance vs publication bias; direct vs conceptual replication; open data/code, literate programming (Sweave/Quarto), version control, repositories; incentive problems (novelty premium, scooping) and “necessary but not sufficient”; the Reproducibility Project, Wicherts (¾ refused to share; refusers more error-prone), and the Duke/Potti genomics debacle (Baggerly & Coombes, ~2,000 hours).
Bryman, Social Research Methods — replication norms and open materials/data sharing as part of credible research practice.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
section: Appendix · why preregistration restores the p-value
text:
Appendix · why preregistration restores the p-value

The forking-paths inflation, formally. Suppose the data are consistent with K analysis paths, each a valid test of level \alpha, and the analyst (consciously or not) reports the most significant. The realised false-positive rate is 
\alpha_{\text{eff}} = \Pr\Big(\min_{k\le K} p_k < \alpha\Big) \approx 1-(1-\alpha)^{K} \gg \alpha,
 the same multiplicity math as explicit multiple comparisons — even when only one test is reported — because the choice of path was data-dependent. Preregistration fixes K=1 in advance, restoring \alpha_{\text{eff}}=\alpha. It is multiplicity control applied to the decisions you’d otherwise make implicitly.
Registered reports and selection. Let publication probability be \pi(\text{sig}). Ordinary publishing has \pi(\text{significant})\gg\pi(\text{null}), so the published effect distribution is the truth conditioned on significance — inflated (the winner’s curse). In-principle acceptance sets \pi independent of the result, so the published distribution is unbiased for the truth. Replication then samples that unbiased process and regresses estimates toward the real effect — the shrinkage in the figure.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Open & Reproducible Science
text:
Connections

Builds on: questionable research practices (the failures this fixes structurally), the replication crisis, and power (what openness can’t fix).
Leads to: communicating findings (transparency in the write-up) and from evidence to decision (trusting a checkable literature).
Embodied in: this manual itself — every figure is executable, reproducible code under version control.

title: VI · Doing Research in the World
text:
Academic training tends to stop at the clean estimate. Real research — in the field, for a client, inside an organisation — begins there. This module is what separates someone who can run a model from someone who can be trusted with a question. It is the project’s distinctive layer, and the source library is unusually strong here.
The groundwork — measurement, research design, ethics & human subjects, and messy data — was laid in the Overture and Foundations. This module is the critical conscience that surrounds all of it: the dysfunctions, incentives, and judgment of research done in the real world.

title: VI · Doing Research in the World
text:
The critical conscience
Two sources give this module its edge. Muller’s The Tyranny of Metrics is the warning that measuring a thing changes behaviour around it — that the number you optimise can quietly become the goal, displacing what it was meant to track. Reinhart’s Statistics Done Wrong keeps the methods honest. Together they make the case that judgement — knowing when not to trust the number — is the final skill of the quantitative researcher.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
