VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text: The analysis ends when someone acts on it — which means the last mile is communication, and it is where rigour most often dies. A correct estimate buried in stars and jargon informs no one; a trivial effect dramatised by a truncated axis misinforms everyone. The same discipline you brought to the model must reach the chart, the number, and the sentence: show the data, show the uncertainty, and never let the presentation claim more than the evidence.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text:
Why it matters
A finding that decision-makers can’t understand — or that quietly misleads them — is worse than no finding. Reinhart and Bryman treat communication as a part of statistical integrity, not polish: the choices of number, frame, and chart determine what people actually believe. (Reinhart 2015; Bryman 2016) This is where the open-science transparency reaches the reader, and where a researcher takes responsibility not just for being right but for being correctly understood.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text:
Communicate uncertainty, not stars
P-values and significance “stars” are a famously poor way to report a result: a p-value says nothing about the size of an effect, only how incompatible the data are with a null. (Reinhart 2015) Reinhart’s quip is that p-values are like mosquitoes — impossible to swat out of the literature despite their faults. (Reinhart 2015) Report confidence intervals and effect sizes instead: a range of plausible values shows how big and how sure in one stroke. (Reinhart 2015)

Natural frequencies beat probabilities
For risks and base rates, how you phrase the number changes whether people grasp it. People understand natural frequencies (“10 out of 1,000”) far better than the mathematically identical probabilities or percentages (“1%”). (Reinhart 2015) The classic case is the mammogram:






NoteThe mammogram, in frequencies



Suppose 0.8% of women have breast cancer and the test has a 7% false-positive rate. Told as probabilities, a positive result sounds terrifying. Reframed as natural frequencies, the base-rate truth is plain: of 1,000 women, about 8 have cancer, while roughly 70 healthy women also test positive — so a positive result means cancer only about 1 time in 10. (Reinhart 2015) Same arithmetic, opposite intuition. The frequency framing makes Bayes’ theorem visible.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text:
Significant ≠ important
The most common reporting error in social science: conflating statistical significance with practical importance. (Reinhart 2015) Two failure modes, opposite directions:

Trivially significant. With a large enough sample (say 70,000 observations), a minuscule, useless effect becomes “statistically significant.” Significance is partly a statement about n, not importance. (Reinhart 2015)
Absence of evidence ≠ evidence of absence. A non-significant result (p>0.05) is not proof of no effect; it often means the study was underpowered and couldn’t tell. (Reinhart 2015) And even a non-significant point estimate remains the best available guess of the truth given the data — “no effect” is rarely the right summary. (Reinhart 2015)

Always pair the question “is it real?” with “is it big enough to matter?” — and answer the second with an effect size and its interval, not a star.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text:
Don’t let the chart lie
Graphics should show the data, not the maker’s cleverness. (Bryman 2016) Bryman leans on Tufte’s principles of graphical integrity: (Bryman 2016)

Honest scales. Truncated or zoomed y-axes that don’t start at zero exaggerate small differences — the single most common deception in business and media charts.
Proportional ink. The size of the effect shown should match the effect in the data (Tufte’s “lie factor”).
No chartjunk. Strip content-free decoration that distracts from the substance.
Show uncertainty. Include error bars — clearly labelled as SD or CI — so readers can see how stable the values are.

The figure makes the axis trick undeniable: identical numbers, two charts, opposite impressions.


Show the figure code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

groups = ["Group A", "Group B"]
values = [99.0, 101.0]
colors = ["#9aa0a6", "#1f8a4c"]

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(7.6, 3.8))

axL.bar(groups, values, color=colors)
axL.set_ylim(98, 102)                       # truncated axis: exaggerates
axL.set_title("Misleading: truncated axis")
axL.set_ylabel("score")
axL.text(0.5, 101.6, "looks like a huge gap!", ha="center", color="#c0392b", fontsize=8.5)

axR.bar(groups, values, color=colors)
axR.set_ylim(0, 110)                         # honest zero baseline
axR.set_title("Honest: zero baseline")
axR.set_ylabel("score")
axR.text(0.5, 60, "a ~2% difference", ha="center", color="#1f3a5f", fontsize=8.5)

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The same data, honest and dishonest. Two groups differ by 2 units (101 vs 99). Left: a y-axis truncated to 98–102 makes the difference look enormous — a doubling, visually. Right: the same bars on a zero-baseline axis show the difference for what it is: about 2%. No number changed; only the axis. Truncated baselines are the most common way a chart misleads while staying ‘technically accurate’.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text:
Know your audience
You are responsible for how your results are interpreted, so tailor the message: (Reinhart 2015; Bryman 2016)

Numeracy. Less numerate readers often prefer verbal risk labels (“likely,” “improbable”) to numbers. Their exact mental values vary between people, but the relative order is consistent — so verbal scales communicate ranking reliably even when they blur magnitude. (Bryman 2016)
Framing effects. “30% will survive” and “70% will die” are identical facts that land very differently; the responsible move is to present both frames so the listener isn’t steered. (Reinhart 2015)

Tailoring the message is not spin — it is meeting the audience where they are so the true picture lands.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text:
Honest write-up
Transparency in reporting is the antidote to the replication crisis: (Reinhart 2015; Bryman 2016)

Report everything you measured, not just what reached significance — selective reporting is outcome-reporting bias and poisons meta-analysis.
Detail the methodology — sample-size justification, stopping rules, and any deviations from the protocol.
Acknowledge the mess. Good research is messy; stating limitations and mistakes makes the work more credible, not less.
Crosstabs before regressions. Lead with the simplest transparent comparison (means, cross-tabs) before the complex model, so readers can see the raw pattern the model claims to explain. (Bryman 2016)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reporting stars instead of intervals. A p-value hides the effect size; give the confidence interval. (Reinhart 2015)
Probabilities where frequencies would clarify. For risk and base rates, “8 in 1,000” beats “0.8%.” (Reinhart 2015)
Truncated axes. A non-zero baseline inflates trivial differences; default to a zero baseline for bar charts and label any break. (Bryman 2016)
Equating significance with importance — or non-significance with zero. Report effect sizes; remember absence of evidence isn’t evidence of absence. (Reinhart 2015)
One-sided framing. “70% survive” vs “30% die” steers the reader; present both. (Reinhart 2015)
Cherry-picking reported outcomes. Report all measures and analyses, including the boring ones. (Reinhart 2015)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — confidence intervals over p-values (“mosquitoes”); natural frequencies and the mammogram base-rate example; statistical vs practical significance (trivial significance at n=70{,}000; absence of evidence is not evidence of absence; the point estimate as best guess); framing effects (30% survive / 70% die); reporting all outcomes / outcome-reporting bias.
Bryman, Social Research Methods — Tufte’s graphical integrity (honest scales, proportional ink, no chartjunk, show uncertainty); audience numeracy and verbal vs numerical risk; honest write-up (methodology detail, acknowledging limitations, crosstabs before regressions).
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
section: Appendix · the numbers behind the framings
text:
Appendix · the numbers behind the framings

The mammogram, by Bayes. With prevalence \Pr(C)=0.008, sensitivity \approx1, and false-positive rate \Pr(+\mid \lnot C)=0.07, 
\Pr(C\mid +)=\frac{\Pr(+\mid C)\Pr(C)}{\Pr(+\mid C)\Pr(C)+\Pr(+\mid\lnot C)\Pr(\lnot C)}
\approx \frac{1\cdot 0.008}{0.008+0.07\cdot0.992}\approx 0.10.
 Per 1,000 women: \approx 8 true positives vs \approx 70 false positives — the natural-frequency statement is just this arithmetic made countable, which is why people reason with it correctly while the percentage version triggers the base-rate fallacy.
Tufte’s lie factor. \text{lie factor}=\dfrac{\text{size of effect shown in graphic}}{\text{size of
effect in data}}. Graphical integrity demands \approx1. A bar chart with a baseline at 98 instead of 0 on values 99 vs 101 shows a 3{:}1 visual ratio for a 1.02{:}1 data ratio — a lie factor near 3.
Why significance tracks n. For a two-group mean difference, the test statistic is t=\dfrac{\hat\delta}{s\sqrt{2/n}}\propto \hat\delta\sqrt{n}. Fixing a real but tiny \hat\delta, t\to\infty as n\to\infty — so any nonzero effect becomes significant with enough data. Significance answers “is it distinguishable from zero?”; the effect size answers “does it matter?”
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text:
Connections

Builds on: confidence intervals and hypothesis testing (what to report and what it means), Bayes’ theorem (natural frequencies), and exploratory data analysis (graphical principles).
Leads to: from evidence to decision (a clearly communicated estimate is the input to a choice) and the limits of quantification.
Guards: every result in the manual against being technically correct and practically misleading.

title: Communicating Quantitative Findings
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Why are confidence intervals and effect sizes better than p-values/stars for communicating a result?”
“Why are natural frequencies easier to understand than probabilities? Work the mammogram example.”
“Explain the difference between statistical significance and practical importance, including why a tiny effect becomes significant at large n and why ‘absence of evidence is not evidence of absence’.”
“What are Tufte’s principles of graphical integrity, and how do truncated axes mislead?”
“How should you tailor communication to an audience (numeracy, verbal vs numerical, framing effects), and what does an honest write-up include?”
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: Sensitivity Analysis & External Validity
text: A causal estimate is the beginning of an argument, not the end. Two honest questions remain. First, how fragile is it — how strong would an unobserved confounder have to be to erase it? Selection-on- observables rests on an untestable assumption, so the grown-up move is not to pretend it holds but to quantify how much it could be violated before your conclusion breaks. Second, where does it apply — a clean effect here need not transport there. This entry is the credibility coda to the summit: stress-testing assumptions, and knowing the reach of an answer.

title: Sensitivity Analysis & External Validity
text:
Why it matters
Every design in this Part rests on an assumption you cannot verify — CIA, exclusion, parallel trends, continuity. Maturity is not asserting the assumption but bounding the consequences of its failure, and being clear about whose effect you actually estimated. (Cunningham 2021) These are the tools that separate a credible empirical claim from a fragile one — and the natural bridge to Part VI, where estimates meet a messy world that rarely matches the study sample.

title: Sensitivity Analysis & External Validity
text:
The core question
Selection-on-observables designs assume that conditioning on X makes treatment as-good-as-random — untestable, because the confounder you fear is unobserved. (Cunningham 2021) Sensitivity analysis converts that worry into a number by asking: how strong would an unobserved confounder have to be to explain away the result? (Cunningham 2021) If the estimate only breaks under an implausibly powerful hidden factor, it is robust; if a whisper of confounding undoes it, it is fragile. You never prove no confounding — you measure how much it would take.

title: Sensitivity Analysis & External Validity
text:
Rosenbaum bounds (Γ)
For matched designs, Paul Rosenbaum’s bounds introduce a parameter \Gamma measuring hidden bias: the differential odds that two units identical on observed X receive treatment. (Cunningham 2021)

\Gamma=1: no hidden bias — matched units are equally likely to be treated (a perfect experiment).
\Gamma=2: one unit is twice as likely as its match to be treated, because of something unseen.

The procedure: raise \Gamma from 1 upward (1.1, 1.2, …) and watch the treatment effect’s p-value; the breaking point is the \Gamma at which significance is lost. (Cunningham 2021) A result that survives to \Gamma=5 is highly robust — only an enormous hidden bias could overturn it; one that dies at \Gamma=1.1 is hostage to the faintest unobserved difference. The figure contrasts the two.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

gamma = np.linspace(1, 5, 200)
# Illustrative worst-case p-value as a function of hidden bias Γ (monotone increasing).
# Robust study: strong signal, p climbs slowly. Fragile: weak signal, p climbs fast.
p_robust = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(2.2 * (gamma - 3.4))))   # crosses 0.05 near Γ≈3.4
p_robust = 0.001 + 0.6 * p_robust
p_fragile = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(6.0 * (gamma - 1.2))))  # crosses 0.05 near Γ≈1.2
p_fragile = 0.02 + 0.7 * p_fragile

def crossing(g, p): return g[np.argmin(np.abs(p - 0.05))]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.2))
ax.plot(gamma, p_robust, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.4, label="robust study")
ax.plot(gamma, p_fragile, color="#d98a29", lw=2.4, label="fragile study")
ax.axhline(0.05, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.4)
ax.text(4.4, 0.065, "p = 0.05", color="#c0392b", fontsize=8)
for p, c in [(p_robust, "#1f3a5f"), (p_fragile, "#d98a29")]:
    gx = crossing(gamma, p)
    ax.axvline(gx, color=c, ls=":", lw=1.2)
    ax.text(gx + 0.05, 0.5, f"breaks at\nΓ ≈ {gx:.1f}", color=c, fontsize=8)
ax.set_xlabel("hidden-bias parameter  Γ  (1 = no hidden bias)")
ax.set_ylabel("worst-case p-value")
ax.set_title("How much unobserved confounding would it take to break the result?")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="center right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Rosenbaum-bounds sensitivity curves. As the hidden-bias parameter Γ rises (a stronger unobserved confounder), the worst-case p-value for the treatment effect climbs. The robust study (blue) stays significant until Γ ≈ 3.4 — only a large unseen bias could explain it away. The fragile study (orange) crosses p = 0.05 at Γ ≈ 1.2 — even a slight departure from as-good-as-random assignment dissolves it. Same point estimate, very different credibility.

title: Sensitivity Analysis & External Validity
text:
Oster’s δ: coefficient stability
A complementary idea, due to Emily Oster (and used in causal ML by Chernozhukov), reads robustness off how the treatment coefficient moves as you add controls. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) The logic: if piling in observed controls barely budges the coefficient while the R^2 rises substantially, then the unobservables — assumed to behave like the observables — are unlikely to drive it to zero. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) The key assumption is \delta=1: unobserved confounders are at most as important as the observed controls in explaining the outcome. Setting \delta=1 yields a bias-adjusted bound on the coefficient; if that bound stays away from zero, the effect is robust. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) (Coefficient stability alone is not enough — it must be paired with the R^2 movement, or a coefficient can be stable simply because the controls explain nothing.)

title: Sensitivity Analysis & External Validity
text:
The E-value
A third framing measures sensitivity in the language of association strength. The E-value is the minimum strength of association — on the risk-ratio scale — that an unmeasured confounder would need with both the treatment and the outcome to fully explain away the observed effect. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) A large E-value (say, the confounder would need a risk ratio of 4 with each) means only a very strong hidden factor could do it; if that exceeds the association of any measured control, the result is credible. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) The E-value’s appeal is interpretability: it puts the burden of doubt in concrete terms a reader can weigh against known predictors.

title: Sensitivity Analysis & External Validity
text:
Internal vs external validity
A study has internal validity if it correctly identifies the effect for the sample under study, and external validity if that finding applies to other populations, settings, and times. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) The designs of this Part are engineered for internal validity — and a recurring theme is that they often buy it by narrowing external validity. A causal estimate can be airtight and parochial.

title: Sensitivity Analysis & External Validity
text:
SATE vs PATE
The distinction sharpens with two estimands: (Gerber and Green 2012)

SATE — the sample average treatment effect, for the units actually in the study. Its standard errors are smaller (it treats the sample’s potential outcomes as fixed).
PATE — the population average treatment effect, for the broader super-population the sample was meant to represent.

The transportability problem: a clean SATE may not equal the PATE if the sample was a convenience sample or if effects are heterogeneous across the population. (Gerber and Green 2012) An experiment on volunteer undergraduates can be internally flawless and still mispredict the effect in the general public — not because the analysis is wrong, but because the people are different.

title: Sensitivity Analysis & External Validity
text:
The local designs, revisited
Two of the most credible designs are precisely the most local — a trade we’ve met before: (Angrist and Pischke 2009)

LATE identifies the effect only for compliers — those moved by the instrument. Always-takers and never-takers are invisible, and their effects may differ. A draft-lottery LATE speaks for lottery-induced soldiers, not volunteers.
RD identifies the effect only at the cutoff; extrapolating to units far away leans on bold functional-form assumptions. (Cunningham 2021)

Internal validity and external validity are often in tension: the design tricks that make a comparison airtight (a narrow window, a specific instrument) are exactly what localise the answer.

title: Sensitivity Analysis & External Validity
text:
Generalising honestly
Three routes extend a local or sample estimate toward a target: (Gerber and Green 2012)

Replication — re-run the study in new contexts and see whether the effect is stable; the single most convincing evidence of transportability.
Meta-analysis — pool many studies (often in a Bayesian frame), weighting by precision and accounting for sampling bias, to triangulate a general effect.
Re-weighting — if the sample’s covariate distribution differs from the target population’s, re-weight (e.g. inverse-probability weighting toward the target) so the estimate reflects their composition, not the study’s. Transportability becomes a weighting problem — if you observe the covariates that drive effect heterogeneity.

title: Sensitivity Analysis & External Validity
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reporting a point estimate with no sensitivity analysis. If CIA is untestable, show how much confounding would break the result (Rosenbaum Γ, Oster δ, E-value). Silence is not robustness. (Cunningham 2021)
Coefficient stability without the R^2 movement. A coefficient can be stable because the controls explain nothing; Oster’s logic needs both. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)
Reading a LATE or RD estimate as a population effect. They are local — to compliers, to the cutoff. Name the subpopulation. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Generalising from a convenience sample. A flawless SATE on volunteers need not be the PATE; re-weight or replicate before extrapolating. (Gerber and Green 2012)
Extrapolating RD far from the cutoff. Beyond the boundary you are trusting functional form, not the design. (Cunningham 2021)

title: Sensitivity Analysis & External Validity
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Cunningham, The Mixtape — sensitivity analysis as the response to untestable CIA; Rosenbaum bounds and the hidden-bias parameter \Gamma (differential odds of assignment; raise \Gamma until significance breaks; robust if it survives large \Gamma); RD estimates as local to the cutoff.
Chernozhukov et al., Applied Causal Inference Powered by ML and AI — Oster’s \delta / coefficient stability (coefficient movement + R^2, \delta=1 assuming unobservables as important as observables) and the E-value (minimum confounder association with treatment and outcome to nullify the effect).
Angrist & Pischke — internal vs external validity; LATE as a local (complier) effect with limited transportability.
Gerber & Green, Field Experiments — SATE vs PATE and the transportability problem from convenience sampling/heterogeneity; replication, meta-analysis, and re-weighting as routes to generalisation.

title: Sensitivity Analysis & External Validity
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Rosenbaum’s odds bound. For two matched units j,k with identical observed X but treatment probabilities \pi_j,\pi_k, hidden bias is bounded by 
\frac1\Gamma \le \frac{\pi_j(1-\pi_k)}{\pi_k(1-\pi_j)} \le \Gamma .
 \Gamma=1 forces \pi_j=\pi_k (no hidden bias). For each \Gamma>1 one computes the worst-case p-value over all assignments consistent with the bound; the smallest \Gamma pushing it above 0.05 is the study’s breaking point — the curve in the figure.
Oster’s bias-adjusted coefficient. Let \dot\beta,\dot R^2 be from the short regression (no controls) and \tilde\beta,\tilde R^2 from the controlled one, with R^2_{\max} the (hypothetical) R^2 from regressing on observables and unobservables. The bias-adjusted estimate is approximately 
\beta^\ast \approx \tilde\beta - \delta\,(\dot\beta-\tilde\beta)\,\frac{R^2_{\max}-\tilde R^2}{\tilde R^2-\dot R^2}.
 Setting \delta=1 (unobservables as important as observables) and a plausible R^2_{\max} gives a bound; robustness means \beta^\ast stays away from 0.
E-value (risk-ratio scale). For an observed risk ratio RR\ge1, the E-value is 
\text{E-value}=RR+\sqrt{RR\,(RR-1)},
 the minimum association (with both treatment and outcome) an unmeasured confounder would need to reduce RR to 1. Larger E-value ⇒ more robust.
Re-weighting SATE to PATE. If effects vary as \tau(x) and the target population has covariate density g(x) while the sample has f(x), 
\text{PATE}=\mathbb E_{g}[\tau(X)]=\int \tau(x)\,g(x)\,dx=\mathbb E_{f}\!\left[\frac{g(X)}{f(X)}\,\tau(X)\right],
 so weighting sample units by g(X)/f(X) transports the estimate — valid only if \tau(x) is identified and the reweighting covariates capture the heterogeneity.

title: Sensitivity Analysis & External Validity
text:
Connections

Builds on: matching & propensity scores (the untestable CIA this stress-tests), IV/LATE and RD (the local effects whose reach this examines), and IPW (re-weighting to a target).
Closes: the summit’s logic — from defining an effect to estimating it to stress-testing and generalising it.
Leads to: Part VI · doing research in the world, where external validity, replication, and the gap between a clean estimate and a messy decision become the whole game.

title: Sensitivity Analysis & External Validity
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“What question does sensitivity analysis answer, and why is it the mature response to the untestable conditional independence assumption?”
“Explain Rosenbaum bounds and the parameter Γ: what does Γ measure, and how does the breaking-point Γ indicate robustness?”
“Explain Oster’s δ / coefficient stability and the E-value as two ways to quantify sensitivity to unobserved confounding.”
“Distinguish internal from external validity, and SATE from PATE. Why might a clean sample estimate fail to transport?”
“Why are LATE and RD estimates ‘local’, and how do replication, meta-analysis, and re-weighting help generalise causal findings?”

title: Randomized Experiments: The Gold Standard
text: Selection bias is the difference between the treated and control groups in their untreated state. Randomization annihilates it: if a coin decides who is treated, treatment status carries no information about anyone’s potential outcomes, so the groups are comparable by construction. A simple difference in means then estimates the average effect — no controls, no model, no assumption you can’t defend. This is the benchmark; every observational design in this Part is an argument for why it is as if you ran this experiment.

title: Randomized Experiments: The Gold Standard
text:
Why it matters
The last entry reduced causal inference to one obstacle: the naive comparison equals the effect plus selection bias, and you can’t see the selection-bias term because it lives in unobserved counterfactuals. A randomized experiment is the gold standard because it makes that term zero by design — severing the statistical link between treatment and every other variable that predicts the outcome, observed or unobserved. (Gerber and Green 2012) Nothing else in this Part achieves that for free; everything else assumes a version of it. Understanding exactly why randomization works tells you exactly what each later method must imitate.

title: Randomized Experiments: The Gold Standard
text:
Independence: the engine
Random assignment — a coin, a lottery, a random-number generator — makes the treatment indicator D statistically independent of the potential outcomes and all background traits X: 
\big(Y(0),\,Y(1),\,X\big)\ \perp\ D.
 Knowing whether someone was treated tells you nothing about what their outcome would have been. (Gerber and Green 2012) Feed that into the selection-bias decomposition: independence forces \mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=1]=\mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=0], so the selection-bias term is zero — and, because it also makes \text{ATT}=\text{ATU}=\text{ATE}, the heterogeneous-effect term vanishes too. (Gerber and Green 2012) What remains is clean: 
\mathbb E[Y\mid D=1]-\mathbb E[Y\mid D=0]\;=\;\text{ATE}.
 The difference in observed group means identifies the average treatment effect for the whole population. (Gerber and Green 2012) That is the entire magic, and it is why the design needs no control variables: randomization balances everything at once, including the confounders you never thought to measure.

title: Randomized Experiments: The Gold Standard
text:
Balance: the observable signature
You can’t check independence directly (it concerns unobserved counterfactuals), but it has a visible fingerprint: covariate balance. Since assignment is random, every pre-treatment characteristic should have the same distribution across arms — \mathbb E[X\mid D=1]=\mathbb E[X\mid D=0]. (Gerber and Green 2012) So researchers report a balance table: group means of age, gender, baseline outcomes, and so on, treated vs control. Large systematic gaps signal a botched randomization; small, chance-sized ones are expected. A joint check regresses assignment on all covariates and tests their joint significance with an F-statistic, ideally judged by randomization inference (below). (Gerber and Green 2012) Balance is necessary, not sufficient — it speaks only to observed covariates — but a clean balance table is the first thing a credible experiment shows.

title: Randomized Experiments: The Gold Standard
text:
The estimator and its uncertainty
The estimator is just the difference in means, \hat\theta=\bar Y_{\text{treat}}-\bar
Y_{\text{control}}, and it is unbiased: averaged over all the random assignments the procedure could have produced, it recovers the true ATE. (Gerber and Green 2012) Unbiased is not the same as precise — any single experiment scatters around the truth by sampling variability, especially in small samples, which is why design (below) and adequate n matter. (Gerber and Green 2012)
How to get a p-value? The corpus draws the classic distinction between Fisher and Neyman: (Gerber and Green 2012)

Fisher’s sharp null asserts no effect for anyone: Y_i(1)=Y_i(0) for every unit. Under that null both potential outcomes are known for everyone (they’re equal to the observed Y_i), so you can recompute the estimate under every possible reassignment of the treatment label and see how often chance alone produces something as extreme as what you saw. This is randomization inference — a permutation test that needs no Normal approximation. (Gerber and Green 2012)
Neyman targets the ATE in a population and supplies (conservative) standard errors that allow the effect to vary across units, instead of assuming it is zero for all. (Gerber and Green 2012)

The figure runs Fisher’s procedure: shuffle the treatment labels thousands of times to build the sharp-null distribution, then locate the experiment’s actual estimate in its tail.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
n = 120
# One realized experiment: a true positive effect of ~0.6.
base = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
D = rng.permutation(np.r_[np.ones(n // 2), np.zeros(n // 2)]).astype(bool)
y = base + 0.6 * D + rng.normal(0, 1, n)
obs = y[D].mean() - y[~D].mean()

# Sharp-null permutation distribution: reshuffle who is "treated".
perms = 5000
m = D.sum()
null = np.empty(perms)
idx = np.arange(n)
for b in range(perms):
    t = rng.permutation(idx)[:m]
    mask = np.zeros(n, bool); mask[t] = True
    null[b] = y[mask].mean() - y[~mask].mean()

p_two = np.mean(np.abs(null) >= abs(obs))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.0))
ax.hist(null, bins=45, color="#c9ccd1", edgecolor="white")
ax.axvline(obs, color="#c0392b", lw=2.2)
ax.text(obs, ax.get_ylim()[1]*0.9, f"  observed = {obs:+.2f}", color="#c0392b", fontsize=9)
ax.text(0.02, 0.86, f"randomization p = {p_two:.3f}", transform=ax.transAxes,
        fontsize=10, color="#1f3a5f", fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("difference in means under reshuffled assignment")
ax.set_ylabel("frequency")
ax.set_title("Fisher's sharp-null distribution vs the observed effect")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Randomization inference (Fisher). Under the sharp null of no effect for anyone, the treatment label is arbitrary, so re-shuffling it across units regenerates the distribution of the difference-in-means that chance alone could produce (grey). The actual experiment’s estimate (red) sits far in the tail — the share of permutations at least as extreme is the exact randomization p-value, computed with no distributional assumption.

title: Randomized Experiments: The Gold Standard
section: Design: buy precision before you run
text:
Design: buy precision before you run
Randomization removes bias; smart design cuts variance. Two standard moves: (Gerber and Green 2012)

Block (stratified) randomization. Partition units into homogeneous blocks on strong pre-treatment predictors (school, region, baseline score), then randomize within each block. This “blocks out” unlucky draws that would imbalance an important covariate, narrowing the sampling distribution — the experimental cousin of adding a good control. (Gerber and Green 2012)
Clustered randomization. Sometimes you must assign whole groups — classrooms, villages, precincts — together, for logistics or to contain spillovers. The price is precision: units within a cluster are correlated, which inflates standard errors (the clustering problem from regression, by design rather than accident). (Gerber and Green 2012)

title: Randomized Experiments: The Gold Standard
section: When reality intrudes: attrition and noncompliance
text:
When reality intrudes: attrition and noncompliance
Two departures from the ideal experiment are common enough to be their own literatures: (Gerber and Green 2012)
Attrition — outcomes go missing for some subjects. If missingness is related to potential outcomes (the most disgruntled controls drop out), the difference in means is biased even though assignment was random. Defences: define the estimand on always-reporters, or compute extreme-value bounds that fill the missing outcomes with worst/best cases and check whether the conclusion survives. (Gerber and Green 2012)
Noncompliance — some assigned to treatment go untreated, and vice versa. Now there are two distinct effects: (Gerber and Green 2012)

Intention-to-treat (ITT) — the effect of being assigned/offered treatment, regardless of uptake. It is unbiased (assignment was random) and is exactly what a policymaker who can only offer a program wants — but it is diluted by anyone who didn’t comply.
Local Average Treatment Effect (LATE / CACE) — the effect of actually taking treatment, for the compliers (those who take it if and only if assigned). It is recovered by scaling ITT up by the compliance rate: \text{LATE} = \text{ITT}_{Y}/\text{ITT}_{D} (the outcome effect over the take-up effect). (Gerber and Green 2012)

The framework sorts everyone into compliers, never-takers, always-takers, and defiers, and the LATE result needs monotonicity — assignment never pushes anyone the wrong way, i.e. no defiers. (Gerber and Green 2012) (This complier logic is exactly the machinery of instrumental variables — random assignment is the perfect instrument.)

title: Randomized Experiments: The Gold Standard
section: Field experiments and the development-RCT revolution
text:
Field experiments and the development-RCT revolution
The clearest demonstration that this design is more than a lab toy is the movement that took it into the villages of the developing world — the work of Banerjee & Duflo in Poor Economics, which earned a share of the 2019 Nobel. Their methodological case is a direct application of everything above: grand debates about whether “Aid” works cannot be settled in the abstract, because the cross-country data are unreliable — “speculating on a grand scale.” (Banerjee and Duflo 2011) A “poverty trap” can’t be theorised into existence or non-existence; it must be checked case by case, and an RCT is what makes the comparison groups “exactly comparable,” isolating a program’s true effect. A series of such experiments across sites then tests whether a finding is robust. (Banerjee and Duflo 2011) Their slogan is the shift from “Aid” (general assistance, argued over ideologically by Sachs vs Easterly) to “aid” — specific, evaluated interventions, progress as “the accumulation of a set of small steps,” each “carefully tested and judiciously implemented.” (Banerjee and Duflo 2011)






NoteThree field experiments that overturned conventional wisdom




Immunization incentives (Udaipur, India). Full-immunization rates were stuck at 6%. Offering parents a bag of dal (lentils) per shot plus steel plates for completing the course raised it sevenfold, to 38% — and, paradoxically, the incentive lowered the cost per immunization by keeping the nurses busy. A tiny nudge beat exhortation. (Banerjee and Duflo 2011)
Bednets and the sunk-cost myth (Kenya, Dupas). Demand for insecticide-treated nets was acutely price-sensitive: nearly everyone took one when free, demand collapsed toward zero at just $0.75. And free nets were used just as much as paid ones — refuting the “sunk-cost” claim that people only value what they pay for, a claim that had justified charging the poor. (Banerjee and Duflo 2011)
Deworming (Kenya, Miguel & Kremer). Treating children for intestinal worms — at $1.36 per child per year — raised their adult earnings by about 20% a year. One of the highest-return interventions ever measured, found only because someone ran the experiment. (Banerjee and Duflo 2011)



The deeper lesson loops back to Part VI: policy failures usually trace not to conspiracy but to the “three Is — ignorance, ideology, and inertia,” and the antidote is a “patient, step-by-step approach” that tests the specific sticking point rather than betting on a grand formula. (Banerjee and Duflo 2011) This is the randomized experiment doing exactly what this entry promised — replacing argument with evidence — at the scale of national policy.

title: Randomized Experiments: The Gold Standard
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Adding controls to “improve” an experiment. Randomization already balances everything in expectation; the diff-in-means is unbiased on its own. Use covariates for precision (blocking, regression adjustment), not to fix bias. (Gerber and Green 2012)
Treating a balance failure as fatal — or a balance pass as proof. Some imbalance is chance; balance concerns only observed covariates. It’s evidence about the randomization, not a guarantee of unconfoundedness. (Gerber and Green 2012)
Ignoring clustering in the standard errors. Assigning groups but analysing individuals as if independent understates uncertainty, sometimes massively. (Gerber and Green 2012)
Reporting per-protocol instead of ITT under noncompliance. Dropping or reclassifying noncompliers reintroduces selection bias. Report ITT; scale to LATE only under monotonicity. (Gerber and Green 2012)
Letting attrition slide. Differential dropout is selection bias wearing a lab coat. Bound it. (Gerber and Green 2012)

title: Randomized Experiments: The Gold Standard
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Gerber & Green, Field Experiments — randomization as the gold standard; the independence assumption (Y(0),Y(1),X)\perp D zeroing the selection-bias term so diff-in-means identifies the ATE; covariate balance and the joint F balance check; unbiasedness of the difference in means; Fisher’s sharp null + permutation/randomization inference vs the Neyman ATE approach with conservative SEs; block and clustered randomization (precision up; SEs up); attrition (bias + extreme-value bounds, always-reporters); noncompliance with ITT, LATE/CACE =\text{ITT}_Y/\text{ITT}_D, the complier/never-taker/always-taker/defier taxonomy, and monotonicity (no defiers).
Moffatt, Experimetrics — experimental design and analysis as an econometric practice (randomization inference, design for precision).
Banerjee & Duflo, Poor Economics — the development-RCT case: bypass “grand universal answers” and ideology, assess poverty traps case by case with exactly comparable randomized groups, and build knowledge from a series of experiments; “Aid” → “aid” and progress as “small steps”; the immunization-incentive (6% → 38%), bednet / sunk-cost (Dupas), and deworming (Miguel & Kremer) field experiments; and the “three Is — ignorance, ideology, inertia.”

title: Randomized Experiments: The Gold Standard
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Why independence identifies the ATE. Independence gives \mathbb E[Y\mid D=1]=\mathbb E[Y(1)\mid D=1]=\mathbb E[Y(1)] and likewise \mathbb E[Y\mid D=0]=\mathbb E[Y(0)], since conditioning on D is uninformative about the potential outcomes. Subtracting, 
\mathbb E[Y\mid D=1]-\mathbb E[Y\mid D=0]=\mathbb E[Y(1)]-\mathbb E[Y(0)]=\text{ATE}.
 No covariates, no functional form — the design does the work.
Why blocking helps. With blocks b of size n_b, the block-stratified estimator is the precision-weighted average of within-block diff-in-means. Because within-block units are more alike, the residual variance entering each block’s estimate is smaller, so the overall sampling variance \operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta) shrinks relative to simple randomization — the same variance reduction a good control buys, secured ex ante.
LATE as a ratio (the Wald form). Let Z be random assignment and D actual take-up. Then 
\text{LATE} = \frac{\mathbb E[Y\mid Z=1]-\mathbb E[Y\mid Z=0]}{\mathbb E[D\mid Z=1]-\mathbb E[D\mid Z=0]}
= \frac{\text{ITT}_Y}{\text{ITT}_D},
 the reduced-form effect of assignment on the outcome divided by the first-stage effect of assignment on take-up. Under monotonicity the denominator is the share of compliers, and the ratio is their average treatment effect — the bridge to instrumental variables.

title: Randomized Experiments: The Gold Standard
text:
Connections

Builds on: potential outcomes (the selection-bias term this design zeroes), resampling (randomization inference is a permutation test), and sampling & survey design (random assignment vs random sampling — different randomness, different jobs).
Leads to: DAGs (when no experiment is possible, draw the confounding and reason about it) and the whole observational toolkit; noncompliance flows straight into instrumental variables.
Reappears in: every later design, each judged by how well it reproduces this benchmark’s independence.

title: Randomized Experiments: The Gold Standard
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Why does random assignment make (Y(0),Y(1),X)\perp D, and how does that zero the selection-bias term so the difference in means identifies the ATE?”
“What is covariate balance, why is it the observable signature of randomization, and how is a joint balance test constructed?”
“Contrast Fisher’s sharp null (and the permutation test) with the Neyman approach to experimental standard errors.”
“How do block (stratified) and clustered randomization each affect bias and precision?”
“Under noncompliance, define ITT and LATE/CACE, the four subject types, and the monotonicity assumption; give the LATE = ITT_Y / ITT_D ratio.”
“How do Banerjee & Duflo argue RCTs settle development questions that ideology can’t, and what did the immunization-incentive, bednet/sunk-cost, and deworming experiments find?”

title: Panel Data & Fixed Effects
text: The deepest threat to causal claims is the confounder you can’t measure: ability, culture, managerial skill. If you observe the same units repeatedly, a near-magical trick becomes available — compare each unit only to itself across time. Anything about a unit that stays fixed, however unobservable, differences away. Fixed effects buys you control over an entire universe of time-invariant confounders for free — and the fine print is everything it cannot touch.

title: Panel Data & Fixed Effects
text:
Why it matters
Matching only handles observed confounders; IV and RD need special variation that isn’t always there. Panel data offers a different escape: by following units over time, you can sweep away all time-invariant unobservables at once. (Li 2023) This is the backbone of the “Furious Five” — difference-in-differences is a two-period fixed- effects design — and the most common way applied researchers defend against unobserved heterogeneity. (Wooldridge 2019)

title: Panel Data & Fixed Effects
section: Panel data and the heterogeneity problem
text:
Panel data and the heterogeneity problem
Panel (longitudinal) data follows the same cross-sectional units — people, firms, countries — across multiple periods. (Li 2023) Model the outcome with a unit-specific term: 
Y_{it}=\beta_0 + X_{it}\beta + \alpha_i + u_{it},
 where \alpha_i is the individual fixed effect — all the unobserved, time-invariant traits of unit i (innate ability in a wage equation, managerial skill in a firm). (Li 2023) The danger: if \alpha_i is correlated with X_{it}, pooled OLS is biased and inconsistent — heterogeneity bias. (Li 2023) (High-ability workers get more schooling and earn more; ignore ability and schooling’s coefficient inherits its effect — the ability bias of Part III, now with a repeated-measures cure.)

title: Panel Data & Fixed Effects
text:
The within (fixed-effects) estimator
The fix is disarmingly simple. Demean every variable by its unit-specific mean over time — the within transformation: (Li 2023) 
(Y_{it}-\bar Y_i) = (X_{it}-\bar X_i)\beta + (u_{it}-\bar u_i).
 Because \alpha_i is constant over time, its own mean is itself (\bar\alpha_i=\alpha_i), so \alpha_i-\bar\alpha_i=0 — it vanishes. (Li 2023) Every fixed trait, observed or not, is swept away, and \beta is identified purely from within-unit variation over time: when a unit’s X changes, how does its Y change? The figure shows why this matters — pooled and within slopes can point in opposite directions.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(6)
beta_true = -1.0
units, T = 6, 8
colors = plt.cm.viridis(np.linspace(0.1, 0.9, units))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
allX, allY = [], []
for u in range(units):
    alpha = 4.0 * u                          # unit fixed effect, correlated with X below
    x = alpha + rng.normal(0, 0.6, T)        # high-alpha units have high X (confounding)
    y = alpha + beta_true * (x - alpha) + rng.normal(0, 0.3, T)  # within-unit slope is negative
    ax.scatter(x, y, color=colors[u], s=22, zorder=3)
    b = np.polyfit(x, y, 1)                   # within-unit fit
    xs = np.linspace(x.min(), x.max(), 10)
    ax.plot(xs, np.polyval(b, xs), color=colors[u], lw=1.6, alpha=0.8)
    allX.append(x); allY.append(y)

allX = np.concatenate(allX); allY = np.concatenate(allY)
bp = np.polyfit(allX, allY, 1)               # pooled OLS
xs = np.linspace(allX.min(), allX.max(), 20)
ax.plot(xs, np.polyval(bp, xs), "k--", lw=2.4, label=f"pooled OLS slope = {bp[0]:+.2f}")
ax.plot([], [], color="#555", lw=1.6, label=f"within-unit slope ≈ {beta_true:+.2f} (true)")
ax.set_xlabel("X (e.g. schooling)"); ax.set_ylabel("Y (e.g. wage)")
ax.set_title("Fixed effects recovers the within-unit relationship")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Fixed effects vs pooled OLS (a Simpson’s-paradox structure). Each colour is one unit observed over several periods. Pooled OLS (dashed black) ignores units and fits across the cloud — sloping upward — because high-α units happen to have high X. But within every unit the relationship is negative: raise that unit’s X and its Y falls. The within (fixed-effects) estimator recovers the negative within-unit slope by demeaning each unit, immune to the time-invariant αᵢ that drives the misleading between-unit pattern.





There’s an immediate corollary: a variable that never changes for a unit — race, gender, distance to a fixed river — is demeaned to zero, so fixed effects cannot estimate its effect. (Li 2023) The very trick that kills the confounding also kills any time-invariant regressor of interest.

title: Panel Data & Fixed Effects
text:
First differencing, and a cousin
An alternative removes \alpha_i by subtracting consecutive periods — first differencing: \Delta Y_{it}=\Delta X_{it}\beta+\Delta u_{it}. (Li 2023) With exactly two periods, FE and FD are numerically identical. (Li 2023) With more, the choice hinges on the errors: FE is more efficient if the idiosyncratic u_{it} are serially uncorrelated, while FD is better if they behave like a random walk (so differencing whitens them). (Li 2023)

title: Panel Data & Fixed Effects
section: Fixed vs random effects, and the Hausman test
text:
Fixed vs random effects, and the Hausman test
A random-effects (RE) model instead treats \alpha_i as part of a composite error v_{it}=\alpha_i+u_{it}. (Li 2023) The bargain: (Li 2023)

RE is more efficient — it uses both within- and between-unit variation and can estimate time-invariant regressors — but only if the RE assumption holds: \operatorname{Cov}(X_{it},\alpha_i)=0, the individual effect is uncorrelated with the regressors.
FE is consistent regardless, even when \alpha_i correlates with X — exactly the case that matters for causal work in observational data, where such correlation is the norm.

The Hausman test adjudicates: under the null \operatorname{Cov}(X,\alpha)=0, FE and RE agree; reject the null → use FE (RE is inconsistent); fail to reject → RE for its efficiency. (Li 2023) For causal inference the default leans FE, because the whole reason you reached for panel data was a suspicion that \alpha_i is correlated with treatment.

title: Panel Data & Fixed Effects
text:
Two-way fixed effects
Add time fixed effects \lambda_t to absorb shocks hitting all units in a period — a recession, a national policy: (Li 2023) 
Y_{it}=\alpha_i+\lambda_t+X_{it}\beta+u_{it}.
 Unit effects sweep away fixed cross-unit differences; time effects sweep away common temporal shocks — and with treatment as the regressor this is difference-in-differences, generalised to many units and periods. (Li 2023) (Which is also why the staggered-adoption critique applies to TWFE here too.)

title: Panel Data & Fixed Effects
text:
What fixed effects cannot fix
FE is powerful but not a magic bullet — and knowing its blind spots is the mark of using it well: (Li 2023)

Time-varying confounders. FE removes only what’s constant. An unobserved trait that changes and correlates with treatment — a city’s shifting economic climate — still biases the estimate.
Reverse causality. If Y also causes X (crime → police spending), FE is biased like any OLS; demeaning doesn’t fix simultaneity.
Time-invariant regressors. As noted, their effects are unidentifiable — they difference out.

The honest summary: fixed effects neutralise time-invariant unobserved confounding and nothing else. That single, large class of threats is what they buy — which is why FE is usually a foundation for a design (DiD, event study), not a complete identification strategy on its own.






NoteWooldridge’s job-training grants



Do training grants raise productivity? With a panel of 54 firms, a pure cross-section would be biased — grants may go to firms that are already well-run (high \alpha_i). Fixed effects differences that fixed firm “skill” away, identifying the effect from firms’ changes across years: the 1988 grant is estimated to cut the 1989 scrap rate by ~34.4% (e^{-0.422}-1). (Wooldridge 2019) The effect is only credible because the same firms were watched over time.

title: Panel Data & Fixed Effects
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Believing FE removes all confounding. It removes only time-invariant unobservables; time-varying confounders and reverse causality remain. (Li 2023)
Trying to estimate a time-invariant effect with FE. Gender, race, fixed geography difference out — use RE (if its assumption is defensible) or another design. (Li 2023)
Defaulting to random effects for efficiency. RE is inconsistent if \alpha_i correlates with X — usually the case in observational data. Run Hausman; lean FE for causal claims. (Li 2023)
Ignoring serial correlation. It changes FE-vs-FD efficiency and demands clustered standard errors; un-clustered SEs badly overstate precision in panels. (Li 2023)
Too little within-variation. If treatment barely changes within units, FE has almost nothing to work with and estimates are imprecise. (Li 2023)

title: Panel Data & Fixed Effects
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Dong Li, Panel Data Econometrics — panel data and unobserved time-invariant heterogeneity \alpha_i; the model Y_{it}=X_{it}\beta+\alpha_i+u_{it} and heterogeneity bias; the within (fixed-effects) estimator via demeaning; first differencing and FE=FD at T=2; random effects and its \operatorname{Cov}(X,\alpha)=0 assumption, efficiency-vs-consistency, and the Hausman test; two-way fixed effects; the limits — time-varying confounders, reverse causality, time-invariant regressors; and DiD as a two-period panel FE model.
Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — the within estimator and the job-training grants / scrap-rate panel (54 firms; 1988 grant \Rightarrow ~-34.4\% scrap rate in 1989).

title: Panel Data & Fixed Effects
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The within transformation kills \alpha_i. Average the model over t for unit i: \bar Y_i=\beta_0+\bar X_i\beta+\alpha_i+\bar u_i (since \bar\alpha_i=\alpha_i). Subtract from the level equation: 
Y_{it}-\bar Y_i=(X_{it}-\bar X_i)\beta+(u_{it}-\bar u_i),
 in which \alpha_i and \beta_0 have disappeared. OLS on the demeaned data is the FE estimator \hat\beta_{FE}=\big(\sum_{it}\ddot X_{it}\ddot X_{it}'\big)^{-1}\sum_{it}\ddot X_{it}\ddot Y_{it} with \ddot X_{it}=X_{it}-\bar X_i — a regression on deviations from unit means, so only within-unit covariation identifies \beta.
FE = FD at T=2. With two periods, demeaning gives X_{i2}-\bar X_i=\tfrac12\Delta X_i and likewise for Y; the factor \tfrac12 cancels in the OLS ratio, so \hat\beta_{FE}=\hat\beta_{FD} exactly. This is why the 2×2 DiD can be read as either.
Why Hausman points to FE on rejection. Under H_0:\operatorname{Cov}(X,\alpha)=0, both estimators are consistent but RE is efficient; under H_1 only FE is consistent. The statistic (\hat\beta_{FE}-\hat\beta_{RE})'[\operatorname{Var}(\hat\beta_{FE})-\operatorname{Var}(\hat\beta_{RE})]^{-1}(\hat\beta_{FE}-\hat\beta_{RE}) is \chi^2 under H_0; a large value signals the estimators disagree, indicting RE’s assumption and favouring FE.

title: Panel Data & Fixed Effects
text:
Connections

Builds on: omitted-variable bias (the unobserved \alpha_i FE removes), multiple regression (FE is OLS on dummies/deviations), and clustering (panel SEs).
Leads to: difference-in-differences (two-way FE with a treatment timing) and synthetic control (when you have one treated unit and FE/DiD has no clean control group).
Reappears in: virtually all applied field work on administrative panels.

title: Panel Data & Fixed Effects
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“What is panel data, and how does the individual fixed effect \alpha_i create heterogeneity bias in pooled OLS?”
“Explain the within (fixed-effects) estimator: the demeaning transformation, why \alpha_i vanishes, and why only within-unit variation identifies \beta.”
“How does first differencing remove \alpha_i, and when does it equal fixed effects?”
“Contrast random effects and fixed effects (assumptions, efficiency, consistency) and explain the Hausman test.”
“What can fixed effects NOT fix (time-varying confounders, reverse causality, time-invariant variables), and how is DiD a two-period fixed-effects design?”

title: Instrumental Variables
text: Matching fails the moment an unobserved confounder drives both treatment and outcome — exactly the case the bridge warned is untestable. Instrumental variables sidesteps it entirely. Find a variable that nudges the treatment but has no other path to the outcome, and the slice of treatment variation it creates is as-good-as-random. IV throws away all the contaminated variation and keeps only that clean slice — buying identification at the price of estimating an effect for a particular subpopulation.

title: Instrumental Variables
text:
Why it matters
Every selection-on-observables method assumes you measured the confounders. When you didn’t — ability in the wage equation, health in the income equation — those methods stay biased, with no test to warn you. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) IV is the first design in this Part that defeats unobserved confounding, and it does so with an idea that recurs everywhere after: isolate a source of variation in the treatment that is exogenous by argument, and use only that. (Cunningham 2021) It is the engine beneath noncompliance, fuzzy RD, and many natural experiments — learn it once, recognise it for the rest of the summit.

title: Instrumental Variables
text:
The endogeneity problem
A regressor D is endogenous when it is correlated with the error term, \operatorname{Cov}(D,u)\ne
0 — from omitted variables, measurement error, or simultaneity. Then OLS is biased and inconsistent: it cannot separate D’s effect on Y from the influence of the unobserved u that moves both. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) IV’s job is to recover the causal effect despite that correlation.

title: Instrumental Variables
text:
What makes an instrument valid
An instrument Z is a variable that perturbs D for reasons unrelated to u. Validity rests on two conditions (plus an independence premise): (Angrist and Pischke 2009; Cunningham 2021)

Relevance (the first stage). Z actually moves the treatment: \operatorname{Cov}(Z,D)\ne0. This is testable — regress D on Z and look. A strong first stage is non-negotiable.
Exclusion restriction. Z affects Y only through D — no direct path, no other channel: \operatorname{Cov}(Z,u)=0. This is not testable; it is an argument about the world.
Independence / as-good-as-random. Z is unrelated to the unobserved confounders, as if randomly assigned.

The whole design is one DAG: \;Z\to D\to Y\; with an unobserved u creating D\leftarrow u\to Y — and no arrow Z\to Y. (Cunningham 2021) That missing arrow is the exclusion restriction; it is a collider-free assumption you draw, not test, and the entire credibility of an IV study lives or dies on defending it.

title: Instrumental Variables
text:
How IV estimates: Wald and 2SLS
With a single binary instrument and no covariates, the estimator is the Wald ratio — the reduced form (effect of Z on Y) divided by the first stage (effect of Z on D): 
\rho_{\text{Wald}}=\frac{\mathbb E[Y\mid Z=1]-\mathbb E[Y\mid Z=0]}{\mathbb E[D\mid Z=1]-\mathbb E[D\mid Z=0]}.
 The intuition is a unit-conversion: the instrument shifts the outcome by the numerator, but only because it shifted treatment by the denominator; dividing rescales “effect of the nudge” into “effect of the treatment.” (Angrist and Pischke 2009) (This is exactly the LATE formula \text{ITT}_Y/\text{ITT}_D — random assignment is the perfect instrument.)
Two-stage least squares (2SLS) generalises it to covariates and multiple instruments: (Angrist and Pischke 2009)

First stage: regress D on Z (and controls X); keep the fitted values \hat D — the part of treatment driven by the instrument, purged of the variation correlated with u.
Second stage: regress Y on \hat D (and X). The coefficient on \hat D is the IV estimate.

The slogan: 2SLS uses only the treatment variation the instrument explains, discarding the endogenous remainder. The figure shows the payoff — OLS is dragged off by the confounder; IV, riding only the instrument’s clean variation, recovers the truth.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(8)
n = 4000
beta = 1.0                                   # true causal effect of D on Y
Z = rng.normal(0, 1, n)                      # the instrument
u = rng.normal(0, 1, n)                      # unobserved confounder
D = 0.8 * Z + 1.2 * u + rng.normal(0, 0.5, n)   # treatment: moved by Z AND by u
Y = beta * D - 1.5 * u + rng.normal(0, 0.5, n)  # u also pushes Y (so OLS is biased)

ols = np.cov(D, Y, bias=True)[0, 1] / np.var(D)
wald = (np.cov(Z, Y, bias=True)[0, 1]) / (np.cov(Z, D, bias=True)[0, 1])  # reduced form / first stage

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(7.8, 3.8))
# scatter with OLS vs true line
idx = rng.choice(n, 500, replace=False)
axL.scatter(D[idx], Y[idx], s=8, color="#c9ccd1", alpha=0.6)
xs = np.linspace(D.min(), D.max(), 20)
axL.plot(xs, ols * xs, color="#d98a29", lw=2.4, label=f"OLS slope = {ols:.2f}")
axL.plot(xs, beta * xs, color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.2, ls="--", label=f"true effect = {beta:.2f}")
axL.set_xlabel("treatment D"); axL.set_ylabel("outcome Y")
axL.set_title("OLS is pulled off by the confounder"); axL.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)

labels = ["true", "OLS", "IV (Wald)"]
vals = [beta, ols, wald]
colors = ["#1f8a4c", "#d98a29", "#1f3a5f"]
axR.bar(labels, vals, color=colors)
for i, v in enumerate(vals):
    axR.text(i, v + 0.03, f"{v:.2f}", ha="center", fontsize=9)
axR.axhline(beta, color="#1f8a4c", ls="--", lw=1.3)
axR.set_ylabel("estimated effect"); axR.set_title("IV lands on the truth")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: IV recovers what OLS can’t. The treatment D is confounded by an unobserved u that also raises Y, so OLS (orange) overstates the true effect (green dashed). The instrument Z shifts D without touching u; the Wald/2SLS estimator uses only that instrument-driven slice of variation — the reduced-form effect of Z on Y divided by its first-stage effect on D — landing on the true slope (blue). Bars compare the three estimates against truth.

title: Instrumental Variables
section: What IV actually estimates: LATE and compliers
text:
What IV actually estimates: LATE and compliers
Here is IV’s subtle, essential catch. When the effect varies across people, IV does not deliver the population ATE. It identifies the Local Average Treatment Effect (LATE) — the average effect for compliers, the people whose treatment status the instrument actually moved. (Angrist and Pischke 2009; Cunningham 2021) Sort the population by how they respond to Z:

Compliers — take treatment iff the instrument says so (D_1=1, D_0=0). These, and only these, IV speaks for.
Always-takers (D_1=D_0=1) and never-takers (D_1=D_0=0) — the instrument doesn’t move them, so they contribute nothing to the estimate.
Defiers (D_1=0, D_0=1) — do the opposite of the nudge.

LATE requires monotonicity: the instrument pushes everyone it affects the same direction — no defiers. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) The consequence is interpretive, not technical: a draft-lottery estimate is the effect of service on men whom the lottery induced to serve, not on volunteers or the unfit. Different instruments for the same treatment estimate different LATEs, on different complier groups — which is why two valid IV studies can honestly disagree.

title: Instrumental Variables
text:
Weak instruments
Relevance isn’t binary — instruments can be weak, barely correlated with D. The damage is double: 2SLS standard errors balloon, and the estimator suffers finite-sample bias back toward OLS, even in large samples — so a weak instrument quietly reintroduces the very bias IV was meant to remove. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) The standard guard is the first-stage F-statistic on the excluded instruments, with the rule of thumb F>10 for the instrument to be considered strong enough. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) A weak first stage is the most common way an IV paper fails.






NoteThree instruments that made the method famous




The Vietnam draft lottery (Angrist 1990). To estimate the effect of military service on earnings, use random draft-eligibility lottery numbers as an instrument for veteran status — random by construction (independence), and plausibly affecting earnings only through service (exclusion). For white men born in 1950 the first stage was 0.159 (eligibility raised service by ~16 points), and the IV estimate was an earnings loss of about $2,400. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Quarter of birth (Angrist & Krueger 1991). Compulsory-schooling laws let those born later in the year leave school with slightly less education. Using quarter-of-birth dummies as instruments for schooling, the IV return to education was 8.9%, against an OLS 7.1%. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Distance to college (Card 1995). Growing up near a four-year college instruments years of schooling: it raised education by 0.327 years (first-stage F=15.8), and the IV return was 13.2%, well above OLS’s 7.5%. (Cunningham 2021)

That IV often exceeds OLS is itself informative — consistent with attenuation from measurement error, or with LATE weighting toward high-return compliers.

title: Instrumental Variables
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




A shaky exclusion restriction. If Z affects Y through any channel besides D, IV is biased — and you can’t test it. Most IV critiques are exclusion critiques. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Weak instruments. Low first-stage F inflates variance and biases 2SLS toward OLS. Report the first stage and its F; treat F<10 as a red flag. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Reading IV as the ATE. Under heterogeneity it’s the LATE for compliers — a local effect on whoever the instrument moved, not the population. State the complier group. (Cunningham 2021)
Forgetting monotonicity. Defiers break the complier interpretation; argue the instrument pushes one way for everyone. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Instrument-shopping. Trying many instruments until one “works” mines for a strong-but-invalid first stage; the exclusion restriction must be defended on substance, not chosen by fit. (Cunningham 2021)

title: Instrumental Variables
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Angrist & Pischke, Mastering ’Metrics / Mostly Harmless — endogeneity and OLS inconsistency; relevance (first stage) and the exclusion restriction + independence; the Wald estimator (reduced form ÷ first stage) and 2SLS (fitted \hat D); LATE and the complier/always-taker/never-taker/defier taxonomy with monotonicity; weak instruments and the first-stage F>10 rule; the draft-lottery (first stage .159, \approx-\$2{,}400) and quarter-of-birth (.089 vs OLS .071) studies.
Cunningham, The Mixtape — the IV DAG Z\to D\to Y with D\leftarrow u\to Y and no Z\to
Y; the exclusion restriction as the missing arrow; Card’s distance-to-college (+0.327 yrs, F=15.8, IV return 13.2\% vs OLS 7.5\%).

title: Instrumental Variables
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Why the IV estimand is unbiased under exclusion. With structural equation Y=\beta D+u, take the covariance with Z: \operatorname{Cov}(Z,Y)=\beta\operatorname{Cov}(Z,D)+\operatorname{Cov}(Z,u). Exclusion sets the last term to zero, so 
\beta=\frac{\operatorname{Cov}(Z,Y)}{\operatorname{Cov}(Z,D)},
 the population Wald/2SLS estimand. The denominator is relevance — if \operatorname{Cov}(Z,D)\to0 (weak instrument) the ratio explodes, which is why weak instruments wreck precision.
2SLS as a projection. \hat D=P_Z D where P_Z=Z(Z'Z)^{-1}Z' projects D onto the instrument space; the second stage gives \hat\beta_{2SLS}=(\hat D'\hat D)^{-1}\hat D'Y. 2SLS keeps only the component of D in the column space of Z — the exogenous slice — and discards the rest.
LATE theorem (Imbens–Angrist). Under independence, exclusion, relevance, and monotonicity, 
\frac{\mathbb E[Y\mid Z=1]-\mathbb E[Y\mid Z=0]}{\mathbb E[D\mid Z=1]-\mathbb E[D\mid Z=0]}
=\mathbb E[\,Y(1)-Y(0)\mid \text{complier}\,].
 The denominator equals the share of compliers, so the ratio reweights the reduced form onto exactly the subpopulation the instrument moves — the formal reason IV is local.

title: Instrumental Variables
text:
Connections

Builds on: randomized experiments (LATE/compliers from noncompliance; assignment as the ideal instrument), DAGs (the exclusion restriction is a missing arrow), and endogeneity (the problem IV solves).
Leads to: fuzzy regression discontinuity (the cutoff as an instrument) and the credibility-revolution natural experiments.
Reappears in: causal machine learning, where double/debiased ML orthogonalises treatment and instrument with flexible first stages.

title: Instrumental Variables
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“What is endogeneity, and what two conditions (relevance, exclusion) must an instrument satisfy? Draw the IV DAG and locate each assumption.”
“Derive the Wald estimator as reduced form over first stage, and explain the two stages of 2SLS.”
“What is LATE? Define compliers, always-takers, never-takers, and defiers, and state the monotonicity assumption.”
“What are weak instruments, why do they bias 2SLS and inflate variance, and what is the first-stage F>10 rule?”
“Explain the draft-lottery, quarter-of-birth, and distance-to-college studies — the instrument, the first stage, and why IV often exceeds OLS.”

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
text: A DAG is a diagram of what causes what, drawn before you touch the data. Its power is that it turns the murky question “which variables should I control for?” into a mechanical rule. Confounders open backdoor paths that must be closed; colliders are dams that you must not open. The same graph that justifies a control exposes the controls that would manufacture bias — Part III’s good-vs-bad controls, now derivable rather than memorised.

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
text:
Why it matters
Randomization is the gold standard, but most questions can’t be randomized — you can’t assign people a gender, a recession, or a war. Then you must reason about confounding explicitly, and a directed acyclic graph is the tool for it: a transparent statement of your causal assumptions that makes the consequences for estimation derivable. (Cunningham 2021) DAGs are how Cunningham bridges theory and econometrics — and they settle, with a rule instead of an argument, the question that ended Part III: which controls help, and which ones hurt?

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
section: The intuitive picture: causal influence and adjustment
text:
The intuitive picture: causal influence and adjustment
Before the formal machinery, the plain-language version — which is how Daniel Kaplan introduces DAGs in Lessons in Statistical Thinking. A DAG is a network of causal influence: each arrow carries influence from cause to effect, and “acyclic” keeps that influence flowing one way, so the reasoning is a clean chain of deduction rather than a circular argument. (Kaplan 2024) A confounder is then easy to see — it is a variable that sends influence down two arrows at once, manufacturing a non-causal association between your explanatory variable and your response and so disguising the true effect size. (Kaplan 2024)
The cure is adjustment — and it is literally the same move as holding a confounder constant in regression. To adjust for a covariate is to account for the variation it injects, blocking the non-causal flow so that what remains is the signal you want, separated from the noise. (Kaplan 2024) That is the bridge worth holding onto: “accounting for variation” in Part III and “closing a backdoor” here are one act seen from two sides. What the rest of this entry adds is the precision Kaplan’s intuition can’t supply on its own — exactly which covariates to adjust for, and the cases where adjusting creates bias instead of removing it.

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
text:
Reading the graph
A DAG has three ingredients: (Cunningham 2021)

Nodes are random variables.
Directed edges (arrows) are direct causal effects: D\to Y means D causes Y. The absence of an arrow is the strongest claim of all — a firm theoretical commitment that no direct effect exists.
Acyclic means no feedback loops: causation flows one way. Reverse causality isn’t drawn as a cycle but by splitting the variable across time (Y_1, Y_2), so the arrows still point forward.

Behind the picture sits a structural causal model — a set of equations in which each variable is generated by its parents (the nodes with arrows into it) plus a random shock. (Cunningham 2021) The graph is the qualitative skeleton of that data-generating process; drawing it forces you to make your assumptions visible and falsifiable.

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
section: Three structures, and how association flows
text:
Three structures, and how association flows
Every DAG is built from three elementary configurations, and association (statistical correlation, causal or not) flows through them differently: (Cunningham 2021)

The chain / mediator D\to M\to Y. D affects Y only through M. Association flows along the chain — that flow is the causal effect.
The fork / confounder D\leftarrow Z\to Y. A common cause Z drives both. This creates a non-causal association between D and Y: they move together because they share an origin, not because one causes the other.
The collider D\to C\leftarrow Y. Two arrows collide into a common effect. Here D and Y are independent — until you condition on C.

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
text:
Paths, backdoors, and the dam
A path is any sequence of edges connecting D and Y, ignoring arrow direction. The one you want is the direct/causal path D\to Y. The troublemakers are backdoor paths — any path that leaves D through an incoming arrow, like D\leftarrow Z\to Y. (Cunningham 2021) A backdoor path is a pipe through which confounding flows, manufacturing correlation that isn’t cause.
Whether a path transmits association depends on what’s on it — the intuition of d-separation: (Cunningham 2021)

A path through a confounder (a fork) is open: association flows. Conditioning on that confounder blocks it.
A path through a collider is naturally blocked. The collider is a dam: it stops the flow — unless you condition on it (or its descendant), which opens the dam and lets spurious association through.

This is the crucial asymmetry. Conditioning closes forks but opens colliders. Controlling is not free; it changes which pipes are open.

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
section: The backdoor criterion: the rule for controls
text:
The backdoor criterion: the rule for controls
Putting it together gives the backdoor criterion, the formal rule for what to condition on: a set of variables identifies the causal effect of D on Y if it (Cunningham 2021)

closes every backdoor path (block each one by conditioning on a non-collider along it), and
opens no new path (never condition on a collider or a descendant of one).

Satisfy it and the leftover association between D and Y is purely causal. This is selection-on-observables stated as graph surgery: the “controls” you need are exactly the ones that shut the backdoors, and not one variable more.


Show the figure code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.2))
pos = {"D": (0.15, 0.30), "Y": (0.85, 0.30), "Z": (0.50, 0.82), "C": (0.50, 0.04)}
for name, (x, y) in pos.items():
    color = {"D": "#1f3a5f", "Y": "#1f3a5f", "Z": "#c0392b", "C": "#d98a29"}[name]
    ax.add_patch(plt.Circle((x, y), 0.055, color=color, zorder=3))
    ax.text(x, y, name, color="white", ha="center", va="center",
            fontsize=12, fontweight="bold", zorder=4)

def arrow(a, b, color, style="-|>", lw=2.0, rad=0.0):
    ax.annotate("", xy=pos[b], xytext=pos[a],
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle=style, color=color, lw=lw,
                                shrinkA=16, shrinkB=16,
                                connectionstyle=f"arc3,rad={rad}"), zorder=2)

arrow("D", "Y", "#1f8a4c", lw=2.8)      # causal effect of interest
arrow("Z", "D", "#c0392b")               # confounder -> D
arrow("Z", "Y", "#c0392b")               # confounder -> Y  (backdoor path D<-Z->Y)
arrow("D", "C", "#d98a29")               # D -> collider
arrow("Y", "C", "#d98a29")               # Y -> collider

ax.text(0.50, 0.40, "causal effect", color="#1f8a4c", fontsize=9, ha="center")
ax.text(0.50, 0.62, "backdoor path (close it: condition on Z)", color="#c0392b",
        fontsize=8.5, ha="center")
ax.text(0.50, 0.13, "collider — a dam (do NOT condition on C)", color="#b9791f",
        fontsize=8.5, ha="center")
ax.set_xlim(0, 1); ax.set_ylim(-0.05, 1.0); ax.axis("off")
ax.set_title("Confounder vs collider: what to control for")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A DAG with both a confounder and a collider. The causal effect of interest is D → Y (green). Z is a confounder opening the backdoor path D ← Z → Y (red), which you must close by conditioning on Z. C is a collider (D → C ← Y): it sits on no backdoor path and is naturally blocked, so conditioning on it would OPEN a spurious path and manufacture bias. The backdoor criterion here: control for Z, never for C.

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
section: Good vs bad controls, now derivable
text:
Good vs bad controls, now derivable
The graph delivers Part III’s control rules as theorems: (Cunningham 2021)

Conditioning on a mediator M in D\to M\to Y blocks the very causal flow you want, stripping out the indirect effect and under-stating the total impact. This is over-control — the occupation-in-the-wage-regression mistake, now visibly a blocked chain.
Conditioning on a collider C opens a closed path and invents association between variables that were independent. This is selection/collider bias — the gravest control error.


Mediation: when the mechanism is the question
Sometimes you want the path through M — you ask not just whether D affects Y but why and how much through which channel. That is mediation analysis, which decomposes the total effect into a direct effect (D\to Y, not through M) and an indirect effect (the part flowing D\to M\to Y). (Gerber and Green 2012) The DAG makes the stakes precise, and they cut two ways:

To estimate the total effect, never condition on the mediator — that is the over-control above, blocking the indirect channel and biasing the total toward zero. (Cunningham 2021)
To estimate the direct/indirect split, you must reason about M — but naively “controlling for M” does not cleanly identify the direct effect, because M is a post-treatment variable and is typically a collider linking D to the unobserved causes of Y. Conditioning on it reopens a backdoor and manufactures bias.

This is why Gerber & Green warn that mediation is one of the most misused techniques in social science: the intuitive “regress Y on D and M” (Baron–Kenny-style) silently assumes the mediator is itself unconfounded with the outcome — an assumption as strong as, and separate from, the one that justified the experiment on D. (Gerber and Green 2012) Credible mechanism work needs experimental manipulation of the mediator itself, or explicit, defended assumptions about M’s confounders — not a third variable bolted into the regression. (Gerber and Green 2012) The graph turns a tempting shortcut into a visible warning.






NoteTwo colliders worth memorising (Cunningham)



Google and the gender pay gap. Google argued that once you control for occupation (plus location, tenure, role), the gender gap disappears. But occupation is a collider: discrimination affects which jobs women get (\text{gender}\to\text{occupation}) and so does unobserved ability (\text{ability}\to\text{occupation}). Conditioning on occupation opens a path between gender and ability and can mask real discrimination. The “control” that made the gap vanish is exactly the one the backdoor criterion forbids. (Cunningham 2021)
The movie-star collider (Cunningham, crediting Gabriel Rossman). Why do film stars seem to be either gorgeous or talented, rarely both? In the population, beauty and talent are independent. But stardom is a collider: \text{beauty}\to\text{star}\leftarrow\text{talent}. Look only at stars (condition on the collider) and a spurious negative correlation appears — an untalented star must be beautiful, a plain star must be talented. Pure selection bias, manufactured by conditioning. (Cunningham 2021)

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
section: Intervention and the front door: Pearl’s do-operator
text:
Intervention and the front door: Pearl’s do-operator
Cunningham’s backdoor language and Judea Pearl’s are two dialects of one theory; Pearl’s Primer supplies the piece that makes “cause” a verb. To intervene is written with the do-operator, do(X=x), and it is sharply different from merely observing X=x. Conditioning on a value “narrows our focus” to the cases where X happened to equal x — it changes your perception.
