Intervening “changes the system”: you reach in and set X, overriding whatever normally produces it. (Pearl et al. 2016) Formally, do(x) is graph surgery — erase every arrow pointing into X, which “emancipates it from all other influences,” so any remaining association with Y is genuinely causal. (Pearl et al. 2016) The backdoor criterion is precisely the condition under which the surgical \Pr(Y\mid do(x)) can be rewritten as ordinary, estimable conditional probabilities — seeing used to compute doing. (Pearl et al. 2016)

When every backdoor is blocked for you: the front-door
Sometimes there is an incurable unobserved confounder U between X and Y — no backdoor set exists, because the variable you’d need to adjust for is unmeasured. Pearl’s front-door criterion is the escape: if the effect of X on Y runs entirely through an observed mediator Z that is itself shielded from the confounder, you can still identify the effect — without any data on U. (Pearl et al. 2016)
His example is the 1950s smoking debate. The tobacco industry posited a “smoking gene” U that caused both the craving to smoke (X) and cancer (Y), which would make the observed link spurious. Pearl’s move: measure tar deposits Z, on the assumptions that smoking causes cancer only through tar (X\to Z\to Y) and the gene does not affect tar. Then estimate X\to Z and Z\to Y (adjusting for X), and chain them together. (Pearl et al. 2016) You have deconfounded U using only observed variables — what Pearl calls an “utter miracle.” (Pearl et al. 2016) The front door is rarer in practice than the backdoor (its “effect flows only through Z” assumption is demanding), but it is the proof that a credible mechanism can rescue identification where adjustment alone is helpless.

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




“Control for everything you have.” Each control can close a backdoor or open a collider. Conditioning is surgery, not insurance. (Cunningham 2021)
Conditioning on a collider (or its descendant). It creates association from nothing — the subtlest bias in applied work, and invisible to R^2 or significance. (Cunningham 2021)
Controlling for a mediator when you want the total effect. You block the channel you meant to measure and under-state the effect. (Cunningham 2021)
Drawing the DAG to fit the result. The graph encodes assumptions; its missing arrows are claims. Draw it from theory, before estimation, and defend the absences. (Cunningham 2021)
Believing a DAG removes unobserved confounding. It only tells you what to condition on given the assumed structure; an omitted common cause you didn’t draw is still a backdoor left open. (Cunningham 2021)

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Cunningham, Causal Inference: The Mixtape — DAGs as a causal language bridging theory and econometrics; nodes/arrows, the meaning of a missing arrow, acyclicity, and the underlying structural causal model; the three structures — chain/mediator D\to M\to Y, fork/confounder D\leftarrow Z\to Y, collider D\to C\leftarrow Y; paths, backdoor paths, open vs blocked and colliders as dams (d-separation); the backdoor criterion; over-control from conditioning on a mediator and selection bias from conditioning on a collider; the Google/occupation gender-gap example and the movie-star (beauty → star ← talent) collider.
Kaplan, Lessons in Statistical Thinking — the intuitive on-ramp: a DAG as a network of causal influence, acyclicity as one-way deductive reasoning; a confounder as a variable that injects non-causal association; and adjustment (covariate adjustment) as the act of accounting for variation that blocks the confounding flow — the same move as regression’s “holding constant,” seen on the graph.
Pearl, Glymour & Jewell, Causal Inference in Statistics: A Primer — the do-operator do(x) and intervention as graph surgery (erase arrows into X); intervening (“changes the system”) vs conditioning (“narrows our focus”); the back-door criterion stated formally; and the front-door criterion with the smoking → tar → cancer example that deconfounds an unmeasured “smoking gene” — the “utter miracle.”

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The backdoor adjustment formula. If a set Z satisfies the backdoor criterion for D\to Y, the causal effect is identified by conditioning then averaging over Z: 
\Pr(Y\mid \operatorname{do}(D=d)) = \sum_{z}\Pr(Y\mid D=d, Z=z)\,\Pr(Z=z),
 where \operatorname{do}(D=d) denotes setting D by intervention (not merely observing it). In means, \text{ATE}=\mathbb E_Z\big[\mathbb E[Y\mid D=1,Z]-\mathbb E[Y\mid D=0,Z]\big] — exactly the subclassification estimator of the next entry. The criterion is what licenses writing the causal \operatorname{do}-probability as ordinary conditional probabilities you can estimate.
Why a collider induces dependence (algebra). Let D\perp Y marginally and C=\alpha D+\beta
Y+\varepsilon. Then \operatorname{Cov}(D,Y)=0, but conditional on C=c we have \beta Y \approx c-\alpha D-\varepsilon, so within a stratum of C a higher D forces a lower Y — a nonzero conditional covariance created purely by conditioning. This is the same algebra as the collider figure in Part III, now located on the graph.
Acyclicity and solvability. Acyclicity guarantees the structural equations can be ordered topologically and solved recursively (each variable from its parents), so the joint distribution factorises as \prod_i \Pr(X_i\mid \text{parents}(X_i)) — the Markov factorisation that makes d-separation read off the graph correspond to real conditional independence in the data.

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
text:
Connections

Builds on: regression and causality (good/bad controls and collider bias, now derived) and potential outcomes (the backdoor adjustment is the conditional version of the selection-bias fix).
Leads to: matching & propensity scores (estimating the backdoor adjustment when the criterion holds) and the quasi-experimental designs (IV, RD, DiD) for when it doesn’t — when an unblockable backdoor remains.
Reappears in: the IV graph (an instrument Z\to D\to Y with no Z\to Y arrow) and the bad-control warnings throughout Part V.

title: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs)
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“What do nodes, arrows, and missing arrows mean in a DAG, and what does ‘acyclic’ encode about reverse causality?”
“Define the chain (mediator), fork (confounder), and collider, and explain how association flows through each.”
“What is a backdoor path, and what does it mean for a path to be open or blocked (d-separation)? Why is a collider a ‘dam’?”
“State the backdoor criterion and explain why you must not condition on a collider or its descendant.”
“Use Cunningham’s Google/occupation and movie-star examples to explain how conditioning on a collider creates spurious association.”
“In Kaplan’s framing, how is ‘adjusting for’ a covariate the same act as ‘holding constant’ in regression, and how does adjustment block the non-causal flow of influence in a DAG?”
“Explain Pearl’s do-operator and ‘graph surgery’: how does intervening with do(x) differ from observing/conditioning on x?”
“State the front-door criterion and walk through Pearl’s smoking → tar → cancer example — how does it identify an effect despite an unmeasured confounder?”

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text: Every design in this Part rests on one untestable assumption — CIA, exclusion, continuity, parallel trends. The mature applied researcher exploits the fact that these assumptions fail in different ways: combine two designs and you can survive the failure of either; run several and a conclusion is believable only when independent strategies — each with its own flaws — agree. This is the orchestration the Furious Five were building toward: not picking a weapon, but commanding the arsenal.

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
Why it matters
A single design is a single point of failure. (Cunningham 2021) The credibility revolution’s mature phase is triangulation: because matching’s selection-on-observables, IV’s exclusion, RD’s continuity, and DiD’s parallel trends are logically independent bets, evidence that converges across them is far harder to dismiss than any one estimate. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) This entry is the synthesis the chapter has been pointing at — and the causal-design cousin of mixed-methods triangulation in Part VI: many imperfect lenses, trusted where they coincide.

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
Two ways to combine
There are two distinct moves, and it’s worth keeping them separate: (Cunningham 2021)

Stacking — strengthen identification. Layer designs so the combined estimator needs a weaker assumption than either alone (matching and DiD; an outcome model and a propensity model).
Triangulating — test robustness. Run designs side by side; if independent strategies disagree, at least one assumption is failing, and you learn which story is fragile.

The rest of the entry is the standard combinations, then the ethic that ties them together.

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
section: Bracketing: when two biased designs bound the truth
text:
Bracketing: when two biased designs bound the truth
The most elegant case: two designs biased in opposite directions sandwich the real effect. Angrist & Pischke’s bracketing result pairs fixed effects with a lagged-dependent-variable (Lagged-DV) model: (Angrist and Pischke 2009)

Fixed effects assumes selection is on a time-invariant unobserved trait \alpha_i — and tends to over-control, biasing one way.
Lagged-DV assumes selection is on the past outcome Y_{i,t-1} — and tends to under-control, biasing the other way.

Because they get selection wrong from opposite sides, the true effect is typically bounded between them. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) Report both: if they’re close, you’ve bracketed the answer to a narrow range; if they’re far apart, the data can’t yet pin it down — itself an honest finding. The figure shows this, plus the broader triangulation picture.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

truth = 1.0
# (label, estimate, std error)  — illustrative
rows = [
    ("Naive comparison",      2.05, 0.10),
    ("Fixed effects (upper)", 1.28, 0.12),
    ("Lagged-DV (lower)",     0.74, 0.12),
    ("Matching + DiD",        1.05, 0.14),
    ("IV (LATE)",             0.98, 0.20),
    ("Triple-difference",     1.08, 0.16),
    ("Doubly robust",         1.02, 0.11),
]
labels = [r[0] for r in rows]
est = np.array([r[1] for r in rows])
se = np.array([r[2] for r in rows])
y = np.arange(len(rows))[::-1]
colors = ["#c0392b", "#1f3a5f", "#1f3a5f", "#1f8a4c", "#1f8a4c", "#1f8a4c", "#1f8a4c"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.4, 4.3))
# bracket band between FE and Lagged-DV
ax.axvspan(0.74, 1.28, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.07)
ax.axvline(truth, color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.0, ls="--", label="true effect")
ax.errorbar(est, y, xerr=1.96 * se, fmt="o", color="none", ecolor="#666", capsize=3, zorder=2)
for xi, yi, c in zip(est, y, colors):
    ax.plot(xi, yi, "o", color=c, ms=8, zorder=3)
ax.text(0.755, len(rows)-0.5, "FE / Lagged-DV bracket", color="#1f3a5f", fontsize=8)
ax.set_yticks(y); ax.set_yticklabels(labels, fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlabel("estimated treatment effect")
ax.set_title("Different designs, different biases — believe the convergence")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="lower right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Bracketing and triangulation. A forest plot of the same effect estimated many ways. The naive comparison (red) is badly biased. Fixed effects and Lagged-DV bias from opposite directions and BRACKET the truth (green line) between them (shaded band). The credible designs — matching, IV, DiD, triple-difference, doubly-robust — cluster tightly around the truth: independent strategies with different flaws converging is what makes a causal claim believable.

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
Doubly robust estimation (AIPW)
The cleanest stacking result marries the two selection-on-observables tools — an outcome-regression model and a propensity-score model — into a single doubly robust (DR) estimator, usually Augmented Inverse-Propensity Weighting (AIPW). (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) Its near-magical property: the estimate is consistent if either working model is correctly specified — you get two shots at the truth and only need to hit one. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) This is exactly the engine under double/debiased ML: use flexible ML for both nuisance functions, lean on Neyman-orthogonality so small errors in each don’t bite, and cross-fit to kill overfitting. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) Doubly robust is the formal reason DML is trustworthy — it tolerates getting one half wrong.

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
Matching + DiD: conditional parallel trends
Parallel trends is a strong, population-wide bet. Matching + DiD (“conditional DiD”) weakens it: match treated and control units on pre-treatment covariates first, then difference. (Cunningham 2021) Now you need only conditional parallel trends — trends parallel within cells of X, not for the whole population. (Cunningham 2021) Mechanically, matching is preprocessing to balance covariates and prune incomparable units; the DiD runs on the matched sample. It’s a clean stack: matching handles the level differences in observables, DiD differences out the fixed unobservables, and the remaining assumption is the most defensible version of parallel trends.

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
Triple differences (DDD)
What if you fear a confounding trend that DiD can’t remove — a shock hitting the treated group (not just the treated units) at treatment time? Add a third difference. (Cunningham 2021) Difference-in-difference-in-differences (DDD) introduces a within-group control — an unaffected subgroup in the same place and time — to net out group-specific shocks.






NoteGruber’s maternity mandate (the textbook DDD)



Gruber (1994) studied state maternity-benefit mandates’ effect on wages. A plain DiD (affected states vs others, before vs after) would be confounded by any state-level economic trend coinciding with the law. The fix: a third difference — compare the wages of those targeted by the mandate (women of childbearing age) to an untargeted group (men and older women) within the same states. The triple interaction nets out both general state trends and national shifts for the targeted demographic, isolating the mandate’s effect. (Cunningham 2021)


The DDD effect is the coefficient on the triple interaction (\text{group}\times\text{time}\times\text{treated}) — one more difference, one fewer thing to assume away.

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
RD + DiD, and synthetic DiD
Two more combinations from the modern frontier: (Cunningham 2021; Baker et al. 2025)

Difference-in-discontinuities fuses RD with DiD: compare the jump at a cutoff across time periods, so anything else that changes discontinuously at the threshold (and would bias a plain RD) differences out. (Cunningham 2021)
Synthetic DiD (Arkhangelsky et al. 2021) blends synthetic control with DiD: use synthetic-control weights to build a well-matched comparison and the DiD double difference with unit and time fixed effects — gaining robustness and sidestepping the staggered-adoption “forbidden comparisons.” (Baker et al. 2025)

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
The credibility ethic
Underneath all of it is a stance: the researcher as skeptical judge of their own claim. (Cunningham 2021) Two habits express it:

Robustness checks. Show the result survives reasonable changes — different bandwidths in RD, alternative instruments, added state-specific trends in DiD. A finding that evaporates under a defensible tweak was never solid. (Cunningham 2021)
Independent agreement. A conclusion is most trustworthy when independent designs, each with different flaws, converge. (Cunningham 2021) Lee’s (2008) incumbency RD earned its credibility partly because the effect showed the predicted pattern across multiple correlated outcomes — internal triangulation that an artefact would be unlikely to fake. (Cunningham 2021)

The lesson that closes the design toolkit: don’t ask “which design?” — ask “do my designs agree, and where do they disagree?”

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Combining two bad designs. Stacking strengthens identification only if each part is plausible; garbage plus garbage is still garbage. (Cunningham 2021)
Reporting only the design that “worked.” Cherry-picking among strategies is p-hacking in a new costume. Report the set, and explain disagreements. (Cunningham 2021)
Reading a tight bracket as proof. FE and Lagged-DV bound the effect only under their respective selection stories; agreement is reassuring, not conclusive. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Assuming doubly robust means assumption-free. AIPW/DML still need unconfoundedness and overlap; it buys robustness to model specification, not to a broken design. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)
Conditional parallel trends taken for granted. Matching + DiD relaxes parallel trends but doesn’t abolish it — defend the conditional version. (Cunningham 2021)

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Cunningham, The Mixtape — designs rest on different untestable assumptions; matching + DiD (conditional parallel trends); triple differences and Gruber (1994); difference-in-discontinuities; the skeptical-judge credibility ethic, robustness checks, and independent-agreement (Lee 2008 across correlated outcomes).
Angrist & Pischke — the bracketing property of fixed effects vs lagged-dependent-variable models (opposite selection assumptions bound the effect).
Chernozhukov et al. — doubly robust / AIPW (consistent if either the outcome or propensity model is correct) and its realisation as double/debiased ML with cross-fitting.
DiD Practitioner’s Guide — synthetic DiD (Arkhangelsky et al. 2021) combining synthetic-control weighting with DiD and robustness to staggered-adoption forbidden comparisons.

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Doubly robust / AIPW. With propensity \hat e(X) and outcome models \hat\mu_d(X)=\hat{\mathbb
E}[Y\mid D=d,X], the AIPW estimate of the ATE is 
\hat\tau_{\text{DR}}=\frac1n\sum_i\Big[\hat\mu_1(X_i)-\hat\mu_0(X_i)
+\frac{D_i\,(Y_i-\hat\mu_1(X_i))}{\hat e(X_i)}
-\frac{(1-D_i)(Y_i-\hat\mu_0(X_i))}{1-\hat e(X_i)}\Big].
 If the outcome models are right, the augmentation terms have mean zero and the regression part is consistent; if instead the propensity is right, the weighting part is consistent and the regression errors cancel. Either suffices — the double robustness. The estimating equation is Neyman-orthogonal, which is why DML plugs ML into \hat e,\hat\mu and keeps \sqrt n-inference.
Triple differences. With group g\in\{targeted, untargeted\}, state s, time t, the saturated model’s effect is the triple interaction 
Y_{gst}=\dots+\beta_{\text{DDD}}\,(\text{Targeted}_g\times\text{Post}_t\times\text{Treated}_s)+\varepsilon,
 which differences out (i) state \times time shocks common to both groups and (ii) group \times time shifts common to all states — identifying \beta_{\text{DDD}} under a weaker parallel-trends assumption than 2×2 DiD (the two ordinary DiDs would have been biased by the same nuisance trend, which the third difference removes).
Why bracketing works. If true selection mixes a time-invariant component and a past-outcome component, FE (controlling only the former) and Lagged-DV (controlling only the latter) misattribute the other component in opposite directions, so \hat\beta_{\text{Lagged-DV}}\le\beta\le\hat\beta_{\text{FE}} (or the reverse) — the truth is sandwiched. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
Connections

Builds on: all of the Furious Five — matching, IV, RD, DiD, panel/FE — plus synthetic control and causal ML / DML (doubly robust).
Leads to: sensitivity analysis & external validity (the other half of credibility — how fragile, how generalisable) and the field-realities ethic of triangulation and honest robustness.
Synthesises: the design toolkit — from “what is an effect?” to orchestrating many imperfect designs into one credible claim.

title: Combining & Triangulating Designs
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Why do researchers combine and triangulate causal designs, and what is the difference between stacking (strengthening identification) and triangulating (robustness)?”
“Explain the bracketing property of fixed-effects vs lagged-dependent-variable models — why do they bound the true effect from opposite sides?”
“What is doubly robust / AIPW estimation, and why is it consistent if either the outcome or the propensity model is correct? How does this connect to double ML?”
“Explain matching + DiD (conditional parallel trends) and triple differences (DDD) with Gruber’s maternity-mandate example.”
“What is difference-in-discontinuities and synthetic DiD, and what is the credibility ethic of robustness checks and independent designs agreeing?”

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
text: A decision tree asks a series of yes/no questions and reads off an answer — the most interpretable model there is. It is also dangerously high-variance: redraw the data and the tree reshuffles. The fix is to grow hundreds of trees and average them. That move — random forests and boosting — sacrifices the flowchart for some of the best prediction accuracy available.

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
text:
Why it matters
Trees are the other great route (besides regularization) to managing the bias–variance trade-off, and they handle something linear models struggle with: non-linearities and interactions you never specified. (James et al. 2021) A single tree is a gift for communication — you can show it to a policymaker. Its ensembles are a gift for accuracy — forests and boosting are among the strongest off-the-shelf predictors on tabular social data. (Bruce et al. 2020) The journey from one tree to a forest is also the cleanest illustration of why averaging decorrelated models is the deepest trick in predictive modelling.

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
section: Decision trees: a flowchart fit to data
text:
Decision trees: a flowchart fit to data
A tree predicts by recursive binary splitting — a greedy, top-down carve-up of the predictor space. (James et al. 2021) Start at the root (all observations); find the single predictor and cutpoint whose split most improves fit; repeat inside each branch; stop at terminal nodes (leaves), where the prediction is just the mean (regression) or majority class (classification) of the observations there. (James et al. 2021) How is “best split” judged? Regression trees minimise RSS; classification trees maximise node purity via the Gini index or cross-entropy (better than raw error rate for growing the tree). (James et al. 2021)






NoteISL’s baseball-salary tree



Predicting log-salary from a player’s career stats, a small 3-leaf tree uses just Years and Hits: players with < 4.5 years experience average about $165,174; experienced players with ≥ 118 hits average $845,346. (James et al. 2021) Two questions, a clear story — “pay rises with experience, then with productivity” — readable by anyone. Trees are even easier to explain than linear regression, and arguably mirror how humans actually decide. (James et al. 2021)

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
section: The flaw: high variance, and pruning
text:
The flaw: high variance, and pruning
A single tree’s fatal weakness: it is high-variance and overfits easily. (James et al. 2021) Grow it deep enough and it carves the data into perfectly pure leaves — memorising the noise — and a slightly different sample yields a wholly different tree. Pruning (cost-complexity / weakest-link pruning) controls this: grow a large tree, then cut it back with a penalty \alpha on the number of leaves, choosing \alpha by cross-validation. (James et al. 2021) But pruning only mitigates the variance — the real cure is to stop relying on one tree.

See it: one jagged tree vs a smooth forest
Below, a single deep tree (orange) fits the data as a wild step function — over-reacting to every wiggle of noise. A forest of many trees, each grown on a bootstrap resample and then averaged (blue), smooths those steps into a stable estimate of the signal. Same data, the variance averaged away.


Show the figure code (a minimal regression tree + bagging)
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

def fit_tree(x, y, depth, max_depth, min_leaf=3):
    if depth >= max_depth or x.size < 2 * min_leaf:
        m = y.mean();  return lambda xq: np.full(xq.shape, m)
    order = np.argsort(x); xs = x[order]
    best = (np.inf, None)
    for i in range(min_leaf, x.size - min_leaf + 1):
        t = 0.5 * (xs[i-1] + xs[i])
        l, r = y[x <= t], y[x > t]
        if l.size and r.size:
            sse = ((l - l.mean())**2).sum() + ((r - r.mean())**2).sum()
            if sse < best[0]: best = (sse, t)
    t = best[1]
    if t is None:
        m = y.mean();  return lambda xq: np.full(xq.shape, m)
    L = fit_tree(x[x <= t], y[x <= t], depth+1, max_depth)
    R = fit_tree(x[x > t],  y[x > t],  depth+1, max_depth)
    return lambda xq: np.where(xq <= t, L(xq), R(xq))

n = 70
x = np.sort(rng.uniform(0, 10, n))
signal = lambda x: np.sin(0.9 * x) + 0.25 * x
y = signal(x) + rng.normal(0, 0.45, n)
grid = np.linspace(0, 10, 400)

single = fit_tree(x, y, 0, max_depth=10)(grid)               # one deep tree
B = 150
forest = np.zeros_like(grid)
for _ in range(B):
    idx = rng.integers(0, n, n)                               # bootstrap resample
    forest += fit_tree(x[idx], y[idx], 0, max_depth=10)(grid)
forest /= B

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.2))
ax.scatter(x, y, s=16, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.6, zorder=2)
ax.plot(grid, signal(grid), color="#7a7f87", lw=1.6, ls="--", label="true signal")
ax.plot(grid, single, color="#d98a29", lw=1.6, label="single deep tree (overfit)")
ax.plot(grid, forest, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.6, label=f"forest: average of {B} trees")
ax.set_xlabel("x"); ax.set_ylabel("y")
ax.set_title("One tree is jagged; a forest is smooth")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A single deep regression tree (orange) overfits — a jagged step function chasing the noise, the hallmark of high variance. A forest (blue), averaging 150 trees each grown on a bootstrap resample, smooths the steps into a stable fit close to the true signal (grey). Averaging decorrelated trees is what tames the variance.

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
text:
Bagging: average away the variance
Bagging (bootstrap aggregating) is the general cure for any high-variance method. (James et al. 2021) Draw B bootstrap resamples, grow a deep (unpruned) tree on each, and average their predictions (or take a majority vote). Averaging many noisy-but-unbiased estimates slashes variance without adding bias — the law of large numbers applied to models. A free bonus: out-of-bag (OOB) error. Each tree omits about a third of the data (the observations not drawn into its bootstrap), so predicting each point with only the trees that didn’t see it gives a built-in test-error estimate — no separate validation set needed. (James et al. 2021)

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
text:
Random forests: decorrelate, then average
Bagging has a subtle weakness, and random forests fix it. (James et al. 2021) If one predictor is very strong, every bagged tree splits on it first — so the trees are highly correlated, and averaging correlated things barely reduces variance (the same correlation penalty as in LOOCV). The fix is elegantly simple: at each split, allow only a random subset of m predictors to be considered. (James et al. 2021) This forces trees to use different variables, decorrelating them, so their average is far more stable. The standard choice is m \approx \sqrt{p}. (James et al. 2021) That one tweak — randomly handicapping each split — is what makes a random forest beat plain bagging.

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
section: Boosting: learn slowly from your mistakes
text:
Boosting: learn slowly from your mistakes
Boosting takes the opposite philosophy to bagging’s parallel averaging: it grows trees sequentially, each new tree fitting the residuals (the errors) of the current ensemble. (James et al. 2021) The model learns slowly, concentrating on the cases it has been getting wrong. Three tuning knobs: the number of trees B (too many can overfit, but slowly), the shrinkage / learning rate \lambda (how fast it learns — typically 0.01 or 0.001), and the tree depth d (often tiny — even stumps, d=1, suffice). (James et al. 2021) Well-tuned boosting is frequently the most accurate method on structured/tabular data.

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
section: Peeking into the black box: variable importance
text:
Peeking into the black box: variable importance
Ensembles buy accuracy by sacrificing the single tree’s interpretability — there’s no one flowchart to read. (James et al. 2021) Variable importance measures partly reopen the box: (James et al. 2021)

Gini / RSS importance — total up how much each predictor’s splits reduced RSS (regression) or the Gini index (classification), averaged over all trees.
Permutation importance — randomly shuffle a predictor’s values and measure how much accuracy drops; a big drop means the model relied on it.







NoteISL’s Heart data



For predicting heart disease, the most important predictors surfaced by a forest were Thal, Ca, and ChestPain. (James et al. 2021) Importance gives a ranking — useful and suggestive — but, as the next entry stresses, a ranking is not a causal effect. (James et al. 2021)

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
section: Trees vs linear models: which wins?
text:
Trees vs linear models: which wins?
It depends entirely on the true data-generating process. (James et al. 2021)

Linear/logistic models win when the relationship is approximately linear — OLS exploits that structure efficiently, and trees only approximate a line with clumsy steps.
Trees and forests win when the truth is highly non-linear with many interactions the researcher never specified — they discover that structure automatically. (James et al. 2021)

A useful division of labour: single trees for communication and exploration; forests and boosting for maximum predictive power. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Trusting a single deep tree. It’s high-variance and overfit; a different sample gives a different tree. Prune it, or move to an ensemble. (James et al. 2021)
Expecting a forest to be interpretable. Averaging hundreds of trees destroys the flowchart. Use variable importance / partial dependence, and accept you’ve traded transparency for accuracy. (James et al. 2021)
Reading variable importance as a causal effect. It measures predictive contribution, not cause; a strong predictor may be a proxy or a collider. (James et al. 2021)
Skipping tuning on boosting. Too high a learning rate or too-deep trees overfit; boosting needs CV on its knobs. (James et al. 2021)
Using trees where a line would do. On near-linear data, regression beats trees and stays interpretable. Match the method to the structure. (James et al. 2021)

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, Introduction to Statistical Learning — recursive binary splitting, leaves and the mean/majority prediction, RSS vs Gini/entropy splits; tree interpretability; high variance and cost-complexity pruning; the Hitters salary tree (Years/Hits; $165,174 and $845,346); bagging and OOB error; random forests, decorrelation, and m\approx\sqrt p; boosting (sequential, residual-fitting, B/\lambda/d, stumps); Gini and permutation variable importance; the Heart data (Thal, Ca, ChestPain); the trees-vs-linear verdict.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — trees and ensembles in the applied data-science idiom; single trees for communication, ensembles for predictive power.

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Split criteria. A regression split sends the data into regions R_1,R_2 to minimise \sum_{i\in R_1}(y_i-\bar y_{R_1})^2 + \sum_{i\in R_2}(y_i-\bar y_{R_2})^2. A classification split minimises a purity measure — the Gini index G=\sum_k \hat p_k(1-\hat p_k) or cross-entropy D=-\sum_k \hat p_k\log\hat p_k — both small when one class dominates a node.
Cost-complexity pruning. For a large tree T_0, choose the subtree T\subseteq T_0 minimising \sum_{m=1}^{|T|}\sum_{i\in R_m}(y_i-\bar y_{R_m})^2 + \alpha|T|, where |T| is the number of leaves and \alpha\ge 0 trades fit against size. Increasing \alpha yields a nested sequence of subtrees; CV picks \alpha.
Why decorrelation reduces variance. The average of B identically-distributed trees each with variance \sigma^2 and pairwise correlation \rho has variance 
\rho\,\sigma^2 + \frac{1-\rho}{B}\,\sigma^2 .
 As B\to\infty the second term vanishes, but the first, \rho\sigma^2, does not — so bagging is capped by how correlated the trees are. Random forests shrink \rho (by restricting splits to m random predictors), pushing the floor down — the exact mechanism behind their gain. (Same correlated-average algebra as the Moulton/clustering and LOOCV results.)

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
text:
Connections

Builds on: the bias–variance trade-off (a high-variance method tamed by averaging), the bootstrap (bagging’s engine), and the LLN (why averaging many models works).
Leads to: interpretability & ML for social science (variable importance and partial dependence as the price of ensembles), and causal forests for heterogeneous treatment effects.
Contrasts with: regression — linear structure vs learned non-linear partitions; the right choice depends on the truth.

title: Trees, Forests & Boosting
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Explain how a decision tree is built by recursive binary splitting, with the ISL baseball-salary example.”
“Why is a single tree high-variance, and what does cost-complexity pruning do?”
“Explain bagging and out-of-bag error, and how random forests improve on bagging by decorrelating trees (m ≈ √p).”
“How does boosting differ from bagging, and what are its tuning parameters?”
“What are Gini and permutation variable importance, and why is importance not the same as a causal effect?”

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text: OLS is unbiased — and when predictors are many or collinear, that unbiasedness comes at a ruinous price in variance. Regularization makes a trade the bias–variance logic predicts will pay: shrink the coefficients toward zero, accept a little bias, and buy a large drop in variance. The Lasso goes further, setting some coefficients exactly to zero — doing variable selection automatically.

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text:
Why it matters
The estimation entry’s shrinkage estimator — biased but lower-MSE — was a curiosity there. Here it becomes a workhorse. When the number of predictors p is large relative to n, OLS overfits badly; when p > n, OLS has no unique solution at all. (James et al. 2021) Regularization rescues both, and the Lasso throws in a bonus social scientists prize: interpretability, by zeroing out the variables that don’t earn their place. (James et al. 2021) This is also the on-ramp to high-dimensional and causal ML, where regularised estimates of many nuisance parameters are routine.

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text:
The idea: penalise big coefficients
Both methods keep OLS’s goal — minimise the residual sum of squares — but add a penalty on the size of the coefficients, so the fit must now balance accuracy against coefficient magnitude. (James et al. 2021) A tuning parameter \lambda \ge 0 sets the exchange rate:

\lambda = 0: no penalty — you get plain OLS.
\lambda \to \infty: the penalty dominates — all coefficients are crushed toward zero (the null model).

In between, \lambda is a bias–variance dial: as it rises, variance falls (the model is less sensitive to the training sample) and bias rises (it assumes coefficients are small). The test error is U-shaped in \lambda, and you pick the bottom by cross-validation. (James et al. 2021)

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text:
Ridge regression (\ell_2)
Ridge adds the sum of squared coefficients: (James et al. 2021)

\min_{\beta}\ \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^n\Big(y_i - \beta_0 - \sum_j \beta_j x_{ij}\Big)^2}_{\text{RSS}}
\;+\; \lambda \sum_{j=1}^p \beta_j^2 .

The \ell_2 penalty shrinks every coefficient toward zero but never to zero (except in the limit \lambda=\infty). (James et al. 2021) So Ridge keeps all predictors — it tames their magnitudes rather than dropping any. Two practical notes: Ridge is not scale-invariant (the penalty treats all \beta_j alike, so a variable measured in dollars is penalised differently from one in years), so you must standardise predictors to mean 0, SD 1 before fitting; and Ridge is especially good under multicollinearity, spreading weight smoothly across correlated predictors instead of letting OLS swing them wildly. (James et al. 2021)

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text:
The Lasso (\ell_1)
The Lasso changes one thing — squared coefficients become absolute values: (James et al. 2021)

\min_{\beta}\ \text{RSS} \;+\; \lambda \sum_{j=1}^p |\beta_j| .

That small change has a dramatic effect: for large enough \lambda, the \ell_1 penalty forces some coefficients to be exactly zero. (James et al. 2021) The Lasso therefore performs automatic variable selection, yielding a sparse model — only the predictors that earn their keep survive — which is far easier for a social scientist to interpret than Ridge’s dense vector of small-but-nonzero coefficients. (James et al. 2021)

Why Lasso zeroes coefficients but Ridge doesn’t
The reason is geometry, and it is beautiful. Each method can be written as “minimise RSS subject to a budget on coefficient size.” The budget’s shape is everything: (James et al. 2021)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

bhat = np.array([1.9, 1.1])                         # OLS estimate
A = np.array([[1.0, 0.5], [0.5, 1.3]])              # RSS contour shape
g1, g2 = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(-0.5, 3, 400), np.linspace(-0.5, 2.6, 400))
Z = (A[0,0]*(g1-bhat[0])**2 + 2*A[0,1]*(g1-bhat[0])*(g2-bhat[1]) + A[1,1]*(g2-bhat[1])**2)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 4.4))
for ax in (axL, axR):
    ax.contour(g1, g2, Z, levels=8, colors="#c0392b", linewidths=0.8, alpha=0.8)
    ax.scatter(*bhat, color="#c0392b", zorder=5); ax.text(bhat[0]+0.06, bhat[1]+0.05, "β̂", color="#c0392b")
    ax.axhline(0, color="#999", lw=0.7); ax.axvline(0, color="#999", lw=0.7)
    ax.set_xlim(-0.5, 3); ax.set_ylim(-0.5, 2.6); ax.set_aspect("equal")
    ax.set_xlabel("β₁"); ax.set_ylabel("β₂")

s = 1.15
axL.add_patch(plt.Polygon([[s,0],[0,s],[-s,0],[0,-s]], closed=True,
              facecolor="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.3, edgecolor="#1f3a5f", lw=2))
axL.scatter(0, s, color="#1f8a4c", zorder=6, s=60)
axL.text(0.06, s+0.06, "solution: β₁ = 0", color="#1f8a4c", fontsize=8)
axL.set_title("Lasso (ℓ₁): diamond → hits a corner")

axR.add_patch(plt.Circle((0,0), 1.15, facecolor="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.3, edgecolor="#1f3a5f", lw=2))
ang = 0.62
axR.scatter(1.15*np.cos(ang), 1.15*np.sin(ang), color="#1f8a4c", zorder=6, s=60)
axR.text(0.55, 0.78, "both β ≠ 0", color="#1f8a4c", fontsize=8)
axR.set_title("Ridge (ℓ₂): circle → no corner")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Why Lasso selects and Ridge doesn’t (ISL’s picture). Red ellipses are RSS contours around the OLS estimate β̂; the blue region is the coefficient budget. Left (Lasso, ℓ1): the budget is a diamond with corners on the axes, so the growing ellipse first touches it at a corner — where β₁ = 0. Right (Ridge, ℓ2): the budget is a smooth circle with no corners, so the contact point has both coefficients non-zero.





The diamond has corners sitting exactly on the axes (where a coefficient is zero), and the expanding RSS ellipse tends to touch the budget at one of those corners — zeroing that coefficient. The circle is smooth and corner-free, so contact almost always lands where both coefficients are non-zero. (James et al. 2021) Sparsity is a consequence of the \ell_1 ball’s pointy shape.

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text:
Choosing \lambda, and which method wins
Pick \lambda by k-fold cross-validation: score a grid of \lambda values, take the one minimising CV error (or the one-standard-error rule’s most parsimonious model within one SE). (James et al. 2021)






NoteISL’s Credit data and the verdict



On the Credit data (predicting card balance), the Ridge paths show Rating, Limit, Income, Student all shrinking smoothly toward zero but staying non-zero; the Lasso paths show variables switching on one at a time as \lambda falls — Rating first, then Student, Limit, finally Income — a readable ranking of importance, with the rest pinned at zero. (James et al. 2021)
Neither method universally wins. In a sparse world (a few predictors really matter) the Lasso excels; in a dense world (many small effects) Ridge does. So: Lasso for interpretability and selection, Ridge for robustness under multicollinearity. (James et al. 2021) (The elastic net blends both.)

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Forgetting to standardise. Ridge and Lasso are scale-dependent; an unstandardised variable is penalised by its units, not its importance. Standardise predictors first. (James et al. 2021)
Reading shrunken coefficients as unbiased effects. Regularization deliberately biases coefficients toward zero — they are tuned for prediction, not for an honest causal magnitude. Don’t interpret a Lasso coefficient like an OLS one. (James et al. 2021)
Treating Lasso’s selection as the “true model.” Which variables survive depends on \lambda, the sample, and correlations among predictors; a dropped variable isn’t proven irrelevant, and with correlated predictors the Lasso picks one somewhat arbitrarily. (James et al. 2021)
Choosing \lambda by training error. Training error falls as \lambda\to 0 (toward OLS); only cross-validated error reveals the right amount of shrinkage. (James et al. 2021)
Using Ridge when you wanted selection (or Lasso under heavy collinearity). Match the tool to the world — sparse vs dense, correlated vs not. (James et al. 2021)

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, Introduction to Statistical Learning — the rationale for shrinkage (prediction accuracy when p is large or p>n; interpretability); Ridge’s objective \text{RSS}+\lambda\sum\beta_j^2, the role of \lambda, no-exact-zeros, and the standardisation requirement; the Lasso’s objective \text{RSS}+\lambda\sum|\beta_j| and exact-zero variable selection / sparsity; the diamond-vs-circle geometry; bias-up/variance-down as \lambda grows; choosing \lambda by CV with the one-SE rule; the Credit-data coefficient paths and the sparse-vs-dense simulation verdict (Lasso for selection, Ridge for correlated predictors).

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Ridge has a closed form. In matrix form, minimising \lVert y-X\beta\rVert^2+\lambda\lVert\beta\rVert^2 gives

\hat\beta^{\text{ridge}} = (X^\top X + \lambda I)^{-1}X^\top y .

The added \lambda I makes X^\top X+\lambda I invertible even when X^\top X is singular (e.g. p>n) — which is precisely why Ridge works where OLS fails. As \lambda\to\infty, \hat\beta^{\text{ridge}}\to 0.
The constrained form. Each penalty is equivalent (by Lagrangian duality) to minimising RSS subject to a budget: Ridge to \sum\beta_j^2\le s, Lasso to \sum|\beta_j|\le s, with a one-to-one \lambda\leftrightarrow s. The geometry of those budget sets — the smooth \ell_2 ball vs the cornered \ell_1 ball — is what the figure draws, and is the exact reason for (no) sparsity.
Soft-thresholding (orthonormal case). When the predictors are orthonormal, the Lasso solution is explicit: \hat\beta_j^{\text{lasso}} = \operatorname{sign}(\hat\beta_j)\,(|\hat\beta_j|-\lambda/2)_+ — each OLS coefficient is pulled toward zero by \lambda/2 and clipped to exactly zero once it’s smaller than that. Ridge instead scales each by 1/(1+\lambda) — shrinking proportionally, never to zero. That contrast — subtract-and-clip vs scale — is the algebra behind the geometry.

title: Regularization: Ridge & Lasso
text:
Connections

Builds on: the bias–variance trade-off (regularization buys variance reduction with bias), the shrinkage estimator (lower MSE via bias), cross-validation (how \lambda is chosen), and OLS / Gauss–Markov (the unbiased baseline being traded away).
Leads to: trees & forests (another route to low variance, via averaging) and high-dimensional causal ML (Lasso for selecting controls; double/debiased ML).
Contrasts with: logistic regression, which the Lasso also regularises for high-dimensional classification.

text:

title: IV · The Computational Turn: Predictive Modelling
text:
This is the hinge of the manual: where statistical thinking (estimate an effect, defend it, quantify uncertainty) meets computational thinking (build something that predicts well on data it has never seen). It is the on-ramp to the causal summit — you meet cross-validation, regularization, and the bias–variance trade-off here, because modern causal inference uses these tools — and it gathers the field’s running debates in one place.
There are two distinct goals people pursue with the same regression output, and conflating them is the source of endless confusion in both academia and industry:

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
text: The bias–variance sweet spot is invisible: it lives in test error, and you don’t have infinite test data. Cross-validation is the trick that manufactures honest test error from a limited sample — by repeatedly hiding part of the data, fitting on the rest, and scoring on what was hidden. It is how every flexibility dial in this chapter actually gets set.
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
text:
Why it matters
The previous two entries established the goal — minimise error on unseen data — and warned that training error is a liar, falling toward zero as you overfit. (James et al. 2021) But if you can’t trust training error and don’t have a giant separate test set, how do you choose a model’s flexibility, or a regularization penalty, or which variables to keep? Cross-validation is the answer, and it is the most important procedure in predictive modelling: the disciplined way to estimate test error and tune a model without fooling yourself. Get its one golden rule wrong and every result downstream is optimistic fiction.
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
text:
The sacred test set
The cleanest discipline splits data three ways: (James et al. 2021)

Training set — fit the model’s parameters.
Validation set — tune its complexity (flexibility, penalty, which variables).
Test set — sacred: held out, untouched, never seen by the algorithm until a single, final, once-and-for-all evaluation.

The test set exists to give an honest estimate of real-world performance, and it only stays honest if you never peek. (James et al. 2021) The cardinal sin is data leakage — letting information from the validation or test data seep into training. A classic leak: standardising the whole dataset before splitting, so the training fold has secretly “seen” the held-out data’s mean and scale. The result is an artificially optimistic score that collapses in production. (James et al. 2021)
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
section: The validation-set approach (and why it’s not enough)
text:
The validation-set approach (and why it’s not enough)
The simplest estimate of test error: randomly split the data once into a training half and a validation (hold-out) half, fit on the first, score on the second. (James et al. 2021)






NoteISL’s Auto example



Predicting a car’s gas mileage from horsepower, ISL splits 392 observations into 196 train / 196 validation. A linear fit gives a validation MSE of 23.30; adding a quadratic term drops it to 18.90 — clear evidence the relationship curves. (James et al. 2021)


But the single-split approach has two flaws: (James et al. 2021)

High variability. The estimate swings depending on which observations land in which half — ISL shows ten different random splits producing ten different error curves.
Wasted data. Training on only half the sample, models perform worse than they would on the full data, so the validation approach overestimates the true test error.

Cross-validation fixes both by reusing every observation for both roles.
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
text:
k-fold cross-validation
The modern standard. Split the data into k roughly-equal folds; hold out one fold as validation, train on the other k-1, score on the held-out fold; rotate so each fold is the validation set exactly once; average the k scores. (James et al. 2021)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

k = 5
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.2))
for row in range(k):                       # one round per row
    for col in range(k):                   # the k folds
        val = (col == row)
        ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((col, k - 1 - row), 0.96, 0.96,
                     facecolor="#d98a29" if val else "#1f3a5f",
                     edgecolor="white", lw=2))
    ax.text(-0.25, k - 1 - row + 0.48, f"round {row+1}", ha="right", va="center", fontsize=9)
ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((k + 0.4, k - 1.5), 0.5, 0.5, facecolor="#1f3a5f"))
ax.text(k + 1.05, k - 1.25, "train", va="center", fontsize=9)
ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((k + 0.4, k - 2.3), 0.5, 0.5, facecolor="#d98a29"))
ax.text(k + 1.05, k - 2.05, "validate", va="center", fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlim(-1.5, k + 2.5); ax.set_ylim(-0.3, k + 0.3)
ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.axis("off")
ax.set_title("5-fold cross-validation: every fold is held out once")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: 5-fold cross-validation. The data is split into 5 folds. In each of 5 rounds, one fold (orange) is held out for validation while the model trains on the other four (blue). Every observation is used for validation exactly once; the 5 validation scores are averaged into one honest test-error estimate. No single split decides the answer.





Why k = 5 or 10? They are the standard compromise. (James et al. 2021) The extreme case k = n is leave-one-out cross-validation (LOOCV) — fit on n-1 points, test on the one left out, repeat n times. LOOCV has very low bias (each fit uses almost all the data) and is perfectly stable (no random splits), but two costs push practitioners toward 5 or 10 folds: (James et al. 2021)

Computation — LOOCV fits the model n times (though OLS has a magic closed-form shortcut).
Higher variance of the estimate — a subtle point. LOOCV’s n models are trained on nearly identical data, so their errors are highly correlated, and averaging strongly-correlated quantities yields a higher-variance average than averaging the 5 or 10 less-correlated estimates of k-fold. So 10-fold often estimates test error better than LOOCV. (James et al. 2021)
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
section: Using CV to tune the model
text:
Using CV to tune the model
This is what cross-validation is for: pick the flexibility that minimises estimated test error. (James et al. 2021) Compute the CV error across a grid of the tuning knob — polynomial degree, the regularization penalty \lambda, tree depth — and find the bottom of the U-shaped curve.






TipThe one-standard-error rule



When the CV curve is flat near its minimum, the lowest point is barely better than its neighbours and is itself noisy. The one-standard-error rule says: compute the standard error of the CV estimates, and pick the simplest (most parsimonious) model whose CV error is within one standard error of the minimum. (James et al. 2021) You give up a sliver of estimated accuracy for a simpler, more robust model — a principled lean toward parsimony.
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
section: The golden rule: keep choices inside the loop
text:
The golden rule: keep choices inside the loop
The single most common — and most damaging — mistake. Any data-dependent choice must be made inside the cross-validation loop. (James et al. 2021) If you use CV to select variables, you must redo the variable selection separately within each training fold, so the validation fold stays genuinely unseen.
The tempting shortcut — screen all the variables (or standardise, or impute) on the whole dataset first, then cross-validate the chosen model — lets the model peek at the answers. The validation folds influenced the choices being “validated,” so the CV score is over-optimistic and won’t generalise. (James et al. 2021) If a decision touches the data, it goes inside the loop. No exceptions.
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Pre-processing before splitting. Standardising, imputing, or selecting variables on the full dataset leaks the held-out data into training. Fit every data-dependent step on the training fold only. (James et al. 2021)
Touching the test set more than once. The moment you use the test set to make a choice, it becomes a validation set and stops being an honest estimate. Hold it sacred for the final number. (James et al. 2021)
Trusting a single train/validation split. Its estimate is high-variance and pessimistic; use k-fold to average over splits and use all the data. (James et al. 2021)
Assuming LOOCV is best because it has lowest bias. Its highly-correlated fits give a higher-variance estimate than 10-fold; the standard k=5 or 10 usually wins. (James et al. 2021)
Reporting the CV error of the selected model as its test error. Having chosen the model to minimise CV error, that minimum is itself optimistic — the honest number comes from the untouched test set.
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, Introduction to Statistical Learning — training error underestimates test error; the train/validation/test split and the sacred test set; the validation-set approach (Auto data, 196/196 split, linear MSE 23.30 vs quadratic 18.90) and its two drawbacks; LOOCV (procedure, low bias, stability, the OLS shortcut); k-fold CV with k=5,10 and the correlation argument for its lower-variance estimate; CV for tuning flexibility/\lambda and the one-standard-error rule; the rule that data-dependent choices belong inside the CV loop.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — the applied train/test idiom and data leakage; the exam-cheating intuition for evaluating on training data.
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The k-fold estimate. Partition the indices into folds C_1,\dots,C_k of size \approx n/k. Let \hat f^{-\kappa} be the model fit with fold \kappa removed. Then

\text{CV}_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{\kappa=1}^{k} \text{MSE}_\kappa,
\qquad \text{MSE}_\kappa = \frac{1}{|C_\kappa|}\sum_{i\in C_\kappa}\big(y_i - \hat f^{-\kappa}(x_i)\big)^2 .

LOOCV is the case k=n, |C_\kappa|=1.
LOOCV’s magic shortcut (OLS). For linear models fit by least squares, LOOCV needs only one fit:

\text{CV}_{(n)} = \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n \left(\frac{y_i-\hat y_i}{1-h_i}\right)^2,

where \hat y_i is the full-sample fitted value and h_i is observation i’s leverage. The leverage inflates each residual by exactly the amount that point pulled the fit toward itself — recovering the leave-one-out error without refitting.
Why LOOCV has higher variance. The estimate is a mean of n fold-errors. For correlated terms, \operatorname{Var}(\bar T) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\big(1+(n-1)\bar\rho\big) — the same clustering inflation (Moulton) logic. LOOCV’s fits are almost identical, so \bar\rho is large and the average is high-variance; k-fold’s fits overlap less, lowering \bar\rho and the variance of the estimate.
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation

title: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
text:
Connections

Builds on: the bias–variance trade-off (CV estimates the test error whose minimum is the sweet spot), explaining vs predicting (out-of-sample is the only honest scorecard), and the bootstrap / resampling (CV is a resampling method).
Leads to: regularization (CV chooses the penalty \lambda) and trees & forests (CV tunes depth and the number of trees).
Reappears in: causal machine learning, where cross-fitting (sample-splitting) is what keeps ML-estimated nuisances from biasing the treatment effect.
crumbs: Out-of-Sample Honesty: The Test Set & Cross-Validation
