title: Count-Data Models: Poisson & Negative Binomial
text: A huge class of social outcomes are counts: arrests this year, doctor visits, protests in a city, patents filed. They are non-negative integers that “pile up at zero,” and a straight line predicts impossible negatives. Poisson regression is the natural model — it uses a log link so predicted counts stay positive and its coefficients read as multipliers on the rate. Its one strong assumption (variance = mean) often fails, and the negative binomial is the standard repair.

title: Count-Data Models: Poisson & Negative Binomial
text:
Why it matters
Count outcomes are everywhere in criminology, health, and political science, and they break OLS in a specific way: the outcome is bounded below at zero, discrete, and usually right-skewed. (Wooldridge 2019) Poisson regression — a generalized linear model with a log link — is the workhorse, and knowing when its assumptions fail (and what to switch to) is the difference between honest and overconfident inference on counts.

title: Count-Data Models: Poisson & Negative Binomial
text:
Poisson regression
The model puts the log link on a count outcome, so the expected count is an exponential of the linear predictor: (Wooldridge 2019) 
\mathbb E[y\mid x] = \exp(X\beta),
 which is always positive — exactly what a count needs. (McNulty 2021)
Coefficients are multiplicative. Because of the exp, e^{\beta_j} is a rate ratio (incidence-rate ratio): a one-unit rise in x_j multiplies the expected count by e^{\beta_j}. (Wooldridge 2019) The book’s example: a coefficient of 0.661 on a group dummy means e^{0.661}\approx 1.93 — the expected count is about 93% higher for that group. (McNulty 2021) (Read this exactly like a Cox hazard ratio or a logistic odds ratio: multiplicative, not additive.)

title: Count-Data Models: Poisson & Negative Binomial
text:
The equidispersion trap
The Poisson distribution is “highly restrictive” because it assumes equidispersion — the variance equals the mean: (Wooldridge 2019) 
\operatorname{Var}(y\mid x) = \mathbb E[y\mid x].
 This is a property of the distribution, not a thing you get to choose, and in real social data it usually fails. (Wooldridge 2019)
Overdispersion — variance exceeds the mean — is the common failure. It happens when events “clump” or show “contagion”: one arrest makes the next more likely, one protest spawns others, so counts spread far wider than Poisson allows. (Wooldridge 2019) Fit Poisson to overdispersed data and the point estimates are roughly fine but the standard errors are too small — false precision, spurious significance.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
mu = 4.0
n = 4000

pois = rng.poisson(mu, n)
# Negative binomial as a Gamma-Poisson mixture -> overdispersion (variance > mean)
rate = rng.gamma(shape=1.0, scale=mu, size=n)     # heterogeneity across units
nb = rng.poisson(rate)

bins = np.arange(0, 22) - 0.5
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.4, 4.0))
ax.hist(pois, bins=bins, density=True, color="#9bb4d4", alpha=0.8,
        label=f"Poisson  (mean={pois.mean():.1f}, var={pois.var():.1f})")
ax.hist(nb, bins=bins, density=True, histtype="step", lw=2.4, color="#1f3a5f",
        label=f"overdispersed  (mean={nb.mean():.1f}, var={nb.var():.1f})")
ax.set_xlabel("count"); ax.set_ylabel("proportion")
ax.set_title("Variance = mean (Poisson) vs variance ≫ mean (overdispersed)")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Equidispersion vs overdispersion. Both samples share the same mean count (~4). The Poisson (light) obeys variance = mean. The overdispersed counts (dark) — events that ‘clump’ — spread much wider, with both more zeros and a longer right tail. Fitting Poisson to the dark data leaves the estimates roughly right but the standard errors far too small; the negative binomial adds a parameter to absorb that extra spread.

title: Count-Data Models: Poisson & Negative Binomial
text:
The fixes
Negative binomial regression is the standard solution for overdispersion. (McNulty 2021) It adds an extra parameter that “governs the degree of overdispersion,” letting the variance grow faster than the mean — so the standard errors are honest when counts clump. (Wooldridge 2019) (When the data are not overdispersed, it collapses back toward Poisson.)
Two more practical tools: (Wooldridge 2019)

Exposure / offset. When units are observed over different windows — arrests per month vs per year, events per differing population — include an offset so the model fits a rate per unit of exposure, not a raw count. Comparing raw counts across unequal exposure is a classic error.
Zero-inflation / hurdle models. When there are “too many zeros” for Poisson — often because the zeros come from a different process than the counts — split the problem into two stages: whether a unit participates at all (0 vs >0) and, given participation, how many. (Wooldridge 2019) (A person who never drinks vs how many drinks a drinker has.)

title: Count-Data Models: Poisson & Negative Binomial
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Ignoring overdispersion. Poisson on clumped counts gives too-small SEs and false significance; test for it and switch to negative binomial. (Wooldridge 2019)
Reading Poisson coefficients additively. e^{\beta} is a rate multiplier; a coefficient of 0.661 means ×1.93, not “+0.661 counts.” (McNulty 2021)
Comparing counts across unequal exposure. Use an offset to model rates when time/space windows differ. (Wooldridge 2019)
Forcing Poisson on a zero-heavy outcome. Excess zeros from a separate process call for a hurdle / zero-inflated model. (Wooldridge 2019)
Running OLS on counts. It predicts negative counts and mis-models the skew; use the log-link GLM. (McNulty 2021)

title: Count-Data Models: Poisson & Negative Binomial
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — Poisson regression with the log link and the equidispersion (variance = mean) assumption; overdispersion from clumping/“contagion” and the negative binomial fix (an extra dispersion parameter); exposure/offset for rates; zero-inflated / hurdle models for excess zeros.
McNulty, Explanatory Statistical Modeling in R — counts that “pile up at zero”; \exp(X\beta) keeping predictions positive; the multiplicative reading of coefficients (e^{\beta} as a rate multiplier; the 0.661\Rightarrow 1.93, “93% higher” example).

title: Count-Data Models: Poisson & Negative Binomial
text:
Connections

Is a: generalized linear model (log link, Poisson/NB random component), fit by maximum likelihood.
Sibling of: logit/probit (binary) and survival models (the rate/hazard view) — all multiplicative-coefficient GLM-family models.
Needs: diagnostics thinking — overdispersion is a variance-assumption failure, like heteroskedasticity for OLS.

title: Count-Data Models: Poisson & Negative Binomial
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Why is OLS wrong for count outcomes, and how does the Poisson log link keep predictions valid?”
“How do you interpret a Poisson coefficient — what is exp(β), and what does 0.661 mean?”
“What is the equidispersion assumption, what is overdispersion (and why does clumping cause it), and how does the negative binomial model fix it?”
“When do you need an offset/exposure term, and when do you reach for a zero-inflated or hurdle model?”

title: Statistical Power & Sample Size
text: Hypothesis testing guards against crying wolf. Power guards against the opposite failure — running a study so small it couldn’t have spotted the wolf if one were standing in the room. A test with low power is a coin flip dressed in the costume of science, and most of the replication crisis traces back to ignoring it.

title: Statistical Power & Sample Size
text:
Why it matters
A test can fail in two directions. It can raise a false alarm — a Type I error, the thing \alpha controls. Or it can miss a real signal — a Type II error. Significance testing obsesses over the first and routinely ignores the second, and that lopsided attention is enormously costly. (Reinhart 2015) A study with too few subjects is, in Reinhart’s phrase, dead on arrival: it cannot detect the very effect it set out to find, so it wastes the money, the participants, and the years — and, worse, pollutes the literature with misleading results. (Reinhart 2015)
Power analysis is the cure, and it belongs before data collection, not after. It is the single highest-leverage thing a researcher does, because no amount of sophisticated analysis can recover a signal a study was never built to see.

title: Statistical Power & Sample Size
text:
Power, defined
Recall the two error rates from the testing entry: \alpha is the false-positive rate, and \beta is the false-negative rate — the chance of missing a real effect. Statistical power is the complement of \beta: (Reinhart 2015)

\text{Power} = 1 - \beta = \Pr(\text{reject } H_0 \mid H_0 \text{ is false}).

In plain words: if the effect is genuinely there, what’s the probability your study actually catches it (gets p<0.05)? A study with 80% power — the conventional minimum — has an 80% chance of detecting a true effect and a 20% chance of missing it to sampling noise. (Reinhart 2015; Martin and Bridgmon 2012)

title: Statistical Power & Sample Size
text:
The four interlocking quantities
Power never stands alone. It is one corner of a four-way balancing act, and the four are bound together so tightly that fixing any three pins down the fourth. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)








Quantity
What it is
Typical value




\alpha
False-positive rate (significance threshold)
0.05


Power (1-\beta)
Chance of detecting a real effect
0.80


Effect size
How big the real-world effect is
the unknown you posit


Sample size n
How many observations
what you solve for



This is the golden rule of design: pre-commit to \alpha = 0.05 and 80% power, posit a target effect size, and the mathematics dictates exactly how many subjects you must recruit. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012) Turn it around and it diagnoses: with \alpha, n, and effect size fixed, you can compute the power a planned study will have — and discover, before spending a cent, whether it’s worth running.

Power is the overlap of two distributions
Here is the whole idea in one picture. There are two sampling distributions in play: the one under the null (no effect, centred at 0) and the one under the truth (a real effect, centred to the right). You reject when your statistic lands past the critical value. Power is the share of the true distribution that falls in the rejection region — and it grows as the true distribution slides rightward, which is exactly what a bigger effect or a larger n does.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from statistics import NormalDist

zc = 1.645                       # one-sided 5% critical value
x = np.linspace(-4, 7, 700)
null_pdf = np.array([NormalDist(0, 1).pdf(v) for v in x])

def draw(ax, alt_mean, label):
    alt_pdf = np.array([NormalDist(alt_mean, 1).pdf(v) for v in x])
    power = 1 - NormalDist(alt_mean, 1).cdf(zc)
    ax.plot(x, null_pdf, color="#9aa0a6", lw=1.6)
    ax.plot(x, alt_pdf,  color="#1f3a5f", lw=1.8)
    ax.fill_between(x, alt_pdf, where=(x >= zc), color="#1f8a4c", alpha=0.55)
    ax.axvline(zc, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.2)
    ax.set_title(f"{label}\npower = {power:.0%}", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_yticks([])
    ax.set_xlabel("Test statistic")

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 3.4), sharey=True)
draw(ax1, 1.5, "Small effect / small n")
draw(ax2, 3.0, "Bigger effect / larger n")
ax1.text(zc + 0.1, 0.38, "reject →", color="#c0392b", fontsize=8)
fig.suptitle("Power is the slice of the truth that clears the critical value", fontsize=12)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Power as overlap. The grey bell is the null (no effect); the blue bell is the truth (a real effect). You reject right of the dashed critical value. Power is the blue area in that region. Left panel: a small effect / small n — only 44% power, a coin flip. Right panel: a larger effect / bigger n slides the truth right, lifting power to 91%.





The grey curve never moves — the null is fixed. What changes is where the truth sits. When the real effect is small relative to noise (left), most of the true distribution piles up below the threshold, so the study misses more than half the time. Push the truth rightward with a stronger effect or more data (right), and almost all of it clears the bar. That is what “buying power” literally buys.

title: Statistical Power & Sample Size
section: Effect size: you must guess the target
text:
Effect size: you must guess the target
To run the power calculation you need a target effect size — a posited magnitude for the real effect, measured independently of sample size. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012) The standard currencies are Cohen’s d (the difference in means divided by the pooled standard deviation) and R^2 / \eta^2 (the proportion of variance explained). (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)
The logic is intuitive and unforgiving: big effects are cheap to detect, subtle ones ruinously expensive. A 50% jump in sales screams through a handful of observations; a 1% shift in an election outcome hides behind a wall of noise that only an enormous sample can see past. (Reinhart 2015) The smaller the needle you hope to detect moving, the more data you must buy.

title: Statistical Power & Sample Size
text:
A priori power analysis
An a priori power analysis runs the calculation before the study, to decide whether it is even worth doing. (Reinhart 2015) You estimate the expected effect size from prior literature or a pilot, fix \alpha and your target power, and solve for n — typically with a tool like G*Power. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)






TipThe heart-attack trial



Suppose a treatment cuts the risk of death from a heart attack in half — from 20% to 10%, a genuinely large clinical effect. How many patients do you need for 80% power? Intuition says maybe 50. The a priori analysis says 400 — two hundred per arm. (Reinhart 2015) Even a dramatic, life-saving effect demands far more data than instinct suggests. This is why so many trials are quietly underpowered: researchers budget by gut, and gut is wildly optimistic.

title: Statistical Power & Sample Size
text:
The perils of underpowered research
Underpowered studies don’t just fail individually — they corrode the whole literature. (Reinhart 2015)

Wasted resources. A study with 50% power is a coin flip: even when the theory is correct, it finds the effect only half the time, after burning the full cost. (Reinhart 2015)
A field-wide problem. This is not hypothetical. Back in 1960, Jacob Cohen found the average social-psychology study had just 48% power to detect a medium effect — worse than a coin flip. Decades on, power in some fields had barely improved or even fallen, helping produce the wave of failed replications we now call a crisis. (Reinhart 2015)

title: Statistical Power & Sample Size
text:
Truth inflation: the winner’s curse
Here is the most insidious consequence, and the one researchers least expect. In an underpowered study, the average outcome doesn’t reach significance — so the only results that cross p<0.05 are the lucky, extreme ones that happened to overshoot. (Reinhart 2015) And those are exactly the ones that get published. The result is truth inflation (a Type M, or magnitude, error): the effect sizes in the literature are systematically exaggerated versions of reality. (Reinhart 2015)
The exaggeration can be staggering. In a small-sample study of gender ratios (Kanazawa), the samples were so thin that any result reaching significance had to overstate the true effect by at least a factor of 20 to clear the bar. (Reinhart 2015) The “discovery” wasn’t just lucky — it was guaranteed to be enormous-or-invisible, with nothing true in between. Low power doesn’t merely hide real effects; it manufactures overstated ones.

title: Statistical Power & Sample Size
text:
“Not significant” is not “no effect”
The mirror-image error closes the loop with the testing entry. A non-significant result (p>0.05) is constantly misread as proof that an effect is absent. It is nothing of the kind. (Reinhart 2015) “Not significant” means only that the data were too uninformative to rule out chance — the study could not distinguish a real effect from noise. (Albers 2017) Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence. Countless clinical trials announce “no difference in side effects” while having had the power to detect only the most catastrophic reactions. (Reinhart 2015)
The diagnostic tool is the confidence interval: a narrow interval hugging zero genuinely suggests the effect is small; a wide interval spanning zero says the study was simply underpowered and inconclusive. Same p-value, opposite meanings — which is why you report the interval. (Reinhart 2015)

title: Statistical Power & Sample Size
text:
Designing an adequately powered study
The practical levers, in order of bluntness: (Reinhart 2015; Gerber and Green 2012)

Aim for ≥ 80% power as the floor for adequacy. (Reinhart 2015)
Increase n. The most direct lever — more observations shrink the standard error and slide the truth-distribution rightward. (Subject, always, to the \sqrt n tax.)
Strengthen the treatment. If subjects are expensive, a more intense intervention raises the effect size itself — the other way to separate the two bells. (Gerber and Green 2012)
Reduce noise. Prognostic covariates, blocking, and matching strip out background variation, making a given effect easier to see — power without extra subjects. (Gerber and Green 2012)
Report confidence intervals, always, so readers can tell “precisely zero” from “we couldn’t tell.” (Reinhart 2015)

title: Statistical Power & Sample Size
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong in practice




Skipping power analysis entirely, then being surprised by a null result. Without it you can’t distinguish “no effect” from “no chance of seeing it.” Run it before collecting data. (Reinhart 2015)
Budgeting sample size by intuition. Gut feeling drastically underestimates the n needed — the heart-attack trial needs 400, not 50. Use a real calculation. (Reinhart 2015)
Trusting effect sizes from small significant studies. Truth inflation guarantees they’re exaggerated; the smaller and noisier the study, the bigger the overstatement. (Reinhart 2015)
Post-hoc “observed power.” Computing power from your observed effect after a null result is circular and uninformative — it just re-encodes the p-value. Power analysis is a design tool.
Reading non-significance as proof of no effect. Check whether the confidence interval is narrow around zero (real null) or wide (underpowered). (Reinhart 2015; Albers 2017)

title: Statistical Power & Sample Size
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — power as 1-\beta and the “dead on arrival” framing; the heart-attack 20%→10% / 400-patient example; Cohen’s 1960 finding of 48% average power and its decline; truth inflation / Type M error and the Kanazawa factor-of-20; non-significance ≠ no effect; the narrow-vs-wide-CI diagnostic; the 80% convention.
Martin & Bridgmon, Quantitative & Statistical Research Methods — the four interlocking quantities and the “fix three, get the fourth” rule; effect-size metrics (Cohen’s d, \eta^2); a priori power analysis with G*Power.
Gerber & Green, Field Experiments — design levers for power: larger samples, stronger treatments, and noise reduction via covariates, blocking, and matching.
Albers, Quantitative Data Analysis — a study can only claim it failed to find a relationship, not that none exists.

title: Statistical Power & Sample Size
text:
Appendix · the full mathematics

Power as a tail probability. For a one-sided test at level \alpha with critical value z_\alpha, write the true standardized effect as \delta (the alternative mean in standard-error units). The test statistic under the truth is \mathcal N(\delta, 1), so

\text{Power} = \Pr(Z > z_\alpha \mid Z \sim \mathcal N(\delta,1)) = 1 - \Phi(z_\alpha - \delta).

Power rises with \delta, and \delta itself grows with both the raw effect size and \sqrt n — which is why both levers work, and why power climbs only as fast as \sqrt n.
Solving for sample size. Inverting the two-error condition for a two-sample mean comparison with effect size d (Cohen’s d) gives the familiar per-group formula

n \;\approx\; \frac{2\,(z_{\alpha/2} + z_{\beta})^2}{d^{2}} .

For \alpha = 0.05 (so z_{\alpha/2}=1.96) and 80% power (z_\beta = 0.84), (1.96+0.84)^2 \approx 7.85, so n \approx 15.7/d^2 per group. A “small” effect d=0.2 needs \approx 393 per group; a “large” d=0.8 needs only \approx 25 — the four-fold-effect, sixteen-fold-sample relationship in one line.
Why truth inflation is forced. Conditioning on significance, the published estimate is drawn from the truncated upper tail of the sampling distribution. When power is low the unconditional mean lies below the threshold, so the conditional mean \mathbb{E}[\hat\theta \mid |\hat\theta| >
c] can sit far above the true \theta — the exaggeration (Type M) factor. The lower the power, the heavier the truncation, the larger the inflation.

title: Statistical Power & Sample Size
text:
Connections

Builds on: hypothesis testing (power is the \beta / Type II side of the error table), confidence intervals (the width diagnostic for null results), and sampling distributions & the CLT (the two overlapping distributions).
Leads to: the replication crisis (underpowered research and truth inflation are two of its deepest causes) and to experimental design (power is the first question a field experiment must answer).
Reappears in: minimum detectable effects in causal design, and the sample-size planning behind every credible study.

title: Statistical Power & Sample Size
text:
Study with NotebookLM
Take these prompts to the source library to push past the synthesis above:

“Explain statistical power and the four interlocking quantities exactly as the sources present them, with a worked sample-size calculation.”
“Walk me through Reinhart’s account of truth inflation / the winner’s curse, including the gender-ratio example and the exaggeration factor.”
“What did Jacob Cohen find about the power of typical studies, and what has happened to power in the social sciences since?”
“Give me the heart-attack trial example in full and explain why intuition about sample size is so badly wrong.”
“How do Gerber & Green suggest increasing power without increasing sample size, and how much can blocking or covariates help?”

title: Sampling & Survey Design
text: Every claim about a population rests on a sample, and every sample rests on how it was drawn. Get the drawing right — give every unit a known, non-zero chance of selection — and a few hundred people can speak for millions. Get it wrong, and ten million ballots can point to the loser.

title: Sampling & Survey Design
text:
Why it matters
The Central Limit Theorem told you that the average of a sample behaves predictably — if the sample is a fair draw from the population. That “if” is the whole of survey design. Inference is a machine for turning a sample into a claim about a population, and like any machine it obeys garbage-in, garbage-out: no amount of clever statistics downstream can repair a sample that was biased the moment it was selected.
This is the least mathematical chapter in Part II and the one practitioners most often skip — to their cost. The famous disasters of empirical social science are almost never failures of arithmetic. They are failures of sampling: the right calculation performed on the wrong people. So before estimators, confidence intervals, and tests, we ask the prior question: who is in your data, and how did they get there?

title: Sampling & Survey Design
section: The core trio: population, sample, frame
text:
The core trio: population, sample, frame
Three ideas, constantly confused, must be kept apart. (Bryman 2016)

The population is the universe of units you want to draw conclusions about. “Units” need not be people — they can be nations, cities, firms, hospital records, even suicide notes. The population is defined by your research question, not by convenience.
The sample is the subset you actually study.
The sampling frame is the concrete list of population units from which you draw — the registrar’s roster, the phone book, the voter file. The frame is the population as the world actually hands it to you, and the gap between frame and population is where much trouble lives.







NoteA worked picture



Your population is all 10,000 students at a university. Your sampling frame is the registrar’s digital list of enrolled students. You randomly select 450 names from that list — those 450 people are your sample. If the registrar’s list silently omits part-time students, your frame no longer matches your population, and every downstream number inherits that flaw. (Bryman 2016)

title: Sampling & Survey Design
section: Probability sampling: the only road to inference
text:
Probability sampling: the only road to inference
The dividing line in all of survey design is whether selection is random. In probability sampling, every unit has a known, non-zero probability of being selected. (Bryman 2016) This is not a stylistic preference — it is the precondition that makes the rest of the book legal. Only when selection probabilities are known can you compute a standard error and attach a margin of error to your estimate. (Bruce et al. 2020) Randomness is what lets a sample be, in Bryman’s phrase, a “population in miniature.”
There are four workhorses, in rising order of sophistication.
Simple random sampling (SRS). Every unit has an equal chance; assign the frame numbers 1 to N and draw n of them with a random number generator. It is the conceptual baseline — mechanical, unbiased, assumption-free. Its cost is practical: you need a complete, enumerated list of the population, which for dispersed or hidden populations may not exist. (Bryman 2016)
Systematic sampling. Order the frame and take every k-th unit after a random start. For a frame of N = 9{,}000 and a desired n = 450, the interval is k = 20; pick a random start between 1 and 20 — say 16 — then take units 16, 36, 56, \dots It is easier to execute than SRS and needs no random draw per unit. The one hazard: if the list has a hidden periodicity that lines up with your interval (every 20th apartment is a corner unit; the roster alternates male/female), the regularity quietly biases the sample. (Bryman 2016)
Stratified random sampling. Divide the population into strata — subgroups defined by some characteristic (faculty, gender, region) — and sample randomly within each. With proportional allocation you draw from each stratum in proportion to its size: if Humanities is 20% of a university and you want n = 450, you draw exactly 90 Humanities students. (Bryman 2016) The payoff is real and worth understanding: stratifying guarantees the subgroups are represented in the right proportions instead of leaving it to luck, and in doing so it removes the between-stratum variation from your estimate’s error. A stratified estimate is never less precise than SRS, and is often dramatically more precise — we will see exactly how much below. The price is information: you must know each unit’s stratum before you sample. (Bryman 2016)
Cluster and multistage sampling. Sample whole clusters — naturally occurring groupings — rather than individuals. For a national student survey you might randomly select 10 universities (clusters), then randomly sample 500 students within each. (Bryman 2016) Clustering exists for one reason: cost. Interviewing 5,000 students at 10 sites is enormously cheaper than chasing 5,000 students scattered across 200 campuses. But there is no free lunch: units within a cluster tend to resemble each other (students at one university share a catchment, a culture, a curriculum), so each new unit carries less fresh information than an independent draw would. The result is larger standard errors for a given n — the so-called design effect. (Bruce et al. 2020)









Method
Core idea
Buys you
Costs you




Simple random
Equal chance for all
Cleanest baseline; no assumptions
A complete enumerated frame


Systematic
Every k-th after random start
Easy execution
Fails on hidden periodicity


Stratified
Random within subgroups
Guaranteed representation; lower variance
Must know strata in advance


Cluster / multistage
Sample groups, then units
Big cost savings on dispersed populations
Higher variance (design effect)




See the gain from stratifying
The claim that stratification reduces the variance of your estimate is not hand-waving — it is visible. Below, the same population is sampled two ways for the same total n = 100. The population has three strata with quite different means (think income across three regions). Simple random sampling lets the number you happen to draw from each region vary by luck, and that luck sloshes around in your estimate. Proportional stratification nails the regional mix down, so only the small within-region noise remains.


Show the simulation code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)

# Three strata: weights, means, and a common within-stratum SD.
weights = np.array([0.5, 0.3, 0.2])
mus     = np.array([40.0, 60.0, 80.0])
sd      = 5.0
true_mean = float(weights @ mus)          # 0.5*40 + 0.3*60 + 0.2*80 = 54

n_total = 100
n_reps  = 30_000

# --- Simple random sampling: stratum membership is itself random ---
choice = rng.choice(3, size=(n_reps, n_total), p=weights)
srs_vals = rng.normal(mus[choice], sd)
srs_means = srs_vals.mean(axis=1)

# --- Stratified proportional allocation: fixed 50 / 30 / 20 ---
a = rng.normal(mus[0], sd, size=(n_reps, 50))
b = rng.normal(mus[1], sd, size=(n_reps, 30))
c = rng.normal(mus[2], sd, size=(n_reps, 20))
strat_means = (a.sum(1) + b.sum(1) + c.sum(1)) / n_total

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.6))
bins = np.linspace(48, 60, 70)
ax.hist(srs_means,   bins=bins, density=True, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.75,
        label=f"Simple random  (SE ≈ {srs_means.std():.2f})")
ax.hist(strat_means, bins=bins, density=True, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.85,
        label=f"Stratified      (SE ≈ {strat_means.std():.2f})")
ax.axvline(true_mean, color="#b5446e", ls="--", lw=1.8, label="True mean = 54")
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_xlabel("Estimated population mean")
ax.set_title("Same n, same population — stratifying sharpens the estimate")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Two ways to estimate one population mean (true value 54, dashed line) with n = 100. Simple random sampling (grey) scatters widely because the stratum mix varies by chance; proportional stratified sampling (blue) is over three times tighter because it removes the between-stratum variation.





Both estimators are unbiased — both clouds centre on the truth, 54. But the stratified estimator is roughly three times tighter. That difference is free: it costs nothing but knowing each unit’s region before you draw. This is the single most useful design lesson in sampling — when you have strata that differ, use them.

title: Sampling & Survey Design
text:
Non-probability sampling: convenient, and uninferable
The opposite of probability sampling is selection by judgement or availability, where some units have no chance of selection and the probabilities are unknown. The common forms: (Bryman 2016)

Convenience sampling — whoever is easiest to reach (the undergraduates in your 9 a.m. lecture, the shoppers who walk past).
Snowball sampling — start with a few relevant people and ask them to nominate others; useful for hidden populations (undocumented workers, niche subcultures) where no frame exists.
Quota sampling — fill pre-set category targets non-randomly (e.g., “5 Asian women aged 25–34”) until each quota is met.

These designs have legitimate uses — exploratory work, qualitative depth, hard-to-frame populations. But they share a disqualifying flaw for inference: because the probability of selection is unknown, you cannot compute a standard error, and without a standard error you cannot say how far your sample statistic might be from the population truth. (Bruce et al. 2020) A convenience sample can describe itself perfectly and tell you nothing certain about anyone else. Generalisation requires randomness; judgement cannot substitute for it.

title: Sampling & Survey Design
section: Representativeness, and the two kinds of error
text:
Representativeness, and the two kinds of error
A sample is representative when it mirrors the population — the “population in miniature.” The engine of representativeness is random selection, which has no opinion about whom it picks and so introduces no systematic tilt. (Bryman 2016) When a sample is unrepresentative, the findings simply do not generalise beyond the people in it.
It helps enormously to split a survey’s total error into two species, because they have different cures. (Bruce et al. 2020)

Sampling error is the unavoidable discrepancy between a sample and its population that arises purely because you looked at a subset. It is the bounce the CLT describes; it exists even in a flawless random sample, and it shrinks predictably as n grows. This is the “honest” error — quantifiable, and the thing confidence intervals measure.
Non-sampling error is everything else: bad question wording, interviewer effects, data-entry mistakes, coverage gaps, non-response. It does not shrink with n — a bigger biased sample is just a more confidently wrong one — and it is invisible to your standard error. This is the dangerous error, because the statistics give you no warning of it.

The whole moral of this chapter lives in that contrast: statistics manages sampling error; only design manages non-sampling error.

The three biases that ruin samples
Non-sampling error usually arrives as one of three biases — systematic, directional errors baked in by the selection or measurement process. (Bruce et al. 2020)

Selection bias — the sample is selected in a way related to the thing you are measuring. In Gerber & Green’s framing, the danger of non-random selection is that receiving the treatment is correlated with the outcome: healthier people self-select into exercise programs, so a naïve comparison credits the program with their pre-existing health. (Gerber and Green 2012)
Coverage / frame bias — the frame fails to list part of the target population, so those people had zero chance from the start (the part-time students missing from the registrar’s list). (Bruce et al. 2020)
Non-response bias — selected units who decline or can’t be reached differ systematically from those who respond. If wealthier households are likelier to refuse a survey about income, the recorded average is biased downward — and collecting more responses from the same cooperative group does not fix it. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: Sampling & Survey Design
section: Sample size: why a thousand can speak for millions
text:
Sample size: why a thousand can speak for millions
Here is the result that surprises every newcomer and underlies the entire polling industry: the precision of an estimate depends on the absolute sample size n, not on the population size N. (Bruce et al. 2020) A well-drawn random sample of about 1,000 people pins down a national proportion to roughly a \pm 3\% margin — and that margin is the same whether the country has 100,000 people or 100 million.
The intuition: each random draw is an independent measurement of the population, and the information you accumulate is governed by how many measurements you take, not by how large the thing you’re measuring is. (The population size matters only when your sample is a sizeable fraction of it — the finite-population correction in the appendix — which for national surveys it essentially never is.) The same draw also reveals the law of diminishing returns: because sampling error falls like 1/\sqrt{n}, each additional thousand respondents buys less precision than the last. Doubling a poll from 1,000 to 2,000 does not halve the margin of error; it shrinks it by only about 30%. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: Sampling & Survey Design
section: A cautionary tale: the Literary Digest, 1936
text:
A cautionary tale: the Literary Digest, 1936
No story makes the point more bluntly. In 1936 the magazine Literary Digest mailed straw-poll ballots to ten million people, drawn from telephone directories and automobile registration lists, and got over two million back. On that mountain of data it confidently predicted a landslide for Alf Landon. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Franklin Roosevelt won 46 states.
The frames — phone and car owners in 1936 — skewed sharply toward the affluent, who favoured Landon; that was coverage bias. And the people motivated to mail back an unsolicited ballot differed from those who didn’t; that was non-response bias. Ten million biased responses are still biased. Meanwhile George Gallup, using a scientifically representative sample of around 2,000, called the election correctly. (Bruce et al. 2020)
The lesson is the founding creed of survey design, and it directly contradicts the instinct of the big-data age: how you sample beats how much you sample. A small representative sample crushes a huge biased one.

title: Sampling & Survey Design
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong in practice




Believing a bigger sample fixes bias. Size only shrinks sampling error. Bias — selection, coverage, non-response — is untouched by n and is the actual cause of the famous failures. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Confusing the frame with the population. Your inference is only ever valid for the frame you drew from. If the frame omits a group, your conclusions silently omit them too — and no statistic will flag it. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Reporting a margin of error for a non-probability sample. Convenience, quota, and snowball samples have no defined selection probabilities, so a “±3%” attached to them is fiction. The margin of error is a property of random sampling. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Ignoring the design effect in clustered data. Treating a cluster sample as if it were a simple random sample understates the standard errors and overstates your certainty. Clustering buys cost savings and costs precision; both must be booked. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Forgetting that non-response is informative. A 70% response rate is not “70% of a good sample” — it is a complete sample only if the 30% who declined are like the 70% who answered, and they usually aren’t. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: Sampling & Survey Design
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bryman, Social Research Methods — the population / sample / frame trio; the four probability designs (simple random, systematic, stratified, cluster/multistage) and their trade-offs; non-probability sampling (convenience, snowball, quota); representativeness as the “population in miniature.”
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — random sampling and selection bias; sampling vs non-sampling error; coverage and non-response bias; precision depends on n not N; the Literary Digest vs Gallup story; the data-quality-beats-data-quantity creed.
Gerber & Green, Field Experiments — selection bias as the correlation between treatment receipt and potential outcomes; why randomisation is what licenses causal generalisation.
Martin & Bridgmon, Quantitative & Statistical Research Methods — the hypothesis-to-results framing that makes sampling the first link in the inferential chain.

title: Sampling & Survey Design
text:
Appendix · the full mathematics

Standard error of a proportion. For a simple random sample of size n estimating a population proportion p with \hat p, the sampling distribution has

\operatorname{SE}(\hat p) = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}.

This is maximised at p = 0.5, where \sqrt{p(1-p)} = 0.5. So for a “50/50” question the margin of error at 95% confidence is about 1.96 \times 0.5/\sqrt{n} \approx 0.98/\sqrt{n}. Setting n = 1{,}000 gives \approx 0.031 — the famous \pm 3\%. Nothing in this expression involves the population size N.
Finite-population correction (FPC). When the sample is a non-negligible fraction of the population, the SE is multiplied by

\sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}}.

For n/N small (a national poll: 1{,}000 from 10^8) this factor is essentially 1, which is why population size drops out. It only bites when you sample a large share of a small population (e.g., 80 of 100 employees), where it correctly shrinks the SE toward zero — a census has no sampling error at all.
Why stratification helps. Decompose the population variance into within-stratum and between-stratum parts, \sigma^2 = \sigma^2_{\text{within}} + \sigma^2_{\text{between}}. A simple random sample’s estimate carries both. A stratified sample with proportional allocation fixes the stratum mix, eliminating the between-stratum component, so its estimator variance is

\operatorname{Var}(\bar x_{\text{strat}}) = \sum_h W_h^2 \,\frac{\sigma_h^2}{n_h},

which depends only on the within-stratum variances \sigma_h^2. In the figure above this gives 0.5^2\frac{25}{50} + 0.3^2\frac{25}{30} + 0.2^2\frac{25}{20} = 0.25, an SE of 0.5, versus the SRS SE of \sqrt{269/100} \approx 1.64 — the threefold gain you saw, because the strata means (40, 60, 80) differ a lot while the within-stratum SD (5) is small.
Design effect (clustering). The cost of cluster sampling is summarised by the design effect \text{DEFF} = 1 + (m-1)\rho, where m is the cluster size and \rho the intraclass correlation (how alike units within a cluster are). The effective sample size is n / \text{DEFF}: with \rho = 0.05 and clusters of m = 100, \text{DEFF} \approx 6, so 5,000 clustered interviews carry the precision of only about 830 independent ones — the price of cheap fieldwork.

title: Sampling & Survey Design
text:
Connections

Builds on: the Law of Large Numbers (why a larger sample homes in on the truth) and sampling distributions & the CLT (the shape of the bounce that sampling error refers to). This entry supplies the design that those theorems assume.
Leads to: estimation and confidence intervals, which quantify the sampling error a good design leaves behind — and are meaningless without one.
Reappears in: the replication crisis (selection and survivorship effects in which studies get published), and the gold standard of randomised experiments, where random assignment plays the role that random sampling plays here.

title: Sampling & Survey Design
text:

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text: The replication crisis is not a story about fraud. It is what happens when honest researchers, pushed by real incentives, use the tools of the previous six entries slightly wrong — flexibly, selectively, repeatedly — and a publication system rewards exactly that. The mathematics of why the literature fills with false positives is the natural capstone of this whole sub-chapter.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Why it matters
Across psychology, medicine, and economics, findings that everyone “knew” to be true have collapsed when independent labs tried to reproduce them. (Reinhart 2015) The unsettling part is the diagnosis: this is driven less by fraud than by the systemic misuse of statistical tools and the misaligned incentives of publishing. (Reinhart 2015) Every mechanism below is something a well-meaning researcher can do without ever feeling dishonest — which is precisely what makes it dangerous, and what makes understanding it a core competence rather than an ethics footnote.
This entry gathers the threads of Part II — p-values, power, confidence intervals, and the base-rate logic of Bayes — into one rigorous account of how science goes wrong at scale, and how it is being fixed.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
section: Researcher degrees of freedom: the garden of forking paths
text:
Researcher degrees of freedom: the garden of forking paths
Hand the same dataset to the same question, and a researcher still faces a thicket of small, individually defensible choices: which outliers to drop, which covariates to control for, how to code a borderline response, where to bin a continuous variable. Gelman and Loken named the result the garden of forking paths. (Reinhart 2015) Each fork looks reasonable in isolation; taken together they grant enormous researcher degrees of freedom — enough latitude to steer an analysis, even unconsciously, toward whatever comes out significant. Simulations show that flexible outlier handling and covariate selection alone can swing an effect size by a factor of two — routinely enough to drag a null result across the p<0.05 line. (Reinhart 2015)
The deep point: you do not need to cheat to exploit the forking paths. You only need to make each choice after glancing at the data and pick, at each fork, the path that looks a little better.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
section: P-hacking: torture the data until it confesses
text:
P-hacking: torture the data until it confesses
When that flexibility is turned, deliberately or not, toward chasing significance, it has a name: p-hacking (or data dredging) — running analysis after analysis until a p-value drops below 0.05, then reporting only the winners. (Bruce et al. 2020) The folk wisdom is exact: “torture the data long enough, and it will confess.” (Reinhart 2015)






NoteProving the Beatles make you younger



In a deliberately outlandish demonstration, researchers “showed” that listening to the Beatles’ When I’m Sixty-Four made participants literally younger in chronological age. They got there purely through flexible analysis — trying combinations of covariates and outcome variables until a significant result surfaced. The absurd conclusion was the point: the same freedom can manufacture a plausible-sounding effect just as easily, and you’d never know. (Reinhart 2015)

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Optional stopping: the peeking problem
A special, seductive form of p-hacking concerns when you stop collecting data. Optional stopping means watching results arrive and halting the moment significance appears. (Reinhart 2015) It feels rational — why gather more once you’ve “found it”? — but it quietly wrecks the error rate. The \alpha=0.05 guarantee assumes a single test at a fixed sample size; peek after every participant and you take many bites at the apple, so the chance of eventually crossing p<0.05 by pure luck climbs far above 5%. (Reinhart 2015) Combine flexible analyses and flexible stopping and simulations put the false-positive rate above 50% for a single dataset — worse than a coin flip. (Reinhart 2015)

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Multiple comparisons: the jelly-bean problem
The cleanest way to see false positives accumulate is to test many things at once. Each test at \alpha=0.05 has a 1-in-20 chance of a false alarm, so testing twenty near-guarantees one.






TipThe green jelly beans



Reinhart’s parable: test whether jelly beans cause acne and find nothing. So you test each colour — 20 of them. Nineteen show nothing; the green one hits p<0.05. Headline: “Green Jelly Beans Linked to Acne!” But you ran 20 tests; finding one false positive at the 5% level is exactly what chance predicts. (Reinhart 2015)


The arithmetic is brutal. For n independent tests, the probability of at least one false positive is

P(\text{at least one false positive}) = 1 - (1 - 0.05)^{n},

which reaches 99% by n = 100. (Reinhart 2015) And n runs into the thousands more often than you’d think — most memorably when researchers slid a dead Atlantic salmon into an fMRI scanner, showed it photographs, and ran thousands of uncorrected per-voxel tests. They found “significant” brain activity. In a dead fish. (Reinhart 2015)
The fixes adjust for the number of tests:

Bonferroni correction — divide \alpha by the number of tests (0.05/n). Simple and strict; it controls the chance of any false positive but costs power. (Reinhart 2015)
False discovery rate (Benjamini–Hochberg) — controls the proportion of significant results that are false alarms rather than the chance of any, retaining far more power when you expect some real effects among many tests. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Publication bias: the file drawer
Even flawless individual studies produce a broken literature if which studies get published depends on their results. Publication bias is journals’ preference for “exciting,” significant findings; the null results get filed away. (Reinhart 2015) So the published record is a biased sample of all studies run — selection bias, returned from the sampling entry to haunt science itself. If 20 labs run the same true-null study, the one that gets lucky at p<0.05 is the one the world sees; the 19 nulls vanish.






NoteWhen the file drawer was opened



When the suppressed trial data for the antidepressant reboxetine was finally unearthed, the “highly significant” benefit from the published studies evaporated: across all trials, the drug was no better than placebo. The published subset had told a confident, and false, story. (Reinhart 2015)

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
section: Why most published findings can be false
text:
Why most published findings can be false
Put p-hacking, low power, and publication bias together and you get John Ioannidis’s famous claim, and it is just the base-rate fallacy wearing a lab coat. The chance that a significant finding is true — its positive predictive value (PPV) — depends on three things: the prior probability the hypothesis is true, the study’s power, and \alpha. (Reinhart 2015)






ImportantThe base-rate fallacy, in science



Suppose a field tests 100 hypotheses of which only 10 are true (a 10% base rate). With 80% power and \alpha=0.05:

True positives: 0.80 \times 10 = 8.
False positives: 0.05 \times 90 \approx 5.

So of the 13 “significant” findings, 5 — nearly 40% — are flukes. (Reinhart 2015) And that’s the optimistic case: drop power, add p-hacking, and the false share climbs past half.


This is the exact structure of the rare-disease Bayes calculation: a test can be individually reliable yet mostly wrong when the thing it screens for is rare. Significant findings are no different.

Watch the predictive value collapse
The PPV is governed by one formula, and plotting it is sobering. As the prior probability of true hypotheses falls — as a field reaches for more surprising, less likely effects — the share of “discoveries” that are real drops, fast, and low power drops it faster.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

alpha = 0.05
prior = np.linspace(0.005, 0.6, 400)

def ppv(power):
    tp = power * prior
    fp = alpha * (1 - prior)
    return tp / (tp + fp)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.8))
ax.plot(prior, ppv(0.80), color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.0, label="80% power")
ax.plot(prior, ppv(0.35), color="#d98a29", lw=2.0, label="35% power (underpowered field)")
ax.axhline(0.5, color="#9aa0a6", ls=":", lw=1.2)
ax.text(0.45, 0.52, "coin flip", color="#9aa0a6", fontsize=8)

# Ioannidis's 10% base-rate example at 80% power
p0 = 0.80 * 0.10 / (0.80 * 0.10 + alpha * 0.90)
ax.scatter([0.10], [p0], color="#c0392b", zorder=5)
ax.annotate(f"10% base rate → PPV ≈ {p0:.2f}", xy=(0.10, p0), xytext=(0.16, 0.45),
            fontsize=9, color="#c0392b",
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color="#c0392b"))

ax.set_xlabel("Base rate: fraction of tested hypotheses that are true")
ax.set_ylabel("P(finding is true | significant)")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
ax.set_title("Most 'discoveries' are false when the truth is rare")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="lower right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The probability a significant finding is actually true (PPV), as a function of how often the tested hypotheses are true (the base rate), at α = 0.05. Even at 80% power, a field chasing unlikely hypotheses (low base rate) sees most of its ‘discoveries’ turn out false. Low power (35%) pushes the crossover further right. The dot marks Ioannidis’s 10%-base-rate example: PPV ≈ 0.64.





The lesson is structural, not moral: a field that prizes surprising results (low base rate) and tolerates underpowered studies (low power) will publish mostly false positives even if every individual researcher is honest. Reform has to change the system, not just scold the scientists.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
HARKing: the story told backwards
One more degree of freedom deserves its own name. HARKing — Hypothesizing After the Results are Known — is presenting a hypothesis cooked up after seeing the data as though it had been predicted in advance. (Reinhart 2015) It weaponises a deep human reflex: our talent for spinning a plausible story around any pattern that catches the eye. The fingerprint is exploratory research dressed as confirmatory — and it dissolves the entire logic of the p-value, which only means anything when the hypothesis is fixed before the data.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
The fixes: a new rigour
The encouraging half of the story is that the social sciences are responding, and the reforms map directly onto the diseases above: (Reinhart 2015; Bruce et al. 2020)

Pre-registration and registered reports — commit to the hypothesis and the full analysis plan before seeing the data; with registered reports the journal accepts the study on its design, before the results exist. This closes the garden of forking paths, p-hacking, optional stopping, and HARKing in one stroke, and defangs publication bias by deciding to publish before the result is known. (Reinhart 2015)
Replication — independent teams rerun studies; a finding earns trust by holding up, not by appearing once. (Reinhart 2015)
The ASA statement on p-values — the American Statistical Association’s six principles, formally warning that a p-value is not the probability a hypothesis is true and not a measure of an effect’s importance — the misreadings from the testing entry, now official. (Reinhart 2015)
Estimation over testing — report effect sizes and confidence intervals instead of bare significance verdicts, so readers see how big and how certain, not just whether. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong in practice




Believing only fraudsters p-hack. The forking paths are walked by honest people making defensible choices. Good intentions are no protection; only pre-committed procedures are. (Reinhart 2015)
Running many tests and celebrating the one that’s significant. With 20 tests, one false positive is expected. Correct for multiplicity (Bonferroni or FDR) or pre-specify your primary outcome. (Reinhart 2015)
Trusting a literature of significant results. Publication bias means the published record overstates effects and hides the nulls. A single significant paper is weak evidence; a registered replication is strong. (Reinhart 2015)
Forgetting the base rate. A significant result for a surprising hypothesis is more likely a fluke than a discovery — exactly the Bayesian point. (Reinhart 2015)
Stopping data collection when it “looks significant.” Fix your sample size in advance (via a power analysis) or use a method designed for sequential peeking. (Reinhart 2015)

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — researcher degrees of freedom and the garden of forking paths (Gelman & Loken); p-hacking and the Beatles example; optional stopping and the >50% false-positive simulations; the jelly-bean parable, the 1-(1-0.05)^n formula, and the dead salmon; Bonferroni; publication bias, the file drawer, and reboxetine; the PPV / Ioannidis base-rate argument; HARKing; pre-registration, replication, the ASA statement, and estimation over testing.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — p-hacking / data dredging; the false discovery rate (Benjamini–Hochberg) as a higher-power alternative to Bonferroni; the push toward effect sizes and intervals.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Appendix · the full mathematics

Family-wise error from multiple tests. For m independent true nulls each tested at level \alpha, the probability of no false positive is (1-\alpha)^m, so the family-wise error rate is

\text{FWER} = 1 - (1-\alpha)^m .

At \alpha = 0.05: m=10 \Rightarrow 0.40; m=100 \Rightarrow 0.99. Bonferroni tests each at \alpha/m, since 1-(1-\alpha/m)^m \le \alpha keeps the FWER at or below \alpha.
Positive predictive value. Let \pi be the prior probability a tested hypothesis is true, 1-\beta the power, \alpha the level. Among many tests,

\text{PPV} = \Pr(\text{true}\mid \text{significant})
= \frac{(1-\beta)\,\pi}{(1-\beta)\,\pi + \alpha\,(1-\pi)} .

With \pi=0.10, power 0.80, \alpha=0.05: \text{PPV} = \frac{0.08}{0.08 + 0.045} = 0.64 — so 36% of significant findings are false, matching the “5 of 13” count once the 0.05\times90 = 4.5 false positives are rounded. As \pi\to 0 or power falls, \text{PPV}\to 0. This is identically the rare-disease Bayes formula with power playing the role of sensitivity and \alpha the role of the false-positive rate.
False discovery rate (Benjamini–Hochberg). Order the m p-values p_{(1)}\le\dots\le p_{(m)}; find the largest k with p_{(k)} \le \frac{k}{m}q and reject H_{(1)},\dots,H_{(k)}. This bounds the expected proportion of false discoveries at q — controlling a fraction rather than a probability, which is why it keeps more power than Bonferroni.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Connections

Builds on: hypothesis testing & p-values (every abuse here is a p-value misused), statistical power (underpowered studies feed truth inflation and low PPV), confidence intervals (estimation as the reform), and the base-rate fallacy (the skeleton of the PPV argument).
Leads to: the Bayesian sub-chapter (one response to p-value abuse is to reason about hypotheses’ probabilities directly), and to the tyranny of metrics in Part VI (what happens when “significant result” becomes a target to be gamed).
Closes: the Classical Toolkit. You now have the whole frequentist apparatus and a clear-eyed view of how it fails — the prerequisite for using it responsibly.

title: The Replication Crisis, Made Rigorous
text:
Study with NotebookLM
