title: I · Foundations: Probability & Data
text:
Where this leads
Into Part II · Inference: once you accept that a statistic has a sampling distribution, confidence intervals and hypothesis tests become careful ways of reading that distribution. The bridge article — Sampling Distributions & the CLT — already lives there.

title: Expectation & Variance
text: A distribution can be complicated; two summaries do most of the work. The expectation is its balance point — the long-run average. The variance is its scatter — how far values stray from that balance. Master two algebraic facts about them — expectation is linear (always), variance of a sum adds only when things are independent — and the standard error, the heart of inference, falls out for free.

title: Expectation & Variance
text:
Why it matters
Expectation and variance are the atoms of quantitative reasoning. “The average treatment effect,” “the mean income,” “the expected return” — all expectations. “The standard deviation,” “the margin of error,” “the volatility” — all built from variance. More than that: the single most important formula in applied statistics, the standard error \text{SE}(\bar X) = \sigma/\sqrt{n} that powers the Central Limit Theorem, is just the variance-of-a-sum rule applied to an average. This entry is where that formula is earned.

title: Expectation & Variance
text:
Expectation: the balance point
The expected value E[X] is a weighted average of the values X can take, each weighted by its probability (Ross 2018): 
E[X] = \sum_x x\,p(x) \quad\text{(discrete)}, \qquad E[X] = \int x\,f(x)\,dx \quad\text{(continuous)}.

Two intuitions, both worth carrying:

Long-run average. Repeat the experiment endlessly and the running average of outcomes settles on E[X] (Ross 2018). (That this must happen is the Law of Large Numbers.)
Centre of gravity. Place a weight equal to each probability at its value along a rod; E[X] is the point where the rod balances (Ross 2018). Skew the weights right and the balance point slides right.


Worked examples (Ross).

Three fair coins, Y = number of heads. The masses are p(0,1,2,3) =
\tfrac18,\tfrac38,\tfrac38,\tfrac18, so E[Y] = 0\cdot\tfrac18 + 1\cdot\tfrac38 + 2\cdot\tfrac38 + 3\cdot\tfrac18 = 1.5. Note the expectation, 1.5, is a value Y can never take — it’s a balance point, not a forecast (Ross 2018).
Uniform on (\alpha,\beta). With constant density, the balance point is the midpoint, E[X] = \dfrac{\alpha+\beta}{2} (Ross 2018).

title: Expectation & Variance
text:
Linearity of expectation — the workhorse
For any random variables X, Y and constants a, b (Ross 2018): 
E[aX + bY] = a\,E[X] + b\,E[Y].







TipWhy linearity needs no independence



This is the most useful fact in the chapter, and its power is that it holds even when X and Y are dependent (Ross 2018). The reason is mechanical: E[X+Y] sums the quantity (x+y) against the joint distribution P\{X=x, Y=y\}. That double sum splits into a sum of the x-part and a sum of the y-part, and each collapses to a marginal — \sum_x x\,P\{X=x\} and \sum_y y\,P\{Y=y\} — regardless of how X and Y are related. Dependence rearranges where the joint mass sits, but it can’t change the marginal totals. (Dependence does matter for the variance of a sum — just not the expectation.)


Linearity lets you compute the mean of a complicated sum without ever finding its distribution — the trick behind the unbiasedness of the sample mean, E[\bar X] = \mu, used in the flagship CLT article.

title: Expectation & Variance
section: LOTUS: the mean of a function, the lazy way
text:
LOTUS: the mean of a function, the lazy way
You often want E[g(X)] — the expected value of some transformation, like X^2. The Law of the Unconscious Statistician says you can compute it directly from X’s distribution, without first deriving the distribution of g(X) (Ross 2018): 
E[g(X)] = \sum_x g(x)\,p(x) \quad\text{or}\quad \int g(x)\,f(x)\,dx.


Worked example (Ross). Let X take values -1, 0, 1 with probabilities 0.2, 0.5, 0.3. Then E[X^2] = (-1)^2(0.2) + 0^2(0.5) + 1^2(0.3) = 0.5 — just reweight the squared values (Ross 2018). (Note E[X^2] = 0.5 \ne (E[X])^2 = 0.01; squaring and averaging don’t commute — exactly the gap the variance measures.)

title: Expectation & Variance
text:
Variance: the scatter
The variance is the expected squared distance from the mean (Ross 2018): 
\operatorname{Var}(X) = E\big[(X-\mu)^2\big] = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2, \qquad \mu = E[X].
 The second form (via LOTUS) is the one you actually compute with. The standard deviation \operatorname{SD}(X) = \sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(X)} returns the scatter to the original units (squared dollars back to dollars).
If E[X] is the centre of gravity, variance is the moment of inertia — how far the distribution’s “mass” sits from the centre (Ross 2018). Same balance point, very different spread:

Worked examples (Ross).

Fair die. E[X] = 3.5 and E[X^2] = \tfrac{1+4+9+16+25+36}{6} = \tfrac{91}{6}, so \operatorname{Var}(X) = \tfrac{91}{6} - 3.5^2 = \tfrac{35}{12} \approx 2.917 (Ross 2018).
Same mean, different spread. Let W \equiv 0; Y = \pm 1 with probability \tfrac12 each; Z = \pm 100 with probability \tfrac12 each. All three have mean 0, yet \operatorname{Var}(W) = 0, \operatorname{Var}(Y) = 1, \operatorname{Var}(Z) = 10{,}000 — identical centres, wildly different scatter (Ross 2018). The mean alone never tells you the spread.

title: Expectation & Variance
section: The variance of a sum — and the standard error
text:
The variance of a sum — and the standard error
Unlike expectation, variance does not add freely. For two variables, 
\operatorname{Var}(X + Y) = \operatorname{Var}(X) + \operatorname{Var}(Y) + 2\operatorname{Cov}(X, Y).
 The covariance term is exactly where dependence re-enters (the extraction’s “correlation only affects the variance of a sum, not its expectation”) (Ross 2018). When X and Y are independent, the covariance is 0 and variances simply add — the fact that unlocks the standard error.






TipWhere SE = σ/√n comes from



Take an average of n independent draws, each with variance \sigma^2. Variances of independent things add, and the constant 1/n pulls out squared: 
\operatorname{Var}(\bar X) = \operatorname{Var}\!\Big(\tfrac1n \textstyle\sum_i X_i\Big)
= \tfrac{1}{n^2}\sum_i \operatorname{Var}(X_i) = \tfrac{1}{n^2}(n\sigma^2) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n}.
 Take the square root: \operatorname{SE}(\bar X) = \sigma/\sqrt{n}. The entire \sqrt{n} law of precision — quadruple the sample to halve the error — is this one line. It is why the CLT chapter can promise that averages get sharp.

title: Expectation & Variance
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Treating the expectation as a likely value. E[Y] = 1.5 heads is the balance point, not a prediction — the variable may never take it. Report spread alongside the mean.
Assuming E[g(X)] = g(E[X]). False in general (Jensen’s inequality). E[X^2] \ne (E[X])^2 is the very gap that defines variance. Squaring then averaging \ne averaging then squaring.
Adding variances that aren’t independent. \operatorname{Var}(X+Y) = \operatorname{Var}(X)
+ \operatorname{Var}(Y) holds only when \operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=0. Forgetting the covariance term is how people understate uncertainty for correlated data.
Reporting variance in squared units. Variance is in units^2; convert to the standard deviation before interpreting “spread” in plain language.

title: Expectation & Variance
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — expectation as a probability-weighted average, with the long-run-average and centre-of-gravity intuitions (three-coins E[Y]=1.5; uniform midpoint); linearity of expectation for any X,Y and the joint-distribution argument for why independence isn’t needed; LOTUS (X\in\{-1,0,1\}, E[X^2]=0.5); variance as E[(X-\mu)^2]=E[X^2]-(E[X])^2 with the moment-of-inertia intuition (fair die \operatorname{Var}=35/12; the W/Y/Z same-mean/different-spread trio); variance of a sum and the role of covariance.

title: Expectation & Variance
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Compute means with linearity, not brute force. Break a complicated quantity into a sum and take expectations term by term — independence not required, so it almost always works.
Use \operatorname{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2. It’s nearly always less work than the definition, and it makes the “squaring doesn’t commute with averaging” point concrete.
Always pair a mean with a spread. A mean without a standard deviation hides whether the average is typical or just a balance point between extremes.
Track independence before adding variances. The covariance term is where clustered or correlated data quietly inflate true uncertainty — flag it early.

title: Expectation & Variance
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Linearity, proof sketch. For discrete X,Y with joint pmf p(x,y), 
E[X+Y] = \sum_x\sum_y (x+y)\,p(x,y) = \sum_x x\sum_y p(x,y) + \sum_y y\sum_x p(x,y) = E[X] + E[Y],
 since \sum_y p(x,y) = p_X(x) and \sum_x p(x,y) = p_Y(y) are the marginals — true for any joint distribution. Scaling by constants is immediate, giving E[aX+bY]=aE[X]+bE[Y].
Computational form of the variance. Expand and use linearity: 
\operatorname{Var}(X) = E[(X-\mu)^2] = E[X^2 - 2\mu X + \mu^2] = E[X^2] - 2\mu E[X] + \mu^2
= E[X^2] - \mu^2.

Variance of a linear combination. With \operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=E[XY]-E[X]E[Y], 
\operatorname{Var}(aX+bY) = a^2\operatorname{Var}(X) + b^2\operatorname{Var}(Y) + 2ab\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y).
 For independent X_1,\dots,X_n (all covariances 0) with common variance \sigma^2, the average \bar X = \tfrac1n\sum X_i has \operatorname{Var}(\bar X) = \sigma^2/n, hence \operatorname{SE}(\bar X)=\sigma/\sqrt n. Covariance and correlation get their own treatment in P8.
Jensen’s inequality (why E[g(X)] \ne g(E[X])). For a convex function g (one that curves upward, like x^2 or e^x), the average of the transformed values exceeds the transform of the average: 
E[g(X)] \;\ge\; g\big(E[X]\big),
 with equality only if g is linear or X is constant. The variance is the canonical case (g(x)=x^2 gives E[X^2] \ge (E[X])^2, the gap being \operatorname{Var}(X)\ge 0). For a concave g (like \log) the inequality flips: E[\log X] \le \log E[X] — which is exactly why the mean of log-income sits below the log of mean income, and why exponentiating a log-scale prediction under-states the level. The size of the gap grows with the curvature of g and the spread of X.

title: Expectation & Variance
text:
Connections

Builds on: Random variables — expectation and variance are summaries of a distribution.
Leads to: the common distributions (each has a signature mean and variance) and directly to the standard error in the CLT.
Deepened by: Covariance & correlation, which unpacks the covariance term in the variance-of-a-sum formula.

title: Expectation & Variance
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Explain expectation with both the long-run-average and centre-of-gravity intuitions, using Ross’s three-coins (E[Y]=1.5) and uniform-midpoint examples.”
“State linearity of expectation and explain, via the joint distribution, exactly why it does not require independence — then contrast with the variance of a sum, which does.”
“Define variance two ways and compute it for a fair die (35/12); use Ross’s W/Y/Z trio to show three distributions with the same mean but very different spread.”
“Derive \operatorname{SE}(\bar X)=\sigma/\sqrt n from the variance-of-a-sum rule, and explain why it needs independence.”

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text: The classical formula P(E) = |E|/|S| turns every probability into two counts: the favourable outcomes over the total. The trouble is that real sample spaces are huge — a 9-person batting order has 362,880 arrangements — so you must count without enumerating. Three tools do almost all the work: the multiplication principle, permutations (order matters), and combinations (order doesn’t).

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
Why it matters
This is the most mechanical entry in the chapter, and the most quietly important. The combination count \binom{n}{r} you meet here is the same \binom{n}{r} that sits at the front of the binomial distribution in P7, which in turn drives the analysis of yes/no survey data, polling, and A/B tests. Sampling with and without replacement — the backbone of survey sampling — is a counting question. Learn to count cleanly now and several later results arrive pre-assembled.
The deeper habit is the one from the De Méré story: intuition about “how many ways” is unreliable, and a small slip in the count becomes a wrong probability. The tools below replace guessing with bookkeeping.

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
The multiplication principle
The foundation: if one choice can be made in m ways and a second independent choice in n ways, the two together can be made in m \times n ways. Extend it to any number of stages by multiplying across them (Ross 2018; Grinstead and Snell 1997).

Worked example (Grinstead & Snell). A meal has 2 appetiser choices, 3 main courses, and 2 desserts. The number of distinct meals is 2 \times 3 \times 2 = 12 (Grinstead and Snell 1997).

This single idea — multiply the choices at each stage — generates both of the formulas below; they are just the multiplication principle applied to specific selection problems.

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
Permutations — when order matters
A permutation is an ordered arrangement. To order n distinct objects, you have n choices for the first slot, n-1 for the second, and so on — so by the multiplication principle there are 
n! = n \times (n-1) \times \cdots \times 2 \times 1
 arrangements (Ross 2018).

Worked example (Ross). A baseball team of 9 players can bat in 9! = 362{,}880 different orders (Ross 2018).

If you arrange only r of the n objects (still caring about order), you stop after r slots: 
{}_nP_r = \frac{n!}{(n-r)!} = n \times (n-1) \times \cdots \times (n-r+1).

When objects repeat, some orderings become indistinguishable, and you divide them out:

Worked example (Ross). The word PEPPER has 6 letters, but with 3 P’s and 2 E’s identical, the number of distinguishable arrangements is 
\frac{6!}{3!\,2!\,1!} = 60,
 not 6! = 720 (Ross 2018). Dividing by 3! and 2! removes the rearrangements of identical letters that don’t produce a new “word.”

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
Combinations — when order doesn’t matter
A combination is an unordered selection of r objects from n. Because order no longer distinguishes selections, you take the ordered count {}_nP_r and divide by the r! orderings of each chosen group: 
\binom{n}{r} = \frac{n!}{r!\,(n-r)!},
 read “n choose r” (Ross 2018; Grinstead and Snell 1997). This is the binomial coefficient.

Worked examples.

Choosing a committee of 5 from 10 people: \binom{10}{5} = \dfrac{10 \cdot 9 \cdot 8 \cdot 7
\cdot 6}{5 \cdot 4 \cdot 3 \cdot 2 \cdot 1} = 252 (Grinstead and Snell 1997).
Choosing a committee of 3 from 20 people: \binom{20}{3} = \dfrac{20 \cdot 19 \cdot 18}{3
\cdot 2 \cdot 1} = 1{,}140 (Ross 2018).








TipThe one question that picks the tool



Before counting, ask: does order matter? A batting order, a ranked podium, a PIN — order matters, use permutations. A committee, a hand of cards, a random sample of respondents — order is irrelevant, use combinations. Most counting mistakes are really this-question-answered-wrong: counting arrangements when you wanted selections (overcounting by a factor of r!), or vice versa.

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
Why social scientists care: sampling
Drawing a sample of n people from a population of N — without replacement and without regard to order — is exactly a combination: there are \binom{N}{n} possible samples. Every statement that a simple random sample is “equally likely to be any subset” rests on this count, and the machinery of sampling distributions (the flagship CLT article) is built on top of it. Counting is not a detour from social science; it is the hidden floor under survey inference.

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Using permutations when order is irrelevant. Counting ordered arrangements for an unordered selection overcounts by exactly r!. A “committee” is a combination, not a permutation.
Forgetting to divide out repeats. Treating PEPPER’s identical letters as distinct inflates the count more than tenfold (720 vs 60). Whenever objects are interchangeable, divide by the factorials of their multiplicities.
Confusing with- and without-replacement. Sampling the same person twice (with replacement) and sampling two different people (without) give different counts and different probabilities — be explicit about which the design uses.
Multiplying when you should add (or vice versa). Multiply across independent stages of a single arrangement (“and”); add across mutually exclusive cases (“or”). Mixing these up is the structural error beneath most wrong counts.

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — the basic principle of counting; permutations n! (the 9-player batting order, 362{,}880); permutations with indistinguishable objects (PEPPER, 60); combinations \binom{n}{r} (committee of 3 from 20, 1{,}140).
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — the multiplication principle (the 2 × 3 × 2 = 12 meal example); combinations (committee of 5 from 10, 252).

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Decide “order or not?” before touching a formula. That single question selects permutation vs combination and prevents the most common error outright.
Translate “and” → multiply, “or” → add. Stages joined by “and” multiply; mutually exclusive cases joined by “or” add. Narrate the problem and the arithmetic follows.
Use the complement when “at least one” appears. Counting the complement (“none”) is usually far smaller than counting every favourable case directly — the same trick as in the axioms entry.
Recognise \binom{n}{r} when it returns. The same coefficient reappears in the binomial distribution and Pascal’s triangle; spotting it early makes P7 feel like review rather than new material.

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Permutations of r from n. By the multiplication principle, the number of ways to fill r ordered slots from n distinct objects is 
{}_nP_r = n(n-1)\cdots(n-r+1) = \frac{n!}{(n-r)!}.

Combinations from permutations. Each unordered selection of r objects can be ordered in r! ways, so {}_nP_r = r! \binom{n}{r}, giving 
\binom{n}{r} = \frac{{}_nP_r}{r!} = \frac{n!}{r!\,(n-r)!}.

Symmetry and Pascal’s rule. Directly from the formula, 
\binom{n}{r} = \binom{n}{n-r}, \qquad
\binom{n}{r} = \binom{n-1}{r-1} + \binom{n-1}{r}.
 The second identity (Pascal’s rule) is the engine of Pascal’s triangle: choosing r from n either includes a fixed element (then choose r-1 from the rest) or excludes it (choose r from the rest).
The binomial theorem. The same coefficients expand powers of a sum: 
(x + y)^n = \sum_{r=0}^{n} \binom{n}{r} x^{r} y^{\,n-r},
 because forming an x^r y^{n-r} term means choosing which r of the n factors contribute an x — a combination. This is exactly why \binom{n}{r} heads the binomial distribution in P7: it counts the number of sequences with r successes.

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
Connections

Builds on: Sample spaces & the axioms — counting is how you evaluate P(E) = |E|/|S| when outcomes are equally likely.
Leads to: Conditional probability, where counting restricted sample spaces becomes second nature.
Pays off in: the common distributions (the binomial coefficient is the heart of the binomial distribution) and survey sampling (a random sample is a combination).

title: Counting: The Arithmetic of Possibility
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Explain the multiplication principle, permutations, and combinations from Ross and Grinstead & Snell, with the exact worked examples each uses (the meal, the batting order, PEPPER, the committees).”
“When does order matter versus not, and how do permutations and combinations differ numerically? Give a worked contrast on the same set of objects.”
“Derive the combination formula \binom{n}{r} from the permutation formula, and prove Pascal’s rule and the binomial theorem.”
“Show how counting underlies sampling without replacement, and connect \binom{N}{n} to the idea of a simple random sample.”

title: Conditional Expectation
text: Plain expectation answers “what’s the average?” Conditional expectation answers the question researchers actually ask: “what’s the average for people like this?” That small addition — an average that depends on what you already know — turns out to be the single most important object in applied social science. It is, quite literally, what a regression estimates.

title: Conditional Expectation
text:
Why it matters
Almost every empirical question is conditional. Not “what is income?” but “what is income given education?” Not “what is recidivism?” but “what is recidivism given the treatment?” The tool that makes “given” precise is conditional expectation, and it is the hinge between the probability you have learned and the regression and causal inference still ahead.
Two results in this entry do enormous work. The law of iterated expectations lets you rebuild a whole-population average from subgroup averages. And the fact that conditional expectation is the best possible predictor is the reason regression — which estimates exactly this object — sits at the centre of the field. Master this and regression stops being a formula and becomes inevitable.

title: Conditional Expectation
section: Conditional expectation: the average given what you know
text:
Conditional expectation: the average given what you know
For a specific known value Y=y, the conditional expectation of X is just the mean of the conditional distribution — the ordinary average, but computed with conditional probabilities (Ross 2018):

E[X \mid Y=y] = \sum_x x\,P(X=x \mid Y=y) \quad\text{(discrete)}, \qquad
E[X \mid Y=y] = \int x\, f_{X\mid Y}(x\mid y)\,dx \quad\text{(continuous)}.

So far this is one number for each y. Now the conceptual leap that trips up every newcomer and unlocks everything:






ImportantE[X | Y] is itself a random variable



E[X\mid Y=y] is a number. But E[X\mid Y] — before you fix a value of Y — is a function of the random variable Y, and therefore a random variable in its own right, with its own distribution, mean, and variance. (Ross 2018) It is the rule “whatever Y turns out to be, report the average of X among cases with that Y.” Because Y is random, that reported value is random too.


A clean Ross example: if X and Y are independent binomials with the same parameters and you are told only their sum X+Y=m, then by symmetry E[X \mid X+Y=m] = m/2 — the total splits evenly in expectation. (Ross 2018) Knowing the sum updates your best guess for the part.

title: Conditional Expectation
text:
The law of iterated expectations
Here is the first powerhouse. The unconditional average equals the average of the conditional averages: (Ross 2018)

E[X] = E\big[\,E[X \mid Y]\,\big].

The intuition is exactly how you’d compute a national average from regional ones: take each subgroup’s mean and average them, weighting by subgroup size. (Ross 2018)






TipTwo quick worked examples



GPA. If women average a 3.5 GPA and men a 3.2, and each is 50% of the population, the overall average is 0.5(3.5) + 0.5(3.2) = 3.35. The whole is the size-weighted blend of the parts. (Ross 2018)
Misprints. Sam is equally likely to grab a probability book (mean 2 misprints) or a history book (mean 5). The expected number he finds is E[X] = 0.5(2) + 0.5(5) = 3.5 — average over which book, each weighted by how often it’s chosen. (Ross 2018)

title: Conditional Expectation
text:
Computing expectations by conditioning
The law of iterated expectations is not just bookkeeping — it is a problem-solving engine. When a quantity is hard to average directly, condition on a helpful first step, then average over the steps. (Ross 2018) Ross’s classic shows the trick at its most elegant.






NoteRoss’s trapped miner



A miner is at a junction with three doors. Door 1 leads out after 3 hours. Door 2 loops back to the junction after 5 hours. Door 3 loops back after 7 hours. Each is equally likely, and a returning miner forgets and chooses afresh. What is the expected time to safety?
Condition on the first door Y. Given door 1, E[X\mid Y{=}1]=3. Given door 2, he spends 5 hours and is back where he started, facing the same problem: E[X\mid Y{=}2]=5+E[X]. Likewise E[X\mid Y{=}3]=7+E[X]. By iterated expectations,

E[X] = \tfrac13(3) + \tfrac13\big(5+E[X]\big) + \tfrac13\big(7+E[X]\big).

Solve: E[X] = 5 + \tfrac23 E[X] \Rightarrow E[X] = \mathbf{15} hours. (Ross 2018) A seemingly hard infinite-loop problem collapses to one line of algebra — the power of conditioning.

title: Conditional Expectation
text:
The law of total variance
If iterated expectations splits the mean, its partner splits the variance. The law of total variance decomposes total variation into two interpretable pieces: (Ross 2018)

\operatorname{Var}(X) = \underbrace{E\big[\operatorname{Var}(X \mid Y)\big]}_{\text{within-group}}
+ \underbrace{\operatorname{Var}\big(E[X \mid Y]\big)}_{\text{between-group}}.

In words: total spread = the average leftover spread within groups + the spread of the group means themselves. (Ross 2018) This is precisely the logic that ANOVA formalises — “between-group versus within-group variation” — and it is the same split that the bias–variance decomposition will echo. Knowing Y can only reduce your remaining uncertainty about X: the between-group part is variation that conditioning explains away.
A compound example shows its reach: if a random number N of customers each spend a random X, the variance of total daily revenue is \operatorname{Var}\!\big(\sum_{i=1}^N X_i\big) = E[N]\operatorname{Var}(X) + (E[X])^2\operatorname{Var}(N) — one part from variation in spending, one from variation in the number of customers. (Ross 2018)

title: Conditional Expectation
section: Conditional expectation is the best predictor
text:
Conditional expectation is the best predictor
Now the result that makes this entry the gateway to modelling. Suppose you must predict Y with some function g(X) of the information X, and you are graded by mean squared error, E\big[(Y-g(X))^2\big]. Which g is best? (Ross 2018)
The answer is unique and beautiful: g(X) = E[Y\mid X]. No other function of X achieves a smaller expected squared error. (Ross 2018) The two limiting cases pin down the intuition:

With no information about X, the best constant predictor of Y is its unconditional mean E[Y] (the variance entry’s balance point).
Once you observe X=x, the best prediction shifts to the conditional mean E[Y\mid X=x].

So conditioning is literally the act of improving your prediction with data.

See it: the regression curve is conditional expectation
The picture below makes the abstract concrete. The cloud is data; the flat grey line is the best guess that ignores X (the unconditional mean E[Y]); the blue curve is E[Y\mid X] — the best guess that uses X. The blue curve threads the conditional averages (the dots), and it is the target every regression aims at.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(4)
n = 600
x = rng.uniform(0, 10, n)
cef = 20 + 5 * np.sqrt(x)              # the true E[Y|X]: a curved, increasing CEF
y = cef + rng.normal(0, 3.5, n)        # data scatters around the CEF

# binned sample conditional means
bins = np.linspace(0, 10, 11)
idx = np.digitize(x, bins)
centres = 0.5 * (bins[:-1] + bins[1:])
cond_means = [y[idx == k].mean() for k in range(1, len(bins))]

xs = np.linspace(0.05, 10, 200)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.0))
ax.scatter(x, y, s=10, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.5, label="data")
ax.axhline(y.mean(), color="#7a7f87", ls="--", lw=1.6,
           label=f"E[Y] = {y.mean():.0f}  (best guess ignoring X)")
ax.plot(xs, 20 + 5 * np.sqrt(xs), color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.6,
        label="E[Y | X]  (best guess using X)")
ax.scatter(centres, cond_means, color="#b5446e", s=36, zorder=5,
           label="average of Y within each X-slice")
ax.set_xlabel("X"); ax.set_ylabel("Y"); ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_title("The regression curve is the conditional-expectation function")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Conditional expectation as the best predictor. Ignoring X, the best guess for Y is the flat unconditional mean (grey). Knowing X, the best guess is the conditional-expectation function E[Y|X] (blue) — which traces the average of Y within each slice of X (dots). Estimating this curve is exactly what regression does.

title: Conditional Expectation
text:
The foundation of regression
This is why the entry sits where it does. The conditional expectation function (CEF) E[Y\mid X] describes how the population average of Y moves across values of X. (Ross 2018) When you run a regression, you are estimating the CEF: if the true relationship is linear, the regression line is the CEF; if it is non-linear, regression delivers the best linear approximation to it. (Ross 2018) Every regression coefficient you will ever interpret is a statement about how a conditional average changes — which is why “regression” and “conditional expectation” are, at bottom, the same idea wearing different clothes.

title: Conditional Expectation
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Forgetting that E[X\mid Y] is random. E[X\mid Y=y] is a number; E[X\mid Y] is a random variable. The whole machinery (iterated expectations, total variance) depends on the second reading. (Ross 2018)
Averaging subgroup means without weighting by size. The law of iterated expectations weights each conditional mean by how common that subgroup is. An unweighted average of group means is a different — and usually wrong — number. (Ross 2018)
Ignoring within-group variance. A difference in group means (between-group) can be swamped by large within-group spread. The total-variance decomposition is exactly what keeps both in view — and what ANOVA tests. (Ross 2018)
Reading the CEF as causal. E[Y\mid X] is the average of Y among units with a given X — an association, not a mechanism. The correlation-isn’t-causation warning applies in full force; closing that gap is the job of Part V.

title: Conditional Expectation
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — conditional expectation as the mean of the conditional distribution; E[X\mid Y] as a random variable (the binomial-sum m/2 example); the law of iterated expectations E[X]=E[E[X\mid Y]] (GPA, misprints); computing expectations by conditioning (the trapped-miner problem, E[X]=15); the law of total variance and the compound-revenue example; conditional expectation as the minimum-mean-squared-error predictor.
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — conditional distributions and expectation as the foundation for averaging over subgroups.
Bridges forward: the CEF-as-regression framing connects to Wooldridge and Angrist & Pischke in Part III and Part V.

title: Conditional Expectation
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Law of iterated expectations. For discrete variables, 
E\big[E[X\mid Y]\big] = \sum_y E[X\mid Y=y]\,P(Y=y)
= \sum_y \sum_x x\,P(X=x\mid Y=y)\,P(Y=y)
= \sum_x x \sum_y P(X=x, Y=y) = \sum_x x\,P(X=x) = E[X],
 using P(X=x\mid Y=y)P(Y=y) = P(X=x,Y=y) and then summing out y to the marginal. The continuous case replaces sums with integrals.
Law of total variance. Apply \operatorname{Var}(X)=E[X^2]-(E[X])^2 and iterated expectations to E[X^2]=E\big[E[X^2\mid Y]\big]. Writing \operatorname{Var}(X\mid Y)=E[X^2\mid Y]-(E[X\mid Y])^2 and m(Y)=E[X\mid Y], 
\operatorname{Var}(X) = E\big[\operatorname{Var}(X\mid Y)\big] + \big(E[m(Y)^2]-(E[m(Y)])^2\big)
= E\big[\operatorname{Var}(X\mid Y)\big] + \operatorname{Var}\big(E[X\mid Y]\big).

Conditional expectation minimises MSE. For any predictor g(X), insert and subtract m(X)=E[Y\mid X]: 
E\big[(Y-g(X))^2\big] = E\big[(Y-m(X))^2\big] + E\big[(m(X)-g(X))^2\big] + 2\,E\big[(Y-m(X))(m(X)-g(X))\big].
 The cross term vanishes: conditioning on X, E[(Y-m(X))\mid X]=0, so by iterated expectations the whole cross term is 0. The first term is fixed; the second is \ge 0 and is minimised (to 0) exactly when g(X)=m(X)=E[Y\mid X]. Hence the CEF is the unique minimum-MSE predictor.
The miner, solved. E[X]=\tfrac13\cdot3+\tfrac13(5+E[X])+\tfrac13(7+E[X]) = 5 + \tfrac23 E[X], so \tfrac13 E[X]=5 and E[X]=15.

title: Conditional Expectation
text:
Connections

Builds on: expectation & variance (conditional expectation is expectation under the conditional distribution), conditional probability (the conditioning operation), and joint distributions & covariance (the conditional distribution lives inside the joint).
Leads to: the Law of Large Numbers and, decisively, regression — which estimates the CEF — and the potential-outcomes machinery, where average treatment effects are differences of conditional expectations.
Reappears as: the “between vs within” logic of ANOVA and the bias–variance decomposition in predictive modelling.

title: Conditional Expectation
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Explain conditional expectation E[X|Y=y] versus the random variable E[X|Y], using Ross’s examples, and why the distinction matters.”
“State and prove the law of iterated expectations, and walk through the GPA and misprints examples.”
“Work through Ross’s trapped-miner problem step by step, showing how conditioning gives E[X] = 15 hours.”
“Explain the law of total variance as within-group plus between-group variation, with the compound-revenue example.”
“Show why the conditional expectation E[Y|X] is the best predictor of Y in the mean-squared-error sense, and why that makes it the foundation of regression.”

title: Bayes’ Theorem: Updating Beliefs
text: You know P(\text{test positive} \mid \text{sick}) — that’s the test’s accuracy. You want P(\text{sick} \mid \text{test positive}) — that’s what the patient cares about. Bayes’ theorem flips one into the other, and the flip is governed by the base rate: how common the condition was to begin with. Ignore the base rate and you will systematically over-read your evidence.

title: Bayes’ Theorem: Updating Beliefs
text:
Why it matters
Bayes’ theorem is two things at once. Mechanically, it is the rule for inverting a conditional probability — turning P(E \mid H) into P(H \mid E). Philosophically, it is the engine of learning from evidence: it tells you exactly how a prior belief should change once data arrive. That second reading is the entire foundation of the Bayesian-inference chapter (built on Lambert), and the reason this entry is the bridge out of pure probability.
It also defuses one of the most consequential reasoning errors in applied work — the base-rate fallacy — which infects medical screening, forensic evidence, fraud detection, and any setting where a fairly accurate test hunts for a fairly rare thing. Getting Bayes into your bones is what stops you from confusing “the test rarely lies” with “a positive result is probably true.” Those are different claims, and the gap between them is the base rate.

title: Bayes’ Theorem: Updating Beliefs
text:
The theorem
Combine the multiplication rule two ways — P(HE) = P(E \mid H)P(H) = P(H \mid E)P(E) — and solve for the conditional you want (Ross 2018):

P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(E)}.

When the hypotheses H_1,\dots,H_m partition the world, the denominator is supplied by the law of total probability, giving the full form (Ross 2018):

P(H_i \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H_i)\,P(H_i)}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{m} P(E \mid H_k)\,P(H_k)}.

The vocabulary that carries into Bayesian statistics (Lambert 2018):

P(H) — the prior: what you believed before seeing the evidence.
P(E \mid H) — the likelihood: how well each hypothesis predicts the evidence.
P(H \mid E) — the posterior: your updated belief, after.
P(E) — the marginal likelihood (the denominator): a normaliser that makes the posterior sum to 1.

In one line: posterior \propto likelihood \times prior. Belief after is belief before, re-weighted by how well it predicted what you saw.

title: Bayes’ Theorem: Updating Beliefs
section: The base-rate fallacy: a positive test that usually isn’t
text:
The base-rate fallacy: a positive test that usually isn’t
This is the example that makes Bayes unforgettable (Grinstead and Snell 1997). A cancer test:

Prevalence (prior): 1 person in 1,000 has the cancer — P(C) = 0.001.
Sensitivity: if you have it, the test is positive 99% of the time — P(+ \mid C) = 0.99.
Specificity: if you don’t, the test is negative 95% of the time — so the false-positive rate is 5%, P(+ \mid \text{no } C) = 0.05.

The test sounds excellent. Yet if you test positive, your probability of actually having the cancer is only about 1.9% (Grinstead and Snell 1997):

P(C \mid +) = \frac{(0.99)(0.001)}{(0.99)(0.001) + (0.05)(0.999)} \approx \frac{0.00099}{0.05094}
\approx 0.019.


Why — see it in natural frequencies
The algebra hides the intuition; counts reveal it. Imagine 100,000 people:




Test positive
Test negative
Total




Has cancer
99
1
100


No cancer
4,995
94,905
99,900


Total
5,094
94,906
100,000



Only 100 people are truly sick, and 99 of them test positive. But 5% of the 99,900 healthy people — nearly 5,000 — also test positive. So among the 5,094 positives, the 99 real cases are a small minority: 99 / 5{,}094 \approx 1.9\% (Grinstead and Snell 1997). The false positives, drawn from a vastly larger healthy pool, swamp the true positives. That is the base-rate fallacy: a rare condition means even an accurate test produces mostly false alarms.


Show the code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

sens, spec = 0.99, 0.95          # sensitivity, specificity
prev = np.logspace(-4, 0, 400)   # prevalence from 0.01% to 100%

# Posterior P(disease | positive) via Bayes' theorem
ppv = (sens * prev) / (sens * prev + (1 - spec) * (1 - prev))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
ax.plot(prev, ppv, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2)
ax.axvline(0.001, color="#b5446e", ls="--", lw=1.4)
ax.scatter([0.001], [(sens*0.001)/(sens*0.001 + (1-spec)*0.999)],
           color="#b5446e", zorder=5)
ax.annotate("prevalence 0.1%\n→ only ~1.9% true",
            xy=(0.001, 0.019), xytext=(0.0025, 0.45),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color="#b5446e"), fontsize=9)
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("Prevalence (base rate), log scale")
ax.set_ylabel("P(disease | positive test)")
ax.set_title("A good test + a rare disease = a positive that usually isn't")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Probability that a positive test is a true positive, as the disease’s prevalence (base rate) varies, for a test with 99% sensitivity and 95% specificity. At the 0.1% prevalence of the example (dashed line), a positive result is right under 2% of the time; the test only becomes trustworthy once the condition is common.

title: Bayes’ Theorem: Updating Beliefs
section: The prosecutor’s fallacy: don’t transpose the conditional
text:
The prosecutor’s fallacy: don’t transpose the conditional
The base-rate fallacy has a cousin that wrecks courtroom and forensic reasoning: confusing P(E \mid H) with P(H \mid E) (Albers 2017). “The probability of this DNA match if the suspect is innocent is one in a million” is not “the probability the suspect is innocent is one in a million.” The two differ by exactly the prior — the base rate of guilt in the relevant pool — which Bayes makes explicit and the fallacy quietly drops.

Albers’ homely version: a well-fed dog rarely barks, so a bark is weak evidence of hunger — the dog might bark 5% of the time even when full. P(\text{bark} \mid \text{hungry}) being high tells you little about P(\text{hungry} \mid \text{bark}) without knowing how often the dog is hungry in the first place (Albers 2017).

title: Bayes’ Theorem: Updating Beliefs
text:
The bridge to Bayesian inference (Lambert)
Lambert frames Bayesian analysis as inversion: data are the effects, parameters are the causes, and Bayes runs the inference backward from effect to cause (Lambert 2018). The three ingredients:

Prior p(\theta) — a full distribution encoding pre-data uncertainty about a parameter. It is explicitly subjective and open to debate — a feature, not a flaw, since it makes assumptions visible (Lambert 2018).
Likelihood p(\text{data} \mid \theta) — how probable the observed data are for each parameter value. Lambert stresses a subtlety: viewed as a function of \theta (data fixed), the likelihood is not a probability distribution — its area need not integrate to 1 (Lambert 2018).
Posterior p(\theta \mid \text{data}) — the goal: a re-weighting of the prior by the likelihood. As data accumulate, the posterior narrows, reflecting growing confidence (Lambert 2018).







TipTwo of Lambert’s pictures worth keeping




The Bayesian coastguard. A ship is lost in a storm. The prior is a wide circle of possible locations; one radio signal sharpens it to an oval; combining a second tower’s data — using the first posterior as the new prior — collapses it to a tiny high-probability patch where the rescue happens. Updating is just yesterday’s posterior becoming today’s prior (Lambert 2018).
The fish game (a “Bayes’ box”). A bowl holds 5 fish, each red or white; you catch one and it’s red. Start with a flat prior — each count of red fish (0–5) equally likely, 1/6 each. Multiply each prior by the likelihood of catching red given that count (2 red fish → 2/5), sum the products to normalise, and read off the posterior. Counts that better explain “caught red” gain probability; “0 red fish” is annihilated (Lambert 2018).

title: Bayes’ Theorem: Updating Beliefs
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Ignoring the base rate. Judging a positive result by the test’s accuracy alone, with no regard for how rare the condition is, is the classic error. For rare conditions, even great tests yield mostly false positives.
Transposing the conditional (prosecutor’s fallacy). P(\text{evidence} \mid \text{innocent}) is not P(\text{innocent} \mid \text{evidence}). Always ask what prior the claim is smuggling.
Treating the likelihood as a probability over \theta. It isn’t (Lambert) — it doesn’t integrate to 1 in \theta. The posterior is what’s normalised.
Forgetting to renormalise. “Posterior \propto likelihood \times prior” needs the denominator P(E) to become a real distribution; skipping it gives numbers that don’t sum to 1.
Pretending you have no prior. You always do — even “all equally likely” is a prior choice. Bayes makes it explicit; the fallacies above come from leaving it implicit.

title: Bayes’ Theorem: Updating Beliefs
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — Bayes’ theorem from the multiplication rule and law of total probability; the prior/likelihood/posterior decomposition.
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — the rare-disease screening example (prevalence 0.001, sensitivity 0.99, specificity 0.95 → P(C\mid+)\approx 0.019) and the base-rate intuition.
Albers, Quantitative Data Analysis — the prosecutor’s fallacy / transposed conditional (the well-fed dog that still barks 5% of the time).
Lambert, A Student’s Guide to Bayesian Statistics — Bayesian inference as inversion; priors, likelihoods (why the likelihood isn’t a distribution), and posteriors; the coastguard and fish-game (Bayes’ box) examples; posteriors narrowing with data.

title: Bayes’ Theorem: Updating Beliefs
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Reach for natural frequencies. “Out of 100,000 people…” makes base-rate problems intuitive where formulas fog them. Build the 2×2 table before trusting any percentage.
Always ask for the base rate. Before reacting to any positive result — test, alert, red flag — ask how common the thing is. Rarity is half the answer.
Name your prior out loud. Even a flat prior is a choice; stating it converts hidden assumptions into debatable ones, which is the honest move.
Think in likelihood ratios. “How many times more likely is this evidence under H than under not-H?” times your prior odds gives the posterior odds — fast, and immune to the prosecutor’s fallacy (see the appendix).

title: Bayes’ Theorem: Updating Beliefs
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Derivation. From the multiplication rule, P(HE) = P(E\mid H)P(H) and also P(HE) = P(H\mid E)P(E). Equate and divide by P(E): 
P(H\mid E) = \frac{P(E\mid H)\,P(H)}{P(E)}.
 Expanding P(E) over a partition \{H_k\} via the law of total probability gives the full form.
Odds form (often the clearest). Write the ratio of posteriors for two hypotheses: 
\underbrace{\frac{P(H\mid E)}{P(H^c\mid E)}}_{\text{posterior odds}}
= \underbrace{\frac{P(E\mid H)}{P(E\mid H^c)}}_{\text{likelihood ratio}}
\times
\underbrace{\frac{P(H)}{P(H^c)}}_{\text{prior odds}}.
 The normaliser P(E) cancels. For the cancer test: prior odds = 0.001/0.999 \approx 1/999, likelihood ratio = 0.99/0.05 = 19.8, so posterior odds \approx 19.8/999 \approx 0.0198, i.e. probability 0.0198/(1+0.0198) \approx 0.0194 — the same \approx 1.9\%. This form makes the prosecutor’s fallacy obvious: a large likelihood ratio is worthless evidence of H if the prior odds are tiny.
Continuous (Bayesian) form. For a parameter \theta with prior density p(\theta) and likelihood p(\text{data}\mid\theta), 
p(\theta \mid \text{data}) = \frac{p(\text{data}\mid\theta)\,p(\theta)}{\displaystyle\int p(\text{data}\mid\theta')\,p(\theta')\,d\theta'} \;\propto\; p(\text{data}\mid\theta)\,p(\theta).
 The integral in the denominator is what makes the posterior a genuine distribution — and is exactly the quantity that is hard to compute in practice, motivating the MCMC methods Lambert develops in the Bayesian chapter.

title: Bayes’ Theorem: Updating Beliefs
text:
Connections

Builds on: Conditional probability & independence — the multiplication rule and the law of total probability are Bayes’ two ingredients.
Leads to: the Bayesian-inference chapter (Lambert), where priors, likelihoods, and posteriors become a full method, and to honest treatment of diagnostic and forensic evidence.
Contrasts with: the frequentist machinery of Part II — same rules of probability, a different philosophy of what a probability attaches to.

title: Bayes’ Theorem: Updating Beliefs
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Derive Bayes’ theorem from the multiplication rule and the law of total probability, and define prior, likelihood, posterior, and marginal likelihood.”
“Work through Grinstead & Snell’s rare-disease example in full, both with the formula and with natural frequencies out of 100,000 people, and explain the base-rate fallacy.”
“Explain the prosecutor’s fallacy using Albers’ transposed-conditional idea, and give a legal or medical example where confusing P(E\mid H) with P(H\mid E) causes harm.”
“Summarise how Lambert introduces Bayesian updating with the coastguard and fish-game examples, and explain why he says the likelihood is not a probability distribution.”

title: The Cast of Characters: Kinds of Variables
text: A variable is never just “a thing you measured.” In any study it plays a role in a story about cause and effect — the suspected cause, the outcome, the lurking troublemaker, the mechanism in between. Learn the cast now and you’ll read every regression, every causal design, and every confounding warning in this manual with the right characters already in mind.
crumbs: The Cast of Characters: Kinds of Variables

title: The Cast of Characters: Kinds of Variables
text:
Why it matters
The single most important question in social science is whether a relationship between two things is real and causal or a mirage created by something else. You cannot even ask that question clearly until you can name the roles variables play. This entry installs that vocabulary — and plants the seed of the whole manual: the climb from description to association to causation.
crumbs: The Cast of Characters: Kinds of Variables

title: The Cast of Characters: Kinds of Variables
text:
The cast
Independent variable (IV) — the presumed cause, the thing whose effect you’re studying (Martin and Bridgmon 2012). Martin & Bridgmon make a crucial distinction:

Active IVs are manipulated by the researcher (a drug dose, a treatment assignment).
Attribute IVs are pre-existing traits you can’t manipulate (age, gender, nationality).

That difference quietly governs what you may conclude — you can randomise an active variable; you can only observe an attribute one, which is why so much of causal inference (Part V) is about coping with attribute variables you couldn’t assign.
Dependent variable (DV) — the presumed outcome, measured in response to the IV (Martin and Bridgmon 2012). The “effect” half of the story.
Control variables — things you deliberately hold constant or statistically account for, to stop them muddying the IV→DV relationship (Bryman 2016). (Calling something a “control” is a claim that it matters and that you’ve handled it — not a free default.)
Confounding / extraneous variables — the troublemakers: contaminating factors that act like an IV and confuse the relationship you care about (Martin and Bridgmon 2012). Left unhandled, they manufacture a spurious relationship — IV and DV look related only because a third variable drives both (Bryman 2016). (Ice-cream sales and drownings rise together; summer causes both.)
Mediating (intervening) variables — the mechanism: the variable that explains why a relationship exists (Bryman 2016). The cause works through it:
\text{Income} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Political attitudes} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Voting behaviour}
Income doesn’t reach into the ballot box directly; it shapes attitudes, which shape the vote.
Moderating variables — the condition: a variable that changes the nature or strength of the IV→DV relationship (Bryman 2016). Bryman’s example: gender moderates the relationship between age and exercise — the age–exercise pattern for men differs from that for women. The relationship isn’t one thing; it depends.






TipMediator vs moderator — don’t confuse them



A mediator answers why/how (it’s a link in the causal chain: A→M→B). A moderator answers for whom / under what conditions (it dials the A→B relationship up or down). “Stress causes illness via a weakened immune system” is mediation; “stress harms illness more for people with weak support networks” is moderation.
crumbs: The Cast of Characters: Kinds of Variables

title: The Cast of Characters: Kinds of Variables
text:
The ladder this builds toward
These roles let us state the manual’s central progression precisely:

Description — what a single variable looks like (Part I).
Association — two variables move together; correlation (Part I) and regression (Part III) measure it.
Causation — the IV genuinely produces change in the DV, with confounders ruled out (Part V, the summit).

Every confounder you can name now is an obstacle the causal-inference chapters will teach you to defeat. The whole arc, in one sentence: learn to tell a real IV→DV effect from a spurious one manufactured by a confounder.
crumbs: The Cast of Characters: Kinds of Variables

title: The Cast of Characters: Kinds of Variables
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




“Control variable” as reflex. Throwing variables into a model as “controls” without an argument can create bias (e.g. controlling for a mediator hides the very effect you want). Role-naming must come before model-building.
Confusing a mediator with a confounder. Both sit “in between,” but a mediator is part of the causal pathway (don’t control it away if you want the total effect); a confounder is off to the side causing both (you must handle it). Same statistics, opposite meaning.
Reading association as causation. A spurious relationship is, statistically, a perfectly real correlation. The numbers can’t tell you which it is — your design and argument must.
Forgetting attribute IVs can’t be randomised. Conclusions about gender, race, or age effects carry caveats that conclusions about assigned treatments don’t.
crumbs: The Cast of Characters: Kinds of Variables

title: The Cast of Characters: Kinds of Variables
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Martin & Bridgmon, Quantitative and Statistical Research Methods — independent (active vs attribute) and dependent variables; extraneous/confounding variables as contaminating “noise.”
Bryman, Social Research Methods — control variables; spurious relationships from a third variable; mediating (intervening) variables (income→attitudes→voting); moderating variables (gender moderating age and exercise).
crumbs: The Cast of Characters: Kinds of Variables

title: The Cast of Characters: Kinds of Variables
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Label every variable’s role before you model. Write “IV / DV / confounder / mediator / moderator” next to each. Most analysis mistakes are role-confusions in disguise.
Draw the arrows. A quick diagram of who-causes-whom (the seed of a DAG, coming in Part V) instantly reveals what to control and what to leave alone.
Treat every strong correlation as guilty until proven innocent. Ask “what third variable could produce both?” before believing a relationship is causal.
Decide mediators on purpose. If you want the total effect of the IV, don’t control its mediators; if you want the direct effect, do. Know which you’re after.
crumbs: The Cast of Characters: Kinds of Variables

title: The Cast of Characters: Kinds of Variables
text:
Connections

Builds on: Concepts & measurement — you can only assign roles to variables you’ve actually measured.
Leads to: A field guide to research designs — designs are strategies for handling confounders so an IV→DV claim survives.
Pays off in: Regression (“holding constant” = controlling) and, above all, Causal Inference, where defeating confounders is the whole game. ## Study with NotebookLM {.unnumbered}


“Define independent, dependent, control, confounding, mediating, and moderating variables, each with a fresh social-science example, from these sources.”
“Explain the difference between a mediator and a confounder, and why it changes whether you should control for the variable.”
“What is a spurious relationship, and what example do the sources use?”
“Explain active vs attribute independent variables and why the distinction matters for what you can conclude.”
crumbs: The Cast of Characters: Kinds of Variables

title: 0 · Overture: How Research Works
text:
A newcomer who jumps straight into probability learns tools without a job. This overture gives the job first: what quantitative social science is trying to do, and the shape of a study from question to credible conclusion. It is deliberately light on technique and heavy on framing, vocabulary, and judgement — the scaffolding everything technical hangs on.
It plants three seeds the whole manual grows:

title: 0 · Overture: How Research Works
text:
Where this leads
Straight into Part I · Foundations: Probability & Data — the grammar of uncertainty that every later technique is written in.

title: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails
text: Good quantitative research is consistent (reliable), accurate (valid), and reproducible (replicable). When those slip — through selective analysis, hidden choices, and unpublished null results — you get the replication crisis: confident findings that vanish when someone reruns them. This entry is the manual’s conscience, and the reason the chapters ahead are so insistent about rigour.
crumbs: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails

title: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails
text:
Why it matters
It is sobering, and clarifying, to learn up front that many celebrated findings in social science don’t replicate. That fact reframes everything that follows: the point of learning statistics rigorously is not to produce significant results, but to produce trustworthy ones. Knowing how research fails is half of knowing how to do it well.
crumbs: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails

title: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails
text:
The criteria for credible research
Three standards, building on the measurement and design ideas you’ve already met (Bryman 2016):

Reliability — consistency. A reliable measure gives the same result on repeated trials: stability (steady over time), internal reliability (a scale’s indicators cohere), and inter-rater reliability (coders agree).
Validity — accuracy (the most important criterion):

Measurement validity — the indicator really reflects the concept (does an IQ test measure intelligence?).
Internal validity — the causal claim is genuine (X truly caused Y, not an unobserved confounder); randomised experiments are the benchmark.
External validity — the result generalises beyond the study’s context.

Replicability vs replication — a distinction worth keeping sharp:

Replicability = the procedures are reported clearly enough that someone could repeat the study.
Replication = someone actually reruns it and checks the findings hold across new samples or times.
crumbs: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails

title: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails
section: The replication crisis — and what causes it
text:
The replication crisis — and what causes it
The replication crisis is the discovery that many widely accepted findings fail to reproduce when rerun (Bryman 2016). Bryman names the mechanisms — and each is a preview of a battle fought later in this manual:

p-hacking — selectively analysing data until something crosses significance. (Made rigorous and exposed in Part II’s hypothesis-testing material.)
The “garden of forking paths” — many individually defensible analytic choices (which cases to drop, which controls to include) lead to different results; flexibility masquerades as discovery.
Publication bias / the “file drawer” — null results go unpublished, so the literature over-represents flukes that happened to reach significance.







WarningThe uncomfortable implication



A single significant result, from a single study, with undisclosed analytic choices, is weak evidence — even when the statistics are flawless. Credibility comes from transparency and repetition, not from a small p-value. Hold this thought; the rest of the manual earns it.
crumbs: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails

title: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Mistaking statistical significance for truth. Significance is a filter that flukes also pass. Replication is the real test.
Reporting too little to be replicable. If no one can rerun your analysis from your description, it isn’t science yet — it’s an anecdote with numbers.
Letting forking paths stay hidden. Every “we tried it this way” you don’t disclose inflates your apparent certainty.
Treating null results as failures. Unpublished nulls are how the literature lies by omission.
crumbs: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails

title: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bryman, Social Research Methods — reliability (stability/internal/inter-rater); validity (measurement/internal/external); the replicability-vs-replication distinction; the replication crisis and its causes (p-hacking, the garden of forking paths, publication bias / the file drawer).
crumbs: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails

title: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Pre-register when you can. Committing to your analysis before seeing the data is the cheapest defence against p-hacking and forking paths.
Write for the replicator. Document every decision so a stranger could reproduce your result — that discipline also catches your own errors.
Report the nulls. Your “it didn’t work” is someone else’s saved year.
Distrust single studies, including your own. Treat a lone significant finding as a hypothesis, not a conclusion. ## Connections {.unnumbered}
Builds on: measurement (reliability/validity) and research designs (internal/external validity).
Leads to: ethics, the other pillar of doing research responsibly.
Pays off in: Part II · Inference — where p-hacking, power, and “the replication crisis” get their full, rigorous treatment — and Part VI, the mature reckoning with metrics and judgement. ## Study with NotebookLM {.unnumbered}


“Distinguish reliability, the three validity types, replicability, and replication, each with an example, from these sources.”
“Explain the replication crisis and its causes — p-hacking, the garden of forking paths, and publication bias — in plain language.”
“What is the ‘file drawer problem’ and how does it distort the published literature?”
“What practical steps (pre-registration, transparency) do the sources suggest for more credible research?”
crumbs: What Makes Research Credible — and How It Fails

title: The Anatomy of a Study
text: A quantitative study is a pipeline: theory → question → hypothesis → design → measurement → data → analysis → conclusion. Every technique in this manual is a tool for one of these steps. Learn the skeleton first, and each later chapter becomes “ah, that’s the part that does step 5.”

title: The Anatomy of a Study
text:
Why it matters
People drown in methods because they learn them as a disconnected toolbox. But methods aren’t a pile — they’re stations on an assembly line. Once you can see the whole line, you always know where you are and what a given technique is for. This entry is that map; the rest of the book fills in each station.

title: The Anatomy of a Study
section: Intuition: research is a sequence, and (almost) everyone agrees on it
text:
Intuition: research is a sequence, and (almost) everyone agrees on it
Two of our sources lay out the sequence explicitly, and they agree far more than they differ.
Bryman’s process of quantitative research — the canonical 12 steps (Bryman 2016):

Theory — the starting point.
Research question(s) — what, precisely, you want to know.
Hypothesis — deduced from theory (sometimes skipped in exploratory work).
Research design — experiment, cross-section, longitudinal, comparative…
Devise measures of concepts — operationalisation.
Select research site(s).
Select respondents/subjects — your sample.
Collect the data — administer the instruments.
Process the data — turn responses into numbers.
Analyse the data — the statistics.
Findings / conclusions.
Write up.

Martin & Bridgmon’s structure — the same arc, tilted toward the statistics (Martin and Bridgmon 2012):

State the problem; identify questions/hypotheses.
Identify, classify, and operationally define the variables.
Choose the research design.
A priori power analysis — work out the sample size you need before collecting data.
Choose the appropriate statistic.
Collect and analyse the data set.
Data screening and analysis (e.g. in SPSS).
Interpret the statistics.
Write the results.







TipRead the two lists together



Bryman emphasises the social-science front end (theory, sites, respondents); Martin & Bridgmon emphasise the statistical spine (operational definitions, power analysis, choosing the right test). The honest model is the union: a study earns its credibility by doing both well. Note step 4 in Martin & Bridgmon — deciding sample size before you collect — is the single most-skipped step in real research, and a recurring villain later in the manual.

title: The Anatomy of a Study
section: A worked study: does therapy reduce adolescent depression?
text:
A worked study: does therapy reduce adolescent depression?
Martin & Bridgmon walk through a study of 145 adolescents that runs the entire pipeline end to end (Martin and Bridgmon 2012) — a perfect first sight of the cycle:

Problem & question. Which is more effective at reducing depressive symptoms — Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy (CBT), Interpersonal Therapy (IPT), or no treatment (control)?
Hypotheses. A null hypothesis H_0: the three groups don’t differ. An alternative: they do.
Design. Three groups, an outcome measured the same way in each.
Power / sample size. Enough adolescents (145) to give the test a fair chance of detecting a real difference.
Choose the statistic. A one-way ANOVA (the right tool for comparing three group means).
Collect & analyse. Run the data through the test.
Decide. The result was p = .003. Because that is below the chosen threshold \alpha = .05, they reject the null and conclude the therapies have genuinely different effects.

That final move — comparing a p-value to a threshold to decide — is the heartbeat of classical inference. You’ll meet it properly in Part II; here, just notice where in the pipeline it sits: step 10 of Bryman, step 8 of Martin & Bridgmon. The statistic is one station, not the whole journey.

title: The Anatomy of a Study
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Mistaking the list for reality. The steps look linear; real studies loop (you revise the question after a pilot, re-operationalise after bad data). The line is a plan, not a promise.
Treating analysis as the whole study. Beginners obsess over step 10 (the statistics) and neglect steps 1–5, where studies are actually won or lost. A perfect ANOVA on a badly operationalised concept is a precise wrong answer.
Skipping the a priori power analysis. Deciding sample size after seeing the data — or not at all — is how underpowered studies and false findings are born.
Letting “write up” hide the messy middle. The clean published pipeline rarely shows the inductive loops that actually happened. Honesty about them is part of rigour.

title: The Anatomy of a Study
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bryman, Social Research Methods — the 12-step process of quantitative research.
Martin & Bridgmon, Quantitative and Statistical Research Methods — the 9-step structure (with the a priori power-analysis step); the 145-adolescent CBT/IPT depression study run end to end, including the p = .003 vs \alpha = .05 decision.

title: The Anatomy of a Study
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Locate yourself on the pipeline, always. When stuck, ask “which step am I on?” Confusion usually means you’ve smuggled a later step’s question into an earlier step.
Do step 5 before step 7. Operationalise your concepts before you choose your sample; you can’t size a study to measure something you haven’t defined.
Run the power analysis as a design check, not a formality. If the honest sample size is unaffordable, you’ve learned something now — before wasting the data collection.
Keep a “loop log.” Note every time reality made you revise an earlier step. It’s the difference between a reproducible study and a story.

title: The Anatomy of a Study
text:
Connections

Builds on: What quantitative social science does — the strategy this pipeline operationalises.
Expands at: measurement (step 5) and research designs (step 4), the next Overture entries.
Leads to: Part II · Inference — steps 9–10, where the p < \alpha decision from the depression study is made rigorous (and its many abuses exposed).

title: The Anatomy of a Study
text:
Study with NotebookLM

title: Appendix · Computing
text: The source library is R-first (Dayal’s two volumes, Explanatory Statistical Modeling in R, ISLR’s labs), so this encyclopedia treats R as the default working language for social science. Python appears where it is the natural choice — simulations, machine learning, data wrangling at scale — and Quarto runs both engines in the same project without friction.

title: Appendix · Computing
text:
Already live
The flagship entry includes an executable Python simulation of the Central Limit Theorem (folded by default). It runs on the project’s .venv with numpy and matplotlib, and is the template for how live code appears throughout: short, seeded, and illustrating exactly one idea. See Sampling distributions & the CLT.

title: Order Statistics
text: Take n random draws and sort them. The sorted values — the order statistics — have their own distributions, and they answer questions the raw draws can’t: how big is the largest of n floods? how small the weakest link? where does the median sit? The minimum and maximum are easy (one line each); the general k-th value follows a clean combinatorial formula; and the whole apparatus underwrites quantiles, reliability theory, and rank-based tests.

title: Order Statistics
text:
Definition
Given n i.i.d. continuous variables X_1,\dots,X_n, the order statistics are the same values sorted into increasing order: (Ross 2018) 
X_{(1)} \le X_{(2)} \le \cdots \le X_{(n)}.
 So X_{(1)} is the minimum, X_{(n)} the maximum, and X_{(j)} the j-th smallest. For an odd sample of size 2n+1, the middle value X_{(n+1)} is the sample median. (Ross 2018) Sorting discards which unit gave which value but keeps the full picture of spread and extremes.

title: Order Statistics
section: Minimum and maximum: the easy ones
text:
Minimum and maximum: the easy ones
The extremes follow from one observation about independence: (Ross 2018)

The maximum is \le x iff every draw is \le x, so 
F_{X_{(n)}}(x) = \Pr\{X_{(n)}\le x\} = [F(x)]^n.

The minimum is >x iff every draw is >x, so \Pr\{X_{(1)}>x\}=[1-F(x)]^n and hence 
F_{X_{(1)}}(x) = 1 - [1-F(x)]^n.


A famous consequence: the minimum of n independent Exponential(\lambda) variables is itself Exponential(n\lambda) — n competing memoryless clocks fail n times as fast. (Ross 2018) (That’s the Poisson-process superposition seen through the first arrival.)

title: Order Statistics
text:
The general k-th order statistic
For the value in position j, Ross gives an intuitive derivation. For X_{(j)}=x, you need exactly: j-1 values below x, n-j values above x, and one equal to x. (Ross 2018) Counting the ways to assign the n draws to those three roles (a multinomial coefficient) gives the density: 
f_{X_{(j)}}(x) = \frac{n!}{(j-1)!\,(n-j)!}\,[F(x)]^{\,j-1}\,[1-F(x)]^{\,n-j}\,f(x).
 (Ross 2018) Worked example: for n=3 draws from a Uniform(0,1), the median X_{(2)} has density f(x)=6x(1-x), giving \Pr\{\tfrac14<X_{(2)}<\tfrac34\}=\tfrac{11}{16}. (Ross 2018)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from math import factorial

n = 5
x = np.linspace(0, 1, 400)

def beta_pdf(x, a, b):                  # Beta(a,b) density by hand (a,b integers)
    coef = factorial(a + b - 1) / (factorial(a - 1) * factorial(b - 1))
    return coef * x**(a - 1) * (1 - x)**(b - 1)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.4, 4.0))
labels = ["min  $X_{(1)}$", "$X_{(2)}$", "median  $X_{(3)}$", "$X_{(4)}$", "max  $X_{(5)}$"]
colors = ["#c0392b", "#9a6a2f", "#1f3a5f", "#3f8f6f", "#7a3f8f"]
for k in range(1, n + 1):
    ax.plot(x, beta_pdf(x, k, n - k + 1), color=colors[k-1], lw=2.2, label=labels[k-1])
ax.set_xlabel("value"); ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("Each order statistic of a uniform sample is a Beta distribution")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Order-statistic densities for n = 5 uniform draws. The k-th order statistic of a Uniform(0,1) sample is a Beta(k, n−k+1) distribution. The minimum (k=1) piles up near 0, the maximum (k=5) near 1, and the median (k=3) is symmetric and concentrated in the middle — each ‘slot’ of the sorted sample has its own predictable shape. Sorting turns one flat distribution into n distinct ones.

title: Order Statistics
text:
Range and spacings
Order statistics quantify a sample’s spread: (Ross 2018)

The sample range R = X_{(n)} - X_{(1)} has \Pr\{R\le a\}=n\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}[F(x+a)-F(x)]^{n-1}f(x)\,dx. For a Uniform(0,1) sample, R is Beta(n-1,2) with density n(n-1)a^{n-2}(1-a). (Ross 2018)
The spacings Y_i = X_{(i)}-X_{(i-1)} are the gaps between consecutive sorted values; for the uniform they are exchangeable (their joint law is symmetric). (Ross 2018)

title: Order Statistics
text:
Why it matters
Order statistics are the theory beneath several things this book uses constantly: (Ross 2018)

Median & quantiles. They give the mathematics of the median, quartiles, percentiles — the robust summaries — without assuming any distributional form.
Reliability / extremes. A series system fails when its weakest link does (X_{(1)}); a parallel system survives as long as its strongest component holds (X_{(n)}); a k-out-of-n system works if at least k components do — modelled by X_{(n-k+1)}. (Ross 2018) This is the math of floods, fatigue, and record values.
Rank-based statistics. Nonparametric tests like the Wilcoxon rank-sum use the ordering of the data, not the raw values — order statistics are what make them distribution-free. (Ross 2018)

title: Order Statistics
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Treating the max like a typical value. X_{(n)} has its own (right-shifted) distribution; the expected maximum of n draws grows with n and is nothing like the mean. (Ross 2018)
Forgetting order statistics are dependent. X_{(1)}\le X_{(2)}\le\cdots by construction, so they are not independent even when the raw draws are. (Ross 2018)
Assuming the simple formulas without continuity / independence. The clean CDFs assume i.i.d. continuous draws (ties have probability 0). (Ross 2018)

title: Order Statistics
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — order statistics X_{(1)}\le\cdots\le X_{(n)} (min, max, median); the extreme CDFs [F(x)]^n and 1-[1-F(x)]^n (and the min of exponentials being Exponential(n\lambda)); the k-th-order-statistic density via the “j-1 below, n-j above, one equal” multinomial logic (the n=3 uniform median example, 11/16); the range (Beta(n-1,2) for the uniform) and spacings (exchangeable); and the applications to quantiles, reliability (series/parallel/k-out-of-n), rank tests, and records (Ignatov’s theorem).

title: Order Statistics
text:
Connections

Builds on: random variables & CDFs and transformations.
Underwrites: location & quantiles, rank-based / nonparametric tests, and extreme-value / reliability analysis.
Pairs with: the Poisson process (first-arrival = minimum) and the Beta distribution (the law of uniform order statistics).

The Poisson process is the continuous-time engine behind count data and survival/hazard models — its interarrival times are the exponential durations of survival analysis.

title: How to use this
text: A reusable, growing reference for becoming a quantitative social science researcher. It is opinionated about pedagogy: intuition and use-cases lead, mathematics follows only where it clarifies a principle or a deviation, and the full derivations live in appendices. The goal is a reader who can move from undergraduate foundations to the academic rigour of causal research and the realities of field and client work.

title: How to use this
text:
The learning loop
This site is one half of a loop. The other half is NotebookLM, where the full source library lives. The intended cycle:

Read an article here for the structured, intuition-first synthesis.
Interrogate the primary sources in NotebookLM using the Study with NotebookLM prompts at the foot of each article — get audio overviews, ask “explain this with a different example,” or push on the math.
Return and let the article be your map; NotebookLM is the territory.


Why this split? The website is the scaffold for rigorous engagement — a logical sequence and a consistent lens. NotebookLM is the engagement itself — grounded, conversational, source-cited. Neither replaces the other.


The source library
The companion NotebookLM holds 22 canonical sources, including:

title: How to use this
section: The house style (for contributors — including future-me)
text:
The house style (for contributors — including future-me)
Articles are written to a fixed template so the encyclopedia stays coherent as it grows:

In one sentence — a single italic intuition kernel under the title.
Why it matters — the motivating use-case before any notation.
Intuition — plain language, concrete examples, analogies that hold up.
Worked examples — taken from the source library, with real studies where possible.
The math, where it earns its place — inline and minimal; if a derivation is longer than the idea it supports, it goes to the appendix.
Common pitfalls — how the concept is misused in practice (the Statistics Done Wrong lens is a recurring guest).
From the sources — explicit per-source attribution.
Appendix · the full mathematics — wrapped in a tinted block the reader may skip.
Connections — links up and down the curriculum.
Study with NotebookLM — 3–5 prompts to take back to the sources.

Math uses common sense first and proof second: notation is introduced because the prose demanded it, never to decorate.

title: How to use this
text:
Building locally
# Render the whole site
quarto render

# Live-preview while editing
