Executable code blocks (e.g. simulations) run on a Python virtual environment in .venv/ with numpy and matplotlib. R support can be added per-document later — Quarto handles both engines in the same project.

title: Markov Chains
text: A Markov chain is a system that moves between states where “the future depends on the present, but not the past.” Knowing where you are now is enough; the route that got you here is irrelevant. That one assumption — the Markov property — makes the whole future computable from a single transition matrix, and leads to a beautiful result: run many chains long enough and they converge to the same stationary distribution, no matter where they started. That convergence is the engine under MCMC.

title: Markov Chains
text:
The Markov property
A Markov chain \{X_n\} takes values in a set of states, and obeys: (Ross 2014) 
\Pr\{X_{n+1}=j \mid X_n=i, X_{n-1}=i_{n-1},\dots,X_0=i_0\} = \Pr\{X_{n+1}=j\mid X_n=i\} = P_{ij}.
 In words — “the future depends on the present, not the past.” (Ross 2014) The entire history collapses to the current state. That’s a strong assumption, but a stunning number of systems satisfy it (or can be made to by enlarging the state), and it’s what makes the chain tractable.

title: Markov Chains
text:
The transition matrix
All the dynamics live in the transition probability matrix \mathbf P, whose entry P_{ij} is the probability of moving from state i to state j in one step. (Ross 2014) Each row is a probability distribution: P_{ij}\ge 0 and \sum_j P_{ij}=1 (from any state you must go somewhere). (Ross 2014)
Multi-step transitions are just matrix powers. The Chapman–Kolmogorov equations, P^{n+m}_{ij}=\sum_k P^{n}_{ik}P^{m}_{kj}, say that to get from i to j in n+m steps you pass through some intermediate state k — and they imply the clean rule (Ross 2014) 
\mathbf P^{(n)} = \mathbf P^{\,n}.
 The n-step transition matrix is the one-step matrix raised to the n-th power. Want the state distribution after n steps? Multiply your starting distribution by \mathbf P^n.

title: Markov Chains
text:
Classifying states
Not all states behave alike: (Ross 2014)

Recurrent vs transient. A state is recurrent if, starting there, you return to it with probability 1; transient if there’s positive probability you never come back. In a finite chain, at least one state must be recurrent. (Ross 2014)
Periodicity. A state has period d if returns are only possible at multiples of d; period 1 is aperiodic. (Ross 2014)

These distinctions decide whether the chain settles down.

title: Markov Chains
text:
The stationary distribution
The payoff. For well-behaved chains, the probability of being in state j after many steps converges to a limit \pi_j that does not depend on where you started. (Ross 2014) If the chain is irreducible (you can get from any state to any other) and ergodic (aperiodic and positive recurrent), it converges to a unique stationary distribution \pi, the solution of (Ross 2014) 
\pi_j = \sum_i \pi_i\,P_{ij}, \qquad \sum_j \pi_j = 1.
 \pi is the distribution that reproduces itself under one more step (\pi\mathbf P=\pi) — an equilibrium the chain forgets its way toward.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A 3-state transition matrix (e.g. sunny/cloudy/rainy)
P = np.array([[0.7, 0.2, 0.1],
              [0.3, 0.4, 0.3],
              [0.2, 0.3, 0.5]])

# Stationary distribution: left eigenvector with eigenvalue 1
vals, vecs = np.linalg.eig(P.T)
pi = np.real(vecs[:, np.argmin(np.abs(vals - 1))]); pi = pi / pi.sum()

steps = 12
start_a = np.array([1.0, 0.0, 0.0])
start_b = np.array([0.0, 0.0, 1.0])
def evolve(d):
    out = [d]
    for _ in range(steps):
        d = d @ P; out.append(d)
    return np.array(out)
A, B = evolve(start_a), evolve(start_b)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.4, 4.0))
colors = ["#1f3a5f", "#3f8f6f", "#9a6a2f"]
for s in range(3):
    ax.plot(A[:, s], "-o", color=colors[s], ms=3, lw=1.6, label=f"state {s} (start A)")
    ax.plot(B[:, s], "--s", color=colors[s], ms=3, lw=1.2, alpha=0.7)
    ax.axhline(pi[s], color=colors[s], ls=":", lw=1.0)
ax.set_xlabel("step n"); ax.set_ylabel("probability of each state")
ax.set_title("Two different starts → the same stationary distribution")
ax.legend(fontsize=7.5, frameon=False, ncol=3, loc="upper center")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Forgetting the start. A 3-state weather chain run from two very different initial distributions (one starts certain of state 0, the other certain of state 2). Both distributions evolve under repeated multiplication by P and converge to the SAME stationary distribution π (dashed) within a handful of steps. Irreducible + aperiodic chains lose all memory of where they began — the fact MCMC exploits.

title: Markov Chains
text:
Signature examples
Ross’s classics: (Ross 2014)

Weather in the “Land of Oz” — a chain over {rain, nice, snow}, predicting tomorrow from today.
Random walks — a particle stepping \pm 1 on a line; the symmetric walk (p=0.5) is recurrent (returns to the origin with probability 1), a surprisingly delicate fact.
Gambler’s ruin — bet $1 repeatedly until you hit $0 (broke) or $N (goal): a chain with two absorbing states, and the question is the probability of each fate.

title: Markov Chains
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Assuming the Markov property holds. If the future genuinely depends on more than the present, enlarge the state (e.g. include yesterday) — or it isn’t a Markov chain. (Ross 2014)
Expecting convergence without the conditions. A periodic or reducible chain may never settle to a unique \pi; check irreducibility and aperiodicity. (Ross 2014)
Confusing a stationary distribution with an absorbing state. Gambler’s ruin absorbs; it doesn’t mix to an interior equilibrium. (Ross 2014)
Reading \pi as “where the chain is.” \pi is the long-run distribution over states, not a single destination — the chain keeps moving, visiting state j a fraction \pi_j of the time.

title: Markov Chains
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, Introduction to Probability Models — the Markov property (“future depends on the present, not the past”); the transition matrix P_{ij} (rows sum to 1); Chapman–Kolmogorov and \mathbf P^{(n)}=\mathbf P^n; recurrent vs transient states and periodicity; the stationary / limiting distribution \pi=\pi\mathbf P under irreducibility + ergodicity; and the Land of Oz weather, random walk (symmetric → recurrent), and gambler’s ruin examples.

title: Markov Chains
text:
Connections

Builds on: conditional probability (the transition probabilities) and matrix algebra.
Powers: MCMC — Markov chain Monte Carlo constructs a chain whose stationary distribution is the Bayesian posterior, then samples by running it.
Cousin of: the Poisson process (its continuous-time relative) and the state-space view of time series.

title: The Poisson Process
text: Customers entering a bank, radioactive atoms decaying, earthquakes striking: events that pop up at random in continuous time. The Poisson process is the canonical model — a process that, at every instant, “probabilistically restarts itself.” Count its events in any window and you get a Poisson distribution; measure the gaps between events and you get the exponential — the unique memoryless waiting time. One process ties those two distributions together.

title: The Poisson Process
text:
Counting processes
A counting process \{N(t),\,t\ge 0\} records the total number of events that have occurred up to time t. (Ross 2014) The Poisson process of rate \lambda>0 is the most important one, pinned down by two intuitive properties: (Ross 2014)

Independent increments — counts in disjoint intervals are independent. What happens between 2:00 and 3:00 tells you nothing about 5:00–6:00.
Stationary increments — the distribution of the count in an interval depends only on its length, not when it sits. A one-hour window is statistically the same at noon or midnight.

From these, the number of events in an interval of length t is Poisson with mean \lambda t: (Ross 2014) 
\Pr\{N(t)=n\} = e^{-\lambda t}\,\frac{(\lambda t)^n}{n!}, \qquad n=0,1,2,\dots
 The rate \lambda is events per unit time; over a window of length t you expect \lambda t of them.

title: The Poisson Process
section: Interarrival times are exponential — and that’s no accident
text:
Interarrival times are exponential — and that’s no accident
Look at the gaps between successive events — the interarrival times T_1, T_2, \dots. Ross’s result: they are independent and identically distributed Exponential(\lambda), each with mean 1/\lambda. (Ross 2014) This is the process’s deepest fact, and it follows from a single idea: stationary + independent increments mean that “at any point in time, the process probabilistically restarts itself.” (Ross 2014) Since the exponential is the unique memoryless continuous distribution, it is the only law a restart-anywhere process can have — the time to the next event cannot depend on how long you’ve already waited. (Ross 2014) (This is exactly the constant-hazard, exponential-survival baseline, seen from the other side.)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from math import factorial

rng = np.random.default_rng(2)
lam = 3.0
T = 20.0
# Generate arrivals by summing Exponential interarrival times
gaps = rng.exponential(1/lam, size=int(lam*T*1.5))
times = np.cumsum(gaps)
times = times[times < T]

fig, (axT, axB) = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(7.6, 5.0))

axT.step(np.concatenate([[0], times]), np.arange(len(times)+1), where="post", color="#1f3a5f", lw=1.6)
axT.plot(times, np.zeros_like(times), "|", color="#c0392b", markersize=12, markeredgewidth=1.5)
axT.set_xlim(0, T); axT.set_xlabel("time  t"); axT.set_ylabel("N(t)")
axT.set_title(f"Counting process N(t), rate λ={lam}  (red ticks = events)")

# Counts in fixed windows of width w
w = 1.0
edges = np.arange(0, T+w, w)
counts = np.histogram(times, bins=edges)[0]
ks = np.arange(0, counts.max()+2)
pmf = np.exp(-lam*w) * (lam*w)**ks / np.array([factorial(k) for k in ks])
axB.hist(counts, bins=np.arange(-0.5, counts.max()+1.5), density=True, color="#9bb4d4",
         alpha=0.85, label="observed per-window counts")
axB.plot(ks, pmf, "o-", color="#c0392b", lw=1.8, label=f"Poisson(λw={lam*w:g}) pmf")
axB.set_xlabel("events per window"); axB.set_ylabel("proportion")
axB.set_title("Counts per fixed window are Poisson")
axB.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A Poisson process of rate λ. Top: event times (ticks) scatter at random; the step function N(t) counts them. The gaps between ticks are i.i.d. Exponential(λ). Bottom: counting events in fixed windows produces a Poisson(λ·width) distribution — here a histogram of per-window counts against the Poisson pmf (red). Same process, two faces: exponential gaps, Poisson counts.

title: The Poisson Process
text:
Superposition and thinning
Two operations make the Poisson process beautifully closed: (Ross 2014)

Superposition. Merge two independent Poisson processes with rates \lambda_1 and \lambda_2 and the combined stream is Poisson with rate \lambda_1+\lambda_2. (All the arrivals from two doors into one shop.)
Thinning. Independently tag each event “Type I” with probability p or “Type II” with 1-p; the two sub-streams are independent Poisson processes with rates \lambda p and \lambda(1-p). (Ross 2014) (Split arrivals into “web” vs “phone” and each is still Poisson.)

These closure rules are why the Poisson process is the default model whenever independent streams combine or split.

title: The Poisson Process
text:
Where it shows up
Ross names the Poisson process the standard model for “rare events” and arrivals: radioactive \alpha-particle emissions, earthquakes, the outbreak of wars, and customers entering a bank or post office. (Ross 2014) In this book it is the continuous-time engine beneath count-data models (a Poisson regression models the rate \lambda) and survival analysis (the exponential interarrival time is the constant-hazard duration).

title: The Poisson Process
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Assuming Poisson when events clump. Independent increments fail if one event makes the next more likely (“contagion”) — that’s overdispersion; use a different process / negative binomial. (Ross 2014)
Forgetting the memoryless catch. “It’s been quiet, so an arrival is overdue” is the gambler’s fallacy in continuous time — the exponential has no memory. (Ross 2014)
Mismatching rate and window. The count is Poisson(\lambda t), not Poisson(\lambda); keep the time units straight (the offset issue). (Ross 2014)

title: The Poisson Process
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, Introduction to Probability Models — the counting process N(t) and the Poisson process of rate \lambda (independent & stationary increments; N(t)\sim\text{Poisson}(\lambda t)); interarrival times i.i.d. Exponential(\lambda) and the “probabilistically restarts itself” / memoryless link; superposition (\lambda_1+\lambda_2) and thinning (\lambda p); and the rare-event examples (radioactive decay, earthquakes, wars, arrivals).

title: The Poisson Process
text:
Connections

Builds on: the Poisson & exponential distributions and memorylessness.
Powers: count-data / Poisson regression (modelling the rate) and the constant-hazard core of survival analysis.
Generalises to: non-homogeneous and compound Poisson processes, and the continuous-time Markov chains of the next entry.

Worked example, already live
The flagship entry’s appendix derives the variance of the sample mean and sketches the characteristic-function proof of the CLT — a model of the level of detail this section targets. See Sampling distributions & the CLT → Appendix.

title: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement
text: You cannot measure “trust,” “depression,” or “social class” directly. You measure a stand-in you’ve defined — and the whole study inherits the quality of that choice. Measurement is the hinge between an idea and a number: get it right and the statistics mean something; get it wrong and you compute precise answers about the wrong thing.
crumbs: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement

title: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement
text:
Why it matters
Every technique later in this manual — averages, regressions, causal estimates — operates on variables, not on concepts. If the variable is a poor stand-in for the concept you care about, no amount of sophisticated analysis downstream can rescue it. This is why experienced researchers spend disproportionate effort before any data is collected, on the unglamorous question: how, exactly, will I measure this?
crumbs: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement

title: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement
text:
From concept to variable: operationalisation
Concepts are the building blocks of theory — the categories (“job satisfaction,” “prejudice,” “political participation”) around which a study is built (Bryman 2016). To investigate one, you must translate it into something researchable and quantifiable: a variable. That translation is operationalisation (Bryman 2016).

An operational definition is the precise recipe for measuring the variable in this study — “the specification of the activities or operations necessary to measure it” (Martin and Bridgmon 2012). Example: depressive symptomatology operationally defined as a score on the Beck Depression Inventory.
When a concept isn’t directly quantifiable, you devise indicators to represent it — drawn from survey questions, structured observation, or official statistics (Bryman 2016). Bryman stresses that multiple indicators (e.g. a 5-item Likert scale) usually give more precision than a single question — one item can catch an idiosyncrasy of wording; several average it out.







TipThe hidden choice



Operationalisation is a decision, not a discovery. “Depression = Beck score” and “depression = a clinician’s diagnosis” are different operational definitions that can disagree. Naming your operational definition out loud — and its limits — is the first act of honest quantitative research.
crumbs: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement

title: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement
section: Levels of measurement: what your numbers are allowed to do
text:
Levels of measurement: what your numbers are allowed to do
Once you have a variable, its level of measurement dictates which operations and statistics are even meaningful (Albers 2017; Martin and Bridgmon 2012). Using the wrong ones is a silent, common error.









Level
Property
Examples
Permissible operations




Nominal
Unordered categories; numbers are arbitrary tags
Gender, religion, city
Counts, frequencies, mode. Means are meaningless.


Ordinal
Ordered categories, but unequal/unknown gaps
Likert agreement, race finishing position
Order comparisons, median


Interval
Equal spacing, no true zero
IQ, °Celsius
Add/subtract; differences are meaningful


Ratio
Equal spacing and a true zero
Weight, height, time, count of children
All arithmetic; ratios (“twice as heavy”) are valid









WarningThe Likert trap



Albers warns explicitly: averaging a single Likert item is invalid, because the distance from “Agree” to “Neutral” is not guaranteed equal to “Neutral” to “Disagree” (Albers 2017). The number looks computable, but the scale doesn’t earn the mean. (A multi-item Likert scale is treated more leniently — but that’s a defended choice, not a default.)
crumbs: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement

title: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement
section: Reliability vs validity: consistency vs accuracy
text:
Reliability vs validity: consistency vs accuracy
These two words are used interchangeably in everyday English but are sharply distinct here — and the relationship between them is one-directional: validity presumes reliability. A measure that is inconsistent cannot be accurate (Bryman 2016).
Reliability — does the measure give the same result on repeated trials? (Consistency.)

Stability (test–retest): the same measure given twice to the same group shouldn’t swing wildly.
Internal reliability: the multiple indicators in a scale should hang together (measure the same thing).
Inter-rater reliability: independent coders should agree — Albers cites Cohen’s Kappa as the standard statistic (Albers 2017).

Validity — does the measure capture the concept you intended? (Accuracy.) The sources distinguish several kinds (Bryman 2016):

Face validity — at minimum, it looks like it measures the concept (to experts).
Criterion validity — it tracks an external benchmark: concurrent (a present criterion like absenteeism) or predictive (a future one).
Construct validity — it behaves as theory says it should; convergent validity checks it against measures of the same thing built by different methods.
Internal validity — confidence the independent variable truly drives the dependent one (causality). External validity — whether results generalise beyond the study; ecological validity — whether they hold in everyday, natural settings.







TipThe dartboard



The classic picture: reliable but not valid = tight cluster of darts, all in the wrong spot (consistent, biased). Valid but not reliable is impossible to sustain — scattered darts can’t be reliably on target. You want tight and centred.
crumbs: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement

title: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Computing means on ordinal/nominal data. Averaging ranks or category codes produces a number with no meaning. Match the statistic to the measurement level.
Mistaking reliability for validity. A bathroom scale that’s 5 kg heavy is perfectly reliable and perfectly wrong. Consistency is necessary, not sufficient.
Single-indicator fragility. One survey item can be derailed by wording, mood, or translation; multiple indicators are insurance.
Treating operationalisation as obvious. “Just measure income” hides a dozen choices (pre/post tax? household/individual? annual/monthly?) that change the findings.
crumbs: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement

title: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bryman, Social Research Methods — concepts as theory’s building blocks; operationalisation; indicators and multiple indicators; the reliability types (stability, internal, inter-rater) and validity types (face, criterion, construct, convergent, internal, external, ecological); validity presumes reliability.
Martin & Bridgmon, Quantitative and Statistical Research Methods — operational definitions as specified operations (Beck Depression Inventory example); active vs attribute variables.
Albers, Quantitative Data Analysis — levels of measurement and their permissible operations; the Likert-averaging warning; Cohen’s Kappa for inter-rater reliability.
crumbs: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement

title: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Decide the measurement level deliberately — it silently constrains every statistic you can later use. Collecting age as bands (ordinal) instead of years (ratio) throws away power you can never recover.
Write the operational definition before you collect anything. If you can’t write it precisely, you don’t yet know what you’re studying.
Check reliability before you trust a result. A finding built on an unreliable measure is noise wearing a number.
Prefer multiple indicators, then verify they cohere (internal reliability) before combining them.
crumbs: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement

title: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement
text:
Connections

Builds on: The anatomy of a study — measurement is step 5, the one beginners skip and experts obsess over.
Leads to: The cast of characters — once measured, variables play roles (cause, effect, confounder).
Reappears in: describing data (levels of measurement decide valid summaries), inference (measurement error widens uncertainty), and the tyranny of metrics (when an indicator is mistaken for the concept itself). ## Study with NotebookLM {.unnumbered}


“Explain operationalisation with three worked examples of turning a concept into an operational definition, drawn from these sources.”
“Lay out the four levels of measurement with the exact statistics each does and does not permit, and explain the Likert-averaging warning.”
“Distinguish all the types of reliability and validity in these sources, with an example of a measure that is reliable but not valid.”
“What is Cohen’s Kappa and when is inter-rater reliability the key concern?”
crumbs: Concepts & Variables: The Problem of Measurement

title: Ethics and the Human-Subjects Frame
text: Quantitative social science measures and sometimes intervenes in real human lives, so it is bound by commitments that precede any technique: don’t harm, get informed consent, protect privacy, justify any deception. And when you run experiments in the field, a second layer of dilemmas appears — because now your study is changing the world it observes.

title: Ethics and the Human-Subjects Frame
text:
Why it matters
Ethics is not a form you sign at the end; it is a design constraint that shapes the study from the start. A finding obtained unethically is not a triumph with an asterisk — it is a failure. Establishing the human-subjects frame here, in the Overture, means every design choice in the chapters ahead carries it by default.

title: Ethics and the Human-Subjects Frame
text:
The core principles
Ethical practice governs the relationship between researchers and the people they study, to prevent exploitation and harm (Bryman 2016):

Informed consent. Participants get enough information — purpose, risks, and the voluntary nature of taking part — to decide freely, usually documented with an information sheet and consent form.
Avoidance of harm. No physical, psychological, or social harm: protect participants from stress, loss of self-esteem, or being induced to do something reprehensible.
Privacy and confidentiality. Keep records secure and ensure individuals are not identifiable in anything published.
Deception. Representing the research as something it isn’t is sometimes used to elicit natural responses, but it is highly controversial, demands strong justification, and requires debriefing afterward.

The role of ethics review (IRB). Most institutions require an Institutional Review Board to evaluate proposed research: it weighs risks, checks confidentiality safeguards, and ensures participants can withdraw. A common yardstick is the minimal-risk threshold — is the anticipated harm no greater than that of ordinary daily life? (Bryman 2016)

title: Ethics and the Human-Subjects Frame
section: The extra dilemmas of field experiments
text:
The extra dilemmas of field experiments
Field experiments raise problems lab studies don’t, because they intervene in real settings where people may not know they’re in a study (Gerber and Green 2012). Gerber & Green work through several:

Protecting the integrity of random assignment. When you collaborate with agencies (police, schools), well-meaning practitioners may try to “help” the neediest by slipping them into the treatment group — subverting randomisation and destroying the study’s logic.
The “no-harm” design principle: encourage, don’t deny. Induce what you believe to be helpful treatments. Studying SAT-prep benefits? Randomly encourage people to take the class — don’t randomly prevent willing people from taking it.
Stepped-wedge designs to protect the control group. When a treatment is expected to help, give it to everyone eventually but randomise the timing; those still waiting serve as the controls. No one is permanently denied a beneficial treatment.
Informed-consent waivers. Where informing people would destroy the very “naturalness” being studied, an IRB may waive consent — if risk is minimal and the research couldn’t otherwise be done.
Third-party responses. Other actors can react to your experiment. If an NGO notices you’ve placed female leaders in certain villages and redirects its aid to male-led ones, it both contaminates the study and raises a fairness question about who gets help.
Tangible vs intangible harm. Judging harm is hard when the outcome is beliefs or opinions (e.g. nudging someone’s political or religious views) rather than physical or financial loss.







TipEthics as design, not paperwork



Notice that every field-experiment safeguard is a design move — encourage-don’t-deny, stepped-wedge timing, protecting randomisation. Ethics and good design are the same craft seen from two angles: a study that treats people well is usually a study that yields cleaner inferences.

title: Ethics and the Human-Subjects Frame
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Consent as a checkbox. A signed form without genuine understanding isn’t informed consent.
Deception without debriefing. If you must mislead, you must explain afterward — skipping that is indefensible.
Letting practitioners “fix” the assignment. Compassionate tampering with randomisation is still tampering; it destroys the causal claim and the fairness rationale.
Ignoring third-party contamination. Outside actors reacting to your treatment can wreck both ethics and validity at once.
Denying a beneficial treatment to a control group when a stepped-wedge design would have given it to everyone eventually.

title: Ethics and the Human-Subjects Frame
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bryman, Social Research Methods — informed consent; avoidance of harm; privacy and confidentiality; deception and debriefing; the IRB and the minimal-risk threshold.
Gerber & Green, Field Experiments — protecting the integrity of random assignment; the encourage-don’t-deny “no-harm” principle (SAT-prep example); stepped-wedge designs; consent waivers under minimal risk; third-party responses (the NGO/village-leaders example); tangible vs intangible harm.

title: Ethics and the Human-Subjects Frame
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Design the ethics in from day one. Ask “who could this harm, and how would I know?” while choosing the design, not after.
Prefer encouragement designs. Randomly offering a likely-beneficial treatment is almost always more defensible than randomly withholding one.
Reach for the stepped wedge whenever the treatment is expected to help — it dissolves the “control group is denied benefit” objection.
Engage the IRB early and honestly. Treat review as a design partner, not a hurdle; surface third-party and intangible-harm risks before they surface you. ## Connections {.unnumbered}
Builds on: research designs — especially experiments, where most ethical force concentrates.
Completes: the Overture. You now have the why, the anatomy, the measurement, the variable roles, the designs, the credibility criteria, and the ethical frame — the full orientation.
Pays off in: Causal Inference, where field-experiment ethics (encouragement designs, stepped wedges) become live methodological choices. ## Study with NotebookLM {.unnumbered}

“What is the IRB’s role and the minimal-risk threshold?”
“Explain the special ethical dilemmas of field experiments in Gerber & Green: the encourage-don’t-deny principle, stepped-wedge designs, and third-party responses.”
“Why is assessing ‘intangible’ harm (to beliefs or opinions) especially difficult?”

title: A Field Guide to Research Designs
text: A research design is the skeleton of a study: when you collect data, from whom, and with what comparison. It sets a hard ceiling on the strength of any conclusion you can draw. Choose it before you analyse anything, because the most sophisticated statistics in the world cannot buy back a causal claim a cross-sectional design was never able to make.
crumbs: A Field Guide to Research Designs

title: A Field Guide to Research Designs
text:
Why it matters
Beginners think credibility comes from the analysis. It comes from the design. The design determines whether you can say “X causes Y” or only “X and Y go together.” This entry is the map of the main designs and what each can — and cannot — license. It’s also the bridge to the summit of the manual: causal inference is, at heart, the art of designing (or reconstructing) a fair comparison.
crumbs: A Field Guide to Research Designs

title: A Field Guide to Research Designs
section: The designs, and what each buys you
text:
The designs, and what each buys you
A research design reflects the priority you place on causal connection, generalisation, and understanding change over time (Bryman 2016).

Experimental
Manipulate an independent variable and use random assignment to make the treatment and control groups comparable.

Strengths: the benchmark for internal validity — it rules out alternative causal explanations. Field experiments, run in real settings, add ecological validity.
Limits: many social variables (gender, ethnicity, class) can’t be manipulated for ethical or practical reasons; lab experiments can suffer reactive effects when people know they’re studied, hurting generalisability.



Cross-sectional / survey
Data on many cases at a single point in time, to find patterns of association.

Strengths: ideal for non-manipulable variables (age, background); with random probability sampling, strong external validity.
Limits: weak internal validity — everything is measured at once, so the direction of causation is ambiguous. It shows relationships, not causes.



Longitudinal / panel
The same sample studied on ≥2 occasions (panel = same people; cohort = people sharing a trait, e.g. birth week).

Strengths: establishes temporal order, enabling stronger causal inference than cross-sectional designs; essential for charting social change.
Limits: expensive and slow; suffers attrition as participants drop out, which can make the survivors unrepresentative.



Comparative
Two or more contrasting cases (e.g. nations) studied with near-identical methods.

Strengths: reveals how a phenomenon varies across socio-cultural settings, guarding against findings that are culturally specific.
Limits: hard to ensure data are truly comparable across contexts (translation of instruments is a classic trap).



Case study
Intensive analysis of a single case — a community, organisation, or person.

Strengths: deep, idiographic insight; supports analytic generalisation (to theory, not population).
Limits: weak external validity — one case rarely represents a wider population.
crumbs: A Field Guide to Research Designs

title: A Field Guide to Research Designs
section: The two rulers: internal vs external validity
text:
The two rulers: internal vs external validity
Every design is judged on two different axes, and they often trade off:

Internal validity — confidence the IV genuinely caused the change in the DV (Bryman 2016). Experiments win (a control group neutralises threats like history and testing effects); cross-sectional designs are weak (association ≠ sequence).
External validity — whether results generalise beyond the study (Bryman 2016). Large random-sample surveys win; lab experiments often lose (artificial settings, unrepresentative participants — frequently students). Field experiments sit in the sweet spot: real participants and contexts give them stronger external validity than the lab while keeping randomisation’s internal validity.







TipThere’s usually no free lunch



The lab maximises internal validity at the cost of external; the survey does the reverse. Much of research craft is choosing which validity your question can’t live without, and paying the other’s price knowingly. Field experiments are prized precisely because they compromise least.
crumbs: A Field Guide to Research Designs

title: A Field Guide to Research Designs
section: Why the randomised experiment is the benchmark
text:
Why the randomised experiment is the benchmark
When you can run one, randomisation is a near-magical tool — it is the standard every other design is measured against (Gerber and Green 2012). It works because random assignment makes treatment statistically independent of everything about the subjects:

Comparable groups / a fair test. No systematic advantage to treatment or control; with a large enough sample, individual differences wash out (Gerber and Green 2012).
Eliminates confounders — even unobserved ones. Randomisation severs the link between treatment and every variable that predicts the outcome, solving the lurking-variable problem that plagues observational research (Gerber and Green 2012). This is the part no statistical “control” can match: you can only control for confounders you thought of and measured; randomisation handles the ones you didn’t.
Unbiased estimation. Replicated infinitely, the average estimate equals the true treatment effect (Gerber and Green 2012).

Concrete example — women heading Indian village councils. Because seats were randomly reserved for female leaders (by law), any difference in, say, sanitation spending between those councils and male-led ones could be attributed to the leader’s gender rather than to pre-existing village differences (Gerber and Green 2012). Randomisation turned an unanswerable observational question into a credible causal one. (You’ll meet this study’s statistics again in sampling distributions.)
crumbs: A Field Guide to Research Designs

title: A Field Guide to Research Designs
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Causal claims from cross-sectional data. “X correlates with Y at one time point” is not “X causes Y.” This is the most common over-reach in applied social science.
Ignoring attrition in panels. If drop-out is related to the outcome, your surviving sample silently biases the result.
Generalising a case study to a population. Case studies generalise to theory, not to people-in-general.
Trusting a lab result in the wild. High internal validity doesn’t imply the effect survives outside the artificial setting.
Assuming you can always randomise. Often you can’t (ethics, law, practicality) — which is exactly why Part V exists: to approximate the experiment you couldn’t run.
crumbs: A Field Guide to Research Designs

title: A Field Guide to Research Designs
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bryman, Social Research Methods — the five designs and their strengths/limits; internal vs external (and ecological) validity; how each design fares on each.
Gerber & Green, Field Experiments — randomisation as the benchmark for causal claims; statistical independence, comparable groups, elimination of observed and unobserved confounders, unbiased estimation; the Indian village-council example.
crumbs: A Field Guide to Research Designs

title: A Field Guide to Research Designs
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Start from the conclusion you need, then pick the design. If you need “X causes Y,” a survey won’t do — design for the claim, don’t retrofit it.
State your validity priority explicitly. Write down whether internal or external validity matters more for this decision; it resolves most design arguments.
When you can’t randomise, say so — and switch to causal-inference tools. The honest move is to name the missing experiment and adopt a design (Part V) that approximates it.
Budget for attrition up front in any longitudinal study; plan retention like you plan sampling.
crumbs: A Field Guide to Research Designs

title: A Field Guide to Research Designs
text:
Connections

Builds on: The cast of characters — designs are strategies for handling confounders so an IV→DV claim survives.
Leads to: What makes research credible — validity and replication as quality criteria.
Pays off in: Sampling & inference (external validity rests on sampling) and, above all, Causal Inference, which is the science of getting causal answers when a clean experiment isn’t available. ## Study with NotebookLM {.unnumbered}

crumbs: A Field Guide to Research Designs

title: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does
text: Quantitative social science is the disciplined attempt to test ideas about human behaviour using numbers: you turn a fuzzy concept into something you can measure, collect data on it, and let the evidence confirm or overturn what you believed. The numbers are not the point — deciding what to count, and what the count is allowed to prove, is the point.
crumbs: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does

title: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does
text:
Why it matters
Every technique in this manual — probability, regression, causal inference — is a tool. Before tools, you need the job: what are we actually trying to do when we “do quantitative research”? Get this wrong and you produce numbers that look authoritative and mean nothing. Get it right and even a simple average can settle a real question. This entry is the frame that gives all the later machinery a purpose.
crumbs: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does

title: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does
section: Intuition: research as a strategy, not a calculation
text:
Intuition: research as a strategy, not a calculation
Quantitative research is best understood not as “doing maths on data” but as a systematic strategy that prioritises quantification in order to test theories (Bryman 2016). Bryman characterises it through three orientations that travel together:

Deductive — theory comes first; you test it. (More on this below.)
Positivist — you study social reality using the methods of the natural sciences.
Objectivist — you treat social facts as real, external things that exist independently of the observer.

Martin & Bridgmon frame the same enterprise more operationally, as the discipline of managing variance — their “MaxMinCon” principle: Maximise the variance due to the thing you’re studying, Minimise error variance, and Control the extraneous variance from everything else (Martin and Bridgmon 2012). That single idea — make the signal big, the noise small, and the confounds held still — is the hidden agenda behind nearly every method later in this book.

Quantitative vs qualitative: a question of stance
Quantitative isn’t “better” than qualitative research; it answers different questions from a different stance. Bryman contrasts them along several dimensions (Bryman 2016):









Quantitative
Qualitative




Medium
Numbers, measurement
Words, images


Whose view counts
The researcher’s (you decide what to measure)
The participants’ (seen through their eyes)


Relationship to subjects
Distant, sometimes no contact at all
Close involvement


Picture of social life
A static snapshot of relationships
An unfolding process


Scale
Large-scale trends (macro)
Small-scale interactions (micro)



The trade is real: quantification buys you scale, comparability, and a way to quantify uncertainty, at the cost of closeness and context. Knowing which you need is the first research decision you ever make.
crumbs: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does

title: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does
section: Deductive and inductive: how theory and data talk to each other
text:
Deductive and inductive: how theory and data talk to each other
The engine of research is the relationship between theory and data, and it runs in two directions.

Deductive (theory → data). You start from what’s known and relevant theory, deduce a hypothesis, and test it against evidence. Theory drives what you collect (Bryman 2016). Martin & Bridgmon call this the hypothetico-deductive model: general theory → specific observations → confirmation or rejection (Martin and Bridgmon 2012).
Inductive (data → theory). Theory is the outcome: you observe, then draw generalisable inferences that become new theoretical ideas (Bryman 2016).







TipThe honest version: it’s a spiral, not a line



Both sources stress these are tendencies, not boxes. Deductive studies contain “inductive loops” where theory is revised after the findings come in; Martin & Bridgmon call the real process the inductive–hypothetico–deductive spiral (Martin and Bridgmon 2012). In practice you loop: theory shapes data, data reshapes theory, repeat.
crumbs: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does

title: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does
section: Worked examples: the two directions in real studies
text:
Worked examples: the two directions in real studies
Deductive — do immigrants’ gender attitudes converge on their host country’s? Bryman uses Röder & Mühlau (2014) (Röder and Mühlau 2014). They began from established theory (national gender attitudes differ; migrants move between cultures), deduced five hypotheses predicting that second-generation immigrants adopt host-country gender values, and confirmed them using European Social Survey data — evidence of an “intergenerational acculturation process.” Theory first, data as the test.
Inductive — why is front-line customer service so poor? Bryman uses O’Reilly et al. (2012) (O’Reilly et al. 2012). The researcher started with open-ended observation and interviews of retail employees — no hypothesis. The concept of “service silos” (management structures that quietly constrained employees) emerged from the data as the theory that explained what she saw. Data first, theory as the product.
crumbs: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does

title: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Treating deductive vs inductive as a rigid choice. Real research spirals between them; pretending you “only test hypotheses” hides the inductive judgement you actually used.
Forgetting the positivist/objectivist assumptions. Treating a measured construct as if it were an objective external fact — when it’s a contestable operationalisation — is the original sin that later resurfaces as the tyranny of metrics.
Forcing numbers onto a qualitative question. If the real question is about meaning, context, or process, quantification can produce precise answers to the wrong question.
Skipping “MaxMinCon” thinking. A study that doesn’t deliberately maximise signal, minimise error, and control confounds is usually measuring noise.
crumbs: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does

title: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bryman, Social Research Methods — quantitative research as a deductive/positivist/ objectivist strategy; the quant-vs-qual dimensions; deductive and inductive logic; the Röder & Mühlau (deductive) and O’Reilly (inductive) examples.
Martin & Bridgmon, Quantitative and Statistical Research Methods — the MaxMinCon variance framing; the hypothetico-deductive model and the inductive–hypothetico–deductive spiral.
crumbs: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does

title: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does
text:
Practitioner’s notes — tips & tricks

Name your direction out loud. Before designing anything, say whether you’re testing a theory or building one. It changes everything downstream — sampling, measurement, how you read a surprising result.
Write the conclusion you’re afraid of. State, in advance, the finding that would prove you wrong. If your design can’t produce it, you’re not testing — you’re decorating.
“MaxMinCon” is a design checklist. For any study ask: what’s my signal, what’s my error, what are my confounds — and what in the design handles each?
crumbs: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does

title: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does
text:
Connections

Leads to: The anatomy of a study, which turns this strategy into a concrete sequence of steps.
Sets up: measurement (concepts → variables), the make-or-break step of the whole book.
Foreshadows: the tyranny of metrics — what happens when the objectivist assumption is taken too literally.
crumbs: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does

title: What Quantitative Social Science Actually Does
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Walk me through the Röder & Mühlau acculturation study as a step-by-step example of the deductive cycle.”

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text: You don’t meet a new distribution for every problem — you meet the same handful, over and over, each encoding a particular story about how chance operates. Two outcomes? Bernoulli. Counting successes? Binomial. Rare events in an interval? Poisson. Waiting for something? Exponential. Many small effects adding up? Normal. Knowing the stories lets you recognise the right model instead of guessing.
crumbs: The Common Distributions and What They Model

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text:
Why it matters
A named distribution is a massive shortcut: identify it and you instantly inherit its mean, its variance, its shape, and a century of results. But the deeper skill is reading the story — the generative assumptions each one encodes — because that tells you when the model fits and, more importantly, when it doesn’t. The chapter’s recurring warning (Bruce & Bruce) lives here: the Normal distribution is so iconic that people reach for it reflexively, yet real social and economic data is routinely long-tailed, and forcing it into a bell underestimates exactly the extreme events that matter most.
These seven also wire directly into later chapters: the Binomial underlies proportions and A/B tests, the Normal underlies the CLT and nearly all classical inference, the Poisson and Exponential underlie event and duration models.
crumbs: The Common Distributions and What They Model

title: The Common Distributions and What They Model
text:
The menagerie at a glance










Distribution

Variance

p^x(1-p)^{1-x}, x\in\{0,1\}

\binom{n}{i}p^i(1-p)^{n-i}
