“What is the zero-prior trap, and why does Lambert insist on weakly-informative priors and sensitivity analysis?”

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
text: The t-test compares two group means. Real studies rarely stop at two, and rarely have means at all — they have three or more groups, categorical outcomes, binary conversions. This entry stocks the rest of the toolkit: ANOVA for many means, chi-square for categories, and proportion tests for rates — each answering “are these groups really different, or is it noise?”

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
text:
Why it matters
A t-test handles exactly two groups of numeric data. But you will constantly face questions it can’t: Do these four web layouts differ in engagement? Are click-throughs independent of which headline a visitor saw? Does price A convert better than price B? Each needs a different test, matched to the number of groups and the type of outcome. Knowing which test when — and why you can’t just paper over the gap with repeated t-tests — is core inferential literacy, and the sources treat all three as essential. (Bruce et al. 2020; Martin and Bridgmon 2012)

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
section: First, the trap: why not just run many t-tests?
text:
First, the trap: why not just run many t-tests?
The tempting move with four groups is to t-test every pair. But four groups means six pairwise tests, and each carries its own 5% false-positive risk. (Bruce et al. 2020) As the replication-crisis entry showed, running many tests makes at least one spurious “hit” nearly inevitable — the multiple-comparisons problem in miniature. The fix is an omnibus test: a single test asking “is there any difference among the groups?” before you go hunting for which pair. (Bruce et al. 2020) That omnibus test is ANOVA.

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
text:
ANOVA and the F-test
Analysis of variance compares three or more group means with one test, and its engine is a beautiful idea: detect differences in means by comparing two kinds of variance. (Bruce et al. 2020)

Between-group variance — how far the group means scatter around the overall grand mean. This is the “signal”: real differences push group means apart.
Within-group variance — how much individual observations scatter inside their own group. This is the “noise”: natural variability that has nothing to do with the groups.

The F-statistic is their ratio: (Bruce et al. 2020; Martin and Bridgmon 2012)

F = \frac{\text{between-group variance}}{\text{within-group variance}}
= \frac{\text{MS}_{\text{treatment}}}{\text{MS}_{\text{error}}}.

When the groups are truly identical, signal and noise are the same thing and F \approx 1. A large F says the group means differ by more than the within-group noise can explain, and the F-distribution converts it to a p-value. (Bruce et al. 2020) This rests on the ANOVA decomposition — total variation splits cleanly, exactly the law of total variance made into a test: (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)

\underbrace{\text{SS}_{\text{total}}}_{\text{all variation}} =
\underbrace{\text{SS}_{\text{between}}}_{\text{explained by group}} +
\underbrace{\text{SS}_{\text{within}}}_{\text{residual noise}},

with degrees of freedom K-1 between (for K groups) and N-K within. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)

Seeing the F-ratio
F is a signal-to-noise ratio, so the same set of group means can be clearly significant or clearly not — it all depends on the within-group scatter. Below, three groups sit at identical means in both panels; only the noise differs.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(2)
means = np.array([168.0, 176.0, 184.0])
n = 30

def f_stat(groups):
    allx = np.concatenate(groups)
    grand = allx.mean()
    k, N = len(groups), len(allx)
    ss_between = sum(len(g) * (g.mean() - grand) ** 2 for g in groups)
    ss_within  = sum(((g - g.mean()) ** 2).sum() for g in groups)
    return (ss_between / (k - 1)) / (ss_within / (N - k))

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 3.8), sharey=True)
for ax, sd, label in zip(axes, [14.0, 3.5], ["large noise", "small noise"]):
    groups = [rng.normal(m, sd, n) for m in means]
    F = f_stat(groups)
    for i, g in enumerate(groups):
        ax.scatter(np.full(n, i) + rng.uniform(-0.08, 0.08, n), g,
                   s=12, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.6)
        ax.plot([i - 0.25, i + 0.25], [g.mean()] * 2, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.5)
    ax.axhline(np.concatenate(groups).mean(), color="#7a7f87", ls="--", lw=1.2)
    ax.set_title(f"{label}:  F = {F:.1f}", fontsize=11)
    ax.set_xticks([0, 1, 2]); ax.set_xticklabels(["Group 1", "Group 2", "Group 3"])
axes[0].set_ylabel("outcome")
fig.suptitle("Same means, different noise — F is signal ÷ noise", fontsize=12)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: ANOVA is signal ÷ noise. Both panels have the same three group means (dashed grey = grand mean). Left: large within-group scatter swamps the gaps between means — F is near 1, not significant. Right: tight groups make the same mean-gaps leap out — a large F, clearly significant. The means didn’t change; the noise did.











NoteBruce & Bruce’s web-page stickiness test



A company compared four page layouts by how long visitors stayed (seconds), 5 visitors per page (N=20). The group means were Page 1: 172, Page 2: 185, Page 3: 176, Page 4: 162, around a grand mean of 173.75. ANOVA gave F = 2.74 on (3, 16) degrees of freedom, with p = 0.0776. Since p > 0.05, the differences are not significant — the spread among those four means is within what chance alone would produce. The eye-catching 23-second gap between the best and worst page is, statistically, noise. (Bruce et al. 2020)


If the omnibus F is significant, you follow up with post-hoc tests like Tukey’s Honestly Significant Difference to find which pairs differ — and, crucially, only after a significant F, which keeps the multiple-comparison error rate in check. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
section: The chi-square test: are categories related?
text:
The chi-square test: are categories related?
When the data are counts in categories, not means, the workhorse is the chi-square test. It asks whether the observed frequencies in a contingency table depart from what you’d expect if the variables were independent. (Bruce et al. 2020) The logic is observed vs expected: compute the counts you’d see under “no relationship,” then measure the total discrepancy: (Bruce et al. 2020)

\chi^2 = \sum_{\text{cells}} \frac{(O - E)^2}{E},
\qquad \text{df} = (\text{rows}-1)\times(\text{columns}-1).

Each cell contributes its squared surprise, scaled by what was expected; a large total means the data stray far from independence. (Bruce et al. 2020)






NoteBruce & Bruce’s headline test



Three headlines (A, B, C) were each shown to 1,000 visitors; clicks were 14, 8, 12 (total 34). Under the null that all headlines convert equally, the expected rate is 34/3000 \approx 1.13\%, so each headline’s expected clicks are \approx \mathbf{11.33}. The statistic works out to \chi^2 = 1.666 on \text{df} = (3-1)(2-1) = 2. That value sits well inside the range of ordinary chance variation: the headlines do not differ significantly. The 14-vs-8 gap is the kind of wobble random clicks produce. (Bruce et al. 2020)


The same machinery does goodness of fit — testing whether one categorical variable matches a hypothesised distribution (are digits uniform? are arrivals as forecast?). And when counts are tiny (expected cells around 5 or fewer), the chi-square approximation breaks down; switch to Fisher’s exact test, which computes the exact probability of the observed table. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
text:
Tests for proportions
The most common metric in applied and data-science work is a binary proportion — converted or not, clicked or not, turned out to vote or not. (Bruce et al. 2020) To compare two such rates, the two-proportion z-test uses the Normal approximation to the binomial. (For two groups this is algebraically equivalent to a 2\times2 chi-square — same question, two dialects.)






NoteBruce & Bruce’s price test



An e-commerce experiment compared two prices. Price A converted 200 of 23,739 (0.842%); Price B 182 of 22,588 (0.806%). Despite 45,000+ visitors, the two-proportion test returned p = 0.3498 — not significant. The lesson is sharp: with very low base rates, statistical power comes from the number of conversions, not the number of exposures. Tens of thousands of visitors can still leave you underpowered if almost none convert. (Bruce et al. 2020) (A direct echo of the power entry.)

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
text:
Which test when?



Outcome type
Two groups
Three or more groups




Numeric (means)
two-sample t-test
ANOVA / F-test (then Tukey HSD)


Categorical (counts)
2\times2 chi-square (or Fisher’s exact if small)
chi-square on the full table


Binary (proportion)
two-proportion z-test
chi-square across groups



Pick by the shape of your outcome and the number of groups; the question — “real difference or noise?” — is always the same.

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Running pairwise t-tests instead of ANOVA. Many comparisons inflate the false-positive rate; use the omnibus F first, post-hoc tests only after. (Bruce et al. 2020; Martin and Bridgmon 2012)
Mining post-hoc pairs after a non-significant F. If the omnibus test is null, hunting for a “significant pair” is p-hacking. The gate exists for a reason. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)
Using chi-square with tiny expected counts. Below ~5 per cell the approximation misleads — switch to Fisher’s exact. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Confusing exposures with conversions. A huge sample with a rare outcome can still be underpowered; precision tracks the count of events, not of trials. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Reporting a significant F as the whole story. ANOVA says some group differs, not which or by how much. Follow up with post-hoc tests and effect sizes. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — the multiplicity problem motivating ANOVA; the F-test as between- over within-group variance; the web-page-stickiness example (means 172/185/176/162; F=2.74, p=0.0776, not significant); the chi-square observed-vs-expected logic and statistic; the headline-click example (14/8/12; expected 11.33; \chi^2=1.666, df 2, not significant); goodness-of-fit and Fisher’s exact for small counts; the two-proportion test and the e-commerce price example (p=0.3498 despite 45,000+ trials).
Martin & Bridgmon, Quantitative & Statistical Research Methods — the ANOVA sum-of-squares decomposition and degrees of freedom (K-1, N-K); post-hoc Tukey HSD and the rule to run it only after a significant omnibus F.

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The one-way ANOVA F-statistic. For K groups of sizes n_j with group means \bar y_j, grand mean \bar y, and total size N:

\text{SS}_{\text{between}} = \sum_{j=1}^K n_j(\bar y_j - \bar y)^2, \qquad
\text{SS}_{\text{within}} = \sum_{j=1}^K \sum_{i=1}^{n_j} (y_{ij} - \bar y_j)^2,


F = \frac{\text{SS}_{\text{between}}/(K-1)}{\text{SS}_{\text{within}}/(N-K)}
= \frac{\text{MS}_{\text{between}}}{\text{MS}_{\text{within}}} \;\sim\; F_{K-1,\,N-K}\ \text{under } H_0.

Under the null (all group means equal) both mean squares estimate the same \sigma^2, so F\approx1; real differences inflate only the numerator. This is the total-variance decomposition \operatorname{Var}(Y) =
\operatorname{Var}(E[Y\mid g]) + E[\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid g)] turned into a test of the between-group term.
Chi-square expected counts. For a contingency table, the expected count under independence is E_{ij} = (\text{row}_i\,\text{total})(\text{col}_j\,\text{total})/N. The headline test: expected = 1000 \times 34/3000 = 11.33 clicks each, and \chi^2 = \frac{(14-11.33)^2}{11.33} + \frac{(8-11.33)^2}{11.33} + \frac{(12-11.33)^2}{11.33} on the clicks, plus the matching non-click cells, \approx 1.67 — small for df =2.
Two-proportion z-test. With pooled proportion \hat p = (x_1+x_2)/(n_1+n_2),

z = \frac{\hat p_1 - \hat p_2}{\sqrt{\hat p(1-\hat p)\left(\tfrac{1}{n_1}+\tfrac{1}{n_2}\right)}}.

Squaring this z gives exactly the 2\times2 chi-square statistic — the two tests are one.

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
text:
Connections

Builds on: hypothesis testing & p-values (the null/alternative logic and the t-test it generalises), the law of total variance (ANOVA’s between/within split), and the common distributions (the F, chi-square, and Normal-approximation-to-binomial).
Sits beside: resampling & distribution-free tests — the assumption-light alternatives when ANOVA’s normality fails.
Leads to: regression, which contains ANOVA as a special case (regression on group dummies reproduces the F-test) and extends it to continuous predictors and controls.

title: Comparing Groups: ANOVA, Chi-Square & Proportions
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Explain why comparing many groups with pairwise t-tests inflates false positives, and how the ANOVA F-test solves it.”
“Walk through Bruce & Bruce’s web-page-stickiness ANOVA, including the means, F = 2.74, and why p = 0.0776 means not significant.”
“Explain the chi-square test’s observed-versus-expected logic with the headline-click example (14/8/12, expected 11.33, χ² = 1.666).”
“When should I use Fisher’s exact test instead of chi-square, and why?”
“Work through the two-proportion z-test and the e-commerce price example, and explain why 45,000 visitors still gave p = 0.35.”

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text: A posterior is the whole answer — but you still have to read it, and for any realistic model you can’t even write it down in closed form. This entry does both: it shows how to summarise a posterior into the interval people actually wanted, how a clever random walk (MCMC) conjures that posterior without the impossible integral, and where, in the end, Bayesian and frequentist thinking each earn their keep.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
Why it matters
This is the last entry of Part II, and it closes two loops. First, it delivers the interval the confidence interval couldn’t — one you may legitimately read as “95% probability the parameter is in here.” Second, it confronts the wall raised in B2: the denominator is an intractable integral, so how does anyone do Bayesian inference on a real problem? The answer — simulation — is the engine behind the entire modern Bayesian toolkit, and once you grasp its core trick the rest is detail. Then we step back and weigh the two paradigms honestly, because a quantitative social scientist should be bilingual, not partisan.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
The credible interval
A credible interval is a range read straight off the posterior that contains a stated share of its probability mass — a 95% credible interval holds 95% of the posterior. (Lambert 2018) And here is the pay-off the whole sub-chapter has been building toward:






ImportantWhat a Bayesian may say



A frequentist cannot say “there is a 95% probability the true value lies in 1%–5%,” because to them the parameter is a fixed constant — the interval either contains it or doesn’t. A Bayesian can say exactly that, because the parameter is a random variable carrying a distribution. (Lambert 2018)


The credible interval describes post-data uncertainty about the specific parameter — your actual state of knowledge now — rather than the long-run behaviour of a procedure. (Lambert 2018) Lambert’s running example: a 95% credible interval of 0\% \le \text{RT} \le 4\% for the proportion of red-tailed penguins is a direct statement that the true proportion is, with 95% probability, in that range. (Lambert 2018) It is the interval everyone always thought they were getting.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
Equal-tailed vs highest-posterior-density
There are two ways to carve a 95% region out of a posterior. (Lambert 2018)

Equal-tailed (central) interval — trim the bottom 2.5% and top 2.5% of the mass; the interval runs between the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles and is centred on the median.
Highest posterior density (HPD) interval — the set of values such that every point inside has higher posterior density than every point outside. It is the shortest region holding 95%, and for a multi-peaked posterior it can be non-contiguous.







TipLambert’s treasure-hunting pirate



A pirate hunts buried gold. A central interval forces him to dig one long contiguous stretch — say 1.5 km to 8.5 km of beach — even where the gold is unlikely in the middle. An HPD interval lets him dig only the truly promising patches — 1–3 km and 7–9 km — skipping the barren middle and saving enormous excavation cost. (Lambert 2018) When the posterior is single-peaked and symmetric the two intervals coincide; when it is skewed or bimodal, HPD is sharper.



Credible vs confidence, on the same data
Here is the contrast made concrete. We flip a coin 20 times, see 14 heads, and form both 95% intervals. The Bayesian posterior (flat prior) is \text{Beta}(15,7); its credible interval is shaded. The frequentist Wald interval sits just below. The numbers nearly coincide — but what they claim could not be more different.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from math import lgamma

def beta_pdf(theta, a, b):
    logB = lgamma(a) + lgamma(b) - lgamma(a + b)
    return np.exp((a - 1) * np.log(theta) + (b - 1) * np.log(1 - theta) - logB)

k, n = 14, 20
a_post, b_post = 1 + k, 1 + (n - k)          # flat prior Beta(1,1) -> Beta(15,7)
theta = np.linspace(1e-4, 1 - 1e-4, 2000)
post = beta_pdf(theta, a_post, b_post)

# 95% equal-tailed credible interval by numerical CDF
cdf = np.cumsum(post) * (theta[1] - theta[0])
cdf /= cdf[-1]
lo_c = theta[np.searchsorted(cdf, 0.025)]
hi_c = theta[np.searchsorted(cdf, 0.975)]

# Frequentist Wald 95% CI
phat = k / n
se = np.sqrt(phat * (1 - phat) / n)
lo_f, hi_f = phat - 1.96 * se, phat + 1.96 * se

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.9))
ax.plot(theta, post, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.2)
ax.fill_between(theta, post, where=(theta >= lo_c) & (theta <= hi_c),
                color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.25)
ax.errorbar(phat, -0.25, xerr=[[phat - lo_f], [hi_f - phat]], fmt="o",
            color="#c0392b", capsize=4, lw=2)
ax.text(hi_c + 0.01, beta_pdf(0.7, a_post, b_post) * 0.6,
        f"credible: [{lo_c:.2f}, {hi_c:.2f}]\n95% prob. θ in here",
        color="#1f3a5f", fontsize=8.5)
ax.text(hi_f + 0.01, -0.25, f"confidence: [{lo_f:.2f}, {hi_f:.2f}]\nprocedure covers 95%",
        color="#c0392b", fontsize=8.5, va="center")
ax.set_xlim(0.3, 1.0)
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_xlabel("θ — the coin's bias")
ax.set_title("Same data, two intervals, two meanings")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Two 95% intervals for a coin’s bias from 14 heads in 20 flips. The blue curve is the Bayesian posterior Beta(15,7); the shaded band is its 95% credible interval — ‘95% probability θ is in here.’ The red bar is the frequentist Wald confidence interval — ‘95% of such intervals would cover the fixed θ.’ Nearly the same numbers; fundamentally different meanings.





The lesson of the picture: agreement on the numbers hides a chasm in meaning. The credible interval is a claim about the parameter; the confidence interval is a claim about the procedure. In this simple, flat-prior case they nearly match — but in small samples with real prior information, or in complex models, they diverge, and only one of them answers the question you actually asked.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
Why we must compute
For all but textbook models, the posterior has no closed form, because the normalising denominator p(\text{data}) = \int p(\text{data}\mid\theta)\,p(\theta)\,d\theta is an integral over every parameter value — and in a multi-parameter model that is high-dimensional. (Lambert 2018) Lambert’s recurring example: test scores across 100 schools can demand a 200-dimensional integral, which no computer can solve exactly or even approximate by brute force. (Lambert 2018) If we had to evaluate that denominator, applied Bayesian statistics would be dead in the water.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
MCMC: sampling without the integral
The escape is Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) — instead of computing the posterior, draw samples from it, and let the cloud of samples stand in for the distribution. (Lambert 2018) The magic is that you can do this without ever computing the denominator.
MCMC is a guided random walk: each new sample depends on the current one, and the walk is rigged so it lingers in regions of high posterior density. Over time the collected points mirror the posterior’s shape. (Lambert 2018) The simplest version is the Metropolis algorithm:

Propose a step to a nearby location.
If the posterior density there is higher (uphill), accept — always move toward more probable values.
If it is lower (downhill), accept only with probability r, the ratio of the new density to the old. So the walk usually climbs but occasionally wanders down, which is what lets it explore the whole distribution instead of getting stuck on the peak. (Lambert 2018)







TipLambert’s explorer in the fog



Imagine mapping a mountain range permanently shrouded in fog. You can’t photograph it from a satellite (you can’t see the whole posterior). But you can take local steps, recording your altitude and coordinates at each. Take enough steps — preferring uphill but sometimes slipping down — and your notebook reconstructs a full contour map of the range. That notebook is your set of MCMC samples. (Lambert 2018)


The crucial sleight of hand lives in step 3: because r is a ratio of two posterior densities, the intractable denominator p(\text{data}) — identical top and bottom — cancels exactly. MCMC needs only the unnormalised product likelihood × prior, which we can always evaluate. (The algebra is in the appendix.)

Watch a Metropolis sampler work
Below, a Metropolis sampler — knowing only the unnormalised target \theta^{14}(1-\theta)^6, never the normaliser — recovers the \text{Beta}(15,7) posterior from the figure above. The left panel is the trace (the walk’s path over time); the right is the histogram of its samples with the true posterior overlaid. The simulation never computed an integral.


Show the sampler code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from math import lgamma

rng = np.random.default_rng(20)

def log_target(t):                      # log of theta^14 (1-theta)^6, up to a constant
    if t <= 0 or t >= 1:
        return -np.inf
    return 14 * np.log(t) + 6 * np.log(1 - t)

n_steps, burn = 8000, 2000
samples = np.empty(n_steps)
t = 0.5
lt = log_target(t)
for i in range(n_steps):
    prop = t + rng.normal(0, 0.08)      # propose a nearby step
    lp = log_target(prop)
    if np.log(rng.random()) < lp - lt:  # accept uphill always, downhill with prob r
        t, lt = prop, lp
    samples[i] = t

post = samples[burn:]

def beta_pdf(theta, a, b):
    logB = lgamma(a) + lgamma(b) - lgamma(a + b)
    return np.exp((a - 1) * np.log(theta) + (b - 1) * np.log(1 - theta) - logB)

fig, (axT, axH) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9, 3.4),
                               gridspec_kw={"width_ratios": [1.5, 1]})
axT.plot(np.arange(burn), samples[:burn], color="#9aa0a6", lw=0.5)
axT.plot(np.arange(burn, n_steps), post, color="#1f3a5f", lw=0.5)
axT.axvline(burn, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.0)
axT.text(burn + 100, 0.92, "burn-in discarded", color="#c0392b", fontsize=8)
axT.set_title("Trace: the 'fuzzy caterpillar'", fontsize=10)
axT.set_xlabel("step"); axT.set_ylabel("θ"); axT.set_ylim(0.3, 1.0)

xs = np.linspace(0.3, 1.0, 300)
axH.hist(post, bins=40, density=True, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.45, orientation="horizontal")
axH.plot(beta_pdf(xs, 15, 7), xs, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.0)
axH.set_title(f"Samples vs Beta(15,7)\nmean ≈ {post.mean():.3f}", fontsize=10)
axH.set_ylim(0.3, 1.0); axH.set_yticks([]); axH.set_xlabel("density")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 2: A Metropolis sampler recovering Beta(15,7) using only the unnormalised target. Left: the trace plot — a healthy ‘fuzzy caterpillar’ oscillating around a stable centre after a short burn-in (grey). Right: the histogram of post-burn-in samples matches the analytic posterior (blue curve) — the denominator was never computed.





The sampler’s mean \approx 0.68 recovers the analytic posterior mean 15/22 = 0.682 — and it did so by walking, never by integrating. Scale this idea up (with smarter proposals like Hamiltonian Monte Carlo) and it handles the 200-dimensional models that defeat pen and paper.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
Summarising with samples
Once MCMC hands you thousands of draws, the Law of Large Numbers does the rest — sample summaries approximate posterior summaries. (Lambert 2018)

Posterior mean ≈ the average of the samples.
95% credible interval ≈ the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles of the sorted samples — no formula, just counting. (Lambert 2018)
Posterior variance ≈ the variance of the samples.

The posterior you couldn’t write down becomes a list of numbers you can summarise any way you like.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
section: Diagnostics: did the walk actually work?
text:
Diagnostics: did the walk actually work?
Because MCMC starts somewhere arbitrary and explores blindly, you must check that it found the real posterior. (Lambert 2018)

Burn-in (warm-up). Early samples are contaminated by the random starting point, so discard them — often the first half of the chain — keeping only draws from the settled distribution. (Lambert 2018)
Trace plots. Plot the walk over time. A healthy chain is a “fuzzy caterpillar” — rapidly oscillating around a stable centre (the left panel above). A trace that drifts or sticks is a warning. (Lambert 2018)
Multiple chains — “Bob’s Bees.” Release one bee in a dark house and it may get stuck in a single room, fooling you into thinking it mapped the whole place. Release several bees from different starting points; if they all converge on the same regions, you can trust the map. (Lambert 2018)
\hat R (R-hat). The quantitative version of Bob’s Bees: it compares variation within chains to variation between chains. Convergence is signalled by \hat R \approx 1.0; values much above 1 mean the chains disagree and haven’t converged. (Lambert 2018)

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
The verdict: Bayesian vs frequentist
Lambert’s closing stance is refreshingly even-handed — the two schools are different paths to the same goal of learning from noisy data. (Lambert 2018)
Where Bayesian wins:

Natural uncertainty statements. “95% probability the effect is positive” is what policymakers actually want, and it is a direct Bayesian output. (Lambert 2018)
Prior information. Past studies and expert knowledge enter the model formally rather than informally. (Lambert 2018)
Small samples. Bayesian inference stays coherent when frequentist asymptotic (large-n) approximations break down. (Lambert 2018)
Flexibility. MCMC fits models of essentially arbitrary complexity — hierarchical, multilevel, custom — that have no closed-form frequentist solution. (Lambert 2018)

Where critics push back:

Subjectivity. Choosing a prior strikes some as unscientific — though Lambert’s retort stands: frequentist model and test choices are equally subjective, just less visible. (Lambert 2018)
Computational cost. MCMC can be slow and demanding for big models. (Lambert 2018)







TipLambert’s practical guidance: don’t be a zealot



Start simple — frequentist OLS or maximum likelihood to get an initial feel for the data — then build up to Bayesian models as complexity and the need for rigorous uncertainty quantification grow. And whatever you fit, treat posterior predictive checks (does data simulated from your fitted model resemble the real data?) as your “best friend.” (Lambert 2018)


That is the right note to end Part II on: the tools are not tribes to join but instruments to choose between, by the job in front of you.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong in practice




Calling a confidence interval a credible interval. Only the Bayesian interval licenses “95% probability the parameter is in here.” Don’t smuggle that reading into a frequentist CI. (Lambert 2018)
Trusting un-diagnosed MCMC. A pretty number from a chain that never converged is fiction. Always check trace plots, run multiple chains, and confirm \hat R \approx 1. (Lambert 2018)
Forgetting burn-in. Including the arbitrary starting transient biases every summary. Discard the warm-up. (Lambert 2018)
Using a central interval on a skewed or bimodal posterior. It can include low-density regions and exclude high-density ones; reach for HPD when the shape isn’t symmetric. (Lambert 2018)
Becoming a zealot — either way. Bayesian isn’t automatically “better.” Match the method to the problem; start frequentist, escalate to Bayesian when the question demands it. (Lambert 2018)

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Lambert, A Student’s Guide to Bayesian Statistics — the whole entry: the credible interval and the “95% probability” contrast with the confidence interval (penguin example); equal-tailed vs HPD and the treasure-hunting pirate; the intractable denominator and the 200-dimensional integral; MCMC as a guided random walk, the Metropolis accept/reject rule, and the explorer-in-the-fog analogy; summarising the posterior from samples via the Law of Large Numbers; burn-in, the fuzzy-caterpillar trace, Bob’s Bees / multiple chains, and \hat R \approx 1; the even-handed Bayesian-vs-frequentist verdict and the “start simple, don’t be a zealot, use posterior predictive checks” guidance.
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — the Law of Large Numbers that justifies approximating posterior quantities by sample averages.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
Connections

Builds on: the Bayesian machinery (the intractable denominator that forces sampling), the Bayesian worldview (the credible interval as the payoff of treating the parameter as random), confidence intervals (the frequentist foil), and the Law of Large Numbers (why sample summaries approximate the posterior).
Closes: Part II. You now hold both inferential languages — the Classical Toolkit and the Bayesian alternative — and can choose between them by the problem, not by tribe.
Leads to: Bayesian and frequentist regression in Part III, the hierarchical and regularised models of Part IV (where MCMC and weakly-informative priors are standard), and the uncertainty quantification behind every causal estimate.

title: Credible Intervals, Computation & the Verdict
text:
Study with NotebookLM
Take these prompts to the source library to push past the synthesis above:

“Explain Lambert’s distinction between a credible interval and a confidence interval, and exactly what each one licenses you to say.”
“Walk me through the equal-tailed versus highest-posterior-density interval using the treasure-hunting analogy.”
“Explain the Metropolis algorithm step by step and the explorer-in-the-fog analogy, and show why the denominator cancels.”
“What MCMC diagnostics does Lambert recommend — burn-in, trace plots, multiple chains, R-hat — and what does each catch?”

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
text: You never get to see the population parameter; you only get an estimate of it, computed from one sample by some rule. The whole craft of estimation is learning to evaluate the rule — how far off it runs on average, how much it wobbles, and how to trade a little of one for a lot of the other — because the rule is the only thing you actually control.
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
text:
Why it matters
A pollster reports 52% support. A lab reports a mean reaction time of 340 ms. An economist reports a wage gap of $3.10. Every one of these is an estimate: a single number squeezed out of a single sample by some formula. Run the study again with a fresh sample and every number would shift. So the honest question is never “is this number right?” — you can’t know — but “is the procedure that produced it a good one?”
That shift in attention, from the estimate to the estimator, is the conceptual core of inference. It lets us ask precise questions — Is the procedure right on average? How much does it bounce? Does it home in on the truth as data accumulates? — and it gives us a vocabulary (bias, variance, consistency, efficiency) that will reappear, unchanged, attached to every regression coefficient and every causal effect later in the book.
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
section: The crucial distinction: estimator vs estimate
text:
The crucial distinction: estimator vs estimate
Keep two words rigorously apart. (Wooldridge 2019)

An estimator is a rule — a formula that turns any possible sample into a number. Because the sample is random, the estimator is itself a random variable: feed it different data and it returns different values. The sample mean \bar X = \frac1n\sum X_i is an estimator.
An estimate is the one number the rule spits out for your actual dataset. “52%” is an estimate.







TipThe recipe and the cake



The estimator is a recipe; the estimate is the cake you baked this morning. The recipe is fixed, but this morning’s flour and eggs (your particular sample) give a cake slightly different from tomorrow’s. We write the parameter as \theta and the estimator with a hat, \hat\theta — the hat is a promise that the thing underneath is random. (Wooldridge 2019; Ross 2018)


Because \hat\theta is a random variable, it has a sampling distribution (the idea from the CLT entry), and every property we care about is a property of that distribution. Three of them matter most.
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
text:
Property 1 — Bias: right on average?
An estimator is unbiased if its expected value equals the parameter it targets: \mathbb{E}[\hat\theta] = \theta. The bias is the gap,

\operatorname{Bias}(\hat\theta) = \mathbb{E}[\hat\theta] - \theta .

Unbiased does not mean “correct.” It means that if you drew infinitely many samples and averaged all their estimates, you would land exactly on the truth — the procedure has no systematic tilt, even though any single estimate misses. (Wooldridge 2019) The headline example: the sample mean \bar X is an unbiased estimator of the population mean \mu whatever the underlying distribution. (Wooldridge 2019)
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
section: Property 2 — Variance: how much does it wobble?
text:
Property 2 — Variance: how much does it wobble?
An unbiased estimator that swings wildly from sample to sample is still nearly useless. The sampling variance measures that swing — the spread of the estimator’s distribution — and its square root is the standard error, the typical distance an estimate falls from its own average. (Ross 2018) For the sample mean,

\operatorname{Var}(\bar X) = \frac{\sigma^2}{n},

which is the engine behind the whole book: precision rises with n, and the standard error falls like \sigma/\sqrt n. Bias asks where the estimator points; variance asks how steadily.
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
section: Property 3 — Mean squared error and the great trade-off
text:
Property 3 — Mean squared error and the great trade-off
Real choices are rarely “unbiased vs biased.” They are “a clean estimator that wobbles a lot vs a slightly-off one that barely moves.” To choose, you need a yardstick that counts both sins. That yardstick is the mean squared error, and it splits cleanly:

\operatorname{MSE}(\hat\theta)
= \underbrace{\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta)}_{\text{wobble}}
+ \underbrace{\big[\operatorname{Bias}(\hat\theta)\big]^2}_{\text{tilt}} .

This identity — MSE = variance + bias² — is one of the most important in all of statistics, and it is the seed of the bias–variance trade-off that organises the whole of predictive modelling in Part IV. (Wooldridge 2019) It says the best estimator is the one with the smallest total error, and that can mean accepting a little bias to kill a lot of variance.
Two source examples pin down the extremes. Wooldridge’s cautionary one: the “estimator” that always guesses 0 has zero variance but enormous bias — perfectly precise, perfectly useless. (Wooldridge 2019) And Ross’s constructive one: a shrinkage estimator that pulls the sample mean gently toward zero is slightly biased, yet can have a smaller MSE than the unbiased sample mean when the true value is modest relative to the noise. (Ross 2018)

See the trade-off
Below, two estimators chase the same true mean (\mu = 1, marked). One is the ordinary sample mean — dead-centred but wide. The other shrinks it halfway to zero — visibly biased (shifted left) but far tighter. Watch which one ends up closer to the truth more often.


Show the simulation code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
mu, sigma, n = 1.0, 3.0, 10
reps = 50_000

samples = rng.normal(mu, sigma, size=(reps, n))
xbar   = samples.mean(axis=1)      # unbiased estimator
shrink = 0.5 * xbar                # biased toward 0, lower variance

def mse(est):  return np.mean((est - mu) ** 2)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.6))
bins = np.linspace(-3, 5, 80)
ax.hist(xbar,   bins=bins, density=True, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.75,
        label=f"Sample mean  X̄   (bias 0,  MSE ≈ {mse(xbar):.2f})")
ax.hist(shrink, bins=bins, density=True, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.85,
        label=f"Shrinkage  ½·X̄   (bias −0.5, MSE ≈ {mse(shrink):.2f})")
ax.axvline(mu, color="#b5446e", ls="--", lw=1.8, label="True mean μ = 1")
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_xlabel("Estimated value")
ax.set_title("Unbiased isn't always best — total error is what counts")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Two estimators of the same mean μ = 1 (dashed line), over 50,000 samples of n = 10. The sample mean (grey) is unbiased but wide; the shrinkage estimator ½·X̄ (blue) is biased toward 0 yet tighter — and here it wins on total error (MSE), the sum of variance and squared bias.





The shrinkage estimator is wrong on average — its cloud is centred at 0.5, not 1 — yet because it wobbles so much less, its squared misses are smaller on the whole. When the signal is weak relative to the noise, a confident-but-slightly-biased rule beats an honest-but-jittery one. This is not a trick; it is the logic behind ridge regression, James–Stein estimation, and every regularised model in Part IV.
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
section: Two more properties for large samples
text:
Two more properties for large samples
Consistency is the minimal demand we place on any serious estimator: as the sample grows without bound, the estimator’s whole distribution should collapse onto the true value. (Wooldridge 2019) Where unbiasedness is about averaging over many small samples, consistency is about one sample getting huge. The two can come apart: the sample standard deviation S is a consistent estimator of \sigma yet is biased in small samples — because the expectation of a square root is not the square root of an expectation, taking \sqrt{\,\cdot\,} bends the estimate. (Wooldridge 2019) An estimator that isn’t even consistent is hard to defend at all.
Efficiency ranks the survivors. Among unbiased estimators, the efficient one is the minimum-variance one — it extracts the most precision from the same data. The gold standard is the Minimum-Variance Unbiased Estimator (MVUE). (Ross 2018) In the regression setting this becomes the celebrated Gauss–Markov theorem: under its assumptions, Ordinary Least Squares is BLUE — the Best Linear Unbiased Estimator, “best” meaning smallest variance — a result we cash in fully in Part III. (Wooldridge 2019)
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
section: Where estimators come from: two recipes
text:
Where estimators come from: two recipes
So far we have judged estimators. Where do they come from? Two general recipes generate most of them.
Method of moments (MoM) — the intuitive one. A population moment (the mean \mu, the variance \sigma^2) is some function of the parameter you want. So replace each population moment with its sample counterpart and solve. The population mean’s sample counterpart is the sample average, which is exactly why \bar X is the method-of-moments estimator of \mu. (Wooldridge 2019) Simple, general, and the historical starting point.
Maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) — the powerful one, and the workhorse of modern modelling. Fix a probability model, observe your data, then choose the parameter values that make the data you actually saw as probable as possible. (Ross 2018) Lambert frames the logic as an inversion: from the effects (data) we reason backward to the most likely cause (parameter). (Lambert 2018)






TipMLE in one coin flip



Flip a coin 10 times, see 7 heads. What bias p best explains that? The likelihood of 7 heads is \binom{10}{7}p^7(1-p)^3; maximise it over p and the answer is exactly \hat p = 7/10 = 0.7. The maximum-likelihood estimate is just the sample proportion — the value that makes “7 heads out of 10” the least surprising thing that could have happened. (Ross 2018; Lambert 2018)
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
section: Why divide by n-1? Degrees of freedom
text:
Why divide by n-1? Degrees of freedom
The most-asked question in introductory statistics: why is the sample variance

S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \bar X)^2

divided by n-1 and not n? Because dividing by n systematically underestimates the true variance — it is biased. (Wooldridge 2019) The intuition is degrees of freedom. To measure spread you must first compute the sample mean \bar X from the very same data; that step spends one piece of information and imposes a constraint (the deviations X_i - \bar X must sum to zero). Once n-1 of the deviations are known, the last is forced. More tellingly: each X_i sits closer to its own sample mean \bar X than to the unseen true mean \mu, so the squared deviations come out too small. Dividing by the smaller n-1 inflates them by exactly the right amount to make \mathbb{E}[S^2] = \sigma^2. (Wooldridge 2019) (A formal proof is in the appendix.)
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong in practice




Judging the estimate instead of the estimator. A single estimate is one draw from a distribution; it can be unlucky even when the procedure is excellent. Evaluate the rule’s long-run behaviour, not whether this one number “looks right.” (Wooldridge 2019)
Worshipping unbiasedness. Unbiased estimators can have ruinous variance. A slightly biased estimator with far lower variance has lower MSE and is genuinely better — the entire premise of regularisation. (Ross 2018; Wooldridge 2019)
Confusing standard deviation with standard error. \sigma (and its estimate S) describes the data’s spread; the standard error \sigma/\sqrt n describes the estimator’s spread. Same confusion that haunts the CLT entry; same cost.
Assuming consistency implies unbiasedness (or vice versa). They are different limits. S is consistent but biased in small samples; some estimators are unbiased yet inconsistent. Check the one you actually need. (Wooldridge 2019)
Forgetting the model behind MLE. Maximum likelihood is only as good as the distribution you assumed. Choose the wrong model and you efficiently estimate the wrong thing. (Ross 2018)
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — estimator vs estimate and hat notation; unbiasedness and the formal bias definition; the \bar X-is-unbiased result; MSE = variance + bias² and the “always guess 0” trivial estimator; consistency as the minimal requirement; S consistent but small-sample biased; method of moments; Gauss–Markov / BLUE; the n-1 degrees-of-freedom argument.
Ross, A First Course in Probability — the estimator as a random variable; the shrinkage estimator with smaller MSE; efficiency and the minimum-variance unbiased estimator; maximum likelihood and the coin-bias example.
Lambert, A Student’s Guide to Bayesian Statistics — MLE as inversion from effects (data) back to likely causes (parameters); the bridge to Bayesian inference.
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — sampling-distribution intuition underlying estimator behaviour.
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
text:
Appendix · the full mathematics

The MSE decomposition. Add and subtract \mathbb{E}[\hat\theta] inside the square:

\operatorname{MSE}(\hat\theta) = \mathbb{E}\big[(\hat\theta - \theta)^2\big]
= \mathbb{E}\Big[\big((\hat\theta - \mathbb{E}\hat\theta) + (\mathbb{E}\hat\theta - \theta)\big)^2\Big].

Expand. The cross term is 2(\mathbb{E}\hat\theta - \theta)\,\mathbb{E}[\hat\theta - \mathbb{E}\hat\theta] = 0 because \mathbb{E}[\hat\theta - \mathbb{E}\hat\theta] = 0. What survives is

\operatorname{MSE}(\hat\theta) = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\big[(\hat\theta - \mathbb{E}\hat\theta)^2\big]}_{\operatorname{Var}(\hat\theta)}
+ \underbrace{(\mathbb{E}\hat\theta - \theta)^2}_{\operatorname{Bias}^2}.

Why n-1 is unbiased. With X_i i.i.d. (\mathbb{E}X_i=\mu, \operatorname{Var}X_i=\sigma^2), use the algebraic identity \sum_i (X_i-\bar X)^2 = \sum_i (X_i-\mu)^2 - n(\bar X-\mu)^2. Take expectations: \mathbb{E}\sum_i(X_i-\mu)^2 = n\sigma^2, and \mathbb{E}[(\bar X-\mu)^2]=\operatorname{Var}(\bar X)=\sigma^2/n, so

\mathbb{E}\!\left[\sum_i (X_i-\bar X)^2\right] = n\sigma^2 - n\cdot\frac{\sigma^2}{n} = (n-1)\sigma^2 .

Dividing by n-1 — not n — therefore gives \mathbb{E}[S^2]=\sigma^2. The “lost” \sigma^2/n is exactly the degree of freedom consumed by estimating \mu with \bar X.
MLE of a coin’s bias. The log-likelihood for k heads in n flips is \ell(p) = k\log p + (n-k)\log(1-p) + \text{const}. Differentiate and set to zero: \ell'(p) = \frac{k}{p} - \frac{n-k}{1-p} = 0 \Rightarrow \hat p = k/n. For k=7, n=10, \hat p = 0.7 — the sample proportion is the maximum-likelihood estimate. The second derivative is negative, confirming a maximum.
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
text:
Connections

Builds on: sampling distributions & the CLT (an estimator has a sampling distribution; that is what bias and variance describe), expectation & variance, and sampling design (a good estimator on a bad sample is still biased).
Leads to: confidence intervals (a point estimate plus a measured margin) and hypothesis testing (is the estimate surprising under a null?). The MSE decomposition is the seed of the bias–variance trade-off in Part IV.
Reappears in: every regression coefficient (OLS is BLUE by Gauss–Markov) and every causal estimate, each of which is an estimator to be judged by exactly these criteria.
crumbs: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess

title: Estimation: From Sample to Best Guess
text:

title: II · Inference
text:
Inference is the discipline of saying something about a population you cannot fully see, using a sample you can, while being honest about the uncertainty. Every tool here is a way of reading the sampling distribution — the idea that turns a single number into a claim.
This part is built as a stand-alone textbook on inference, in two sub-chapters. The first walks the dominant classical (frequentist) paradigm end to end — from drawing a sample to bounding an estimate to testing a claim, and on to the ways the whole edifice gets abused. The second is a dedicated treatment of Bayesian inference, so you leave genuinely bilingual rather than with a footnote.

title: II · Inference
text:
A recurring theme
This part carries the manual’s critical conscience: Statistics Done Wrong sits beside every test, because knowing how inference is misused (p-hacking, optional stopping, underpowered studies) is part of knowing how to use it — the thread that the replication-crisis entry gathers into one rigorous account.

title: II · Inference
text:
Where this leads
Inference is the grammar that Part III · Regression speaks: a regression coefficient is an estimate, and everything you learn here about uncertainty attaches to it.

title: Multiple Testing & the False-Discovery Rate
text: A single test at \alpha=0.05 risks a false positive 5% of the time. Run twenty and the odds of at least one fluke “significant” leap to 64%; run a hundred and it’s a near-certain 99%. This is the multiple-comparisons problem, and it quietly invalidates a huge amount of published research. The fixes trade off two philosophies: control the chance of any false positive (Bonferroni), or control the fraction of your discoveries that are false (the false-discovery rate).

title: Multiple Testing & the False-Discovery Rate
text:
Why it matters
The logic is almost tautological: the more questions you ask of a dataset, the more likely you are to find a “significant” answer purely by chance. (Reinhart 2015) Modern research asks many questions — dozens of outcomes, subgroups, model specifications — so the nominal 5% error rate is a fiction unless you account for the multiplicity. This is one of the central engines of the replication crisis, and spotting it is a core part of reading any study critically.

title: Multiple Testing & the False-Discovery Rate
text:
The arithmetic of luck
If you run m independent tests of true nulls at level \alpha, the chance of at least one false positive is 1-(1-\alpha)^m: (Reinhart 2015)

m=20 at \alpha=0.05: 1-0.95^{20}\approx \mathbf{0.64} — a 64% chance of a spurious “finding.”
m=100: \approx \mathbf{0.99}.



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

m = np.arange(1, 101)
p_any = 1 - (1 - 0.05)**m
# Under Bonferroni each test uses alpha/m, so familywise stays ~ 1-(1-0.05/m)^m -> ~0.05
p_bonf = 1 - (1 - 0.05/m)**m

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.0))
ax.plot(m, p_any, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.6, label="uncorrected: P(≥1 false positive)")
ax.plot(m, p_bonf, color="#c0392b", lw=2.4, label="Bonferroni (familywise ≈ 0.05)")
ax.axhline(0.05, color="#9aa0a6", ls=":", lw=1.2)
for mm in (20, 100):
    ax.plot(mm, 1-(1-0.05)**mm, "o", color="#1f3a5f")
    ax.annotate(f"{1-(1-0.05)**mm:.0%}", (mm, 1-(1-0.05)**mm), textcoords="offset points",
                xytext=(-28, -2), fontsize=9, color="#1f3a5f")
ax.set_xlabel("number of tests m"); ax.set_ylabel("P(at least one false positive)")
ax.set_title("Run enough tests and a 'significant' fluke is nearly guaranteed")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, frameon=False, loc="center right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The 5% promise erodes fast. With every null true, the probability of at least one ‘significant’ result by chance, 1−(1−0.05)^m, climbs to 64% by 20 tests and 99% by 100. Bonferroni (red) holds the familywise error at 5% by testing each hypothesis at the far stricter α/m — restoring the guarantee at the cost of power.











NoteGreen jelly beans, and the garden of forking paths



Reinhart’s favourite illustration is the xkcd cartoon: scientists find “no link” between acne and 19 colours of jelly bean, then a link with green ones (p<0.05) — splashed as a headline. They tested 20 colours, so one lucky anomaly was nearly guaranteed. (Reinhart 2015) The subtler cousin is the garden of forking paths: even without running 20 explicit tests, a chain of individually defensible choices — which covariates to include, which outliers to drop — lets different analysts reach different “significant” conclusions from the same data. The multiplicity is hidden in the analysis decisions. (Reinhart 2015)

title: Multiple Testing & the False-Discovery Rate
section: Fix 1 — control the familywise error rate (Bonferroni)
text:
Fix 1 — control the familywise error rate (Bonferroni)
The familywise error rate (FWER) is the probability of making at least one Type I error across the whole family of tests. (Reinhart 2015) The Bonferroni correction controls it by brute force: test each hypothesis at \alpha' = \alpha/m. (Reinhart 2015) For 20 tests at the 0.05 level, you only reject when p<0.0025. It works — the familywise error stays near 5% — but it is severely conservative: dividing \alpha by m slashes power, so you miss many real effects. (Reinhart 2015) (Holm’s step-down procedure is a uniformly more powerful way to control the same FWER.)

title: Multiple Testing & the False-Discovery Rate
text:
Fix 2 — control the false-discovery rate (Benjamini–Hochberg)
When you’re screening thousands of hypotheses (genomics, A/B tests, brain imaging), FWER control is too strict — you’d find nothing. The false-discovery rate (FDR) changes the target: instead of “never make a single false positive,” it controls the expected fraction of your declared discoveries that are false. (Reinhart 2015) The Benjamini–Hochberg procedure sorts the p-values and applies a sliding threshold, guaranteeing that, on average, no more than a chosen fraction q (say 10%) of your “discoveries” are flukes. (Reinhart 2015) It is far less conservative than Bonferroni — you accept a known, small rate of false discoveries in exchange for catching many more true effects. The right tool depends on the cost of a false positive: FWER when one wrong call is disastrous, FDR when you’re generating leads to follow up.

title: Multiple Testing & the False-Discovery Rate
section: The link to p-hacking and replication
text:
The link to p-hacking and replication
Multiplicity is the mechanism behind p-hacking — selectively reporting, or “dredging” the data, until something clears p<0.05. (Reinhart 2015) Unreported multiplicity is why so many findings fail to replicate: the pharmaceutical company Bayer could fully replicate only 14 of 67 highly-cited studies. (Reinhart 2015) Pre-registration and correction for multiple comparisons are the structural antidotes — the subject of the replication-crisis and open-science entries.

title: Multiple Testing & the False-Discovery Rate
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reporting the one significant result out of many. Twenty outcomes, one “hit” — that’s the jelly-bean fallacy. Correct for all the tests you ran, not just the winner. (Reinhart 2015)
Hidden multiplicity in flexible analysis. The garden of forking paths inflates false positives even with a single reported test; pre-register the analysis to close it. (Reinhart 2015)
Defaulting to Bonferroni on huge screens. It’s so conservative you’ll miss real effects; use FDR/BH when generating hypotheses. (Reinhart 2015)
Treating FDR like FWER. FDR allows a controlled fraction of false discoveries — appropriate for screening, not for a single high-stakes confirmatory test. (Reinhart 2015)

title: Multiple Testing & the False-Discovery Rate
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — multiple comparisons as “the more questions you ask, the more likely a significant answer by chance”; the 1-(1-\alpha)^m arithmetic (64% at 20, 99% at 100); the green jelly beans (xkcd) and the garden of forking paths; FWER and the Bonferroni (\alpha/m) correction and its power cost; the false-discovery rate and Benjamini–Hochberg sliding threshold; p-hacking/dredging and the Bayer 14/67 replication failure.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — multiple comparisons in the data-science / A/B-testing idiom and FDR for large-scale screening.

title: Multiple Testing & the False-Discovery Rate
text:
Connections

Builds on: hypothesis testing (the single-test \alpha this multiplies) and statistical power (what corrections cost).
Leads to: the replication crisis and questionable research practices (p-hacking, the garden of forking paths) and their fix in open science.
Reappears in: large-scale ML/feature screening and any study with many outcomes or subgroups.

title: Multiple Testing & the False-Discovery Rate
text:
Study with NotebookLM

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text: Every test in the last few entries leaned on an assumption — usually Normality, or a large enough sample for the CLT to rescue you. Real social data is skewed, outlier-ridden, ordinal, and often small. Resampling and rank-based tests throw the assumptions out: they build the null distribution from your own data by shuffling it, or sidestep distribution entirely by working with ranks. Often the most honest p-value is one you simulated.

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text:
Why it matters
The t-test, ANOVA, and the Normal-approximation proportion tests all assume something about the shape of the data or the size of the sample. (Bruce et al. 2020) When income is log-skewed, when a few outliers dominate, when responses are 1–5 Likert ratings, or when n is simply too small for the CLT to kick in, those assumptions fail — and the p-values they produce can’t be trusted. (Bruce et al. 2020; Martin and Bridgmon 2012)
Two families fix this. Resampling tests (permutation and bootstrap) manufacture the null distribution directly from the observed data, assuming almost nothing. Rank-based non-parametric tests discard the raw numbers for their ranks, immunising against skew and outliers. Together they are the assumption-light backbone of modern data analysis — and the engine behind every A/B test.

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text:
The permutation test
The permutation (or randomization) test is the most intuitive idea in all of inference. (Bruce et al. 2020) Its logic: if the two groups are really the same, the labels are arbitrary. “Page A” versus “Page B” is meaningless under the null, so you can shuffle the labels and the data shouldn’t care. Do that thousands of times, and you see how big a group difference chance alone manufactures — then ask where your real difference falls in that pile. (Bruce et al. 2020)
The procedure: (Bruce et al. 2020)

Combine both groups into one pooled dataset.
Shuffle and deal out new groups of the original sizes.
Recompute the statistic (e.g. difference in means) on the shuffled groups.
Repeat thousands of times to build the permutation distribution — the null, made from your own data.
The p-value is the fraction of shuffles whose difference equals or exceeds the one you actually observed.

Because the null distribution is built from the data, the test needs almost no distributional assumptions — no Normality, no minimum sample size. (Bruce et al. 2020)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(5)
# Two groups of skewed session times (lognormal); B a bit higher than A.
group_a = rng.lognormal(4.7, 0.6, 21)
group_b = rng.lognormal(4.9, 0.6, 15)
obs_diff = group_b.mean() - group_a.mean()

pooled = np.concatenate([group_a, group_b])
nA = len(group_a)
n_perm = 10_000
perm_diffs = np.empty(n_perm)
for i in range(n_perm):
    rng.shuffle(pooled)
    perm_diffs[i] = pooled[nA:].mean() - pooled[:nA].mean()

p_val = np.mean(np.abs(perm_diffs) >= abs(obs_diff))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.7))
ax.hist(perm_diffs, bins=60, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.8)
ax.axvline(obs_diff, color="#c0392b", lw=2.0)
ax.axvline(-abs(obs_diff), color="#c0392b", lw=1.0, ls=":")
ax.text(obs_diff, ax.get_ylim()[1]*0.9, f"  observed = {obs_diff:.1f}s",
        color="#c0392b", fontsize=9)
ax.set_title(f"Permutation null distribution — p ≈ {p_val:.3f}")
ax.set_xlabel("difference in group means under shuffled labels")
ax.set_yticks([])
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A permutation test in action. The grey histogram is the null distribution of the difference in group means, built by shuffling the combined data 10,000 times. The red line is the actually-observed difference. The p-value is the share of shuffles at least as extreme (shaded) — here a middling value, so the observed gap is well within what chance produces.











NoteBruce & Bruce’s web-stickiness test



A company asked whether Page A or Page B held visitors longer. Page A: 21 visitors, mean 126.33 seconds; Page B: 15 visitors, mean 162.00 seconds — an observed gap of 35.67 seconds. They pooled all 36 session times, reshuffled them into groups of 21 and 15 1,000 times, and found a difference that large arose by chance about 12.4% of the time (p = 0.124). The verdict: not significant — the 35.67-second lead can’t be distinguished from luck. No Normality was ever assumed. (Bruce et al. 2020)


Full vs randomized. An exhaustive permutation test enumerates every possible relabeling for an exact p-value — feasible only for small samples. For anything larger, a randomized test uses many random shuffles (10,000, say) and lands at a near-identical answer. (Bruce et al. 2020) The figure above is the randomized kind.

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text:
A/B testing: the framework underneath
An A/B test is just a randomized experiment in digital dress: group A gets the control, group B the new treatment, subjects are randomly assigned, and you compare a pre-chosen metric. (Bruce et al. 2020) Two disciplines make it trustworthy:

Randomization ensures any group difference is due to the treatment or to chance — never to systematic bias in who got what. (Bruce et al. 2020) (The same logic that powers causal inference.)
A single, pre-specified test statistic (conversion rate, click rate, session time) chosen before the test — so you can’t fish through metrics for one that’s significant. (Bruce et al. 2020)

And how do you analyse the result? Usually with exactly the permutation and bootstrap machinery of this entry — letting the data gauge chance variation without leaning on a formula. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text:
The bootstrap hypothesis test
The bootstrap you met building confidence intervals can also test. (Bruce et al. 2020) The twist: to test a null, first transform the data so the null is true — e.g. shift the two groups to share a common mean — then resample with replacement and see how often a resampled difference reaches your observed one. (Bruce et al. 2020) The contrast with the CI is worth fixing:

A bootstrap confidence interval measures a statistic’s variability to locate the true parameter.
A bootstrap hypothesis test computes the probability of your result if the null held.

Same resampling engine, aimed at two different questions. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text:
Rank-based non-parametric tests
The classical distribution-free alternatives predate the computer: replace each value by its rank (1st, 2nd, 3rd…) and test on the ranks. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012) Ranks are immune to skew and outliers — the largest value is just “rank n,” however extreme — and they work for inherently ordinal data like Likert scales. Each parametric test has a rank twin:



Parametric test
Rank-based (distribution-free) twin
Compares




Two-sample t-test
Mann–Whitney U / Wilcoxon rank-sum
two independent groups


Paired t-test
Wilcoxon signed-rank
before/after, matched pairs


One-way ANOVA
Kruskal–Wallis
three or more groups



Reach for them when the data is skewed, outlier-heavy, ordinal (Likert), or so small that Normality can’t be checked. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012) They ask a subtly different question — whether one group tends to rank higher than another — but for most purposes they answer “are these groups different?” without the parametric baggage.

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
section: Multi-arm bandits: testing that optimises as it goes
text:
Multi-arm bandits: testing that optimises as it goes
A brief look forward. A fixed A/B test runs to completion before you act — exposing many users to the losing version meanwhile. Multi-arm bandit algorithms instead learn in real time: they start by showing variants equally, then shift traffic toward whichever “arm” is winning, while still occasionally exploring the others to confirm the trend. (Bruce et al. 2020) The epsilon-greedy algorithm is the classic. The aim shifts from “cleanly measure the difference” to “maximise payoff during the experiment” — trading some inferential tidiness for fewer users sent to the inferior option. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Defaulting to the t-test on ugly data. Heavy skew, wild outliers, or tiny n break the parametric assumptions; a permutation or rank test is the honest call. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Too few permutations. A handful of shuffles gives a noisy p-value. Use thousands (10,000 is common) so the permutation distribution is smooth. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Forgetting to impose the null in a bootstrap test. Unlike a bootstrap CI, a bootstrap test requires shifting the data so the null holds first — skip it and you’re not testing anything. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Choosing the metric after seeing the data. A/B tests demand a pre-specified statistic; picking the winning metric post-hoc is p-hacking. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Treating a rank test as identical to its parametric twin. Rank tests compare typical ranks, not means; with very different group shapes they answer a slightly different question. Know what you’re claiming. (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — the permutation/randomization test logic and procedure; the web-stickiness example (Page A n=21 mean 126.33; Page B n=15 mean 162.00; observed 35.67s; p=0.124 over 1,000 shuffles); full vs randomized permutation; the A/B testing framework (control/treatment, randomization, pre-specified metric) analysed by resampling; the bootstrap hypothesis test and how it differs from the bootstrap CI; multi-arm bandits and epsilon-greedy.
Martin & Bridgmon, Quantitative & Statistical Research Methods — rank-based non-parametric tests as distribution-free alternatives: Mann–Whitney/Wilcoxon rank-sum, Wilcoxon signed-rank, Kruskal–Wallis, and when to use them (skew, outliers, ordinal data, small samples).

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The permutation p-value. Let t_{\text{obs}} be the observed statistic and t_1,\dots,t_M the values from M random relabelings. The (two-sided) p-value is

\hat p = \frac{1 + \#\{m : |t_m| \ge |t_{\text{obs}}|\}}{1 + M},

the “+1” counting the observed arrangement itself (so \hat p is never exactly 0 and the test is valid). Its justification is exchangeability: under the null, every relabeling of the pooled data is equally likely, so t_{\text{obs}} is just one draw from the permutation distribution.
Why it needs no Normality. The reference distribution is the empirical distribution of t over relabelings, not a theoretical t or F curve. Nothing about the population shape is assumed — only that, under H_0, the labels are exchangeable.
Bootstrap test vs CI. For two groups, recentre each to a common mean (impose H_0: \tilde x_i = x_i - \bar x_g + \bar x_{\text{pool}}), resample each group with replacement B times, and compute the proportion of |\bar x_B^{*1}-\bar x_B^{*2}| \ge |t_{\text{obs}}|. Contrast with the percentile CI, which resamples the un-shifted data and reads off the 2.5th/97.5th percentiles — same engine, null imposed in the test but not in the interval.
Rank-sum sketch. Mann–Whitney pools the two groups, ranks all N values, and sums the ranks W in one group. Under H_0, W has mean n_1(N+1)/2 and a known variance, giving a Normal approximation for large samples and exact tables for small ones — all from ranks, no raw-value distribution required.

title: Resampling & Distribution-Free Tests
text:
Connections

Builds on: hypothesis testing & p-values (the null/p-value logic it reimplements by simulation), confidence intervals (the bootstrap, here turned into a test), and the Law of Large Numbers (why thousands of shuffles converge on the true permutation distribution).
Sits beside: ANOVA, chi-square & proportions — the parametric tests these methods replace when assumptions fail.
Leads to: A/B tests and randomized experiments in the causal summit, where randomization plus resampling is the cleanest inference there is.

text:

“Map each parametric test to its rank-based non-parametric twin (Mann–Whitney, Wilcoxon signed-rank, Kruskal–Wallis) and explain when ranks are preferable.”
