title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text: Every statistic you compute is a random variable in disguise: run the study again and you’d get a slightly different number. The sampling distribution is the set of numbers you could have gotten, and the Central Limit Theorem tells you that — for averages — that set is almost always a bell curve.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Why it matters
You run a survey, compute an average, and report it. But you only ran the survey once. Had a different 1,200 people answered the phone, your average would have come out a little different. So the real question behind every empirical claim is: how much would my answer have bounced around if chance had dealt a different sample?
That bounce is not a nuisance to apologize for — it is the thing inference is about. Margins of error, confidence intervals, p-values, standard errors: every one of them is a statement about a sampling distribution. Get this idea and the rest of statistics stops being a bag of formulas and becomes one question asked many ways.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Intuition
There are two different distributions that beginners constantly confuse, and keeping them apart is most of the battle. (Bruce et al. 2020)

A data distribution describes the individual values in one dataset — the heights of the people in your sample, say. Plot a histogram of your data and that’s what you see.
A sampling distribution describes how a summary of the data — the mean, a proportion, a treatment effect — would vary across many hypothetical datasets drawn the same way.

The leap is this: a statistic is a function of random data, so it is itself random. The sample mean \bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i is a sum of random variables, and a sum of random variables has its own probability distribution. (Ross 2018) Grinstead & Snell make this concrete by simulation: keep drawing samples, compute the mean each time, and watch those means scatter into a shape of their own. (Grinstead and Snell 1997) In an experiment, Gerber & Green give the cleanest gloss — the sampling distribution is “the collection of estimates that could have been generated by every possible random assignment.” That is exactly what lets you ask whether your result was a lucky draw. (Gerber and Green 2012)

Watch it happen
The most convincing argument here is not a proof — it’s a picture. Start with a population that is emphatically not bell-shaped (a heavily skewed one), draw samples, and plot the distribution of the sample mean as the sample size grows.


Show the simulation code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

# A strongly right-skewed "population": an exponential distribution.
# (Think: income, donation sizes, time-to-event — common in social data.)
pop_mean = 1.0
population = rng.exponential(scale=pop_mean, size=200_000)

sample_sizes = [2, 10, 30]
n_reps = 50_000

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(11, 3.1))

# Panel 0: the raw population
axes[0].hist(population, bins=60, range=(0, 6), color="#9a6a2f", alpha=0.85)
axes[0].set_title("Population\n(raw data, skewed)", fontsize=10)
axes[0].set_yticks([])

# Panels 1-3: sampling distribution of the mean at increasing n
for ax, n in zip(axes[1:], sample_sizes):
    means = population[rng.integers(0, population.size, size=(n_reps, n))].mean(axis=1)
    ax.hist(means, bins=60, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.85, density=True)
    ax.set_title(f"Mean of n = {n}", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_yticks([])
    # overlay the Normal the CLT predicts: mean = pop_mean, sd = pop_sd / sqrt(n)
    pop_sd = population.std()
    xs = np.linspace(means.min(), means.max(), 200)
    se = pop_sd / np.sqrt(n)
    normal = np.exp(-0.5 * ((xs - pop_mean) / se) ** 2) / (se * np.sqrt(2 * np.pi))
    ax.plot(xs, normal, color="#b5446e", lw=1.8)

fig.suptitle("The sample mean forgets the shape it came from", fontsize=12)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A skewed population (left) versus the sampling distribution of its mean at n = 2, 10, and 30. The raw data is lopsided, yet the averages march toward a bell.





The leftmost panel is the world as it actually is: lopsided. Yet by the time we average just 30 observations, the distribution of that average is almost perfectly Normal (the pink curve is the bell the theorem predicts). The sample mean forgets the shape it came from. That forgetting is the Central Limit Theorem.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
In plain language: if you add up or average enough independent random pieces, the result is approximately Normal — no matter what distribution the pieces came from. Francis Galton called it the “supreme law of unreason”: order emerging reliably out of individual chaos. (Grinstead and Snell 1997)
Stated carefully: let X_1, X_2, \dots be independent and identically distributed with finite mean \mu and finite variance \sigma^2. Then as n \to \infty, the standardized sample mean converges in distribution to a standard Normal:

Z_n \;=\; \frac{\bar{X}_n - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \;\xrightarrow{d}\; \mathcal{N}(0, 1).

Two things deserve emphasis, because they are where the theorem is most often misremembered:

What it needs: independence, and a finite mean and variance. It does not need the data to be Normal — that’s the whole point. A version even allows the pieces to have different distributions, as long as no single one dominates the sum. (Ross 2018)
How fast: remarkably fast for symmetric data — a fair die’s average looks bell-shaped by n \approx 10. The famous “n \ge 30” is only a rule of thumb, and a bad one for heavily skewed data, which can need far more. (Bruce et al. 2020; Reinhart 2015)







NoteThe LeBron James of statistics (Wheelan)



Charles Wheelan calls the CLT “the LeBron James of statistics” — a near-“miracle” that is the power source for almost all inference. (Wheelan 2013) His intuition pump: a bus of marathon runners goes missing, and you find a broken-down bus whose passengers average over 220 pounds. Without checking a single ID you radio in — “I think it’s the wrong bus. Keep looking” — because a random sample of 60 marathoners being that heavy is astronomically unlikely. (The bus was bound for the International Festival of Sausage.) (Wheelan 2013) That reflex is the CLT: the average of a sample has a predictable, bell-shaped spread, so an average far in its tail signals the sample didn’t come from the population you assumed — “consistent with” it, or an “aberration.” The crescendo, in Wheelan’s word: it holds even when the underlying population is not Normal (household income, say). (Wheelan 2013)
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Standard deviation vs standard error
This is the second great confusion, and it costs people dearly. (Reinhart 2015)

Standard deviation (\sigma) measures the spread of individual data points. How much do people differ from the average person? That’s the SD.
Standard error (SE) measures the spread of a statistic across hypothetical samples. It is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution. How much would my estimate bounce around if I redid the study? That’s the SE. (Bruce et al. 2020)

They are linked by the single most useful formula in applied statistics:

\text{SE}(\bar{X}) \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}.

The \sqrt{n} is doing quiet, brutal work. Because uncertainty shrinks with the square root of the sample size, to halve your standard error you must quadruple your sample. This is why precision is expensive, and why a poll of 1,200 is “good enough” while a poll of 4,800 is only twice as good. (Bruce et al. 2020)
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Worked examples
An astronomer measures a distance. (Ross 2018) Atmospheric distortion and human error make any single telescope reading noisy. So the astronomer takes n independent readings and averages them. By the CLT, that average is approximately Normal around the true distance — and its spread, the SE, shrinks like \sigma/\sqrt{n}. More readings, sharper answer.
A pollster samples a nation. (Grinstead and Snell 1997) The CLT is why a sample of roughly 1,200 people pins down a national preference to about a \pm 3\% margin — and, strikingly, why that barely depends on whether the country has 100,000 or 100 million people. It’s the sample size that drives precision, not the population size.
Female village councils in India. (Gerber and Green 2012) When researchers asked whether reserving council-head seats for women shifted budget priorities, they used the sampling distribution of the estimated effect to ask: is the spending difference we observed bigger than what random assignment alone could have produced? That comparison is the inference.
The RAND Health Insurance Experiment. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) Before trusting any treatment effect, the researchers used standard errors to check that groups assigned to different insurance plans were balanced at baseline — comparable starting points, within the bounds of chance.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong in practice




Thinking your data should be Normal. The CLT is about the sampling distribution of a statistic, not your raw observations. Social-science data is routinely skewed and long-tailed (income, donations, durations). Forcing a Normal model onto raw data underestimates extreme “black swan” values. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Trusting n \ge 30 blindly. For strongly skewed data, t-tests and Normal approximations can be badly off even at moderate n — Reinhart’s example is charitable donations, where a few huge gifts wreck the approximation. (Reinhart 2015)
Eyeballing overlapping error bars. Two confidence intervals can overlap and the difference can still be significant. “Your eyeball is not a well-defined statistical procedure” — run the actual test. (Reinhart 2015)
Treating the bootstrap as a cure for small samples. Resampling your own data reveals the variability already present; it does not manufacture information you never collected. It frees you from assuming Normality, not from needing enough data. (Bruce et al. 2020)
The gambler’s fallacy and optional stopping. A run of heads does not make tails “due” — the Law of Large Numbers governs the long run, not short streaks. And peeking at your data repeatedly and stopping the moment p < 0.05 massively inflates false positives. (Grinstead and Snell 1997; Reinhart 2015)
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Ross, A First Course in Probability — the sample mean as a random variable; the formal CLT; the astronomer example.
Grinstead & Snell, Introduction to Probability — CLT via simulation; the polling example; the Law of Large Numbers and gambler’s fallacy.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — data vs sampling distribution; SD vs SE; the bootstrap; the “raw data isn’t Normal” warning.
Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — small-sample failures; overlapping error bars; optional-stopping inflation of false positives.
Gerber & Green, Field Experiments — the sampling distribution as “every possible random assignment”; the India village-council study.
Angrist & Pischke, Mostly Harmless Econometrics — standard errors for baseline balance in the RAND experiment.
Wheelan, Naked Statistics — the CLT as “the LeBron James of statistics” and the marathon-bus intuition for why a sample mean far in the tail signals the wrong population.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Appendix · the full mathematics

Mean and variance of the sample mean. Let X_1,\dots,X_n be i.i.d. with \mathbb{E}[X_i]=\mu and \operatorname{Var}(X_i)=\sigma^2. Then by linearity of expectation,

\mathbb{E}[\bar{X}] = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \mathbb{E}[X_i] = \mu,

so the sample mean is unbiased. For the variance, independence makes the cross terms vanish:

\operatorname{Var}(\bar{X})
= \operatorname{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_i\right)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\sum_{i=1}^n \operatorname{Var}(X_i)
= \frac{1}{n^2}\,(n\sigma^2)
= \frac{\sigma^2}{n}.

Taking the square root gives the standard error, \text{SE}(\bar X)=\sigma/\sqrt{n}, and with it the \sqrt{n} rule.
The Central Limit Theorem (Lindeberg–Lévy form). Define the standardized mean

Z_n = \frac{\bar X_n - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt n} = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt n}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \mu).

Then for every real z,

\lim_{n\to\infty} \Pr(Z_n \le z) = \Phi(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{z} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-t^2/2}\,dt .

Sketch of why. Work with the characteristic function \varphi(t)=\mathbb{E}[e^{itZ_n}]. Writing Y_i=(X_i-\mu)/\sigma (mean 0, variance 1) and using independence,

\varphi_{Z_n}(t) = \left[\varphi_Y\!\left(\tfrac{t}{\sqrt n}\right)\right]^{n}.

A second-order Taylor expansion gives \varphi_Y(s) = 1 - \tfrac{1}{2}s^2 + o(s^2), so

\varphi_{Z_n}(t) = \left[1 - \frac{t^2}{2n} + o\!\left(\tfrac{1}{n}\right)\right]^{n}
\;\longrightarrow\; e^{-t^2/2},

which is the characteristic function of the standard Normal. Lévy’s continuity theorem then delivers convergence in distribution. The finite-variance assumption is exactly what makes the quadratic term — and nothing higher — survive in the limit; when variance is infinite (heavy tails), the limit is a different, non-Normal stable law, which is the deep reason the CLT can fail.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:
Connections

Builds on: random variables, expectation and variance (the \sigma/\sqrt{n} standard error), the Normal distribution, and the Law of Large Numbers (which gives the destination the CLT then puts a shape around).
Leads to: confidence intervals and hypothesis testing, where the sampling distribution becomes the engine of inference.
Reappears in: the standard errors attached to every regression coefficient and every causal estimate.
crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem
text:

crumbs: Sampling Distributions & the Central Limit Theorem

title: The Bootstrap
text: Classical inference needs a formula for the sampling distribution — and for anything past a mean, those formulas get ugly or don’t exist. The bootstrap sidesteps the algebra entirely: treat your sample as a stand-in for the population, resample it with replacement thousands of times, recompute your statistic each time, and read the standard error and confidence interval straight off the spread of those replicates. It works for the median, a correlation, a complicated machine-learning estimator — any statistic — which is why it’s an indispensable, one-size-fits-all tool.

title: The Bootstrap
text:
Why it matters
For a mean, the CLT hands you a standard error. For a median, a correlation, a ratio, or a tangled multi-step estimator, the analytic formula is hard or nonexistent. (Bruce et al. 2020) The bootstrap is the “one size fits all” answer: a computer-intensive method that replaces a derivation with brute-force resampling, giving honest standard errors and confidence intervals for statistics you could never tackle by hand. (Bruce et al. 2020)

title: The Bootstrap
section: The core idea: resample with replacement
text:
The core idea: resample with replacement
You only have one sample — so make it work harder. Treat the observed sample of size n as a “hypothetical population,” and draw a new sample of size n from it, with replacement — a bootstrap sample. (Bruce et al. 2020) Recompute your statistic on that bootstrap sample. Repeat B times (typically 1,000 or 10,000), and the collection of bootstrap replicates approximates the statistic’s sampling distribution. (Bruce et al. 2020) Bruce & Bruce’s image: “imagine replicating the original sample thousands or millions of times so that you have a hypothetical population that embodies all the knowledge from your original sample.” (Bruce et al. 2020)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(5)
sample = rng.exponential(scale=4.0, size=80)        # one skewed sample
B = 5000
boot_medians = np.array([np.median(rng.choice(sample, size=len(sample), replace=True))
                         for _ in range(B)])

se_boot = boot_medians.std()
lo, hi = np.percentile(boot_medians, [2.5, 97.5])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.0))
ax.hist(boot_medians, bins=40, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.85, edgecolor="white")
ax.axvline(np.median(sample), color="#3f8f6f", lw=2.2, label=f"sample median = {np.median(sample):.2f}")
ax.axvline(lo, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.8)
ax.axvline(hi, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.8, label=f"95% percentile CI [{lo:.2f}, {hi:.2f}]")
ax.text(0.02, 0.9, f"bootstrap SE = {se_boot:.2f}", transform=ax.transAxes, fontsize=10,
        color="#1f3a5f", fontweight="bold")
ax.set_xlabel("median of a bootstrap resample"); ax.set_ylabel("frequency")
ax.set_title("5,000 bootstrap medians ≈ the sampling distribution")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The bootstrap distribution of a median. From one skewed sample (n=80), we resample with replacement 5,000 times and recompute the median each time. The histogram of those 5,000 bootstrap medians IS the (approximate) sampling distribution — for a statistic with no easy formula. Its standard deviation is the bootstrap standard error; its 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles (red) are a 95% percentile confidence interval.

title: The Bootstrap
text:
The plug-in principle
Why is resampling your data legitimate? The plug-in principle: in the absence of the true population distribution, use the empirical distribution of the sample — which puts probability 1/n on each observed value — as a stand-in. (Bruce et al. 2020) For large n this empirical distribution is “an arbitrarily good approximation to the actual distribution,” (Ashley 2012) and the intuition is Dayal’s: “if the sample is like the population, then many samples from the sample are representative of many samples from the population.” (Bruce et al. 2020) Resampling the sample mimics resampling the population.

title: The Bootstrap
text:
Standard errors and confidence intervals
Two outputs fall straight out of the replicates: (Bruce et al. 2020)

Bootstrap standard error — just the standard deviation of the bootstrap replicates. It “sums up the variability in the sampling distribution,” and shines for complex estimators with no analytic SE. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Percentile confidence interval — the most intuitive CI: sort the B replicates and trim 2.5% from each end; the 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles are your 95% interval. (Bruce et al. 2020) No Normality assumed — if the bootstrap distribution is skewed, the interval is too.
Bias-corrected (BCa) — because the bootstrap can reveal asymmetry and bias the percentile method ignores, the BCa interval adjusts for both, at the cost of more replicates ($$10,000) to stabilise. (Wooldridge 2019; James et al. 2021)

title: The Bootstrap
section: When it works — and when it fails
text:
When it works — and when it fails
The bootstrap is powerful but “not a panacea.” (Ashley 2012) Know its boundaries: (Ashley 2012)

Works for “standard” statistics — means, variances, regression coefficients, correlations.
Small samples. It “does not compensate for a small sample size.” If n is tiny (say 10), the empirical distribution is a poor stand-in for the population and the bootstrap fails. (Ashley 2012)
Sample extremes. It is “unlikely to be satisfactory” for maxima, minima, or the range — statistics driven by the tails, which a resample can’t reproduce. (Ashley 2012)
Dependent data. A naive bootstrap “fails to replicate the dependence” in time-series or clustered data. The fix is the block bootstrap — resample whole blocks (or clusters) to preserve the internal structure. (Ashley 2012)

title: The Bootstrap
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Bootstrapping a tiny sample. Garbage in, garbage out: the method can’t manufacture information the sample doesn’t contain. (Ashley 2012)
Bootstrapping the max/min/range. Tail-driven statistics break the plug-in approximation; use extreme-value theory instead. (Ashley 2012)
Ignoring dependence. Time-series or clustered data need a block bootstrap; the naive version understates uncertainty. (Ashley 2012)
Too few replicates for a CI. A stable percentile interval (and especially BCa) needs thousands of resamples, not a few hundred. (Wooldridge 2019)

title: The Bootstrap
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — the bootstrap as resampling with replacement to build a “hypothetical population”; $B=$1,000–10,000 replicates; the plug-in / empirical distribution; the bootstrap standard error (SD of replicates) and the percentile CI (trim 2.5% each end); the “one size fits all” virtue for any statistic.
Ashley, Fundamentals of Applied Econometrics — the plug-in justification (“arbitrarily good approximation”); the boundary conditions — small samples, extremes, and dependent data (the block bootstrap fix); “not a panacea.”
Wooldridge / James et al. (ISL) — bias-corrected (BCa) intervals and replicate counts; bootstrapping regression statistics. Grinstead & Snell — the bootstrap as a Monte Carlo technique (“poor man’s” posterior; “spinning gold from straw”).

title: The Bootstrap
text:
Connections

Builds on: sampling distributions & the CLT (what it approximates), estimation (the SE it produces), and Monte Carlo (the bootstrap is a Monte Carlo on the empirical distribution).
Powers: confidence intervals without formulas and the bootstrap hypothesis test.
Reappears in: ML (bagging is bootstrap aggregating; uncertainty for complex estimators).

title: The Bootstrap
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Explain the bootstrap: how does resampling with replacement approximate the sampling distribution?”
“What is the plug-in principle, and why does treating the sample’s empirical distribution as the population justify the bootstrap?”
“How do you get a bootstrap standard error and a percentile confidence interval? What does BCa add?”
“When does the bootstrap fail — small samples, extremes, dependent data — and what is the block bootstrap?”

title: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions
text: “Linear regression” is linear in the parameters, not the variables — which means you can fit curves, percentage effects, group differences, and effects-that-depend-on-other-effects, all with OLS. The catch is interpretation: a coefficient on \log x, on x^2, on a 0/1 dummy, or on an interaction each means something different. This entry is the dictionary.
crumbs: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions

title: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions
text:
Why it matters
Real relationships are rarely straight lines through raw variables. Income is skewed and its effects are percentage effects; experience pays off with diminishing returns; the wage gap is a difference between groups; the return to education might differ by gender. Each is captured by a change of functional form — and each changes how you read \hat\beta. (Wooldridge 2019) Getting this wrong is one of the most common errors in applied work: reporting a log coefficient as a dollar effect, or a quadratic’s first coefficient as “the” effect. The forms below cover the vast majority of applied specifications.
crumbs: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions

title: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions
text:
Logarithms: percentages and elasticities
Taking logs does three jobs at once: it tames skew in positive variables, it encodes constant-percentage effects, and (in log-log form) it yields elasticities. (Wooldridge 2019) The interpretation depends on what is logged:









Model
y
x
Interpretation of \beta_1




Level–level
y
x
\Delta y = \beta_1 \Delta x (a unit change)


Level–log
y
\log x
a 1% rise in x changes y by \beta_1/100


Log–level
\log y
x
a one-unit rise in x changes y by 100\beta_1 percent


Log–log
\log y
\log x
a 1% rise in x changes y by \beta_1 percent — an elasticity



The “100\beta_1 percent” reading is an approximation (the semi-elasticity) that’s excellent for small coefficients; for large ones, use the exact percentage change 100\,[\exp(\hat\beta_1)-1]. (Wooldridge 2019)






NoteTwo Wooldridge workhorses



Returns to education (log–level). \widehat{\log(\text{wage})} = 0.584 + 0.083\,\text{educ} (n=526, R^2=0.186): each extra year of schooling raises wages by about 8.3% — a constant percentage return, which is why log-wage is the standard outcome. (Wooldridge 2019)
CEO pay (log–log). \widehat{\log(\text{salary})} = 4.822 + 0.257\,\log(\text{sales}): the estimated elasticity is 0.257 — a 1% increase in firm sales is associated with a 0.257% increase in CEO salary. (Wooldridge 2019)
crumbs: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions

title: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions
section: Quadratics: diminishing returns and turning points
text:
Quadratics: diminishing returns and turning points
To let an effect change as x grows, add a squared term: (Wooldridge 2019)

y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \beta_2 x^2 + u.

Now the effect of x is not constant — it’s the derivative, \Delta y \approx
(\beta_1 + 2\beta_2 x)\,\Delta x, which depends on where you start. (Wooldridge 2019) When \beta_1 and \beta_2 have opposite signs the curve has a turning point — a peak or trough — at

x^* = \left|\frac{\beta_1}{2\beta_2}\right|.







NoteWage and experience



Wooldridge’s WAGE1 estimate: \widehat{\text{wage}} = 3.73 + 0.298\,\text{exper} - 0.0061\,\text{exper}^2. The first year of experience adds about 30 cents, but the negative squared term means each further year adds less. The peak is at x^* = 0.298/[2(0.0061)] \approx \mathbf{24.4} years — after which predicted wages actually decline. Reporting only the +0.298 would badly mislead. (Wooldridge 2019)
crumbs: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions

title: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions
text:
Dummy variables: differences between groups
A dummy (indicator) variable is 1 if a condition holds, 0 otherwise, and it shifts the intercept between groups. (Wooldridge 2019) Its coefficient is the average difference between that group and the omitted base category, holding the other regressors fixed. (Wooldridge 2019)






NoteThe gender wage gap



\widehat{\text{wage}} = -1.57 - 1.81\,\text{female} + 0.572\,\text{educ} + \cdots — holding education and other factors constant, women earn about $1.81 less per hour than men, the base group. (Wooldridge 2019)


One trap: with an overall intercept and a full set of mutually exclusive categories, you must omit one as the reference — including all of them plus the intercept creates perfect collinearity (the dummy-variable trap, an MLR.3 violation). (Wooldridge 2019)
crumbs: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions

title: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions
section: Interactions: when one effect depends on another
text:
Interactions: when one effect depends on another
Sometimes the effect of x_1 isn’t fixed — it depends on x_2. Capture that with their product: (Wooldridge 2019)

y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \beta_3 (x_1\cdot x_2) + u.

Now the partial effect of x_2 is \beta_2 + \beta_3 x_1 — it moves with x_1. (Wooldridge 2019) The interaction coefficient \beta_3 answers “does the slope of one variable change as the other changes?”






NoteDoes the return to education differ by gender?



\log(\text{wage}) = \beta_0 + \delta_0\,\text{female} + \beta_1\,\text{educ} +
\delta_1(\text{female}\cdot\text{educ}) + u. Here \beta_1 is the return to education for men, \beta_1 + \delta_1 the return for women, and \delta_1 is the difference in returns. A significant \delta_1 means the wage–education profiles are not parallel — education pays differently across groups. (Wooldridge 2019)



Seeing curves and branches
Two functional forms that change the shape of the fit: a quadratic (an effect that peaks) and an interaction (slopes that differ by group).


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 3.8))

# Quadratic: wage = 3.73 + 0.298 exper - 0.0061 exper^2
exper = np.linspace(0, 45, 200)
wage = 3.73 + 0.298 * exper - 0.0061 * exper ** 2
xstar = 0.298 / (2 * 0.0061)
axL.plot(exper, wage, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.4)
axL.axvline(xstar, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.4)
axL.text(xstar + 0.6, wage.min() + 0.3, f"turning point\n≈ {xstar:.1f} yrs",
         color="#c0392b", fontsize=8)
axL.set_title("Quadratic: diminishing returns to experience")
axL.set_xlabel("years of experience"); axL.set_ylabel("predicted wage ($)")

# Interaction: steeper return to education for one group
educ = np.linspace(8, 20, 50)
axR.plot(educ, 1.0 + 0.40 * educ, color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.4, label="Group A (steeper return)")
axR.plot(educ, 1.0 + 0.25 * educ, color="#d98a29", lw=2.4, label="Group B (flatter return)")
axR.set_title("Interaction: non-parallel slopes")
axR.set_xlabel("years of education"); axR.set_ylabel("predicted log wage")
axR.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Left: the quadratic wage–experience curve, rising then falling, peaking at the turning point ≈ 24.4 years — diminishing returns made visible. Right: an interaction — the wage–education slope is steeper for one group than another, so the lines aren’t parallel; the interaction coefficient is the gap between the slopes.
crumbs: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions

title: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading a log coefficient as a level effect. \hat\beta = 0.083 in a log-wage model is 8.3%, not 8.3 cents. Match the interpretation to the form. (Wooldridge 2019)
Reporting a quadratic’s linear term as ‘the effect.’ With x^2 in the model the effect is \beta_1+2\beta_2 x — it varies with x. Quote the marginal effect at meaningful values and the turning point. (Wooldridge 2019)
Falling into the dummy-variable trap. Omit one category as the base; never include a full set of dummies and an intercept. (Wooldridge 2019)
Interpreting a main effect alone when an interaction is present. With x_1\cdot x_2 in the model, \beta_1 is the effect of x_1 only when x_2=0. The full effect is \beta_1+\beta_3 x_2. (Wooldridge 2019)
Adding curves to chase R². Higher-order terms can overfit; include them for substantive reasons or when diagnostics (RESET) flag misspecification, not reflexively.
crumbs: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions

title: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — the four log forms and their interpretations (semi-elasticity 100\beta vs the exact 100[\exp(\beta)-1]; log-wage 8.3%/year; log-log CEO-pay elasticity 0.257); quadratics, the marginal effect \beta_1+2\beta_2x and turning point |\beta_1/2\beta_2| (wage–experience peak at 24.4 years); dummy variables, the base category, the gender-gap example (−$1.81), and the dummy-variable trap; interaction terms, the partial effect \beta_2+\beta_3x_1, and the education-by-gender example with non-parallel slopes.
crumbs: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions

title: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Why log-level is a percentage. Differentiate \log y = \beta_0+\beta_1 x: d(\log y) = \beta_1\,dx, and d(\log y)=dy/y is the fractional change in y. So \%\Delta y \approx 100\beta_1\,\Delta x. For a discrete change the exact figure is 100[\exp(\beta_1\Delta x)-1], which exceeds 100\beta_1\Delta x as the coefficient grows (the gap is the curvature of \exp).
Why log-log is an elasticity. With \log y=\beta_0+\beta_1\log x, \dfrac{dy/y}{dx/x}=\beta_1 — the ratio of percentage changes, i.e. the elasticity, constant at every x.
The turning point. For \hat y=\hat\beta_0+\hat\beta_1x+\hat\beta_2x^2, set the derivative \hat\beta_1+2\hat\beta_2 x=0 to get x^*=-\hat\beta_1/(2\hat\beta_2); it is a maximum when \hat\beta_2<0, a minimum when \hat\beta_2>0.
Interaction = two-line model. Writing the female-interaction model by group: for men (\text{female}=0) the slope on educ is \beta_1; for women (\text{female}=1) it is \beta_1+\delta_1, and the intercept shifts by \delta_0. The single regression with an interaction is two regression lines estimated jointly.
crumbs: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions

title: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions
text:
Connections

Builds on: transformations of random variables (logs and the log-normal), inference for coefficients (testing \delta_1, \beta_2), and multiple regression (these are all multiple regressions with constructed regressors).
Connects to: comparing groups — a regression on a set of group dummies reproduces the ANOVA F-test, so dummies are how regression absorbs ANOVA.
Leads to: diagnostics (RESET catches the wrong functional form) and limited dependent variables (a different nonlinearity — in the outcome).
crumbs: Functional Form: Logs, Polynomials, Dummies & Interactions

title: Generalized Linear Models: One Family
text: You’ve now met regression for continuous, binary, and (next) count outcomes. The generalized linear model (GLM) reveals they are the same model in three costumes. Keep the linear predictor X\beta; change two things — the distribution of the outcome and the link function that connects it to X\beta — and OLS becomes logistic becomes Poisson. Learn the family and you stop memorising separate models and start configuring the right one.

title: Generalized Linear Models: One Family
text:
Why it matters
OLS assumes a continuous, roughly Normal outcome. Force it onto a 0/1 or a count and it makes “nonsensical predictions” — probabilities above 100%, negative counts. (McNulty 2021) The GLM framework is the principled fix: it keeps everything you know about linear regression — coefficients, a linear predictor, inference — and bends the output into the valid range for whatever outcome you have. It is the umbrella that unifies half of Part III.

title: Generalized Linear Models: One Family
text:
The three components
Every GLM is built from exactly three parts: (McNulty 2021)

Random component — the distribution of the outcome Y, drawn from the exponential family (Gaussian, Binomial, Poisson, and more). This is what you change to match the data type.
Systematic component — the linear predictor \eta = X\beta, the familiar linear combination of coefficients and predictors. This never changes.
Link function — a function g(\cdot) that connects the mean of the outcome to the linear predictor: g(\mu) = X\beta. This is the hinge between the two.

title: Generalized Linear Models: One Family
section: The link function does the work
text:
The link function does the work
The link is what keeps predictions sensible, and choosing it is choosing the model: (McNulty 2021)









Model
Outcome
Link g(\mu)
Keeps predictions…




OLS
continuous
identity \mu
(any real number)


Logistic
binary
logit \ln\frac{\mu}{1-\mu} (log-odds)
in [0,1]


Poisson
count
log \ln\mu
positive



Why a link is needed: a raw linear predictor X\beta ranges over all of (-\infty,\infty), but a probability must live in [0,1] and a count must be non-negative. The link (and its inverse) constrains the output so it can’t escape its valid range. (McNulty 2021) The logit squashes the line into an S-curve between 0 and 1; the log link’s inverse, \exp(X\beta), keeps counts positive.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

eta = np.linspace(-4, 4, 300)          # the linear predictor X*beta

identity = eta
logistic = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-eta))      # inverse logit -> probability in [0,1]
poisson  = np.exp(eta)                 # inverse log   -> positive count

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9.4, 3.4))
for ax, y, title, col, note in [
    (axes[0], identity, "identity link (OLS)", "#1f3a5f", "any real value"),
    (axes[1], logistic, "logit link (logistic)", "#3f8f6f", "constrained to [0, 1]"),
    (axes[2], poisson,  "log link (Poisson)",   "#9a6a2f", "constrained > 0")]:
    ax.plot(eta, y, color=col, lw=2.6)
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10, color=col, fontweight="bold")
    ax.set_xlabel(r"linear predictor  $X\beta$")
    ax.text(0.5, 0.04, note, transform=ax.transAxes, ha="center", fontsize=8.5, style="italic")
axes[0].set_ylabel("predicted mean  $\\mu$")
axes[1].set_ylim(-0.05, 1.05); axes[1].axhline(0, color="#ccc", lw=0.8); axes[1].axhline(1, color="#ccc", lw=0.8)
axes[2].set_ylim(0, 20)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: One linear predictor, three link functions. The horizontal axis is the linear predictor Xβ, which ranges over all real numbers. The IDENTITY link (OLS) passes it through unchanged. The LOGIT link’s inverse squashes it into a probability in [0,1] (logistic). The LOG link’s inverse, exp(Xβ), maps it to a positive count (Poisson). Same Xβ, three valid ranges — that is the whole idea of a GLM.

title: Generalized Linear Models: One Family
text:
Estimation: maximum likelihood
Because the link makes the model nonlinear, there’s no closed-form OLS formula. GLMs are fit by maximum likelihood — find the \beta that makes the observed data most probable under the chosen distribution. (McNulty 2021) (Numerically, by iteratively reweighted least squares, but the principle is pure MLE.) This is why logit/probit, Poisson, and Cox survival all share the same inferential machinery — they’re all GLMs (or close cousins) fit by likelihood.

title: Generalized Linear Models: One Family
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Using OLS on a bounded outcome. A linear probability model can predict below 0 or above 1; a line on counts predicts negatives. Pick the link that fits the range. (McNulty 2021)
Reading GLM coefficients on the linear-predictor scale. A logit coefficient is in log-odds, a Poisson coefficient in log-counts; back-transform (the inverse link) before interpreting in real units. (McNulty 2021)
Forgetting which distribution you assumed. The random component encodes a variance assumption (e.g. Poisson’s mean = variance); if it’s wrong, the SEs are wrong even when the link is right.
Treating GLM as a different beast from OLS. It’s the same linear predictor and MLE machinery; OLS is just the identity-link, Gaussian special case.

title: Generalized Linear Models: One Family
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






McNulty, Explanatory Statistical Modeling in R — the GLM as the unifying family over OLS, logistic, and Poisson; the three components (random/exponential-family response, systematic linear predictor \eta=X\beta, link function g(\mu)=X\beta); the standard links (identity/logit/log) and why a link is needed to avoid “nonsensical predictions” (probabilities >1, negative counts); estimation by maximum likelihood (no closed form).
Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics / Moffatt, Experimetrics — the binary and count members of the family and their likelihood-based fitting.

title: Generalized Linear Models: One Family
text:
Connections

Generalises: simple & multiple regression (identity link) and logit/probit (logit link).
Specialises to: count-data models (log link) and underlies survival/Cox.
Runs on: maximum likelihood — the estimation engine for every non-Gaussian member.

title: Generalized Linear Models: One Family
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“What are the three components of a generalized linear model, and which one stays the same across OLS, logistic, and Poisson?”
“What does a link function do, and why is one needed — give the identity, logit, and log links and the ranges they protect?”
“Why can’t GLMs be solved with the closed-form OLS formula, and what are they fit by instead?”
“Show how OLS, logistic regression, and Poisson regression are all special cases of one family.”

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
text: A regression coefficient is a statistic, so it has a sampling distribution: run the study again and it would land somewhere slightly different. Everything in this entry — the standard error, the t-test, the confidence interval, the F-test — is the Part II inference machinery attached to \hat\beta. The discipline is the same: never report a coefficient without its uncertainty.

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
text:
Why it matters
The printout says the effect of education on log-wage is 0.092. Is that real, or could a fresh sample have produced 0? You answer with the coefficient’s standard error and the tests built on it. (Wooldridge 2019) This is where regression stops being description and becomes inference: every “significant effect,” every “we cannot reject,” every error bar in an empirical paper is one of the procedures below. And the statistical-vs-practical distinction returns with a vengeance — with enough data, everything is significant.

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
section: The sampling distribution of a coefficient
text:
The sampling distribution of a coefficient
To do exact inference at any sample size, Wooldridge adds one assumption to the Gauss–Markov five: (Wooldridge 2019)

MLR.6 (Normality): the error u is independent of the regressors and distributed \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2).

MLR.1–MLR.6 are the Classical Linear Model. Under them, each OLS estimator is Normally distributed around the truth: (Wooldridge 2019)

\hat\beta_j \sim \mathcal{N}\!\big(\beta_j,\ \operatorname{Var}(\hat\beta_j)\big).

(Even without MLR.6, the CLT delivers approximate Normality in large samples — which is why regression inference works so widely.) The standard error \operatorname{se}(\hat\beta_j) is the estimated standard deviation of this distribution, built from the variance formula with \sigma^2 replaced by its estimate \hat\sigma^2 (the SER squared). (Wooldridge 2019) The standard error is the whole ball game: it is what converts a point estimate into a claim.

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
section: Testing a single coefficient: the t-test
text:
Testing a single coefficient: the t-test
To ask “could the true effect be zero?”, test H_0:\beta_j = 0 with the t-statistic — the estimate measured in standard errors: (Wooldridge 2019)

t = \frac{\hat\beta_j}{\operatorname{se}(\hat\beta_j)}.

If |t| exceeds the critical value from the t-distribution with n-k-1 degrees of freedom, reject H_0 — the coefficient is statistically significant. (Wooldridge 2019) The companion confidence interval gives the range of values you can’t reject: (Wooldridge 2019)

\hat\beta_j \pm c\cdot\operatorname{se}(\hat\beta_j),

with c \approx 2 for 95%. The interval and the test are duals: the coefficient is significant at 5% exactly when its 95% interval excludes zero.






NoteWooldridge’s WAGE1 t-test



In the log-wage regression (n=526), the education coefficient is \hat\beta_{\text{educ}} = 0.092 with \operatorname{se} = 0.007. So t = 0.092/0.007 \approx 13.1 — vastly beyond any critical value. Education is overwhelmingly statistically significant: a sampling fluke essentially could not produce a t of 13. (Wooldridge 2019) (The 95% interval, 0.092 \pm 2(0.007) = [0.078, 0.106], nowhere near zero.)



Significance is estimate ÷ standard error
The t-statistic makes vivid that significance is not about the size of an effect but about its size relative to its noise. Below, two coefficients have the identical estimate (0.5); only their standard errors differ. The precise one is clearly significant; the noisy one can’t be distinguished from zero — same number, opposite verdict.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from statistics import NormalDist

est = 0.5
xs = np.linspace(-1.0, 2.0, 600)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3.7))
for se, color, label in [(0.10, "#1f3a5f", "se = 0.10  (t = 5.0, significant)"),
                         (0.35, "#d98a29", "se = 0.35  (t = 1.4, not significant)")]:
    nd = NormalDist(est, se)
    ax.plot(xs, [nd.pdf(v) for v in xs], color=color, lw=2.0, label=label)
    lo, hi = est - 1.96 * se, est + 1.96 * se
    y = -0.25 if se == 0.10 else -0.5
    ax.errorbar(est, y, xerr=1.96 * se, fmt="o", color=color, capsize=4, lw=2)
ax.axvline(0, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.6)
ax.text(0.02, ax.get_ylim()[1] * 0.85, "H₀: β = 0", color="#c0392b", fontsize=9)
ax.set_yticks([]); ax.set_xlabel("coefficient value")
ax.set_title("Same estimate (0.5), different precision, different verdict")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Significance = estimate ÷ standard error. Both coefficients are estimated at 0.5. The precise estimate (blue, se = 0.10, t = 5) has a 95% interval far from zero — significant. The noisy estimate (orange, se = 0.35, t = 1.4) has an interval straddling zero — not significant. The point estimate is the same; the standard error decides.

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
section: Testing several coefficients at once: the F-test
text:
Testing several coefficients at once: the F-test
Some questions involve groups of coefficients: “do any of these four neighbourhood variables matter?”, “should this block of regressors be in the model at all?” Testing them one t-test at a time invites the multiple-comparisons problem; the F-test asks the joint question in one shot. (Wooldridge 2019) It compares a restricted model (the suspect coefficients forced to zero) against the unrestricted model (all included), via their residual sums of squares: (Wooldridge 2019)

F = \frac{(\text{SSR}_r - \text{SSR}_{ur})/q}{\text{SSR}_{ur}/(n-k-1)},

where q is the number of restrictions. The logic is pure ANOVA: does dropping those regressors make the fit significantly worse? A special case — the test of overall significance — restricts all slopes to zero (H_0:\beta_1=\cdots=\beta_k=0), asking whether the model explains anything at all. (Wooldridge 2019) (This F is what reproduces the ANOVA F-test when the regressors are group dummies — regression contains ANOVA.)

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
text:
Statistical vs practical significance — again
Wooldridge hammers the distinction, because regression makes it so easy to confuse: (Wooldridge 2019)

Statistical significance (the t-stat / p-value) asks whether the effect is distinguishable from zero — a question about noise.
Practical (economic) significance (the magnitude of \hat\beta_j) asks whether the effect is big enough to matter — a question about substance.

With a huge sample, a trivially small coefficient can be “highly significant” (t enormous, effect meaningless); with a small sample, a large, important effect can be “insignificant.” (Wooldridge 2019) Always report and interpret the coefficient’s size in real units, not just its stars. A significant 0.2%-per-year effect and a significant 8%-per-year effect are both “significant” and worlds apart.

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
text:
Prediction: two kinds of interval
Inference isn’t only about coefficients — often the goal is to predict y for a new case with regressor values x_0. The point prediction is just the fitted value, \hat y_0 = \hat\beta_0 +
\hat\beta_1 x_{0}+\cdots. (Wooldridge 2019) But “how uncertain is that prediction?” has two different answers, and confusing them is a classic error:

A confidence interval for the mean, E[y\mid x_0] — the average outcome for everyone with characteristics x_0. Its only uncertainty comes from the estimated coefficients, so it is narrow and shrinks toward zero as n\to\infty.
A prediction interval for a single new individual y_0 — that person’s actual outcome. It carries the coefficient uncertainty plus the irreducible scatter of individuals around the line, the error variance \sigma^2. So it is much wider and does not vanish as n grows: even with infinite data and a perfectly known line, individuals still scatter by \sigma. (Wooldridge 2019)



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from statistics import NormalDist

rng = np.random.default_rng(2)
n = 60
x = rng.uniform(0, 10, n)
sigma = 2.5
y = 1.0 + 0.8 * x + rng.normal(0, sigma, n)

xbar, Sxx = x.mean(), np.sum((x - x.mean()) ** 2)
b1 = np.cov(x, y, bias=True)[0, 1] / np.var(x)
b0 = y.mean() - b1 * xbar
resid = y - (b0 + b1 * x)
s = np.sqrt(np.sum(resid ** 2) / (n - 2))            # estimated sigma
tcrit = 1.96

xs = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)
se_mean = s * np.sqrt(1 / n + (xs - xbar) ** 2 / Sxx)        # SE of the mean prediction
se_pred = s * np.sqrt(1 + 1 / n + (xs - xbar) ** 2 / Sxx)    # + 1 for an individual
fit = b0 + b1 * xs

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.2))
ax.scatter(x, y, s=16, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.6, zorder=2)
ax.fill_between(xs, fit - tcrit * se_pred, fit + tcrit * se_pred,
                color="#d98a29", alpha=0.18, label="95% prediction interval (an individual)")
ax.fill_between(xs, fit - tcrit * se_mean, fit + tcrit * se_mean,
                color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.35, label="95% confidence interval (the mean)")
ax.plot(xs, fit, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.2)
ax.set_xlabel("x"); ax.set_ylabel("y")
ax.set_title("Confidence interval (mean) vs prediction interval (individual)")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 2: Two intervals, two questions. The inner blue band is the 95% confidence interval for the mean outcome E[y|x] — narrow, and narrowest near the centre of the data. The outer band is the 95% prediction interval for a single new individual: far wider because it adds the irreducible person-to-person scatter (σ), which no amount of data removes. Predicting an average is easy; predicting a person is not.





The practical upshot: a regression can pin down an average tightly while still being nearly useless for forecasting any single outcome — the prediction band is wide because people are not their average. (For log-outcome models, predicting the level needs a retransformation correction, since \exp of the predicted log under-predicts the mean — the Jensen gap again.)

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading stars instead of magnitudes. Significance is not importance. A tiny, precisely-estimated effect is significant and may be useless; report the size and a confidence interval. (Wooldridge 2019)
Mining many t-tests for the significant one. Each coefficient at 5% has a 1-in-20 false-positive rate; with many regressors, use the F-test for joint questions and pre-specify hypotheses. (Wooldridge 2019)
Trusting standard errors when MLR.5 fails. Heteroskedasticity or clustering makes the printed SEs wrong (the coefficients are still fine). Use robust/clustered SEs. (Wooldridge 2019)
Interpreting a wide interval around zero as ‘no effect.’ It may mean underpowered, not null — the power lesson, in regression dress.
Forgetting inference assumes a valid model. A perfect t-statistic on an endogenous coefficient precisely estimates a biased number. Inference quantifies sampling noise, not specification error.

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — MLR.6 normality and the Classical Linear Model; the Normal sampling distribution of \hat\beta_j and the standard error; the t-statistic t=\hat\beta_j/\operatorname{se}(\hat\beta_j), the n-k-1 degrees of freedom, and confidence intervals; the WAGE1 example (\hat\beta_{\text{educ}}=0.092, \operatorname{se}=0.007, t\approx13.1); the F-test for joint/exclusion restrictions and overall significance; the statistical-vs-practical-significance distinction and the large-sample caution.

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

From Normal to t. Under the CLM, (\hat\beta_j-\beta_j)/\operatorname{sd}(\hat\beta_j) is exactly standard Normal. Replacing the unknown \sigma in \operatorname{sd} with the estimate \hat\sigma inflates the tails to a t distribution with n-k-1 degrees of freedom — the same \sigma-is-estimated penalty as the one-sample t-test, now with k+1 parameters consumed instead of one.
The F as a fit comparison. Adding regressors can only lower (never raise) the SSR, so \text{SSR}_r \ge \text{SSR}_{ur}. The numerator (\text{SSR}_r-\text{SSR}_{ur})/q is the average improvement per restriction; the denominator \text{SSR}_{ur}/(n-k-1) estimates \sigma^2. Under H_0 both estimate the same noise, so F\approx1; a real contribution makes F large. F\sim F_{q,\,n-k-1} under the null.
t and F coincide for one restriction. For q=1, F = t^2 exactly — the joint test of a single coefficient is just the square of its t-test.
An R²-form of the overall F. Dividing through by SST, the overall-significance F equals \dfrac{R^2/k}{(1-R^2)/(n-k-1)} — large when the model explains a big share of variance relative to its degrees of freedom.

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
text:
Connections

Builds on: the Gauss–Markov assumptions (the variance formula behind the SE; MLR.6 for exact normality), estimation (a coefficient is an estimator with a sampling distribution), and confidence intervals & testing (the t/F machinery, applied to \hat\beta).
Leads to: functional form (interpreting the coefficients you’re now testing) and diagnostics (when the standard errors are wrong and how to fix them).
Reappears in: every causal estimate, each reported with a standard error, a t-stat, and an interval generated exactly this way.

title: Inference for Regression Coefficients
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Explain the sampling distribution of an OLS coefficient and what the standard error measures, using the Classical Linear Model assumptions.”
“Walk through the t-test for a coefficient and the WAGE1 example (β̂ = 0.092, se = 0.007, t = 13.1), and the matching confidence interval.”
“What is the F-test for joint hypotheses, what is the restricted-vs-unrestricted formula, and what is the test of overall significance?”
“Explain the difference between statistical and practical significance in regression, and why a large sample makes everything significant.”
“Why does t² = F for a single restriction, and how does the overall F relate to R²?”

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text: A huge share of social data is nested: pupils within classrooms within schools, patients within hospitals, repeated measures within a person. Observations in the same group share unmeasured influences, so they are not independent — and ordinary regression, blind to this, reports standard errors that are far too small. Mixed-effects (multilevel, hierarchical) models fix it by splitting effects into a fixed part shared by all and a random part that varies by group — and, as a bonus, they let data-poor groups borrow strength from data-rich ones.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text:
Why it matters
OLS assumes independent observations. (Li 2023) Clustered data breaks that assumption: students in the same class share a teacher and environment, making them more alike than students in other classes. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) The consequence is precise but subtle — OLS stays unbiased, but it is inefficient and its standard errors are wrong, usually badly underestimated, because your effective sample size is closer to the number of clusters than the number of individuals. (Li 2023) Report those SEs and you will claim significance you haven’t earned. Mixed models are the principled response — and the natural model whenever the clustering that diagnostics only patched is something you’d rather understand.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text:
Fixed and random effects
A mixed model literally mixes two kinds of coefficient: (Li 2023)

Fixed effects are the same for every unit — the average return to education across the whole population.
Random effects are allowed to vary across clusters, drawn from a distribution the model estimates.

Two flavours cover most needs: (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

Random intercepts — each group gets its own baseline. Graphically: parallel lines, one per school, shifted up or down. (Some schools start higher.)
Random slopes — the effect of a predictor varies by group: the return to education might differ for every person, so each group’s line gets its own tilt.


y_{ij} = \underbrace{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{ij}}_{\text{fixed}} + \underbrace{u_{j}}_{\text{random intercept}} + \varepsilon_{ij},
\qquad u_j \sim \mathcal N(0,\sigma_u^2),\ \ \varepsilon_{ij}\sim\mathcal N(0,\sigma_e^2).

Here i indexes individuals, j their group; u_j is the group’s random departure from the common intercept.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text:
Partial pooling: the big idea
The conceptual heart, and what makes multilevel models more than “regression with cluster dummies.” There’s a spectrum of how to treat groups: (Lambert 2018)

Complete pooling — ignore groups, fit one global mean. Simple, but “too simplistic,” and it reports “insufficient uncertainty.” (Lambert 2018)
No pooling — a separate estimate per group (a dummy per school). It overfits, especially for small groups, where a handful of noisy observations produce a wild estimate. (Lambert 2018)
Partial pooling (the multilevel model) — assume the group parameters share a common distribution, u_j \sim \mathcal N(\mu, \sigma_u^2), and estimate that distribution and the groups together. (Lambert 2018)

The payoff of partial pooling is shrinkage: each group’s estimate is pulled toward the overall mean, and small or extreme groups shrink most — they have the least information of their own, so they lean hardest on the crowd. (Lambert 2018) This is how data-poor groups “borrow statistical strength” from data-rich ones, (Jacobucci et al. 2023) trading a little bias for a large cut in variance — exactly the bias–variance bargain of regularization, here arising naturally from the grouping.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
grand = 50.0          # true global mean
tau   = 8.0           # between-group SD (true spread of group means)
sigma = 12.0          # within-group SD (noise per observation)

n_groups = 30
sizes = rng.integers(2, 60, n_groups)                 # group sizes vary a lot
true_means = grand + rng.normal(0, tau, n_groups)
raw_means = true_means + rng.normal(0, sigma / np.sqrt(sizes))   # noisy group means

# Partial-pooling (shrinkage) weight: reliability of each group's own mean
#   w_j = tau^2 / (tau^2 + sigma^2/n_j)  -> small groups get small w (shrink more)
w = tau**2 / (tau**2 + sigma**2 / sizes)
pooled = w * raw_means + (1 - w) * grand

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.4, 4.2))
ax.axhline(grand, color="#9a6a2f", ls="--", lw=1.4, label="complete pooling (global mean)")
for s, r, p in zip(sizes, raw_means, pooled):
    ax.annotate("", xy=(s, p), xytext=(s, r),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color="#9aa0a6", lw=1.0))
ax.scatter(sizes, raw_means, s=22, color="#c0392b", label="raw group mean (no pooling)", zorder=3)
ax.scatter(sizes, pooled, s=22, color="#1f3a5f", label="partial-pooled estimate", zorder=3)
ax.set_xlabel("group size $n_j$"); ax.set_ylabel("estimated group mean")
ax.set_title("Small, noisy groups shrink toward the global mean")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Partial pooling and shrinkage. Each point is a group’s raw mean (no pooling), plotted against group size; the dashed line is the global mean (complete pooling). The partial-pooled estimates (arrows’ heads) pull every group toward the global mean — and SMALL groups (left) shrink far more than large ones (right), because they carry less information of their own. Multilevel modelling is this compromise, done optimally.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text:
Variance components and the ICC
Because the model separates group-level from individual-level randomness, it decomposes the total variance into a between-group part \sigma_u^2 and a within-group part \sigma_e^2. (Li 2023) Their ratio is the intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC): 
\rho = \frac{\sigma_u^2}{\sigma_u^2 + \sigma_e^2},
 the proportion of total variation attributable to the grouping. (Li 2023) A high \rho means group members behave very similarly — so sampling more individuals from the same group adds little new information (and ignoring the clustering would most badly mislead your SEs). (Li 2023) The ICC is both a substantive finding (“how much is about the school?”) and a diagnostic for how much clustering matters.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
section: Random effects vs fixed effects: the Hausman choice
text:
Random effects vs fixed effects: the Hausman choice
Econometrics frames the same structure as a choice of estimator, and it comes with a famous test: (Li 2023)

The random-effects (RE) estimator is more efficient — it estimates the variance of the group intercepts rather than N-1 separate dummy coefficients. But it is consistent only if the group effect is uncorrelated with the regressors. (Li 2023)
The fixed-effects (FE) estimator (the panel workhorse) makes no such assumption — it sweeps the group effect away by within-group differencing — at the cost of efficiency and of dropping anything time-invariant.

The Hausman test decides between them: it compares the FE and RE estimates, and if their difference is systematic (significant), the RE exogeneity assumption has failed and you should trust the FE within-group variation instead. (Li 2023) In causal language: RE is a modelling convenience; FE is the one that defends against a confounded grouping.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
section: The Bayesian view: hierarchy all the way up
text:
The Bayesian view: hierarchy all the way up
Mixed models are most natural in the Bayesian framework, because a hierarchy is just a prior extended to multiple levels. (Lambert 2018) Instead of fixing the group distribution’s parameters, you give them their own hyperpriors and infer them from the data — the data tell you how much groups vary, and therefore how much to shrink. (Lambert 2018) The reward is honest uncertainty: the hierarchical posterior reflects the true uncertainty at every level — individual, classroom, school — rather than a single point estimate that pretends the group means were known. (Lambert 2018) Partial pooling, from this angle, isn’t a trick; it’s what Bayes does when you admit the groups are exchangeable draws from a population.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text:
The social-science bridge






NoteMixed models in the wild




Education. Pupils nested in classes nested in schools — the canonical case; school quality and state policy induce exactly the dependence multilevel models are built for (SAT/test-score studies). (Lambert 2018; Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Tennessee STAR. The class-size experiment is the standard cautionary tale: randomisation was at the classroom level, so honest inference must account for clustering there. (Li 2023)
Labour economics. Wage equations where unobserved ability is a person-level effect u_i — the same object panel fixed effects sweep away. (Wooldridge 2019; Li 2023)
Repeated measures / growth. Rat growth curves, spinal bone-mineral density across ages and ethnicities — measures within an individual, modelled with random effects per subject. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Ignoring clustering entirely. OLS SEs on nested data are “usually significantly underestimated” — the effective n is the number of clusters. Cluster, or model the levels. (Li 2023)
No pooling on small groups. A dummy per group overfits tiny groups into noise; partial pooling shrinks them sensibly. (Lambert 2018)
Using RE without checking exogeneity. RE is inconsistent if the group effect correlates with a regressor; run the Hausman test and fall back to FE when it rejects. (Li 2023)
Forgetting random slopes. If an effect plausibly varies by group, a random-intercept-only model understates uncertainty in that effect. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Reading shrinkage as a bug. A shrunken small-group estimate is more accurate, not censored — it’s the bias–variance trade working for you. (Lambert 2018)

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Dong Li, Panel Data Econometrics — clustered data breaks OLS independence (unbiased but inefficient, underestimated SEs, effective n ≈ number of clusters); variance components and the ICC \rho=\sigma_u^2/(\sigma_u^2+\sigma_e^2); the RE estimator (efficient; consistent only under exogeneity) and the Hausman test (FE vs RE); ability as a person-level effect; Tennessee STAR clustering.
Lambert, A Student’s Guide to Bayesian Statistics — complete / no / partial pooling; shrinkage toward the mean; hierarchical models as priors extended over levels with hyperpriors; honest multi-level uncertainty.
Jacobucci, Grimm & Zhang, ML for Social and Behavioral Research — multilevel structure (students in classes/schools), random intercepts/slopes, borrowing statistical strength; repeated-measures/growth examples.
Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — unobserved individual effects in wage equations; the link to fixed-effects panel estimation.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The variance-components model. With y_{ij}=\mathbf x_{ij}^\top\beta + u_j + \varepsilon_{ij}, u_j\sim\mathcal N(0,\sigma_u^2), \varepsilon_{ij}\sim\mathcal N(0,\sigma_e^2), two observations in the same group j have covariance \operatorname{Cov}(y_{ij},y_{i'j})=\sigma_u^2, so their correlation is \rho=\sigma_u^2/(\sigma_u^2+\sigma_e^2) — the ICC. OLS ignores this nonzero off-diagonal covariance, which is precisely why its SEs are wrong; GLS/REML weights by it.
Shrinkage, exactly. The partial-pooled estimate of group j’s mean is the precision-weighted average of its own mean \bar y_j and the grand mean \mu: 
\hat\theta_j = w_j\,\bar y_j + (1-w_j)\,\mu,\qquad
w_j=\frac{\sigma_u^2}{\sigma_u^2+\sigma_e^2/n_j}.
 w_j\to 1 for large n_j (trust the group) and w_j\to 0 for small n_j (trust the crowd) — the algebra of “small groups shrink most,” and the curve drawn in the figure.
RE vs FE. FE estimates \beta from within-group deviations y_{ij}-\bar y_j, eliminating u_j and so needing no assumption about \operatorname{Cov}(u_j,\mathbf x). RE (FGLS) uses both within- and between-group variation, gaining efficiency but requiring \operatorname{Cov}(u_j,\mathbf x)=0. Hausman compares \hat\beta_{FE}-\hat\beta_{RE} to its variance; a large gap rejects RE.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text:
Connections

Builds on: multiple regression, diagnostics & clustered SEs (the patch this entry upgrades to a model), and the Normal/variance machinery.
Bridges to: panel data & fixed effects (FE is the causal sibling; Hausman chooses) and Bayesian machinery (hierarchical priors).
Echoes: regularization — shrinkage as variance reduction, here earned from the grouping rather than imposed by a penalty.

title: Mixed-Effects & Multilevel Models
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Why does ordinary regression underestimate standard errors on nested/clustered data, and how is the effective sample size related to the number of clusters?”
“Explain fixed vs random effects, and random intercepts vs random slopes, with an example.”
“Define complete, no, and partial pooling, and explain shrinkage — why do small or extreme groups shrink most?”
“What is the intraclass correlation, and what does a high ICC tell you?”
“When is the random-effects estimator consistent, and how does the Hausman test decide between RE and FE?”
