title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text: You can now fit, interpret, and stress-test a regression. The last and hardest question is the one that matters most: when is a coefficient a cause, not just a correlation with controls? The honest answer is humbling — almost never automatically. Regression estimates a conditional average; turning that into a causal effect takes an assumption that data alone can’t verify, and usually a research design. This entry is the bridge to the summit.
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text:
Why it matters
It is dangerously easy to run a regression, add some controls, and announce “the effect of X on Y.” But holding things constant only neutralises the confounders you observed and included. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) The unobserved ones — ability, motivation, health, a thousand others — sit in the error term, and if they correlate with your regressor, the coefficient is biased no matter how clean the output looks. This entry assembles the threats into one clear-eyed account of regression’s causal limits, and explains why credible causal claims need more than a longer control list — they need the methods of Part V.
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text:
The one condition: exogeneity
Strip everything away and a single condition decides whether a slope is causal: the regressor must be exogenous, E[u\mid x]=0 — equivalently, treatment is as good as randomly assigned once you condition on the controls (the conditional independence or selection-on-observables assumption). (Angrist and Pischke 2009; Wooldridge 2019) When it holds, the regression line is the causal conditional expectation function. When it fails, regression still faithfully estimates the best linear approximation to the CEF — but the CEF is descriptive, not causal. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) Regression is an extraordinary describing machine; whether its description is also an explanation is a question about the world, not the math.
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text:
Threat 1 — omitted variables (the central one)
The dominant threat, revisited from multiple regression: leave out a confounder and its influence loads onto your coefficient. The logic is the “short = long + bias” identity — the coefficient you get omitting a control equals the true effect plus (\text{effect of omitted on }y)\times(\text{its relation to the included regressor}). (Angrist and Pischke 2009) The classic case is ability bias: regress wages on education without ability, and since ability raises wages and correlates with schooling, the education coefficient is biased upward. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
The uncomfortable truth: you can never be certain you’ve controlled for everything. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) There is no test that confirms exogeneity; the unobserved confounder is, by definition, unobserved. This is why “I controlled for a lot of stuff” is an argument, not a proof — and why more controls buy credibility, never certainty.
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
section: Threat 2 — bad controls (when a control hurts)
text:
Threat 2 — bad controls (when a control hurts)
The instinct “control for everything” is wrong, and dangerously so. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) A bad control is a variable that is itself an outcome of the treatment — a post-treatment variable. Conditioning on it doesn’t remove confounding; it introduces bias. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)






NoteOccupation, the textbook bad control



Study the effect of education on wages, and it’s tempting to control for occupation. Don’t: education causes occupation (more schooling opens better jobs), so holding occupation fixed closes off one of the very channels through which education raises wages. You’d be measuring only the within-occupation effect and throwing away the part that flows through getting a better job — biasing the total effect, usually toward zero. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) The rule: good controls are predetermined (fixed before treatment); post-treatment variables are bad controls.



Collider bias: how conditioning creates correlation
The subtlest version: conditioning on a common consequence of two variables manufactures an association that isn’t there. This is collider (or selection) bias. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) The picture below is the canonical example: talent and beauty are uncorrelated in the population, but among people selected on their sum (say, working actors, hired for one or the other), they appear negatively correlated — condition on the collider and a spurious relationship springs into being.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(4)
n = 2000
talent = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
beauty = rng.normal(0, 1, n)          # independent of talent in the population
hired = (talent + beauty) > 1.6       # selected on a common consequence (a collider)

r_all = np.corrcoef(talent, beauty)[0, 1]
r_sel = np.corrcoef(talent[hired], beauty[hired])[0, 1]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.4))
ax.scatter(talent[~hired], beauty[~hired], s=8, color="#c9ccd1", alpha=0.5,
           label=f"everyone (r = {r_all:+.2f})")
ax.scatter(talent[hired], beauty[hired], s=16, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.8,
           label=f"hired (selected) (r = {r_sel:+.2f})")
# trend line within the selected group
b = np.cov(talent[hired], beauty[hired], bias=True)[0, 1] / np.var(talent[hired])
xs = np.linspace(talent[hired].min(), talent[hired].max(), 30)
ax.plot(xs, beauty[hired].mean() + b * (xs - talent[hired].mean()),
        color="#c0392b", lw=2.2)
ax.set_xlabel("talent"); ax.set_ylabel("beauty")
ax.set_title("Conditioning on a collider invents a correlation")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Collider/selection bias. In the full population (grey), talent and beauty are independent — no relationship. But the ‘hired’ subsample (blue), selected because talent + beauty is high, shows a spurious negative correlation: among the selected, more of one implies less of the other. Conditioning on a common consequence manufactures an association — which is exactly what a bad (post-treatment) control can do.
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text:
Threat 3 — reverse causality
Regression coefficients are symmetric; the data don’t know which way the arrow points. Reverse causality (simultaneity) is when y also causes x. (Wooldridge 2019) More police and more crime appear together — but high-crime cities hire more police, so the naive regression confuses “police cause crime” with “crime causes police.” Likewise income and health: wealth buys health, and health enables earning. When x and y co-determine each other, OLS estimates a tangle of both directions, not the effect you want. (Wooldridge 2019)
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text:
Threat 4 — measurement error and attenuation
If your regressor is measured with noise — self-reported income, a proxy for ability, a mismeasured treatment — the coefficient is biased, and in a predictable direction. Under classical measurement error, the bias is attenuation: the coefficient is pulled toward zero. (Wooldridge 2019) The intuition: noise in x adds variation that carries no signal about y, so the estimated slope is diluted — a real effect looks weaker than it is. Mismeasure the outcome and you mostly lose precision; mismeasure the regressor and you bias the effect downward.
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
section: The verdict: you need a design, not just controls
text:
The verdict: you need a design, not just controls
Put the threats together and the lesson is stark. Adding control variables cannot, by itself, deliver a credible causal effect — it addresses observed confounders while leaving unobserved confounding, reverse causality, and measurement error untouched, and it can create bias through bad controls. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) Selection-on-observables is an assumption you must argue for, not a box the software ticks.
What does deliver credibility is a research design that makes exogeneity defensible by construction: (Angrist and Pischke 2009)

Randomized experiments — random assignment forces X \perp u, the gold standard.
Instrumental variables — a lever that moves X but affects Y only through X.
Natural experiments — regression discontinuity, difference-in-differences — where nature or policy supplies as-good-as-random variation.

This is the hinge of the whole book. Regression is the engine every one of those designs runs on — but the design is what licenses the causal claim. Part V is the study of building that license.
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




“I controlled for confounders, so it’s causal.” Only for the confounders you observed. Unobserved ones remain, and there’s no test for exogeneity. Controls build credibility, not proof. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Controlling for everything available. Post-treatment (bad) controls and colliders add bias. Control for predetermined variables; never for outcomes of the treatment. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Ignoring the direction of causation. If y could plausibly cause x, the coefficient blends both directions. Ask which way the arrows run before interpreting. (Wooldridge 2019)
Trusting a precise coefficient on a noisy regressor. Measurement error attenuates toward zero; a small “insignificant” effect may be a real effect, diluted. (Wooldridge 2019)
Believing a tiny standard error rescues a biased estimate. Inference quantifies sampling noise, not specification error. A precise estimate of the wrong number is still the wrong number. (Wooldridge 2019)
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Angrist & Pischke, Mastering ’Metrics / Mostly Harmless Econometrics — a coefficient is causal only under selection-on-observables / conditional independence; the “short = long + bias” OVB logic and ability bias; bad controls (post-treatment variables; occupation in the education–wage regression) and the good-controls-are-predetermined rule; selection/collider bias from conditioning on a common consequence; and the case that credible causal inference needs a design (randomization, IV, natural experiments), with regression as the engine.
Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — exogeneity E[u\mid x]=0 as the causal condition; endogeneity from omitted variables, simultaneity / reverse causality (police–crime, income–health), and measurement-error attenuation (classical error in a regressor biases toward zero).
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

When OLS is causal. Writing the structural model y=\beta x + u (the data-generating equation whose \beta is the true causal effect, as opposed to a merely descriptive fit), OLS estimates \hat\beta \xrightarrow{p} \beta + \dfrac{\operatorname{Cov}(x,u)}{\operatorname{Var}(x)}, where \xrightarrow{p} denotes the large-sample (probability) limit — the value OLS converges to as n\to\infty. The second term — the bias — vanishes iff \operatorname{Cov}(x,u)=0, i.e. exogeneity. Every threat below is a reason \operatorname{Cov}(x,u)\neq 0.
Attenuation, derived. Let the true regressor be x^* but you observe x=x^*+e with classical error (e independent of x^* and u, variance \sigma_e^2). Then (writing \operatorname{plim}, the probability limit, for that same large-sample value) 
\operatorname{plim}\hat\beta = \beta\cdot\frac{\sigma_{x^*}^2}{\sigma_{x^*}^2+\sigma_e^2} = \beta\cdot\lambda,
\qquad 0<\lambda<1,
 so the estimate is the truth times a reliability ratio \lambda<1 — shrunk toward zero. The noisier the measure, the smaller \lambda, the worse the attenuation.
Why a collider induces dependence. If C = x + y + \varepsilon is a common consequence and x,y are marginally independent, then conditioning on C=c ties them: y \approx c - x - \varepsilon, so within a stratum of C, higher x forces lower y — a negative conditional correlation from a zero marginal one. Controlling for a post-treatment collider does exactly this.
Simultaneity bias. With y=\beta x+u and x=\gamma y+v, solving the system makes \operatorname{Cov}(x,u)\neq0, so OLS is inconsistent — the coefficient mixes \beta and \gamma. The fix is an instrument that shifts x without touching u — the subject of Part V.
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text:
Connections

Builds on: multiple regression (OVB and good/bad controls), the Gauss–Markov assumptions (exogeneity and its failure modes), conditional expectation (regression as the best-linear-approximation to the CEF, which is descriptive), and correlation is not causation (now formalised).
Leads to: the entire causal-inference summit — potential outcomes, randomized experiments, instrumental variables, regression discontinuity, and difference-in-differences — each a strategy for making exogeneity credible.
Closes: Part III. You can fit and interpret regressions and judge when their coefficients deserve a causal reading — the prerequisite for everything that follows.
crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: What Regression Can — and Can’t — Tell You
text:
Study with NotebookLM

crumbs: What Regression Can — and Can't — Tell You

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
text: Correlation tells you whether two things move together; regression tells you by how much, with an equation. Fit a line through a scatter so that it misses the points by as little as possible, and you have the single most-used tool in empirical social science — and, it turns out, an estimate of the conditional expectation you met in Part I.
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
text:
Why it matters
Almost every empirical claim in the social sciences is, under the hood, a regression coefficient: the return to a year of schooling, the effect of class size on test scores, the gender wage gap. Where correlation gives a single unitless number, regression gives a formula — \hat y = \hat\beta_0 +
\hat\beta_1 x — whose slope you can read in real units and whose machinery extends, in the next entries, to holding other things constant. (Wooldridge 2019) This entry builds the one-predictor case completely: what the line means, how it’s chosen, and how to tell whether it fits.
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
text:
The model
The simple linear regression model describes a relationship in the population: (Wooldridge 2019)

y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + u.


y is the dependent (outcome, explained) variable; x is the independent (regressor, explanatory) variable. (Wooldridge 2019)
\beta_0 is the intercept — the expected y when x=0.
\beta_1 is the slope, the parameter of interest: the ceteris paribus effect of x on y.
u is the error term — everything else that affects y but isn’t x (all the unobserved factors). (Wooldridge 2019)

Interpreting the slope is the whole game: a one-unit increase in x is associated with a \beta_1 change in y, on average — formally, holding the unobservables fixed (\Delta u = 0), \Delta y = \beta_1 \Delta x. (Wooldridge 2019)






TipRegression is the conditional expectation function



This connects straight back to conditional expectation. Under the zero conditional mean assumption — the unobservables average out to zero at every value of x, E[u\mid x]=0 — the regression line is the CEF: (Wooldridge 2019)

E[y \mid x] = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x.

So the regression line threads the average value of y for every slice of x. That is why regression is the best predictor machinery made practical — and why E[u\mid x]=0 (which we scrutinise in R3) is the assumption that does all the work.
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
text:
Ordinary least squares
We never see the population line; we estimate it from a sample of n points. Ordinary least squares (OLS) picks the line that makes the total squared miss as small as possible — it chooses \hat\beta_0, \hat\beta_1 to minimise the sum of squared residuals: (Wooldridge 2019)

\min_{\hat\beta_0,\hat\beta_1} \sum_{i=1}^n \big(y_i - \hat\beta_0 - \hat\beta_1 x_i\big)^2.

Why squared? Squaring makes every miss positive (so they can’t cancel), and penalises big misses disproportionately. Solving this minimisation (calculus in the appendix) gives two beautifully interpretable formulas: (Wooldridge 2019)

\hat\beta_1 = \frac{\sum_i (x_i-\bar x)(y_i-\bar y)}{\sum_i (x_i-\bar x)^2}
= \frac{\widehat{\operatorname{Cov}}(x,y)}{\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}(x)},
\qquad
\hat\beta_0 = \bar y - \hat\beta_1 \bar x.

The slope is just the covariance of x and y divided by the variance of x — co-movement, rescaled by how much x itself varies. The intercept then sits wherever it must for the line to pass through the average point (\bar x, \bar y).

Least squares, made visible
The picture below is the method: OLS slides and tilts a line until the sum of the squared vertical gaps (the residuals, in red) is as small as possible. No other line through this cloud makes that total smaller.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
n = 70
educ = rng.integers(6, 19, n).astype(float)            # years of schooling
wage = -0.90 + 0.54 * educ + rng.normal(0, 1.6, n)     # WAGE1-style relationship

# OLS by the covariance/variance formula
b1 = np.cov(educ, wage, bias=True)[0, 1] / np.var(educ)
b0 = wage.mean() - b1 * educ.mean()
fit = b0 + b1 * educ

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.2))
ax.vlines(educ, np.minimum(wage, fit), np.maximum(wage, fit),
          color="#c0392b", lw=0.9, alpha=0.7)          # residuals
ax.scatter(educ, wage, s=22, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.8, zorder=3)
xs = np.linspace(6, 18, 50)
ax.plot(xs, b0 + b1 * xs, color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.4,
        label=f"OLS:  wage = {b0:.2f} + {b1:.2f}·educ")
ax.set_xlabel("years of education"); ax.set_ylabel("hourly wage ($)")
ax.set_title("OLS minimises the total squared residual (red)")
ax.legend(fontsize=9, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Ordinary least squares on simulated wage–education data. The blue line minimises the total squared length of the red residual segments — the vertical gaps between each point and the line. Its slope (≈ 0.54) says each extra year of schooling predicts about 54 cents more per hour, echoing Wooldridge’s WAGE1 estimate.
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
section: Fitted values, residuals, and OLS’s built-in properties
text:
Fitted values, residuals, and OLS’s built-in properties
For each observation the line gives a fitted value \hat y_i = \hat\beta_0 + \hat\beta_1 x_i (the prediction) and a residual \hat u_i = y_i - \hat y_i (the miss). (Wooldridge 2019) By construction, OLS guarantees three algebraic facts that hold for any dataset: (Wooldridge 2019)

The residuals sum to zero, \sum_i \hat u_i = 0 — positive and negative misses cancel exactly.
The residuals are uncorrelated with x — the line has wrung all the linear information out of x; whatever is left is, by definition, unrelated to it.
The line passes through (\bar x, \bar y) — plug in the average x and you get the average y.

These aren’t assumptions to check; they’re identities baked into the least-squares solution.
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
section: Goodness of fit: R² and the standard error
text:
Goodness of fit: R² and the standard error
How much of y’s variation did the line actually capture? Decompose the total variation exactly as ANOVA does — the same between/within split: (Wooldridge 2019)

\underbrace{\sum_i (y_i-\bar y)^2}_{\text{SST (total)}} =
\underbrace{\sum_i (\hat y_i-\bar y)^2}_{\text{SSE (explained)}} +
\underbrace{\sum_i \hat u_i^2}_{\text{SSR (residual)}}.

The coefficient of determination is the explained share: (Wooldridge 2019)

R^2 = \frac{\text{SSE}}{\text{SST}} = 1 - \frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} \in [0,1].

An R^2 of 0.60 means x explains 60% of the sample variation in y. (Wooldridge 2019) Two facts worth carrying: in simple regression R^2 is exactly the square of the correlation coefficient, R^2 = r_{xy}^2 — regression and correlation measuring the same association two ways; and the standard error of the regression, \text{SER} = \sqrt{\text{SSR}/(n-2)}, estimates the typical vertical distance of points from the line, in the units of y. (Wooldridge 2019) (Low R^2 is not failure: in social data, individual behaviour is noisy, and a small-but-precise slope can still be the answer you want.)
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
section: A fully worked example: wage and education
text:
A fully worked example: wage and education
Wooldridge’s canonical case, estimated on n = 526 workers (the WAGE1 data): (Wooldridge 2019)

\widehat{\text{wage}} = -0.90 + 0.54\,\cdot\,\text{educ}, \qquad R^2 = 0.171.


Slope 0.54: each additional year of schooling predicts about 54 cents more per hour. Put comparatively (the honest framing): comparing two groups one year of schooling apart, the more-schooled group earns about $0.54/hour more on average. (Wooldridge 2019)
Intercept -0.90: literally, zero education predicts a negative wage — nonsensical, a reminder that the intercept is often just a mathematical anchor, not a meaningful x=0 value. (Wooldridge 2019)
R^2 = 0.171: education explains about 17% of the variation in wages. The other 83% lives in u — ability, experience, luck, industry — which is exactly why we move to multiple regression next. (Wooldridge 2019)
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading the slope as causal. \hat\beta_1 is an association unless E[u\mid x]=0 holds — and with one regressor it rarely does (ability is in u and correlates with education). The causal reading waits on much more. (Wooldridge 2019)
Over-interpreting the intercept. \hat\beta_0 is the fitted value at x=0, often outside the data’s range and meaningless (the -0.90 wage). Don’t force a story onto it. (Wooldridge 2019)
Worshipping R^2. A high R^2 doesn’t make a model correct or causal, and a low R^2 doesn’t make a slope wrong. Social-behaviour models routinely have small R^2 and useful coefficients. (Wooldridge 2019)
Extrapolating beyond the data. The line is fitted where the points are; predictions far outside the observed x range are unsupported.
Forgetting the squared-miss objective. OLS minimises squared residuals, so it is sensitive to outliers — one extreme point can swing the line.
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — the model y=\beta_0+\beta_1x+u and the meaning of each term; the slope as the ceteris-paribus effect and the CEF link under E[u\mid x]=0; OLS as minimising the SSR; the slope =\widehat{\operatorname{Cov}}(x,y)/\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}(x) and intercept formulas; fitted values, residuals, and the three algebraic properties; SST=SSE+SSR, R^2, R^2=r_{xy}^2, and the SER; the WAGE1 example (\widehat{\text{wage}}=-0.90+0.54\,\text{educ}, R^2=0.171, n=526).
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — regression as fitting an equation (vs correlation’s single number); fitted values and residuals in the predictive idiom.
Angrist & Pischke, Mastering ’Metrics — the comparative (“two groups one unit apart”) framing of a slope, foreshadowing the causal reading.
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Deriving the OLS estimators. Minimise S(\hat\beta_0,\hat\beta_1)=\sum_i(y_i-\hat\beta_0-\hat\beta_1 x_i)^2. The first-order conditions (set partial derivatives to zero) are the normal equations:

\sum_i (y_i-\hat\beta_0-\hat\beta_1 x_i)=0, \qquad \sum_i x_i(y_i-\hat\beta_0-\hat\beta_1 x_i)=0.

The first gives \hat\beta_0=\bar y-\hat\beta_1\bar x (the line through the means). Substituting into the second and simplifying gives \hat\beta_1=\dfrac{\sum_i(x_i-\bar x)(y_i-\bar y)}{\sum_i(x_i-\bar x)^2}. The two normal equations are the algebraic properties “residuals sum to zero” and “residuals uncorrelated with x.”
Why R^2=r_{xy}^2 in simple regression. With \hat y_i-\bar y=\hat\beta_1(x_i-\bar x), \text{SSE}=\hat\beta_1^2\sum(x_i-\bar x)^2. Insert \hat\beta_1=S_{xy}/S_{xx}: \text{SSE}=S_{xy}^2/S_{xx}, so R^2=\text{SSE}/\text{SST}=S_{xy}^2/(S_{xx}S_{yy})=r_{xy}^2.
The SER and degrees of freedom. \text{SER}=\sqrt{\text{SSR}/(n-2)}; the n-2 is the two degrees of freedom spent estimating \hat\beta_0 and \hat\beta_1, making \text{SER}^2 an unbiased estimate of \operatorname{Var}(u).
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

title: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit
text:
Connections

Builds on: conditional expectation (the regression line as the CEF / best predictor), covariance & correlation (the slope is Cov/Var; R^2=r^2), and estimation (OLS is an estimator).
Leads to: multiple regression (adding regressors to hold things constant and shrink u), and inference for coefficients (standard errors and tests on \hat\beta_1).
Mirrors: the ANOVA decomposition (SST = SSE + SSR is the between/within split) and the law of total variance.
crumbs: Simple Linear Regression: The Line of Best Fit

text:

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
text: Once a series is stationary, forecasting becomes tractable. The ARIMA family says the future is a blend of two memories — the series’ own past values (AR) and its past shocks (MA) — applied to a differenced (I) series. The Box–Jenkins method is the disciplined cycle for choosing, fitting, and checking such a model, and its forecasts come with one unavoidable honesty: the further ahead you look, the less you know.

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
text:
Why it matters
Forecasting is the explicit goal for vast swaths of applied time-series work — central banks, firms, governments all need a number for next quarter. (Enders 2014) ARIMA models are the classical workhorse, and even where modern ML competes, the ARIMA concepts — autoregression, the memory of shocks, differencing, and widening forecast uncertainty — remain the vocabulary. This entry turns the stationarity foundations into actual predictions, and brings the bias–variance and parsimony themes of the chapter to bear on choosing a model.

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
section: The building blocks: AR, MA, ARMA
text:
The building blocks: AR, MA, ARMA
Autoregressive — AR(p). Model today as a regression on its own recent past: (Enders 2014)

y_t = a_0 + a_1 y_{t-1} + a_2 y_{t-2} + \cdots + a_p y_{t-p} + \epsilon_t .

The AR(1) case y_t = a_0 + a_1 y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t depends only on yesterday. It is stationary iff |a_1| < 1; at a_1 = 1 it is the random walk. (Enders 2014) An AR process has infinite memory — a shock propagates forward through the chain of lagged y’s, decaying as a_1^h.
Moving average — MA(q). Model today as a weighted sum of recent shocks: (Enders 2014)

y_t = \epsilon_t + \beta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \cdots + \beta_q \epsilon_{t-q} .

The contrast with AR is the key intuition: MA has finite memory — a shock \epsilon_t affects the series for exactly q periods and then vanishes completely. (Enders 2014)
ARMA(p,q) combines both, often fitting a series with fewer total parameters than a pure AR or MA would need — the parsimony principle at work: (Enders 2014)

y_t = a_0 + \sum_{i=1}^p a_i\,y_{t-i} + \epsilon_t + \sum_{i=1}^q \beta_i\,\epsilon_{t-i}.

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
text:
ARIMA: adding the “I” for integrated
Most economic series are non-stationary in levels — they trend. (Enders 2014) The I in ARIMA handles that: a series is integrated of order d (I(d)) if it must be differenced d times to become stationary. So ARIMA(p,d,q) = difference the series d times (usually d = 1 for economic data), then fit an ARMA(p,q) to the stationary result \Delta^d y_t. (Enders 2014) The whole pipeline: difference to stationarity, model the dependence, forecast, then un-difference.

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
text:
The Box–Jenkins method
Box and Jenkins gave the field its disciplined, three-stage iterative cycle for finding the right model. (Enders 2014)
Stage 1 — Identification. On the stationary series, read the ACF and partial ACF (PACF) to propose tentative orders p and q. The PACF at lag s is the correlation between y_t and y_{t-s} after removing the linear influence of the intervening lags y_{t-1},\dots,y_{t-s+1} — it isolates the direct dependence at lag s, whereas the ordinary ACF blends direct and indirect channels together. The signature patterns: (Enders 2014)








Process
ACF
PACF




AR(p)
decays geometrically (smoothly or oscillating)
cuts off after lag p


MA(q)
cuts off after lag q
decays geometrically


ARMA(p,q)
decays (after lag q)
decays (after lag p)



The mnemonic: the PACF fingerprints AR order; the ACF fingerprints MA order.
Stage 2 — Estimation. Fit the tentative model by maximum likelihood or non-linear least squares. Enders stresses the principle of parsimony — the simplest adequate model, to avoid overfitting the sample’s idiosyncrasies — and compares candidates with the AIC and BIC/SBC information criteria (lower is better; both reward fit and penalise extra parameters). (Enders 2014)
Stage 3 — Diagnostic checking. A good model leaves residuals that are white noise — no structure left to exploit. The Ljung–Box Q-test checks whether a batch of residual autocorrelations is jointly non-zero; if it is, the model is inadequate and you loop back to Stage 1. (Enders 2014)

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
section: Forecasting: mean reversion and widening intervals
text:
Forecasting: mean reversion and widening intervals
With a fitted model, point forecasts come from iterating the conditional expectation forward — for AR(1), the one-step forecast is \mathbb{E}_t[y_{t+1}] = a_0 + a_1 y_t, then feed it back in for two steps, and so on. (Enders 2014) Two properties define how those forecasts behave:

Mean reversion. For any stationary ARMA model, the forecast \mathbb{E}_t[y_{t+j}] converges to the series’ unconditional mean as the horizon j grows. (Enders 2014) Far enough out, your best guess is simply “the long-run average” — the dependence that made the near future predictable has decayed away.
Widening intervals. Forecasts are (approximately) unbiased but less certain the further out you look: each additional period adds a new unknown shock \epsilon_{t+j} to the accumulated error, so the forecast-error variance grows with the horizon. (Enders 2014)



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
a1, mu, sigma = 0.7, 10.0, 1.0
a0 = mu * (1 - a1)
T = 80
y = np.empty(T); y[0] = mu
for t in range(1, T):
    y[t] = a0 + a1 * y[t-1] + rng.normal(0, sigma)

H = 30
fc = np.empty(H); last = y[-1]
for h in range(H):
    last = a0 + a1 * last
    fc[h] = last
# forecast-error variance at horizon h+1: sigma^2 * (1-a1^{2(h+1)})/(1-a1^2)
var_h = sigma**2 * (1 - a1**(2*(np.arange(1, H+1)))) / (1 - a1**2)
band = 1.96 * np.sqrt(var_h)

t_hist = np.arange(T); t_fut = np.arange(T, T + H)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.5, 4.0))
ax.plot(t_hist, y, color="#1f3a5f", lw=1.4, label="observed series")
ax.axhline(mu, color="#9aa0a6", ls="--", lw=1.3, label="unconditional mean")
ax.plot(t_fut, fc, color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.2, label="point forecast")
ax.fill_between(t_fut, fc - band, fc + band, color="#1f8a4c", alpha=0.18,
                label="95% forecast interval")
ax.axvline(T - 0.5, color="#c0392b", lw=0.8, ls=":")
ax.set_xlabel("time"); ax.set_ylabel("y")
ax.set_title("AR(1) forecast: mean reversion + widening uncertainty")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Forecasting a stationary AR(1) series (a₁ = 0.7, mean 10). The point forecast (blue) reverts toward the unconditional mean (grey dashed) as the horizon extends, and the 95% forecast interval (shaded) widens with each step — each future period adds a fresh unknown shock. For a stationary series the band widens toward a constant (the unconditional spread); for a random walk it would grow without bound.

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
text:
Honesty about the method
Enders is candid that Box–Jenkins is “a blend of skill, art, and perseverance,” not a mechanical science. (Enders 2014) The ambiguities are real: in his interest-rate-spread analysis, AIC and BIC disagree — one favouring an AR(7), the other an AR(6) — and only judgment resolves it. (Enders 2014) You must also watch for common factors — AR and MA terms that algebraically cancel. Writing L for the lag operator, which shifts a series back one period (Ly_t=y_{t-1}, so L^k y_t = y_{t-k}), a model such as (1-0.5L)y_t = (1-0.5L)\epsilon_t carries the same factor on both sides; it cancels to plain white noise y_t=\epsilon_t, signalling an over-parameterised model that a simpler one would capture. (Enders 2014) The discipline is the cycle; the craft is the choices within it.

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Fitting ARMA to a non-stationary series. Difference to stationarity first (the “I”); modelling trending levels invites spurious results. (Enders 2014)
Over-fitting with too many lags. A high-order model fits the sample’s noise and forecasts worse. Favour parsimony; let AIC/BIC and the test of residual whiteness discipline p and q. (Enders 2014)
Trusting long-horizon point forecasts. Stationary forecasts revert to the mean, so far-out predictions carry little information beyond the average — and their intervals are wide. Report the interval, not just the point. (Enders 2014)
Skipping diagnostic checking. If the residuals aren’t white noise (Ljung–Box rejects), the model is missing structure — respecify, don’t forecast. (Enders 2014)
Ignoring the common-factor trap. Cancelling AR and MA roots mean you’ve over-parameterised; simplify. (Enders 2014)

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Enders, Applied Econometric Time Series — the AR(p) model and the AR(1) stationarity condition |a_1|<1; the MA(q) model and its finite memory; ARMA parsimony; ARIMA(p,d,q) and the integrated/differencing component; the Box–Jenkins three stages (ACF/PACF identification with the cut-off/decay rules, ML/NLS estimation, parsimony and AIC/BIC, diagnostic checking with Ljung–Box); forecasting by iterated conditional expectation, mean reversion to the unconditional mean, and forecast-error variance growing with the horizon; the “skill, art, and perseverance” caveat, the interest-rate-spread AIC-vs-BIC ambiguity, and the common-factor problem.

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

AR(1) moments. For |a_1|<1, taking expectations of y_t=a_0+a_1y_{t-1}+\epsilon_t gives the unconditional mean \mu = a_0/(1-a_1), and the variance \gamma_0=\sigma^2/(1-a_1^2). The ACF is \rho_s = a_1^{\,s} — geometric decay, the AR fingerprint.
Why intervals widen (AR(1)). Write y_{t+h}-\mu = a_1^h(y_t-\mu) + \sum_{i=0}^{h-1} a_1^{i}\epsilon_{t+h-i}. The conditional forecast is \mathbb{E}_t[y_{t+h}] = \mu + a_1^h(y_t-\mu) (→ \mu as h\to\infty: mean reversion), and the forecast-error variance is

\operatorname{Var}_t(y_{t+h}) = \sigma^2\sum_{i=0}^{h-1} a_1^{2i} = \sigma^2\,\frac{1-a_1^{2h}}{1-a_1^2},

which increases in h and, for |a_1|<1, converges to the unconditional variance \sigma^2/(1-a_1^2) — so the band widens toward a constant. For a unit root (a_1=1) the sum is h\sigma^2, growing without bound (\sqrt h intervals) — the forecasting signature of non-stationarity.
Information criteria. \text{AIC} = -2\ln L + 2k and \text{BIC} = -2\ln L + k\ln n for k parameters and likelihood L. BIC’s heavier penalty (\ln n > 2 for n>7) favours smaller models — the usual source of the AIC-vs-BIC disagreement Enders flags.

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
text:
Connections

Builds on: time-series fundamentals (stationarity, the ACF, differencing, white noise), maximum likelihood (estimation), and cross-validation / parsimony (AIC/BIC as the model-selection analogue).
Closes: Part IV. You now have both the predictive paradigm (bias–variance, CV, regularization, ensembles, interpretability) and the dedicated time-series toolkit.
Leads to: Part V · Causal Inference — including time-series causal designs (difference-in-differences, event studies, synthetic control) that rest on these foundations.

title: Forecasting with ARIMA
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Write the AR(p), MA(q), and ARMA(p,q) model equations and explain the AR-infinite-memory vs MA-finite-memory contrast.”

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
text: Half of supervised learning predicts a label, not a number: voted or not, defaulted or not, relapsed or not. The squared-error machinery of bias–variance still governs the trade-offs, but the scoreboard changes. A single accuracy number lies when one class is rare; the honest picture is a confusion matrix and the sensitivity–specificity trade it encodes. And just as regression had an irreducible-error floor, classification has the Bayes error rate — the best any classifier could ever do.
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
text:
Why it matters
Everything so far measured a predictor by mean squared error — a regression loss. But the outcomes social scientists most often predict are categorical: a yes/no, a class, a diagnosis. (James et al. 2021) Classification is arguably more common than regression in applied social science — predicting loan defaults, vote choice, dropout, recidivism, clinical diagnosis — and it changes two things at once: the loss you minimise and the metrics you report. (Bruce et al. 2020) The modelling philosophy is unchanged — fit on a training set, judge on unseen data, tune flexibility by the bias–variance trade — but you need a new vocabulary to say what “good” means when there is no residual to square.
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
section: The Bayes classifier: the floor for labels
text:
The Bayes classifier: the floor for labels
Regression had irreducible error \operatorname{Var}(\epsilon) — a floor no model beats. (James et al. 2021) Classification has the exact analog: the Bayes classifier. Given the predictors X=x_0, it assigns the class with the highest conditional probability \Pr(Y=j \mid X=x_0) — for two classes, predict 1 whenever \Pr(Y=1\mid X=x_0) > 0.5. (James et al. 2021) This rule is unbeatable: no classifier can have a lower expected error rate.
Its error — the Bayes error rate — is the floor, and it is usually above zero because the classes overlap in the population: at the same x_0 you sometimes see a 1 and sometimes a 0, so even perfect knowledge of the probabilities cannot separate them. (James et al. 2021) This is the lesson to carry: just as no model predicts noise, no classifier achieves 100% accuracy when classes overlap — and chasing it means overfitting. The catch is that the Bayes rule needs the true \Pr(Y\mid X), which we never have; real methods estimate it (as logistic regression does directly) or approximate the rule by looking at nearby points — which is exactly what K-nearest neighbours does.
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
text:
K-nearest neighbours: the simplest learner
K-nearest neighbours (KNN) is the canonical non-parametric classifier and the natural first method to meet. (James et al. 2021) It makes no assumption about the shape of the boundary. To classify a new point x_0: find the K training observations closest to it, and take a majority vote of their labels. (James et al. 2021) That is the whole algorithm — it estimates the Bayes rule locally, using the neighbourhood’s class proportions as a stand-in for \Pr(Y=j\mid X=x_0).
KNN is worth knowing not because it always wins but because it makes two universal lessons concrete:

K is a flexibility dial. K=1 is maximally flexible — every point gets its nearest neighbour’s label, carving a jagged, overfit boundary (low bias, high variance). A large K averages over a wide neighbourhood, giving a smooth, rigid boundary (high bias, low variance). (James et al. 2021) The bias–variance U-curve reappears, now indexed by 1/K.
Scaling is not optional. Because KNN measures distance, a predictor on a large numeric scale (income in dollars) swamps one on a small scale (years of age) — the large-scale variable silently dominates “closeness.” You must standardise the predictors first so each contributes comparably. (James et al. 2021; Bruce et al. 2020) (This distance-sensitivity is why KNN degrades in high dimensions, where “nearest” neighbours are not actually near — the curse of dimensionality.)
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
section: The confusion matrix: the heart of classification metrics
text:
The confusion matrix: the heart of classification metrics
You cannot summarise a classifier with one number. Start from the confusion matrix — for a binary outcome, the 2\times2 table cross-tabulating predictions against truth: (Bruce et al. 2020)




Actual 1 (positive)
Actual 0 (negative)




Predicted 1
True Positive (TP)
False Positive (FP)


Predicted 0
False Negative (FN)
True Negative (TN)



Every metric is a ratio drawn from this table. The reason it matters — and the reason raw accuracy (\text{TP}+\text{TN})/n is dangerous — is the rare-class problem (the accuracy paradox). (Bruce et al. 2020) If only 1% of students attempt self-harm, a model that predicts “never” for everyone is 99% accurate and completely useless: it never flags the cases you built it to catch. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) Accuracy hides the failure; the confusion matrix exposes it.
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
text:
Sensitivity, specificity, precision
The table yields the metrics that actually matter, each answering a different question: (Bruce et al. 2020)

Sensitivity (= recall, the true-positive rate) =\dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FN}} — of the actual positives, what fraction did we catch? How good are we at finding the 1s?
Specificity (the true-negative rate) =\dfrac{\text{TN}}{\text{TN}+\text{FP}} — of the actual negatives, what fraction did we correctly clear?
Precision (positive predictive value) =\dfrac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP}+\text{FP}} — when we predict positive, how often are we right?

Which one rules depends on the costs of the two errors, and in social science they are rarely equal. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) Missing a high-risk student (a false negative) may be far costlier than offering unneeded tutoring to a safe one (a false positive) — so you would prize sensitivity even at the price of precision. A spam filter inverts the costs: a false positive (a real email lost to the spam folder) hurts more, so you prize precision. The metric is a policy choice, not a statistical default.
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
section: The threshold and the ROC curve
text:
The threshold and the ROC curve
Here is the move that ties it together. A classifier like logistic regression outputs a probability, and turning that into a label requires a cutoff. The default 0.5 is just the Bayes rule under equal costs — but you can move it. (Bruce et al. 2020) Lower the threshold and you flag more positives: sensitivity rises, but more false alarms drop specificity. Raise it and the reverse. Sensitivity and specificity are on a see-saw, and the threshold is the fulcrum.
Plot that entire trade-off and you get the ROC curve (Receiver Operating Characteristic): the true-positive rate against the false-positive rate (1-\text{specificity}) as the threshold sweeps from high to low. (Bruce et al. 2020) A useless (coin-flip) classifier rides the 45° diagonal; a good one bows toward the top-left corner (high sensitivity and high specificity at once). The single summary is the AUC — the Area Under the Curve: 0.5 is no better than chance, 1.0 is perfect separation. (Bruce et al. 2020) AUC has a clean interpretation — the probability that the model scores a randomly chosen positive above a randomly chosen negative — and, crucially, it judges the model across all thresholds at once, so you compare classifiers before committing to an operating point.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
n = 2000
# Latent scores: positives shifted up, but the classes OVERLAP (Bayes error > 0).
neg = rng.normal(0.0, 1.0, n)
pos = rng.normal(1.6, 1.0, n)
scores = np.concatenate([neg, pos])
labels = np.concatenate([np.zeros(n), np.ones(n)])

# Sweep thresholds high -> low, build the ROC by confusion-matrix counts.
thr = np.linspace(scores.max() + 0.5, scores.min() - 0.5, 250)
tpr = np.array([(pos >= t).mean() for t in thr])   # sensitivity
fpr = np.array([(neg >= t).mean() for t in thr])   # 1 - specificity
auc = np.sum(np.diff(fpr) * (tpr[:-1] + tpr[1:]) / 2)  # area under ROC (trapezoid)

t0 = 0.8                                            # one illustrative cutoff
tpr0, fpr0 = (pos >= t0).mean(), (neg >= t0).mean()

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(7.6, 3.7))

# Left: overlapping score distributions with a threshold.
axL.hist(neg, bins=40, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.7, label="actual 0")
axL.hist(pos, bins=40, color="#1f8a4c", alpha=0.6, label="actual 1")
axL.axvline(t0, color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.6)
axL.text(t0 + 0.1, axL.get_ylim()[1] * 0.9, "threshold", color="#c0392b", fontsize=8)
axL.set_xlabel("model score"); axL.set_ylabel("count")
axL.set_title("Overlapping scores"); axL.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)

# Right: the ROC curve.
axR.plot(fpr, tpr, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.2)
axR.fill_between(fpr, tpr, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.10)
axR.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], color="#9aa0a6", ls="--", lw=1.3)
axR.plot(fpr0, tpr0, "o", color="#c0392b", ms=7)
axR.text(fpr0 + 0.03, tpr0 - 0.08, "this cutoff", color="#c0392b", fontsize=8)
axR.text(0.55, 0.25, f"AUC = {auc:.2f}", color="#1f3a5f", fontsize=11, fontweight="bold")
axR.set_xlabel("false-positive rate (1 − specificity)")
axR.set_ylabel("true-positive rate (sensitivity)")
axR.set_title("ROC curve"); axR.set_xlim(0, 1); axR.set_ylim(0, 1)

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: From overlapping scores to the ROC curve. Left: a classifier outputs higher scores for positives (green) than negatives (grey), but the distributions overlap, so no single cutoff separates them perfectly — that overlap is the Bayes error made visible. Sliding the threshold (dashed line) trades false negatives for false positives. Right: sweeping every threshold traces the ROC curve; the area under it (AUC) summarises the model’s separating power across all cutoffs at once. The diagonal is a coin flip.
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
text:
Validation: same loop, different scoreboard
Does any of the cross-validation machinery change? The process does not: you still split train/validation/test, still use k-fold CV to tune flexibility, and still treat the test set as sacred. (James et al. 2021) Two things differ: (Bruce et al. 2020)

The metric. Replace MSE with a classification loss — the error rate, or better, a probability-aware loss like log-loss (cross-entropy), which penalises confident mistakes more than hesitant ones and rewards well-calibrated probabilities.
The threshold is itself a tuning choice. Pick the operating point to match your misclassification costs — and, like every data-driven choice, do it inside the CV loop, never on the test set, or you inherit the same optimism/leakage that inflates every other accuracy estimate. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reporting accuracy on imbalanced data. With a rare positive, “95% accurate” can mean “never finds the thing.” Always show the confusion matrix and the sensitivity for the class you care about. (Bruce et al. 2020; Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Treating 0.5 as sacred. The cutoff is a policy lever set by the relative cost of false negatives vs false positives — not a fixed constant. (Bruce et al. 2020)
Forgetting to scale before KNN (or any distance method). Unstandardised predictors let the largest-scale variable dictate “nearness,” silently. (James et al. 2021)
Chasing zero error. When classes overlap, the Bayes error rate is a hard floor; a classifier near it is finished, not deficient. Pushing past it fits noise. (James et al. 2021)
Tuning the threshold on the test set. That is leakage by another name. Choose it inside cross-validation. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, Introduction to Statistical Learning — the classification setting and the error rate; the Bayes classifier (predict the most probable class) and the Bayes error rate as the irreducible floor created by overlapping classes; KNN (majority vote of the K nearest points), K as a flexibility/bias–variance dial, the need to standardise, and the curse of dimensionality.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — the confusion matrix as the heart of classification metrics; sensitivity/recall, specificity, precision; the rare-class (accuracy) problem; thresholds, the ROC curve, and AUC.
Jacobucci, Grimm & Zhang, Machine Learning for Social & Behavioral Research — why the costs of false positives vs false negatives are unequal in social science, the accuracy paradox on rare outcomes, log-loss, and keeping the threshold/metric choices inside the validation loop.
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The Bayes rule and its error. For classes j, the Bayes classifier is 
\hat C_{\text{Bayes}}(x_0) = \arg\max_{j}\ \Pr(Y=j \mid X=x_0).
 At a point x_0 its error is 1-\max_j \Pr(Y=j\mid X=x_0), and the overall Bayes error rate is the average of this over the distribution of X: 
1 - \mathbb{E}_X\!\Big[\max_j \Pr(Y=j\mid X)\Big].
 It is zero only if some class has probability 1 everywhere — i.e. the classes never overlap. KNN estimates the inner probability by the local frequency \hat\Pr(Y=j\mid X=x_0)=\frac1K\sum_{i\in \mathcal N_K(x_0)} \mathbb 1(y_i=j) over the K nearest neighbours \mathcal N_K(x_0), then applies the same \arg\max. As n\to\infty with K growing slowly, KNN’s error approaches the Bayes rate.
Log-loss (cross-entropy). For predicted probabilities \hat p_i=\hat\Pr(Y_i=1\mid X_i) and labels y_i\in\{0,1\}, 
\text{log-loss} = -\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n \big[y_i\ln \hat p_i + (1-y_i)\ln(1-\hat p_i)\big].
 It is minimised (at 0) only by confident, correct probabilities and explodes when the model is confidently wrong (\hat p_i\to0 while y_i=1). This is exactly the negative log-likelihood that logistic regression maximises — the bridge from the explanatory model to the predictive scoreboard.
AUC as a probability. The area under the ROC equals \Pr\big(\hat s(X^+) > \hat s(X^-)\big) — the chance the model scores a random positive above a random negative — which is the Mann–Whitney U statistic rescaled. A coin flip gives 0.5; perfect ranking gives 1.
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

title: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category
text:
Connections

Builds on: bias–variance (the Bayes error rate is the classification floor, K is its flexibility dial) and logistic regression (the probability model whose output a threshold turns into a label).
Leads to: cross-validation (the same tuning loop, with a classification metric) and trees & forests (classifiers split on Gini/entropy, scored by these same metrics).
Reappears in: interpretability & ML and the prediction problems of Part V, where propensity scores are themselves classifiers whose calibration matters.
crumbs: Classification: When the Outcome Is a Category

text:
Study with NotebookLM

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text: Everything so far asked why: estimate an effect, defend it, attach a standard error. Predictive modelling asks a different question — what next? — and keeps score completely differently. A coefficient that’s wrong-but-significant fails the first goal; a black box that forecasts perfectly fails the second’s interpretability but wins its accuracy. Knowing which game you’re playing is the whole of this chapter.
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
Why it matters
The same fitted regression can be a triumph or a disaster depending on what you wanted from it, and conflating the two goals is the source of endless confusion across academia and industry. (James et al. 2021) Social science has traditionally lived in the explanatory culture — theory, hypotheses, interpretable effects. The predictive culture — machine learning — optimises something else entirely, and judges success on data the model has never seen. This entry draws the line cleanly, because every method in Part IV (cross-validation, regularization, trees) only makes sense once you accept the predictive scorecard, and because modern causal inference now borrows these tools.
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
Two goals









Explanatory (statistical / inferential)
Predictive (machine learning)




Asks
Why? — the mechanism
What next? — the forecast


Wants
unbiased, interpretable effects with valid uncertainty (p-values, CIs)
the most accurate prediction on new data


Will trade away
predictive accuracy, flexibility
interpretability, unbiasedness — happily treats the model as a black box


A “failure” is
a biased or uninterpretable coefficient
poor accuracy on unseen data



(James et al. 2021; Jacobucci et al. 2023) The point is not that one is better — it’s that they answer different questions and reward different models. A wrong-but-significant coefficient is a failure for explanation even if it predicts; a black box is a success for prediction even if no coefficient means anything. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
The statistical-learning frame: Y = f(X) + \epsilon
ISL gives the unifying notation. Assume the inputs X (features, predictors) relate to the output Y (the response) through (James et al. 2021)

Y = f(X) + \epsilon,

where f is the systematic information X carries about Y, and \epsilon is a random error, independent of X with mean zero. All of statistical learning is estimating f from training data to get \hat f — and there are exactly two reasons to do it: (James et al. 2021)

Prediction — produce a guess \hat Y = \hat f(X) for new observations. Here \hat f can be a black box; you only care that \hat Y is close to Y.
Inference — understand the form of f: which predictors matter, in what direction, linearly or not. Here you must be able to read \hat f.

This single fork — predict with \hat f vs interpret \hat f — is the two-cultures divide, formalised. (James et al. 2021)
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
Reducible vs irreducible error
Even a perfect estimate of f can’t predict perfectly, and ISL’s decomposition says why. The expected prediction error splits into two pieces: (James et al. 2021)

Reducible error — because \hat f \neq f. Better methods and more data shrink it; in principle it can go to zero. This is the part we work on.
Irreducible error — the \epsilon itself. Since Y depends on \epsilon, and \epsilon is by definition unpredictable from X, this error never goes away. It is an absolute ceiling on accuracy. (James et al. 2021)

Why does \epsilon exist? It holds the unmeasured and unmeasurable — a patient’s mood that day, a shopper’s whim, all the causes you didn’t (or couldn’t) record. (James et al. 2021) No algorithm, however clever, predicts what it has no information about.
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
The in-sample / out-of-sample paradox
Here is the crux of why a great explanatory model can be a terrible predictor. (James et al. 2021)

In-sample fit measures performance on the same data used to train — the R^2 and MSE you’ve optimised all through Part III.
Out-of-sample accuracy measures performance on a held-out test set (or via cross-validation) — data the model has never seen.

The two can diverge sharply through overfitting: a flexible model can fit the training data perfectly by memorising its noise — the random idiosyncrasies of those particular cases — rather than the signal f. (James et al. 2021)






TipLambert’s exam analogy



Overfitting is like cheating on a test by memorising the answer key beforehand: the student aces that exam and flunks the next one. (James et al. 2021) A model that has memorised its training data looks brilliant in-sample and fails the moment it meets new data — which is the only test that counts for prediction.



Watch it happen: training error always falls, test error doesn’t
Fit polynomials of rising flexibility to the same noisy data. The training error falls monotonically — more flexibility always fits the seen data better. But the test error is U-shaped: it improves, bottoms out at a sweet spot, then climbs as the model starts fitting noise. That gap is overfitting.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
def f_true(x):  return np.sin(2.2 * x) + 0.4 * x          # smooth signal

x_tr = np.sort(rng.uniform(-3, 3, 30))
y_tr = f_true(x_tr) + rng.normal(0, 0.45, x_tr.size)
x_te = np.sort(rng.uniform(-3, 3, 1000))
y_te = f_true(x_te) + rng.normal(0, 0.45, x_te.size)

degrees = range(1, 14)
train_mse, test_mse = [], []
for d in degrees:
    coef = np.polyfit(x_tr, y_tr, d)
    train_mse.append(np.mean((y_tr - np.polyval(coef, x_tr)) ** 2))
    test_mse.append(np.mean((y_te - np.polyval(coef, x_te)) ** 2))

train_mse, test_mse = np.array(train_mse), np.array(test_mse)
best = degrees[int(np.argmin(test_mse))]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.0))
ax.plot(list(degrees), train_mse, "o-", color="#9aa0a6", lw=2, label="training error (in-sample)")
ax.plot(list(degrees), test_mse, "o-", color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.2, label="test error (out-of-sample)")
ax.axvline(best, color="#1f8a4c", ls="--", lw=1.5)
ax.text(best + 0.2, test_mse.max() * 0.7, f"sweet spot\n(degree {best})", color="#1f8a4c", fontsize=8)
ax.axvspan(best + 0.5, 13.5, color="#c0392b", alpha=0.06)
ax.text(10.5, test_mse.max() * 0.9, "overfitting", color="#c0392b", fontsize=9, ha="center")
ax.set_xlabel("model flexibility (polynomial degree)"); ax.set_ylabel("mean squared error")
ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_title("Training error always falls; test error is U-shaped")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper center")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Training vs test error as model flexibility grows. The training error (grey) falls forever — a flexible enough model memorises the data it was shown. The test error (blue), on data never seen, is U-shaped: it falls, hits a minimum at moderate flexibility, then rises as the model fits noise. The minimum marks the best predictor; everything to its right is overfitting.





The lesson that organises the whole chapter: in-sample fit is not the goal — it can be made perfect and meaningless. The goal is the bottom of that blue curve, and finding it is what bias–variance and cross-validation are for.
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
section: Flexibility vs interpretability: no free lunch
text:
Flexibility vs interpretability: no free lunch
A second trade-off runs through everything: as flexibility rises, interpretability falls. (James et al. 2021)

Restrictive, interpretable methods — linear regression, the Lasso — can only bend so far, but you can read exactly how each predictor acts. Ideal for inference.
Flexible, opaque methods — boosting, random forests, neural networks — fit wildly non-linear relationships and often predict better, but produce an \hat f so complex that no single predictor’s role is legible. Ideal for prediction. (James et al. 2021)

There is no method that is both maximally flexible and maximally interpretable — choosing where to sit on that frontier is choosing your culture. (We return to it in interpretability.)
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
Breiman’s two cultures
Leo Breiman named the divide. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

The data-modelling culture assumes Nature follows a specific stochastic model (a linear regression, say) and estimates its parameters. This has dominated social science.
The algorithmic-modelling culture treats Nature’s mechanism as unknown and uses algorithms to find whatever function maps X to Y best, judged by prediction.

For social scientists this is more than a technical choice — it’s a methodological shift. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) Used well, predictive tools let researchers test whether existing theories actually hold up out of sample, discover heterogeneous treatment effects (how an intervention helps different people differently), and — because out-of-sample validation is built in — provide a principled guard against the p-hacking and fishing that plague the explanatory culture. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) The cultures are complements, not enemies — which is exactly why this part sits between regression and the causal summit.
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Judging a predictive model by in-sample R^2. Training fit can be made perfect by overfitting; it says nothing about new data. Evaluate out-of-sample, always. (James et al. 2021)
Judging an explanatory model by predictive accuracy (or vice versa). They optimise different things. A model can predict well with biased, uninterpretable coefficients — useless for a causal question — and an unbiased causal model can predict poorly. Pick the scorecard first. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Forgetting the irreducible error. No model beats the \epsilon ceiling; chasing zero error guarantees overfitting. Some of the world is genuinely unpredictable from your features. (James et al. 2021)
Assuming flexible = better. More flexibility lowers bias but raises variance and destroys interpretability; the right amount is a choice, not “as much as possible.” (James et al. 2021)
Treating a black box as an explanation. A forest can predict default beautifully and tell you nothing trustworthy about why — reading mechanism off a predictive model is a category error. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, Introduction to Statistical Learning — the Y=f(X)+\epsilon frame; estimating f for prediction vs inference; reducible vs irreducible error and the accuracy ceiling; in-sample vs out-of-sample and overfitting; the flexibility–interpretability trade-off (restrictive/interpretable vs flexible/opaque methods); the exam-cheating analogy for overfitting.
Jacobucci, Grimm & Zhang, Machine Learning for Social & Behavioral Research — the explanatory vs predictive cultures for researchers; Breiman’s data-modelling vs algorithmic-modelling cultures; using ML to test theory out-of-sample, find heterogeneous effects, and resist p-hacking.
Bruce & Bruce, Practical Statistics for Data Scientists — the applied train/test framing of predictive accuracy.
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The reducible/irreducible split. For a fixed \hat f and test point X=x_0, with Y=f(x_0)+\epsilon,

\mathbb{E}\big[(Y-\hat f(x_0))^2\big]
= \underbrace{\big(f(x_0)-\hat f(x_0)\big)^2}_{\text{reducible}}
+ \underbrace{\operatorname{Var}(\epsilon)}_{\text{irreducible}} .

The cross term vanishes because \epsilon has mean zero and is independent of \hat f(x_0). No choice of \hat f touches \operatorname{Var}(\epsilon) — hence the ceiling. Averaging the reducible part over training sets is what produces the bias–variance decomposition of the next entry.
Why training error understates test error. Training error is \frac1n\sum_i (y_i-\hat f(x_i))^2 evaluated at the same points used to choose \hat f, so \hat
f is tuned to those \epsilon_i. A flexible enough \hat f can drive it to 0 by interpolating the noise, while test error — evaluated at independent points with fresh \epsilon — rises. The gap is the optimism of the training error, and it grows with flexibility.
crumbs: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures

title: From Explaining to Predicting: Two Goals, Two Cultures
text:
Connections

Builds on: estimation (the MSE = variance + bias² seed, now the organising principle), simple regression (R^2 as in-sample fit), and the replication crisis (out-of-sample validation as a discipline against overfitting/p-hacking).
Leads to: the bias–variance trade-off (the U-shape, formalised), cross-validation (estimating test error honestly), and regularization (buying lower variance with a little bias).
Bridges to: causal inference, where ML predictions of nuisance functions power modern estimators, and the ML Track.

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text: The flexible methods that predict best are the hardest to read, and social science cares about why. This entry navigates that tension: the accuracy–interpretability frontier, the tools that partly open a black box (importance, partial dependence, SHAP), the iron rule that interpretable is not causal, and the genuinely exciting part — how machine learning, used carefully, comes back to serve the causal questions of Part V.

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
Why it matters
This entry closes the predictive sub-chapter by confronting its central discomfort for researchers: the most accurate models are black boxes, and a discipline built on explanation cannot simply accept “it predicts well, trust me.” (Jacobucci et al. 2023) So we do two things. First, survey the tools that make a complex model partly legible. Second — and more importantly — show that the right response isn’t to reject ML but to bend it back toward causation: ML is now a power tool inside modern causal inference, not a rival to it. This is the bridge from prediction to the summit.

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
The accuracy–interpretability frontier
The trade-off from the opening entry, now made central: as model flexibility rises, interpretability falls. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

Restrictive, transparent — linear and logistic regression, the Lasso: each coefficient is a ceteris paribus effect you can hand a policymaker. Limited in shape, but legible.
Flexible, opaque — random forests, boosting, neural nets: they bend to complex non-linearities and high-order interactions and often predict far better, but their input–output map runs through thousands of parameters that defy human explanation. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

methods = [
    ("Subset selection / Lasso", 0.92, 0.22),
    ("Linear / logistic regression", 0.96, 0.32),
    ("Generalized additive models", 0.70, 0.50),
    ("Decision trees", 0.80, 0.45),
    ("Bagging / random forests", 0.34, 0.74),
    ("Boosting", 0.28, 0.80),
    ("Neural nets / SVM", 0.15, 0.95),
]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.6))
for name, interp, flex in methods:
    ax.scatter(interp, flex, s=70, color="#1f3a5f", zorder=3)
    right = interp > 0.55                                   # label points inward
    ax.annotate(name, (interp, flex), textcoords="offset points",
                xytext=(-8 if right else 8, 4), fontsize=8.5,
                ha="right" if right else "left")
ax.annotate("", xy=(0.12, 0.97), xytext=(0.97, 0.20),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-", color="#c0392b", ls="--", lw=1.4))
ax.text(0.45, 0.50, "the frontier:\nno model is both", color="#c0392b", fontsize=8.5, rotation=-34)
ax.set_xlim(0.0, 1.05)                         # interpretability increasing rightward (ISL convention)
ax.set_ylim(0.1, 1.05)
ax.set_xlabel("Interpretability  →"); ax.set_ylabel("Flexibility / predictive accuracy  →")
ax.set_title("You choose where to sit — there's no free lunch")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The accuracy–interpretability frontier (after ISL). Methods trade off: linear/logistic regression and the Lasso are highly interpretable but inflexible; random forests, boosting, and neural nets are flexible and accurate but opaque. Social science has clustered in the lower-right (interpretable); predictive modelling pushes up-left. There is no method in the top-right corner.





Social science has historically clustered at the interpretable end, because a model that predicts perfectly but explains nothing is of limited value for theory-building. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) But that needn’t mean abandoning flexible models — only learning to interrogate them.

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
Opening the box: interpretable ML
A toolkit has grown up to extract human-readable insight from complex models. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

Variable (feature) importance — a heuristic ranking of which predictors drive the model, via Gini/RSS reduction or permutation. Answers “which features matter?”
Partial dependence plots (PDP) — show how the prediction changes as one feature varies, with all others averaged out. They reveal the shape of a learned relationship — linear, S-shaped, threshold — even inside a forest. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
SHAP and LIME — local explanations of a single prediction. SHAP (Shapley Additive exPlanations) attributes a prediction to its features by their exact additive contributions, naming the “key drivers” of this case; LIME fits a simple linear model locally around one point to see what mattered there. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) They answer “why did the model predict this for this person?”

These make a black box accountable — but accountable about its predictions, not about the world.

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
The iron rule: interpretable ≠ causal
The most important caveat in the chapter. A feature-importance ranking or a partial-dependence plot is descriptive and predictive — never, by itself, a causal effect. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) It shows how the model uses information to guess, not how the world works. A predictor can rank high because it’s a proxy, a collider, or a confounded correlate.
Causation still requires an identification strategy — randomization, instruments, a credible design — to strip out selection bias. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) As Chernozhukov puts it: however impressive a model looks, its outputs carry no causal interpretation unless the data satisfy conditions like conditional exogeneity / unconfoundedness — the very assumptions Part III hammered. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) Accuracy is not identification.

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
ML comes back to causation
Here is the exciting turn. Rather than competing with causal inference, modern ML has become a power tool inside it. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

High-dimensional controls. With thousands of potential confounders, you can’t hand-pick controls. Use the Lasso to systematically select which ones are needed to make a “other things equal” comparison credible. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Heterogeneous treatment effects (HTE). Instead of one average effect, causal forests (a causal adaptation of random forests) and BART automate the hunt for interactions — discovering for whom a treatment works best. Crucially, they are “honest”: they use separate subsamples for choosing the splits and for estimating effects, so they don’t manufacture illusory interactions (the garden of forking paths, defused by design). (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Double / debiased machine learning (DML). Use ML to flexibly learn the nuisance parts — how the outcome and the treatment each depend on the controls — then partial them out (an FWL-style residualisation) and estimate the treatment effect on what’s left. This keeps the final effect estimate valid for inference, dodging the bias that arises from plugging ML directly into a coefficient. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Prediction-policy problems. Some policy questions are genuinely predictive, not causal: targeting interventions to those most at risk — of suicide, of loan default, of dropping out. Here a good black-box predictor is exactly what’s wanted. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

This is the substance of the causal summit’s modern wing — and why prediction sits between regression and causation in this book.

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
Risks: bias, fishing, and gaming
Power brings hazards social scientists must manage. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

Encoded bias. Models learn the prejudices in their training data — text embeddings absorb gender and racial bias from the corpus — and can propagate or amplify them. Audit and correct. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Overfitting and fishing. Flexible models excel at mistaking noise for signal; without cross-validation and a sacred test set, “discoveries” won’t replicate. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Gaming (Goodhart’s law). As The Tyranny of Metrics warns, when a measure becomes a target, it stops being a good measure — people game the predictor. Combined with the duty to report everything (every algorithm, transformation, and tuning choice) to prevent p-hacking, this is the transparency the next part demands. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading importance or a PDP as a causal effect. They explain the model, not the world. Causal claims need identification, not a high feature ranking. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Rejecting ML wholesale because it’s a black box. Interpretable-ML tools and causal-ML methods (DML, causal forests) make flexible models genuinely useful to researchers. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Plugging ML predictions straight into a regression coefficient. Naïvely using ML for inference biases the effect; DML’s cross-fitting and orthogonalisation exist precisely to fix this. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Trusting a fair-looking model on biased data. ML faithfully reproduces the discrimination in its training data. Fairness must be checked, not assumed. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Optimising a deployed metric and forgetting Goodhart. Once people know the predictor, they game its inputs; a model that worked in validation can rot in deployment. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Jacobucci, Grimm & Zhang, Machine Learning for Social & Behavioral Research — the accuracy–interpretability trade-off and why social science resists black boxes; interpretable-ML tools (feature importance, partial dependence, SHAP, LIME); interpretable ≠ causal and the need for identification (citing Chernozhukov on unconfoundedness); ML for causal questions (high-dimensional Lasso controls, causal forests / BART for honest heterogeneous effects, double/debiased ML, prediction-policy problems); the risks of encoded bias, overfitting/fishing, and metric-gaming.
James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, Introduction to Statistical Learning — the flexibility–interpretability frontier and the placement of methods along it.
Angrist & Pischke — identification (randomization, IV) as the source of causal claims that ML accuracy cannot supply.

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Partial dependence, defined. The partial dependence of the prediction on feature x_S averages the fitted model over the empirical distribution of the other features x_C:

\widehat{\text{PD}}_S(x_S) = \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n \hat f\big(x_S,\, x_{C,i}\big).

Sweeping x_S traces the marginal shape the model learned — interpretable, but still a property of \hat f, not a causal dose–response.
Why naïve ML biases an effect (the DML idea). For Y=\theta D + g(X)+\epsilon, D=m(X)+v, regressing Y on D with an ML estimate \hat g plugged in carries the regularisation bias of \hat g into \hat\theta. DML instead forms residuals \tilde Y = Y-\hat g(X) and \tilde D = D-\hat m(X) (each fit on a separate fold — cross-fitting) and regresses \tilde Y on \tilde D. The resulting

\hat\theta = \frac{\sum_i \tilde D_i \tilde Y_i}{\sum_i \tilde D_i^2}

is Neyman-orthogonal: first-order insensitive to errors in \hat g,\hat m, so it stays \sqrt n-consistent and supports valid confidence intervals. It is exactly Frisch–Waugh–Lovell partialling-out, with ML doing the partialling and sample-splitting protecting the inference.

title: Interpretability & ML for Social Science
text:
Connections

Builds on: explaining vs predicting (the two cultures), trees & forests (the black boxes being interpreted), regularization (Lasso for high-dimensional control selection), and regression & causality (identification, which ML cannot replace).
Leads to: causal inference — causal forests, double ML, and high-dimensional controls are its modern, ML-powered wing — and to the tyranny of metrics (Goodhart, gaming, transparency).
Closes: the predictive paradigm sub-chapter; next, the dedicated time-series treatment.
