title: Time-Series Fundamentals
text: Everything so far assumed observations were a random sample — order irrelevant, each draw independent. Time series breaks both: the data come in order, and today is correlated with yesterday. That single change voids the i.i.d. machinery, introduces a spectacular trap (spurious regression), and forces a new central concept — stationarity — before any forecasting can begin.

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
text:
Why it matters
GDP, inflation, public opinion, crime rates, stock prices — vast tracts of social data arrive as time series, and the tools of the previous chapters can mislead badly when applied to them. (Enders 2014) The defining feature is temporal ordering: in a cross-section the order of rows is meaningless, but in a time series the sequence is everything, because the past affects the future. (Wooldridge 2019) This breaks the i.i.d. assumption under all of Parts II–IV, and it opens a notorious trap — regressing two unrelated trending series yields a beautiful, entirely fake result. This entry builds the foundations (dependence, stationarity, unit roots) that the forecasting entry then turns into models.

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
section: What a time series is made of
text:
What a time series is made of
A series is conventionally decomposed into four components: (Enders 2014)

Trend — long-run direction (real GDP’s steady climb).
Seasonality — regular fluctuations within a year (December retail, spring housing starts).
Cycles — periodic swings longer than a year (the business cycle).
Irregular / random — the unpredictable noise left after the rest.

But the deeper feature is dependence: today’s GDP is highly correlated with yesterday’s, so the observations are emphatically not independent. (Wooldridge 2019) Quantifying that dependence is the first task.

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
text:
Autocorrelation and the ACF
Autocorrelation (serial correlation) is the correlation of a series with its own past. The autocorrelation function (ACF) reports it at each lag s: (Enders 2014)

\rho_s = \frac{\gamma_s}{\gamma_0},

where \gamma_s = \operatorname{Cov}(y_t, y_{t-s}) is the autocovariance at lag s and \gamma_0 = \operatorname{Var}(y_t). Plotting \rho_s against s gives the correlogram, the basic diagnostic of a series’ memory. For a well-behaved (stationary) series the correlogram decays toward zero as lags grow — the distant past eventually stops mattering. (Enders 2014)

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
section: Stationarity: the stability a series needs
text:
Stationarity: the stability a series needs
For inference and forecasting to be possible at all, the process must be stable over time — stationary. The working definition is weak (covariance) stationarity: (Enders 2014)

\mathbb{E}[y_t] = \mu, \qquad \operatorname{Var}(y_t) = \sigma^2, \qquad
\operatorname{Cov}(y_t, y_{t-s}) = \gamma_s \text{ (depends only on the lag } s, \text{ not on } t).

In words: constant mean, constant variance, and an autocovariance that depends only on the gap between observations, not on when you look. (Enders 2014) Why insist on this? Because if a process changes its basic nature at every instant, a single historical path tells you nothing about the future. (Enders 2014) Stationarity is precisely what lets the law of large numbers and the CLT apply to time averages — restoring the ground the i.i.d. assumption used to provide.
The atom of it all is white noise: a sequence \{\epsilon_t\} with mean zero, constant variance \sigma^2, and zero autocorrelation at every non-zero lag. (Enders 2014) White noise is the purest stationary series and the building block of every model in the next entry.

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
section: The random walk and the unit root
text:
The random walk and the unit root
The most important non-stationary process — and the one social data so often resembles — is the random walk: (Enders 2014)

y_t = y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t.

Each value is last period’s plus a fresh shock. Embed it in y_t = \rho_1 y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t; the random walk is the knife-edge case \rho_1 = 1, a unit root. (Enders 2014) Two consequences make it treacherous:

The variance grows with time: \operatorname{Var}(y_t) = t\sigma^2. The series has no tendency to revert to any mean — it meanders wherever the shocks push it. (Enders 2014)
Shocks are permanent. In a stationary series a shock fades; in a random walk every shock shifts all future values forever. (Enders 2014)

Add a constant and you get a random walk with drift, y_t = a_0 + y_{t-1} + \epsilon_t, combining a deterministic trend with the stochastic wandering. (Enders 2014) To test for a unit root, the Dickey–Fuller test regresses the change on the lagged level, \Delta y_t = \gamma y_{t-1} + \cdots; failing to reject \gamma = 0 means a unit root is present. (Enders 2014)

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
text:
The trap: spurious regression
Here is the warning that makes this entry essential. Regress one trending, non-stationary series on another, completely unrelated one, and you get a deceptively high R^2 and wildly significant t-statistics — pointing to a strong relationship that does not exist. (Wooldridge 2019) Both series wander under their own stochastic trends, and by sheer coincidence they appear to move together. First diagnosed by Granger and Newbold (1974), this is spurious regression, and its lesson is blunt: in time-series levels, a high R^2 is often a sign of trouble, not of a good fit. (Enders 2014)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
T = 200
y = np.cumsum(rng.normal(0, 1, T))      # random walk 1
x = np.cumsum(rng.normal(0, 1, T))      # random walk 2 — INDEPENDENT of y

# regress y on x
b1 = np.cov(x, y, bias=True)[0, 1] / np.var(x)
b0 = y.mean() - b1 * x.mean()
fit = b0 + b1 * x
ss_res = np.sum((y - fit) ** 2); ss_tot = np.sum((y - y.mean()) ** 2)
r2 = 1 - ss_res / ss_tot
se = np.sqrt(ss_res / (T - 2) / np.sum((x - x.mean()) ** 2))
tstat = b1 / se

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 3.8))
axL.plot(y, color="#1f3a5f", lw=1.6, label="series Y (random walk)")
axL.plot(x, color="#d98a29", lw=1.6, label="series X (random walk)")
axL.set_title("Two independent random walks"); axL.set_xlabel("time")
axL.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)

axR.scatter(x, y, s=12, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.6)
xs = np.linspace(x.min(), x.max(), 30)
axR.plot(xs, b0 + b1 * xs, color="#c0392b", lw=2.2)
axR.set_title(f"Regress Y on X:  R² = {r2:.2f},  t = {tstat:.1f}")
axR.set_xlabel("X"); axR.set_ylabel("Y")
fig.suptitle("Spurious regression: a strong 'relationship' that isn't there", fontsize=12)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Spurious regression. Left: two completely independent random walks — neither has any relationship to the other. Right: regress one on the other and OLS reports a high R² and a hugely ‘significant’ t-statistic. The relationship is pure coincidence of two wandering trends. In time-series levels, a high R² is a warning, not a triumph.





A “t of 10” and an “R^2 of 0.7” between two series that are, by construction, unrelated — that is the whole danger in one picture. Standard inference simply does not work on non-stationary levels.

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
text:
The fix: differencing and integration
The cure for a unit root is differencing. The first difference \Delta y_t = y_t - y_{t-1} of a random walk is — by construction — just \epsilon_t, i.e. white noise, which is stationary. (Enders 2014) More generally, a series is integrated of order d, written I(d), if it must be differenced d times to become stationary: (Enders 2014)

I(0): already stationary (white noise, a stationary AR series).
I(1): one unit root — a random walk. Most highly persistent economic series (interest rates, the S&P 500) are treated as I(1). (Enders 2014)

A series made stationary by differencing is difference-stationary. Once differenced to stationarity, the honest forecasting models of the next entry — the “I” in ARIMA — apply. (Enders 2014)

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Running OLS on non-stationary levels. Spurious regression gives fake high R^2 and huge t-stats between unrelated series. Test for stationarity (Dickey–Fuller) and difference first. (Wooldridge 2019)
Treating a high R^2 as success in time series. In levels it often signals shared trends, not a real relationship — the opposite of the cross-sectional intuition. (Enders 2014)
Assuming shocks fade. In an I(1) series shocks are permanent; “it’ll revert to trend” is false for a random walk. (Enders 2014)
Ignoring autocorrelation. Serially correlated errors make ordinary standard errors wrong (too small), inflating significance — the time-series cousin of clustering. (Wooldridge 2019)
Over-differencing. Differencing an already-stationary series injects an artificial MA unit root and needless noise. Difference only as much as a unit-root test demands. (Enders 2014)

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Enders, Applied Econometric Time Series — temporal ordering and the trend/seasonality/cycle/ irregular decomposition; the ACF \rho_s=\gamma_s/\gamma_0 and the correlogram; weak (covariance) stationarity and why it underwrites inference; white noise; the random walk, the unit root (\rho_1=1), growing variance t\sigma^2, permanent shocks, drift, and the Dickey–Fuller test; spurious regression (Granger–Newbold 1974); differencing, I(d) integration, and difference-stationarity.
Wooldridge, Introductory Econometrics — time series vs cross-section and the breakdown of i.i.d.; serial correlation and its effect on inference; the spurious-regression warning.

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Why a random walk’s variance grows. Iterating y_t=y_{t-1}+\epsilon_t from y_0 gives y_t = y_0 + \sum_{i=1}^t \epsilon_i. With i.i.d. shocks of variance \sigma^2, \operatorname{Var}(y_t)=t\sigma^2 — unbounded in t, so no stationary variance exists. The autocovariance \operatorname{Cov}(y_t,y_{t-s})=(t-s)\sigma^2 depends on t, not just s — violating stationarity directly.
Shocks are permanent. \partial y_{t+h}/\partial \epsilon_t = 1 for every horizon h\ge 0 (the shock enters the running sum and never leaves), versus a stationary AR(1) where the effect is \rho_1^h \to 0. Permanence is the signature of a unit root.
Why spurious regression happens. Regressing one I(1) series on an independent I(1) series, the OLS t-statistic does not converge to a standard normal; it diverges with the sample size, so “significance” grows with T no matter what. The fix — differencing to I(0), or cointegration when a genuine long-run relation exists — restores standard asymptotics. Two I(1) series are cointegrated when some linear combination of them is itself I(0)/stationary: each wanders without bound, yet they wander together, tethered by a long-run equilibrium (the textbook case is a drunk and her dog — both random walks, but never far apart). When that holds, a regression in levels is no longer spurious but estimates the genuine equilibrium relation.
First-differencing a random walk. \Delta y_t = y_t - y_{t-1} = \epsilon_t, which is white noise: mean 0, variance \sigma^2, zero autocorrelation — stationary by inspection. Hence a random walk is I(1).

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
text:
Connections

Builds on: covariance & correlation (autocorrelation is correlation with the past), the LLN & CLT (which stationarity restores for time averages), and regression diagnostics (serial correlation as a cousin of heteroskedasticity/clustering).
Leads to: forecasting with ARIMA — AR, MA, and the “I” that this entry’s differencing supplies.
Warns: any regression on time-series levels, where spurious results lurk, and connects to predictive evaluation (time-aware splits, not random folds).

title: Time-Series Fundamentals
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Why is time-series data different from cross-sectional data, and what are the four components of a series?”
“Define the autocorrelation function and the correlogram, and what they look like for a stationary series.”
“Define weak (covariance) stationarity and explain why it matters for inference and forecasting.”
“Explain the random walk and the unit root — why is the variance t·σ² and why are shocks permanent?”
“What is spurious regression (Granger–Newbold), and how does differencing to a stationary series fix it?”

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text: The whole of Part V chased a single number: an average effect. But “never cross a river that is on average four feet deep.” Causal machine learning estimates the effect as a function of who you are — the conditional average treatment effect \tau(x) — using the flexibility of ML without inheriting its bias. The trick is to estimate the effect, not the outcome, and to protect that estimate from overfitting with orthogonalisation and cross-fitting. It closes the loop: the predictive culture, disciplined back into the service of cause.

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text:
Why it matters
An ATE can hide everything that matters for policy: a drug that helps the young and harms the old can average to “no effect.” (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) Modern social science wants heterogeneous treatment effects — for whom does this work? — and the obvious idea, “throw a random forest at it,” fails in a way worth understanding deeply. This entry is where Part IV’s machinery — regularization, cross-validation, forests, the bias–variance trade — is repurposed for causation, and where the double/debiased ML preview is finally cashed out. It is the natural summit of the summit: the predictive and causal cultures, reconciled.

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text:
From ATE to CATE
The target shifts from a scalar to a function — the conditional average treatment effect: 
\tau(x)=\mathbb E\big[Y(1)-Y(0)\mid X=x\big].
 \tau(x) is the effect for units with characteristics x — the engine of personalised policy, letting you move from a uniform rollout to targeting whoever benefits most. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) Everything from potential outcomes still holds; we’ve simply stopped averaging away the variation we now want to see.

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
section: Why naive ML is biased for effects
text:
Why naive ML is biased for effects
The tempting move — fit a flexible ML model of Y on (D,X) and read off D’s influence — is biased, for a reason that is subtle and important: (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)

Regularization bias. ML methods are built to minimise prediction error for Y, so they regularize — shrinking coefficients, pruning splits — toward whatever predicts the outcome best. When the treatment effect is small relative to other predictors, the algorithm cheerfully shrinks the treatment’s role toward zero, biasing the effect. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)
Effect ≠ prediction. Estimating an effect is fundamentally harder than predicting an outcome: it targets the difference between two counterfactual states, only one of which is ever observed. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) A model superb at predicting Y can be terrible at isolating \partial
Y/\partial D.

The fix is to stop estimating the outcome and start estimating the effect, with the nuisances partialled out.

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text:
Double/debiased machine learning
Double machine learning (DML), due to Chernozhukov and coauthors, lets you use any flexible ML for the hard prediction parts while keeping valid inference on the causal parameter. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) Three moves:

Estimate two nuisance functions with ML: an outcome model g_0(X)=\mathbb E[Y\mid X] and a treatment/propensity model m_0(X)=\mathbb E[D\mid X]. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)
Orthogonalise (partial out). Residualise both Y and D on X — regress the Y-residual on the D-residual. This is Frisch–Waugh–Lovell with ML doing the conditioning, and it yields a Neyman-orthogonal moment: the effect estimate is first-order insensitive to small errors in g_0 and m_0. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) You can plug in imperfect ML nuisances and the errors don’t spill into the treatment effect — the “debiasing.”
Cross-fit. Estimate the nuisances on one fold and the effect on a held-out fold, so no unit’s own outcome contaminates its own nuisance prediction — removing overfitting bias. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) (Exactly the cross-validation discipline, now load-bearing for inference, not just tuning.)

Orthogonality kills the regularization bias; cross-fitting kills the overfitting bias. Together they let ML serve identification instead of sabotaging it. The figure shows the payoff DML and causal forests deliver — recovering an effect that varies with x.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(21)
n = 6000
x = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)
D = rng.integers(0, 2, n)                  # randomized treatment (clean identification)
tau_true = 0.5 + 3.0 * x                   # effect grows with x (heterogeneous)
y = 1.0 + 2.0 * x + tau_true * D + rng.normal(0, 1.0, n)

# Honest binned CATE: within each x-bin, treated mean - control mean (+ 95% CI).
edges = np.linspace(0, 1, 11)
centers, est, lo, hi = [], [], [], []
for a, b in zip(edges[:-1], edges[1:]):
    m = (x >= a) & (x < b)
    yt, yc = y[m & (D == 1)], y[m & (D == 0)]
    d = yt.mean() - yc.mean()
    se = np.sqrt(yt.var(ddof=1)/yt.size + yc.var(ddof=1)/yc.size)
    centers.append((a+b)/2); est.append(d); lo.append(d-1.96*se); hi.append(d+1.96*se)
centers, est = np.array(centers), np.array(est)
ate = (y[D==1].mean() - y[D==0].mean())

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
xs = np.linspace(0, 1, 100)
ax.plot(xs, 0.5 + 3.0*xs, color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.4, label="true CATE  τ(x)")
ax.errorbar(centers, est, yerr=[est-np.array(lo), np.array(hi)-est], fmt="o",
            color="#1f3a5f", ms=5, capsize=3, label="honest binned estimate")
ax.axhline(ate, color="#9aa0a6", ls="--", lw=1.6, label=f"ATE = {ate:.2f} (hides heterogeneity)")
ax.set_xlabel("covariate x"); ax.set_ylabel("treatment effect")
ax.set_title("Who benefits? The effect as a function of x")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Heterogeneous treatment effects. The true CATE τ(x) (green) rises with a covariate x — the treatment helps high-x units far more than low-x units, a pattern the ATE (grey dashed) completely hides. An honest, binned effect estimator (blue points, with 95% intervals) recovers τ(x) by comparing treated and control outcomes within strata of x — the intuition behind causal forests, which split to maximise effect heterogeneity and estimate honestly for valid intervals.

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text:
Causal forests and honesty
To estimate \tau(x) flexibly, causal forests (generalized random forests) adapt random forests with two changes: (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)

Causal splitting. Instead of splitting to reduce prediction error (RSS), trees split to maximise the heterogeneity of the estimated treatment effect across the children — actively hunting for the covariates along which the effect varies. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)
Honesty. A tree is honest if it uses one subsample to choose the splits and a separate subsample to estimate the effect in each leaf. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) This separation is what makes the leaf estimates statistically valid — it earns trustworthy confidence intervals on \tau(x), instead of the overconfident ones you’d get from estimating effects in the very leaves you carved to maximise them.

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text:
Policy learning
The point of knowing \tau(x) is often to act. Policy learning turns CATEs into an assignment rule \pi(x). (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) The unconstrained optimum is simple — treat everyone with a positive predicted effect: 
\pi^*(x)=\mathbf 1\{\tau(x)\ge 0\}.
 Framed as a classification problem, it predicts the sign of the effect, weighted by its magnitude — misclassifying a large positive effect costs more than fumbling a near-zero one (cost-sensitive classification). (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) This is where causal inference meets decision-making: not “what is the effect?” but “whom should we treat?”

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
section: The cautions: ML is not a license
text:
The cautions: ML is not a license
The most important sentence in the chapter: machine learning does not fix identification. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024) Every method here delivers causal answers only if the identifying assumptions still hold — conditional exogeneity (unconfoundedness) and overlap 0<\Pr(D=1\mid X)<1. DML and causal forests make you efficient and flexible under those assumptions; they cannot conjure them. Throwing a forest at confounded data yields a beautifully estimated biased effect. Two further risks: (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)

Fairness. ML models absorb the prejudices in their training data (gender and racial bias in text embeddings, biased historical decisions) — a Part IV worry with higher stakes when the output assigns treatment.
Interpretability ≠ mechanism. Tools like SHAP or distilling a forest into a shallow tree explain how the model guesses, not the causal mechanism of the world. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Naive ML on the outcome. Regularization shrinks small treatment effects toward zero. Orthogonalise (DML) — estimate the effect, not Y. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)
Skipping cross-fitting. Using a unit’s own data to fit its nuisance reintroduces overfitting bias into the effect. Split the folds. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)
Dishonest forests. Choosing splits and estimating effects on the same data gives invalid, overconfident intervals on \tau(x). Use honest estimation. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)
Believing ML earns identification. Unconfoundedness and overlap are still required; ML buys flexibility, not exogeneity. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)
Acting on a biased CATE. A policy rule is only as causal as the \tau(x) beneath it — garbage identification in, harmful targeting out. (Chernozhukov et al. 2024)

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Chernozhukov et al., Applied Causal Inference Powered by ML and AI — the CATE \tau(x)=\mathbb E[Y(1)-Y(0)\mid X=x] and the “four-feet-deep river”; regularization bias from naive outcome-prediction ML; double/debiased ML — nuisances g_0(X),m_0(X), Neyman orthogonality, cross-fitting; causal forests / GRF with causal splitting and honest estimation for valid CIs; policy learning \pi^*(x)=\mathbf 1\{\tau(x)\ge0\} as cost-sensitive classification; and the cautions — ML doesn’t fix identification (needs unconfoundedness + overlap), fairness, and interpretability-vs-mechanism.
Jacobucci, Grimm & Zhang, ML for Social & Behavioral Research — heterogeneous effects for social science, encoded bias/fairness, and interpretability tools (SHAP, distillation) describing the model, not the mechanism.

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The orthogonalised (partialling-out) estimator. In the partially linear model Y=\theta D+g_0(X)+\varepsilon, D=m_0(X)+v, define residuals \tilde Y=Y-\mathbb E[Y\mid X] and \tilde D=D-m_0(X). The DML estimate solves the Neyman-orthogonal moment 
\hat\theta=\frac{\frac1n\sum_i \tilde D_i\,\tilde Y_i}{\frac1n\sum_i \tilde D_i^2},
 each nuisance fit on a separate fold. Orthogonality means \partial_{\eta}\mathbb E[\psi(W;\theta,\eta)]=0 at the truth: small errors \hat g-g_0, \hat m-m_0 enter the estimating equation only at second order, so \hat\theta stays \sqrt n-consistent and asymptotically normal even though the nuisances converge slowly. This is FWL made robust to ML noise.
Honest CATE intervals. Split the data: use sample \mathcal I_1 to grow the tree (choose splits maximising \sum_{\text{children}} n_c\,\hat\tau_c^2, effect heterogeneity), and the independent sample \mathcal I_2 to estimate each leaf’s \hat\tau(x). Because the leaf was not chosen using \mathcal I_2’s outcomes, the leaf estimate is (asymptotically) unbiased with a usable variance — the formal basis for confidence intervals on \tau(x).
Policy value. A rule \pi earns welfare V(\pi)=\mathbb E[\,Y(\pi(X))\,]=\mathbb E[\,\pi(X)\tau(X)\,]+\text{const}, maximised pointwise by \pi^*(x)=\mathbf 1\{\tau(x)\ge0\} — treat exactly where the effect is positive; the regret of any rule is \mathbb E[(\pi^*-\pi)\tau], which weights mistakes by effect size.

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text:
Connections

Builds on: interpretability & ML (the DML/causal- forest preview, now full), regularization and cross-validation (the bias they cause and cure here), FWL partialling-out, and matching/overlap (the identification ML still needs).
Closes: the summit — from the fundamental problem to estimating who benefits and whom to treat.
Leads to: combining & triangulating designs (doubly robust estimation is the stacking of the propensity and outcome models seen here), then sensitivity analysis & external validity (how fragile and how generalisable any of these estimates are), and onward to Part VI · doing research in the world, where these estimates meet measurement, incentives, fairness, and the tyranny of metrics.

title: Causal Machine Learning: Heterogeneous Effects
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Define the CATE \tau(x) and explain why it matters more than the ATE for policy (‘never cross a river that is on average four feet deep’).”
“Why does naively applying prediction-focused ML to the outcome give biased treatment-effect estimates (regularization bias)?”
“Explain double/debiased machine learning: nuisance functions, Neyman orthogonality, and cross-fitting. How does each remove a source of bias?”
“What are causal forests, causal splitting, and honest estimation, and why is honesty needed for valid confidence intervals on \tau(x)?”
“What is policy learning (\pi^*(x)=\mathbf 1\{\tau(x)\ge0\}), and what cautions remain — why does ML not fix identification?”

title: Difference-in-Differences
text: When a policy hits one group and not another, you can’t just compare the two — they differed to begin with. And you can’t just track the treated group over time — everything else moved too. Difference-in- differences does both subtractions at once: the treated group’s change minus the control group’s change. The first difference erases fixed group gaps; the second erases shocks common to all. What survives is the treatment effect — if the groups would have moved in parallel anyway.

title: Difference-in-Differences
text:
Why it matters
RD needs a sharp cutoff; IV needs a clever instrument. DiD needs only a policy change, a comparison group, and data before and after — which is why it is the most widely used quasi-experimental design in economics and policy. (Cunningham 2021) It exploits a different kind of as-good-as-random variation: the timing of a treatment that some units get and others don’t. And it is the design where the field has most recently discovered it was doing things wrong — making this entry both a how-to and a cautionary tale. (Baker et al. 2025)

title: Difference-in-Differences
text:
The canonical 2×2
The simplest DiD has two groups — treated (D=1) and control (D=0) — and two periods, before and after. The estimator double-differences four means: (Baker et al. 2025) 
\hat\delta_{DD}=\big(\bar Y_{\text{treat,post}}-\bar Y_{\text{treat,pre}}\big)
-\big(\bar Y_{\text{ctrl,post}}-\bar Y_{\text{ctrl,pre}}\big).
 Read the two subtractions: (Baker et al. 2025)

The first difference (post − pre, within each group) wipes out every time-invariant group trait — the treated group can be richer, sicker, bigger; if it doesn’t change, it cancels.
The second difference (treated change − control change) wipes out any shock common to both groups in that period — a recession, a seasonal swing, a nationwide trend.

Two confounding layers removed by arithmetic, no controls required. What remains is attributed to the treatment.






NoteCard & Krueger and the minimum wage



The study that made DiD famous — and overturned a textbook prediction. In 1992 New Jersey raised its minimum wage from $4.25 to $5.05; neighbouring Pennsylvania held at $4.25. Card & Krueger surveyed ~400 fast-food restaurants before and after, measuring full-time-equivalent employment: (Cunningham 2021)




Pre
Post
Change




New Jersey (treated)
20.44
21.03
+0.59


Pennsylvania (control)
23.33
21.17
−2.16



DiD = (+0.59)-(-2.16) = \mathbf{+2.76}. Employment fell in Pennsylvania (a common regional shock) but rose relative to it in New Jersey — the opposite of the competitive prediction that a higher minimum wage destroys jobs. Note the logic: NJ’s raw +0.59 alone would be misleading; only by netting out PA’s -2.16 regional slump does the policy’s relative effect appear. (Cunningham 2021)

title: Difference-in-Differences
text:
The one assumption: parallel trends
DiD’s entire credibility rests on parallel trends: absent the treatment, the treated and control groups would have moved in parallel. (Baker et al. 2025) The control group’s change is used as the treated group’s missing counterfactual change — valid only if their paths would have stayed parallel. Two things to internalise: (Baker et al. 2025)

It is fundamentally untestable. It concerns the treated group’s unobserved no-treatment path after the intervention — a counterfactual we never see.
Pre-trends are the evidence. If the groups tracked each other before treatment, it’s more plausible they’d have continued. So researchers plot the pre-period and check for parallel paths — necessary support, not proof. (Levels may differ wildly; it is the slopes that must match.)



Show the figure code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

t = [0, 1]                       # pre, post
nj = [20.44, 21.03]             # treated
pa = [23.33, 21.17]             # control
nj_cf = [20.44, 20.44 + (pa[1] - pa[0])]   # counterfactual: NJ + PA's change
did = (nj[1] - nj[0]) - (pa[1] - pa[0])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.2))
ax.plot(t, nj, "o-", color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.4, label="New Jersey (treated)")
ax.plot(t, pa, "o-", color="#9aa0a6", lw=2.4, label="Pennsylvania (control)")
ax.plot(t, nj_cf, "o--", color="#c0392b", lw=2.0, label="NJ counterfactual (parallel)")
ax.annotate("", xy=(1, nj[1]), xytext=(1, nj_cf[1]),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="<->", color="#c0392b", lw=2.0))
ax.text(1.02, (nj[1] + nj_cf[1]) / 2, f"DiD = {did:+.2f}", color="#c0392b", fontsize=10, va="center")
ax.set_xticks(t); ax.set_xticklabels(["pre (Feb 1992)", "post (Nov 1992)"])
ax.set_ylabel("FTE employment per restaurant")
ax.set_title("Each group its own control: subtract the common shock")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="lower left")
ax.set_xlim(-0.15, 1.35)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Difference-in-differences (Card–Krueger). New Jersey (treated) and Pennsylvania (control) start at different employment levels — DiD allows that. Pennsylvania’s change (−2.16) estimates the common shock both states faced; applying it to New Jersey gives the counterfactual (dashed) — where NJ would have landed with no minimum-wage hike. The gap between NJ’s actual post value and that counterfactual is the DiD effect, +2.76.

title: Difference-in-Differences
text:
TWFE and the event study
In practice DiD is run as a two-way fixed effects (TWFE) regression, which generalises the four means to many units and periods: (Baker et al. 2025) 
Y_{it}=\eta_i+\theta_t+\beta D_{it}+\epsilon_{it}.
 The unit fixed effects \eta_i absorb all time-invariant differences between units (the first difference); the time fixed effects \theta_t absorb shocks common to all units in a period (the second difference); \beta is the DiD effect. (Baker et al. 2025) It is the panel/fixed-effects machinery of the next entry — DiD is a fixed-effects design.
The event study enriches this by replacing the single D_{it} with dummies for periods relative to treatment: (Baker et al. 2025) 
Y_{it}=\eta_i+\theta_t+\sum_{\tau=-q}^{m}\delta_\tau D_{i,\tau}+\epsilon_{it}.
 The leads (\tau<0) should be near zero — a visual parallel-trends check; the lags (\tau\ge0) trace how the effect grows, fades, or plateaus after treatment. (Baker et al. 2025) An event study is the single most persuasive DiD graphic: flat before, a clear break after.

title: Difference-in-Differences
section: The DiD revolution: when TWFE lies
text:
The DiD revolution: when TWFE lies
Here is the cautionary tale, and why this entry cites a 2020s practitioner’s guide. When units are treated at different times (staggered adoption), the trusty TWFE regression can become biased — even sign-flipped. (Baker et al. 2025)

Forbidden comparisons. With staggering, TWFE implicitly uses already-treated units as controls for later-treated ones. If effects change over time (dynamic effects), those already-treated “controls” are themselves still moving because of their own treatment, contaminating the comparison. (Baker et al. 2025)
Goodman-Bacon decomposition. TWFE’s \beta is a weighted average of all possible 2×2 DiDs — and in staggered designs some weights can be negative. The estimate can come out the wrong sign even if every unit’s true effect is positive. (Baker et al. 2025)
New estimators. “Forward-engineered” methods fix it by using only clean controls: Callaway–Sant’Anna (compare each treated cohort only to never-treated or not-yet-treated units), Sun–Abraham (a saturated cohort-by-time interaction), and de Chaisemartin–D’Haultfœuille (robust when treatment switches on and off). (Baker et al. 2025)

The lesson is humbling and current: a method everyone trusted for decades was quietly mis-estimating staggered rollouts, and the corpus’s most recent source exists to set it right.

title: Difference-in-Differences
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Asserting parallel trends from a good story. It’s untestable; show pre-trends / event-study leads near zero, and reason about confounders that could break parallelism. (Baker et al. 2025)
A control group on its own trajectory. If the control was already diverging (a differential trend), the second difference doesn’t cancel it and DiD is biased. (Baker et al. 2025)
Default TWFE under staggered adoption. Negative weights and forbidden comparisons can flip the sign; use Callaway–Sant’Anna / Sun–Abraham / dCDH and report a Goodman-Bacon decomposition. (Baker et al. 2025)
A shock that hits only one group. Parallel trends fails if something else changed for the treated group at the same time (a concurrent policy). (Cunningham 2021)
Anticipation. If units change behaviour before treatment (expecting it), pre-periods are contaminated and leads aren’t a clean test. (Baker et al. 2025)

title: Difference-in-Differences
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






DiD Practitioner’s Guide (Baker, Callaway, Cunningham, Goodman-Bacon, Sant’Anna) — the 2×2 and double-differencing; parallel trends, its untestability, and pre-trend/event-study evidence; TWFE Y_{it}=\eta_i+\theta_t+\beta D_{it}+\epsilon_{it} and the event-study leads/lags; the staggered-adoption problem — forbidden comparisons, the Goodman-Bacon decomposition and negative weights, and the new Callaway–Sant’Anna / Sun–Abraham / de Chaisemartin– D’Haultfœuille estimators.
Cunningham, The Mixtape — DiD intuition and the Card–Krueger minimum-wage study (NJ 20.44\to21.03, PA 23.33\to21.17, DiD =+2.76).
Angrist & Pischke — DiD as a fixed-effects regression within the credibility-revolution toolkit.

title: Difference-in-Differences
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Why double-differencing identifies the effect. Write potential outcomes with additive group and time components: Y_{it}(0)=\gamma_i+\lambda_t+\varepsilon_{it}, and Y_{it}(1)=Y_{it}(0)+\delta. Parallel trends is the statement that \lambda_t is common across groups (the no-treatment trend is the same). Taking each group’s post−pre difference removes \gamma_i; subtracting the control’s difference removes the common \lambda_{\text{post}}-\lambda_{\text{pre}}, leaving exactly \delta. The assumption is precisely “the \lambda_t path is shared,” which is why differing levels \gamma_i are harmless but differing trends are fatal.
TWFE recovers the 2×2. In the saturated two-period, two-group case, the OLS coefficient on D_{it} in Y_{it}=\eta_i+\theta_t+\beta D_{it}+\epsilon_{it} equals \hat\delta_{DD} exactly — the unit dummies are the first difference, the time dummies the second.
Goodman-Bacon weights. With staggered timing, \hat\beta_{TWFE}=\sum_k s_k\,\hat\beta_k over all 2×2 sub-experiments k, with weights s_k proportional to subsample size and treatment variance. Comparisons that use already-treated units as controls can receive negative s_k when those controls’ effects are still evolving — so a positive-everywhere effect can aggregate to a negative \hat\beta_{TWFE}. The new estimators restrict attention to clean (never- / not-yet-treated) controls, forcing non-negative weighting.

title: Difference-in-Differences
text:
Connections

Builds on: panel & fixed effects (TWFE is its two-way special case), potential outcomes (parallel trends as an assumption about counterfactual changes), and regression FE intuition.
Leads to: synthetic control — what to do when no single control group has parallel trends, by building a weighted one.
Reappears in: modern applied policy work throughout Part VI, where staggered rollouts are the norm and the new estimators are now standard.

title: Difference-in-Differences
text:
Study with NotebookLM

title: V · The Summit: Causal Inference
text: The summit of the whole manual, and the centre of gravity of the source library. The question is deceptively simple — what is the effect of X on Y? — and answering it honestly requires a framework for imagining the world that didn’t happen, plus the design and computational tools built in every part before this one.

title: V · The Summit: Causal Inference
text:
The ladder of causation
Judea Pearl gives the whole summit its organizing picture: the Ladder of Causation, a three-rung hierarchy of the questions you can ask, each strictly above the last. (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018)

Association — seeing. “What if I see…?” Find regularities in data: a customer who buys toothpaste also buys floss. This is the rung of correlation, ordinary statistics, and “model-blind” machine learning — it can predict, but it cannot say why. (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018) Everything in Parts I–IV lives here.
Intervention — doing. “What if I do…?” Predict the effect of an action that changes the world — raise the price, administer the drug. Pearl’s line: “seeing smoke tells us a totally different story about the likelihood of fire than making smoke.” (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018) This Part is the climb to rung 2.
Counterfactuals — imagining. “What if I had done…?” and “Why?” Reason about the world that didn’t happen for a specific unit — comparing reality to a “fictitious and contradictory world.” This, Pearl argues, is what most distinguishes human reasoning from a pattern-matcher. (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018)

The rungs are a one-way gate: “you cannot answer a question on rung two… using only data collected from rung one.” (Pearl and Mackenzie 2018) No amount of correlation becomes causation on its own, because “data are profoundly dumb about causal relationships” — they need a push from a causal model (a DAG, a potential-outcomes assumption, or a design). That push is exactly what this Part supplies. Read the rest of the summit as the engineering of the climb from rung 1 to rungs 2 and 3.






TipLevel



graduate. The deepest theme: Cunningham’s Mixtape, Gerber & Green, Mostly Harmless, Mastering ’Metrics, Panel Data and Experimetrics all converge here.

title: V · The Summit: Causal Inference
text:
The “Furious Five”
The applied core, as Angrist & Pischke frame it: regression/matching adjustment, instrumental variables, regression discontinuity, difference-in-differences (with panel fixed effects as their backbone). Each is a different argument for why a comparison is fair — a different way of approximating the experiment you wish you could have run.

title: V · The Summit: Causal Inference
text:
Where this leads
Down from the summit to Part VI · Doing Research in the World: having learned what a credible causal claim is, you learn what survives contact with measurement, incentives, and a client who needs an answer by Friday.

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
text: Sometimes treatment isn’t random, but it is as-good-as-random once you condition on what you observe. That bet — the conditional independence assumption — is the backdoor criterion cashed out as an estimator. Matching and propensity scores build the missing counterfactual by pairing the treated with comparable untreated units, approximating the experiment you couldn’t run. The catch never goes away: they balance only what you measured, so the assumption is an argument, never a proof.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
text:
Why it matters
The DAG told you which variables close the backdoors; this entry is how you actually adjust for them to recover an effect. (Cunningham 2021) It matters because “selection on observables” is the workhorse of applied social science — the strategy behind most “we controlled for X, Y, Z” papers — and doing it as matching rather than a single regression makes its assumptions, and its weaknesses, impossible to hide. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) The reward is a transparent approximation of an experiment; the price is honesty about the one assumption that can’t be tested.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
text:
The two conditions
Everything rests on two requirements: (Cunningham 2021)

Conditional Independence Assumption (CIA) — also unconfoundedness or ignorability: 
\big(Y_i(1),Y_i(0)\big)\ \perp\ D_i \ \big|\ X_i.
 Within cells of the observed covariates X, treatment is as good as randomly assigned — independent of the potential outcomes. It is randomization’s independence, but only conditional on X. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)
Common support / overlap — for every covariate value, some chance of either arm: 0 < \Pr(D_i=1\mid X_i) < 1. If a kind of person is always treated (or never), they have no counterpart in the other group and no comparison is possible for them. (Cunningham 2021)

CIA is the identifying assumption; overlap is what makes it operational. Both must hold.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
section: Subclassification: the idea in its purest form
text:
Subclassification: the idea in its purest form
The most transparent estimator simply stratifies. Partition the sample into cells (strata) of X; within a cell, units are alike on X, so under CIA the treated–control difference is causal; then average those within-cell differences, weighting by each stratum’s share of the population. That weighted average is the ATE — the backdoor adjustment formula made arithmetic. (Cunningham 2021)






NoteCunningham’s Titanic



Did riding first class raise your chance of surviving the Titanic? The naive survival gap (the simple difference in outcomes) is 35.4 percentage points. But first-class passengers were disproportionately women and the better-off — and “women and children first” plus age drove survival directly. Stratify on age and gender, take the within-stratum first-class effect, and reweight: the estimate falls to 16.1 points. (Cunningham 2021) Less than half the naive gap was the cabin; the rest was who was in it — selection bias, removed by conditioning on the confounders the DAG flagged.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
section: Matching, and why many covariates break it
text:
Matching, and why many covariates break it
Matching generalises the idea: impute each unit’s missing counterfactual from the closest units in the other arm. (Cunningham 2021)

Exact matching pairs a treated unit with controls having identical X — perfect, when feasible.
Nearest-neighbour matching pairs with the closest available X when exact matches run out.

The wall it hits is the curse of dimensionality. With many covariates, the space is vast and the data sparse: cells empty out, exact matches vanish, and common support fails — you simply can’t find a treated and a control unit in most cells. (Cunningham 2021) Matching on more than a few covariates at once is infeasible in finite samples. This is the problem the propensity score was invented to dissolve.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
section: The propensity score: one number to balance them all
text:
The propensity score: one number to balance them all
The propensity score is the probability of treatment given covariates, 
e(X) = \Pr(D=1\mid X).
 The Rosenbaum–Rubin (1983) theorem is the magic: if CIA holds given the full vector X, then it also holds given the single scalar e(X): (Cunningham 2021) 
\big(Y(1),Y(0)\big)\perp D \ \big|\ e(X).
 The score is a balancing score — among units with the same propensity, the entire distribution of X is identical across treated and control. So you can condition on one number instead of a high-dimensional vector, sidestepping the curse of dimensionality entirely. (Cunningham 2021) You estimate e(X) (usually a logit), then match, stratify, or weight on it.
The figure shows what overlap looks like — and where it fails.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(5)
n = 4000
X = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
# True propensity rises with X; treatment assigned accordingly (confounding by X).
e = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-1.3 * X))
D = rng.uniform(size=n) < e

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.0))
bins = np.linspace(0, 1, 41)
ax.hist(e[D],  bins=bins, color="#1f8a4c", alpha=0.6, label="treated", density=True)
ax.hist(e[~D], bins=bins, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.6, label="control", density=True)
ax.axvspan(0.0, 0.12, color="#c0392b", alpha=0.08)
ax.axvspan(0.88, 1.0, color="#c0392b", alpha=0.08)
ax.text(0.02, ax.get_ylim()[1]*0.85, "few treated\n(no controls' match here?)", fontsize=7.5, color="#b9791f")
ax.text(0.62, ax.get_ylim()[1]*0.92, "common support\n(matching works)", fontsize=8, color="#1f3a5f")
ax.set_xlabel("estimated propensity score  e(X) = P(D=1 | X)")
ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("Overlap is what makes matching possible")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper center")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Common support in propensity scores. The estimated probability of treatment, e(X), for treated (green) and control (grey) units. Where the histograms overlap, every treated unit has comparable controls and matching works. In the tails — control units near 0, treated units near 1 — there is no counterpart in the other group (the shaded ‘no-overlap’ zones); those units must be trimmed, and the effect is identified only on the region of common support.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
text:
Inverse probability weighting
Instead of matching, you can reweight. Inverse probability weighting (IPW) builds a synthetic sample in which treatment is independent of X by up-weighting the rare cases — a treated unit with a low propensity stands in for the many similar units who went untreated. (Cunningham 2021) The ATE estimator weights each outcome by treatment status over (one minus) the propensity: 
\widehat{\text{ATE}} = \frac1N\sum_i Y_i\cdot\frac{D_i-\hat e(X_i)}{\hat e(X_i)\,(1-\hat e(X_i))}.
 Weighting and matching are two ways to spend the same propensity score; both lean entirely on getting e(X) — and CIA — right. (Note the danger lurking in the denominator: propensities near 0 or 1 produce explosive weights, which is the overlap problem returning in a new guise.)
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
text:
Regression is matching in disguise
Where does ordinary regression fit? Angrist & Pischke call it an “automated matchmaker.” (Angrist and Pischke 2009) Regression with controls is also a selection-on-observables estimator built on CIA, and it can be written as a weighted average of within-cell treatment–control contrasts — i.e. a matching estimator. The difference is the weights: matching for the ATT weights cells by the covariate distribution among the treated, while regression weights them by the variance of treatment within each cell, \,\Pr(X)(1-\Pr(X)) — giving most weight to cells where treatment is a coin flip (near 0.5), because those are the most precise. (Angrist and Pischke 2009) Same identifying assumption, different weighting — which is why matching and regression usually agree, and why, when they disagree, the culprit is the weighting or the functional form.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
section: The limitation you can never escape
text:
The limitation you can never escape
Here is the line that separates this whole family from randomization: matching and propensity scores balance only the observables. (Cunningham 2021) An experiment balances everything, seen and unseen; selection-on-observables balances X and prays the rest goes with it. CIA is fundamentally untestable — it concerns the missing counterfactuals — so if unobserved ability or motivation drives both treatment and outcome, every method here stays biased, no matter how clean the balance table. (Cunningham 2021) Matching is not a machine for removing bias; it is a machine for removing bias due to observed confounders, conditional on an assumption you must defend on substance.






NoteLaLonde, and when it works (NSW / Dehejia–Wahba)



The famous test: the National Supported Work job-training program had a true experimental benchmark of about +$1,794 in earnings. LaLonde (1986) discarded the experimental controls and substituted observational comparison groups (from the CPS and PSID) — the naive gap swung to −$8,498, catastrophic negative selection (trainees were far more disadvantaged to begin with). (Cunningham 2021) Dehejia & Wahba (1999) then applied propensity-score matching with trimming to enforce common support, conditioning on demographics and two years of pre-treatment earnings; their screened estimate, +$1,362, landed close to the experimental truth. (Cunningham 2021) The moral cuts both ways: matching can recover the experiment — if the selection really is on observables you have (here, rich earnings histories) — and fails badly when it isn’t.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Treating CIA as established. It’s untestable. “We controlled for a lot” is an argument about unobservables, not a proof; a hidden confounder leaves the estimate biased. (Cunningham 2021)
Ignoring common support. Comparing units with no counterpart in the other arm extrapolates off a cliff; check overlap and trim non-overlapping units. (Cunningham 2021)
Matching on many covariates directly. The curse of dimensionality empties the cells — use the propensity score to collapse X to one number. (Cunningham 2021)
Extreme IPW weights. Propensities near 0 or 1 blow up the weights and the variance; it’s the overlap problem in disguise. (Cunningham 2021)
Reading balance on X as balance overall. A perfect balance table says nothing about the unobservables — the very things that make this weaker than an experiment. (Cunningham 2021)
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Cunningham, Causal Inference: The Mixtape — the CIA/unconfoundedness (Y(1),Y(0))\perp D\mid X and common support 0<\Pr(D=1\mid X)<1; subclassification and the Titanic example (SDO 35.4\%\to weighted ATE 16.1\% stratifying on age/gender); exact and nearest-neighbour matching and the curse of dimensionality; the propensity score e(X)=\Pr(D=1\mid X) and the Rosenbaum–Rubin balancing theorem; IPW formulas for ATE and ATT; the untestability of CIA; and the NSW / LaLonde / Dehejia–Wahba evaluation (experimental \$1{,}794; naive -\$8{,}498; PSM \$1{,}362).
Angrist & Pischke, Mostly Harmless / Mastering ’Metrics — regression as an automated matchmaker and a weighted matching estimator; regression’s variance weighting \Pr(X)(1-\Pr(X)) vs matching’s treated-distribution weighting; CIA as the shared foundation.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Identification under CIA. Under (Y(1),Y(0))\perp D\mid X and overlap, 
\mathbb E[Y\mid D=1,X]-\mathbb E[Y\mid D=0,X]=\mathbb E[Y(1)-Y(0)\mid X],
 and averaging over the distribution of X gives the ATE — the backdoor adjustment. Subclassification estimates the inner contrast cell by cell and averages with weights \Pr(X=x).
The balancing-score theorem (sketch). Rosenbaum & Rubin show D\perp X\mid e(X): by construction \Pr(D=1\mid X,e(X))=e(X) depends on X only through e(X), so within a level of e(X) the covariate distribution is identical across arms. Combined with CIA-given-X, this yields CIA-given-e(X) — so conditioning on the scalar e(X) suffices. The high-dimensional X is compressed to one number with no loss for identification.
Why regression weights by treatment variance. With saturated controls, OLS recovers \sum_x w(x)\,\big(\mathbb E[Y\mid D=1,x]-\mathbb E[Y\mid D=0,x]\big) with w(x)\propto \Pr(X=x)\,\operatorname{Var}(D\mid X=x)=\Pr(X=x)\,e(x)(1-e(x)). The factor e(x)(1-e(x)) peaks at e=1/2, so cells with the most treatment variation — the most informative comparisons — dominate the OLS estimand. This is why the regression number can differ from a matching ATT even under the same CIA.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores

title: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
text:
Connections

Builds on: DAGs (the backdoor criterion this estimates), potential outcomes (CIA is conditional independence of D and the Y(d)), and logistic regression (how e(X) is fit).
Leads to: the designs for when CIA fails — instrumental variables, regression discontinuity, and difference-in-differences find leverage beyond the observables.
Reappears in: causal machine learning, where flexible ML estimates e(X) and the outcome model and double/debiased ML combines them to be robust to errors in each.
crumbs: Selection on Observables: Matching & Propensity Scores
