title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
text: A causal effect is a comparison between two worlds: the outcome with the treatment and the outcome without it, for the same unit at the same moment. Exactly one of those worlds is ever realised — the other is a counterfactual we can never see. That single missing number is the fundamental problem of causal inference, and every method in this Part is a strategy for filling it in credibly. Master this language and the whole summit becomes one idea applied many ways.

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
text:
Why it matters
The bridge from regression left us with a verdict: a coefficient is causal only under an assumption data can’t verify, and you usually need a design. To state those assumptions precisely we need a language for causation itself — one that defines an effect before any estimator, so we can say exactly what we are trying to learn and exactly what could go wrong. That language is the potential outcomes framework (the Rubin Causal Model), shared by Cunningham, Gerber & Green, and Angrist & Pischke alike. (Cunningham 2021; Gerber and Green 2012) It reframes causal inference as a missing-data problem — which is the most clarifying sentence in this entire book.

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
text:
Two potential outcomes, one effect
For each unit i and a binary treatment, imagine two outcomes: (Cunningham 2021)

Y_i(1) — what i’s outcome would be if treated,
Y_i(0) — what i’s outcome would be if untreated (the control state).

The individual treatment effect is their difference, the gap between the two worlds for that one person: 
\tau_i = Y_i(1) - Y_i(0).

These are defined for everyone, treated or not — they are properties of the unit, not of what happened to it. (Cunningham 2021) A small intuition pump from the corpus: to know the effect of an article on your mood, compare your mood had you read it (Y(1)=3) with your mood had you not (Y(0)=5); the effect is \tau=-2. (Cunningham 2021) Nothing statistical yet — just a careful definition of what “effect” means.

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
section: The fundamental problem of causal inference
text:
The fundamental problem of causal inference
Here is the catch that defines the field. For any unit you observe only one potential outcome — the one corresponding to the treatment it actually received. The other is a counterfactual, and it is missing. (Cunningham 2021) This is the fundamental problem of causal inference: the individual effect \tau_i is never observable, because it requires two numbers and reality hands you one. (Cunningham 2021; Gerber and Green 2012)
The link between the unobservable potential outcomes and the single number you do see is the switching equation (or observation equation), where D_i\in\{0,1\} indicates treatment: 
Y_i = D_i\,Y_i(1) + (1-D_i)\,Y_i(0).
 Set D_i=1 and you observe Y_i(1), with Y_i(0) forever hidden; set D_i=0 and the reverse. (Cunningham 2021) The treatment indicator is a switch that reveals one world and conceals the other. Causal inference is the science of reasoning about the concealed one.

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
section: What we can hope to learn: the estimands
text:
What we can hope to learn: the estimands
Since individual effects are out of reach, we retreat to averages over groups — the population quantities we actually try to estimate (the estimands): (Cunningham 2021)

Average Treatment Effect (ATE) — the average effect across the whole population: \;\text{ATE} = \mathbb E[Y_i(1)-Y_i(0)].
Average Treatment effect on the Treated (ATT) — the average among those who actually got treated: \;\text{ATT} = \mathbb E[Y_i(1)-Y_i(0)\mid D_i=1].
Average Treatment effect on the Untreated (ATU) — the average among the untreated: \;\text{ATU} = \mathbb E[Y_i(1)-Y_i(0)\mid D_i=0].

These can differ sharply. A job-training program may help those who enrol (ATT) far more than it would help those who stayed away (ATU) — which average you want depends on the policy question (expand the program to everyone? then ATE; evaluate the current program? then ATT). (Cunningham 2021) Being explicit about the estimand is half of thinking clearly about causation.

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
section: Why a naive comparison fails: selection bias
text:
Why a naive comparison fails: selection bias
The tempting estimator is the naive difference in group means: average outcome of the treated minus average outcome of the untreated, \mathbb E[Y_i\mid D_i=1]-\mathbb E[Y_i\mid D_i=0]. It is usually biased, because the groups were not comparable to begin with. (Cunningham 2021) Using the switching equation, the naive comparison decomposes exactly: (Cunningham 2021) 
\underbrace{\mathbb E[Y\mid D=1]-\mathbb E[Y\mid D=0]}_{\text{naive comparison}}
= \underbrace{\text{ATT}}_{\text{the effect we want}}
+ \underbrace{\mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=1]-\mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=0]}_{\textbf{selection bias}}.

The second term is selection bias: the difference between the groups in their untreated state. It asks — how would the treated and control groups have compared even if no one had been treated? If sicker people check into hospital, comparing the hospitalised to the healthy makes hospitals look lethal, because the hospitalised “would have been sicker even if they’d stayed home.” (Cunningham 2021) The bias has nothing to do with the treatment’s effect; it is baked into who ends up where.






NoteCunningham’s surgery-vs-chemo reversal



The starkest illustration in the Mixtape: a surgeon assigns patients to surgery when it would extend their life and to chemotherapy otherwise — optimal medicine, ruinous statistics. Across the patients the true ATE is +0.6 years (surgery genuinely helps on average), yet the naive difference in means is -0.4 — surgery looks worse. The culprit is a selection bias of -4.8: the surgery group was naturally far sicker, so their good-outcome-under-no-surgery baseline was much lower. Selection bias doesn’t just shrink the estimate — here it flips its sign. (Cunningham 2021)


The figure makes the mechanism visible: when assignment depends on the untreated baseline, the observed gap measures who got sorted where as much as what the treatment did.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
n = 400
severity = rng.uniform(0, 1, n)            # 1 = sickest
y0 = 8.0 - 6.0 * severity + rng.normal(0, 0.4, n)   # survival w/o surgery: sicker -> lower
tau = 0.6 + 0.0 * severity                  # surgery adds ~0.6 yr on average (true ATE>0)
y1 = y0 + tau + rng.normal(0, 0.1, n)
# Sicker patients are routed to surgery (selection on the untreated baseline).
D = (severity + rng.normal(0, 0.15, n)) > 0.5

ate = np.mean(y1 - y0)
obs_treated = y1[D].mean()
obs_control = y0[~D].mean()
naive = obs_treated - obs_control
sel_bias = y0[D].mean() - y0[~D].mean()     # difference in untreated baselines

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
ax.scatter(severity[~D], y0[~D], s=10, color="#c9ccd1", alpha=0.6, label="control: observed Y(0)")
ax.scatter(severity[D], y1[D], s=10, color="#1f8a4c", alpha=0.6, label="treated: observed Y(1)")
ax.axhline(obs_control, color="#9aa0a6", lw=1.6, ls="--")
ax.axhline(obs_treated, color="#1f8a4c", lw=1.6, ls="--")
# selection-bias bracket: treated group's hidden Y(0) baseline vs control's
ax.axhline(y0[D].mean(), color="#c0392b", lw=1.4, ls=":")
ax.annotate("", xy=(0.04, y0[D].mean()), xytext=(0.04, obs_control),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="<->", color="#c0392b", lw=1.8))
ax.text(0.06, (y0[D].mean()+obs_control)/2, f"selection bias\n= {sel_bias:+.1f}",
        color="#c0392b", fontsize=8, va="center")
ax.text(0.62, obs_treated+0.3, f"treated observed mean = {obs_treated:.1f}", color="#1f8a4c", fontsize=8)
ax.text(0.62, obs_control+0.3, f"control observed mean = {obs_control:.1f}", color="#6b6f76", fontsize=8)
ax.set_title(f"True ATE = {ate:+.1f}, but naive comparison = {naive:+.1f}")
ax.set_xlabel("severity  (sicker →)"); ax.set_ylabel("survival (years)")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper right")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Selection bias can reverse a real effect. Each patient has two potential outcomes; surgery genuinely adds survival (true effect positive, dashed arrows up). But sicker patients (low baseline Y(0)) are the ones routed to surgery, so the treated group’s observed mean (green) sits below the control group’s (grey) — a negative naive comparison masking a positive ATE. The selection-bias gap is the difference in the groups’ untreated baselines (red bracket).





The lesson the whole Part builds on: identification is about making the selection-bias term vanish. A randomized experiment forces it to zero by design; every observational method is an argument that, conditional on something, it is as if zero.

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
section: SUTVA: the assumption that makes outcomes well-defined
text:
SUTVA: the assumption that makes outcomes well-defined
The framework quietly assumes the potential outcomes even exist as stable, single numbers. That is the Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption (SUTVA), and it has two parts: (Cunningham 2021)

No interference (no spillovers). Unit i’s potential outcomes depend only on i’s own treatment, not on whether others are treated. (Cunningham 2021) Tutoring a student who then helps a control-group friend violates it — the friend’s Y(0) was altered by someone else’s treatment.
No hidden variations of treatment. There is one well-defined “version” of treatment, so Y_i(1) is unambiguous. A vague or inconsistently delivered treatment has many versions and no single potential outcome. (Cunningham 2021)

Classic violations to watch for: spillovers/contagion between units; resource displacement (moving equipment from control to treatment classrooms changes the controls’ baseline); and general-equilibrium effects (a job-training program so large it shifts the market wage, changing the “no-treatment” world itself). (Cunningham 2021) SUTVA is why causal claims need a clearly localised, non-interfering treatment — and why scaling a program up can break the very estimate that justified it.

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reading the naive difference as the effect. It equals ATT plus selection bias; unless the groups are comparable in their untreated state, it can shrink, inflate, or even reverse the truth. (Cunningham 2021)
Confusing the estimands. ATE, ATT, and ATU answer different policy questions and can differ greatly under effect heterogeneity. State which one you mean before estimating. (Cunningham 2021)
Forgetting the effect is unit-level and unobservable. \tau_i exists for everyone but is never seen; all we ever estimate are averages, and only under assumptions. (Cunningham 2021)
Ignoring SUTVA. Spillovers, multiple treatment versions, and general-equilibrium effects quietly invalidate the potential outcomes themselves — no estimator fixes a mis-defined estimand. (Cunningham 2021)

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Cunningham, Causal Inference: The Mixtape — the Rubin Causal Model; potential outcomes Y_i(1),Y_i(0) and \tau_i; the fundamental problem and the switching equation Y_i=D_iY_i(1)+(1-D_i)Y_i(0); ATE/ATT/ATU; the selection-bias decomposition (naive = ATT + selection bias, and the fuller ATE version with the heterogeneous-effect term (1-\pi)(\text{ATT}-\text{ATU})); the surgery-vs-chemo numerical reversal (ATE +0.6, naive -0.4, selection bias -4.8); and SUTVA with its spillover / resource-displacement / general-equilibrium violations.
Gerber & Green, Field Experiments — potential outcomes and the fundamental problem framed as missing data; averages (ATE) as the learnable target; the groundwork for randomization as the fix.

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Deriving the selection-bias decomposition. The naive comparison uses observed outcomes, which the switching equation ties to potential outcomes: \mathbb E[Y\mid D=1]=\mathbb E[Y(1)\mid D=1] and \mathbb E[Y\mid D=0]=\mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=0]. Subtract, then add and subtract \mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=1] (the treated group’s missing untreated baseline): 
\mathbb E[Y\mid D=1]-\mathbb E[Y\mid D=0]
= \underbrace{\big(\mathbb E[Y(1)\mid D=1]-\mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=1]\big)}_{\text{ATT}}
+ \underbrace{\big(\mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=1]-\mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=0]\big)}_{\text{selection bias}}.

The fuller ATE form. Let \pi=\Pr(D=1). Writing the naive comparison in terms of the ATE, Cunningham shows (Cunningham 2021) 
\mathbb E[Y\mid D=1]-\mathbb E[Y\mid D=0]
= \text{ATE} + \underbrace{\big(\mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=1]-\mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=0]\big)}_{\text{selection bias}}
+ \underbrace{(1-\pi)\,(\text{ATT}-\text{ATU})}_{\text{heterogeneous-effect bias}}.
 Two distinct biases, then: the groups differ in their baselines (selection bias) and the treatment may affect the treated differently from the untreated (heterogeneity). Random assignment kills both at once, because it makes D independent of both potential outcomes: (Y(1),Y(0))\perp D forces \mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=1]=\mathbb E[Y(0)\mid D=0] and \text{ATT}=\text{ATU}=\text{ATE} — the subject of the next entry.

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
text:
Connections

Builds on: what regression can’t tell you (exogeneity, OVB, the “you need a design” verdict) and conditional expectation (the \mathbb E[\cdot\mid D] objects throughout).
Leads to: randomized experiments (assignment that zeroes the selection-bias term) and the entire toolkit — matching, IV, RD, DiD — each an argument that selection bias is, conditionally, zero.
Reappears in: every estimand in Part V; the DAG view of confounding is the same idea drawn as a graph.

title: Potential Outcomes & the Fundamental Problem
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Define potential outcomes Y_i(1), Y_i(0), and the individual treatment effect, and state the fundamental problem of causal inference and the switching equation.”
“Define ATE, ATT, and ATU, and give a policy question for which each is the right estimand.”
“Derive the decomposition: naive difference in means = ATT + selection bias. What is the selection bias term, intuitively?”
“Walk through Cunningham’s surgery-vs-chemotherapy example where selection bias reverses the sign of the estimated effect.”
“State SUTVA’s two components and give examples of spillover, resource-displacement, and general-equilibrium violations.”

title: Regression Discontinuity
text: Wherever a sharp rule assigns a treatment by a threshold — pass at 80, eligible at 65, win at 50% — the people just on either side are, by luck of a few decimal points, interchangeable. The jump in the outcome right at the cutoff can only be the treatment, because nothing else changes discontinuously there. RD is the observational design closest to an experiment, with the most defensible identifying assumption in this Part — and the narrowest reach.

title: Regression Discontinuity
text:
Why it matters
IV needed an exclusion restriction you can only argue for. RD needs something far easier to believe: that the world is smooth except where the rule cuts. (Cunningham 2021) That makes it one of the most credible strategies in observational social science — its key assumption is nearly visible in a scatterplot. (Cunningham 2021) Rules with thresholds are everywhere in administrative data — test scores, age, income, vote share — so RD turns the bureaucracy’s arbitrariness into identification.

title: Regression Discontinuity
section: The idea: a running variable and a cutoff
text:
The idea: a running variable and a cutoff
RD applies when treatment D is assigned by whether a continuous running variable (a.k.a. forcing or score variable) X crosses a known cutoff c. (Cunningham 2021) The engine is comparability at the boundary: a student scoring 79.9 and one scoring 80.1 are effectively identical in preparedness — one just had marginally better luck on exam day — yet only the second gets the scholarship. (Cunningham 2021) So any jump in the outcome exactly at c is caused by the treatment, not by the running variable, which varies smoothly across it. The cutoff manufactures as-good-as-random assignment in a narrow window — a local experiment.

title: Regression Discontinuity
text:
Sharp vs fuzzy
Two flavours, by how strictly the rule binds: (Cunningham 2021)

Sharp RD. Treatment is a deterministic step of the running variable: D_i=\mathbf 1[X_i\ge
c] — probability jumps cleanly from 0 to 1 at c. The RD estimate is simply the jump in the outcome at the cutoff.
Fuzzy RD. Crossing the cutoff changes the probability of treatment, not treatment itself (some above stay untreated; some below get treated). Now the threshold is an instrument: the effect is the jump in the outcome divided by the jump in treatment probability at c — a LATE for compliers (those the rule actually moves). (Cunningham 2021) Fuzzy RD is IV with the cutoff as Z.

title: Regression Discontinuity
text:
The identifying assumption: continuity
RD rests on continuity of potential outcomes at the cutoff: \mathbb E[Y(0)\mid X] and \mathbb
E[Y(1)\mid X] are smooth functions of X through c. (Cunningham 2021) In words: absent the treatment, nothing about the outcome would jump at c — the cutoff is special only because it triggers treatment. Then the observed discontinuity in Y is the causal effect, full stop. Continuity is weaker and more credible than IV’s exclusion or matching’s CIA: it allows the running variable to be related to the outcome in any smooth way; it forbids only a coincidental jump at the exact threshold.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(2)
n = 600
X = rng.uniform(-1, 1, n)              # running variable, cutoff at 0
tau = 2.0                              # true jump (causal effect) at the cutoff
Y = 1.0 + 1.5 * X + 0.8 * X**2 + tau * (X >= 0) + rng.normal(0, 0.5, n)

def local_fit(mask):
    b = np.polyfit(X[mask], Y[mask], 1)
    return b
bl = local_fit(X < 0); br = local_fit(X >= 0)
xl = np.linspace(-1, 0, 50); xr = np.linspace(0, 1, 50)
jump = np.polyval(br, 0) - np.polyval(bl, 0)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
ax.scatter(X[X < 0], Y[X < 0], s=10, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.5, label="untreated (X < c)")
ax.scatter(X[X >= 0], Y[X >= 0], s=10, color="#1f8a4c", alpha=0.5, label="treated (X ≥ c)")
ax.plot(xl, np.polyval(bl, xl), color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.4)
ax.plot(xr, np.polyval(br, xr), color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.4)
ax.axvline(0, color="#c0392b", ls=":", lw=1.4)
ax.annotate("", xy=(0, np.polyval(br, 0)), xytext=(0, np.polyval(bl, 0)),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="<->", color="#c0392b", lw=2.2))
ax.text(0.04, (np.polyval(br,0)+np.polyval(bl,0))/2, f"jump = {jump:.2f}\n(true {tau})",
        color="#c0392b", fontsize=9, va="center")
ax.set_xlabel("running variable  X   (cutoff c = 0)"); ax.set_ylabel("outcome Y")
ax.set_title("The treatment effect is the jump at the cutoff")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A sharp regression discontinuity. Treatment switches on at the cutoff (X ≥ 0). Below and above, the outcome trends smoothly with the running variable; local-linear fits on each side estimate the boundary limits, and their vertical gap at the cutoff (red) is the causal effect. The estimate is identified only at the threshold — it says nothing about units far from it.

title: Regression Discontinuity
text:
Estimation: stay local, stay simple
Since few units sit exactly at c, you estimate the outcome’s limits from each side and take their difference. The modern default is local linear regression: fit a separate line to the data within a bandwidth h on each side of the cutoff. (Cunningham 2021) Two choices matter:

Bandwidth h is a bias–variance dial in disguise. Narrow h uses only the most comparable units (low bias) but little data (high variance); wide h adds precision but risks bias if the smooth trend curves. (Cunningham 2021)
Keep the polynomial low. High-order global polynomials (X^3, X^4) were once standard but are now discouraged: they overfit noise and can manufacture illusory jumps at the boundary. Prefer linear or quadratic local fits. (Cunningham 2021)

title: Regression Discontinuity
text:
The threat: manipulation
RD has one characteristic failure: sorting. If units can precisely manipulate their position relative to the cutoff, those just below are no longer comparable to those just above — think people misreporting income to drop just under a benefits threshold. (Cunningham 2021) The diagnostic is the McCrary density test: examine the distribution of the running variable for a discontinuous jump in density at c. Bunching just on the favourable side is the fingerprint of manipulation and invalidates the design. (Cunningham 2021) (Continuity of the density is something you can look at — a rare testable implication in this Part.)

title: Regression Discontinuity
section: What you get: local, but clean
text:
What you get: local, but clean
RD’s bargain is sharp. Internal validity near the cutoff is exceptional — close to experimental. But external validity is local: the estimate is the effect for units at the threshold and need not generalise. (Cunningham 2021) A scholarship’s effect estimated at a score of 80 says nothing about how it would help A-students or C-students. You trade reach for credibility — the recurring price of a clean natural experiment.






NoteFour RD classics




Close elections & incumbency (Lee 2008). The 50% vote-share threshold is a sharp RD: winning a close race is as-good-as-random. Incumbency raises a party’s reelection probability by ~40 percentage points. (Cunningham 2021)
The drinking age (Carpenter & Dobkin 2009). Age is the running variable, 21 the cutoff. Mortality from external causes (car crashes, etc.) jumps at 21 — roughly +7.7 deaths per 100,000. (Cunningham 2021)
The original RD (Thistlethwaite & Campbell 1960). Compared National Merit award winners just above and below the qualifying score — the design’s debut. (Cunningham 2021)
Class size, Maimonides’ rule (Angrist & Lavy 1999). Israeli law caps classes at 40, so a 41st pupil forces a split into two ~20-pupil classes — a fuzzy RD. Smaller classes raised achievement, a Wald estimate of about −0.27 SD (per added pupil) for fourth-graders’ scores. (Angrist and Pischke 2009)

title: Regression Discontinuity
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Manipulable running variables. Precise sorting around c breaks comparability. Run the McCrary density test; look for bunching. (Cunningham 2021)
High-order polynomials. They overfit and fabricate boundary jumps. Use local linear/quadratic and show robustness to bandwidth. (Cunningham 2021)
Over-claiming generality. RD identifies a local effect at the cutoff; don’t extrapolate to units far away. (Cunningham 2021)
Another rule at the same cutoff. If a different program also switches at c (e.g. multiple benefits at the same income line), continuity fails and the jump conflates them. (Cunningham 2021)
Ignoring the fuzzy denominator. In fuzzy RD you must scale by the jump in treatment probability; the raw outcome jump understates the effect. (Cunningham 2021)

title: Regression Discontinuity
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Cunningham, The Mixtape — RD as among the most credible observational designs; running variable & cutoff, the 79.9-vs-80.1 comparability intuition; sharp D=\mathbf 1[X\ge c] vs fuzzy (cutoff as instrument, effect = outcome jump ÷ treatment-probability jump, a LATE); continuity of potential outcomes as the identifying assumption; local linear estimation, bandwidth bias–variance, the case against high-order polynomials; manipulation and the McCrary density test; local external validity; Lee (2008) incumbency \approx 40pp and Carpenter–Dobkin MLDA-21 mortality \approx +7.7/100{,}000; Thistlethwaite–Campbell (1960).
Angrist & Pischke — RD in the credibility-revolution toolkit; Angrist–Lavy (1999) Maimonides’ rule class-size fuzzy RD (\approx-0.27 SD).

title: Regression Discontinuity
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The sharp RD estimand. Under continuity, the effect at the cutoff is the difference of one-sided limits, 
\tau_{\text{SRD}}=\lim_{x\downarrow c}\mathbb E[Y\mid X=x]-\lim_{x\uparrow c}\mathbb E[Y\mid X=x]
=\mathbb E[Y(1)-Y(0)\mid X=c].
 Continuity is what lets the two limits estimate the treated and untreated potential-outcome means at the same point c.
Fuzzy RD as a ratio (Wald at the cutoff). 
\tau_{\text{FRD}}=\frac{\lim_{x\downarrow c}\mathbb E[Y\mid x]-\lim_{x\uparrow c}\mathbb E[Y\mid x]}
{\lim_{x\downarrow c}\mathbb E[D\mid x]-\lim_{x\uparrow c}\mathbb E[D\mid x]},
 the outcome discontinuity over the treatment-probability discontinuity — the Wald/IV estimator with the threshold-crossing indicator as the instrument, identifying the LATE for cutoff compliers.
Bandwidth and the bias–variance tradeoff. Local linear estimation has bias O(h^2) (from curvature of the conditional mean) and variance O(1/(nh)). Minimising MSE \propto h^4 + 1/(nh) gives an optimal h^\star\propto n^{-1/5} — formalising “narrow enough to be comparable, wide enough to be precise,” the same logic as kernel/CV bandwidth selection.

title: Regression Discontinuity
text:
Connections

Builds on: instrumental variables (fuzzy RD is IV at the cutoff), bias–variance (bandwidth choice), and potential outcomes (the estimand as a limit of conditional means).
Leads to: difference-in-differences and panel methods — other natural-experiment designs when no single sharp cutoff exists.
Reappears in: policy evaluation throughout Part VI, where eligibility thresholds are a standard source of identification.

title: Regression Discontinuity
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Explain the RD design: running variable, cutoff, and why units just above and below are comparable (a local experiment).”
“Distinguish sharp from fuzzy RD, and explain why fuzzy RD is an instrumental-variables problem.”
“State the continuity assumption and explain why it’s more credible than IV’s exclusion restriction or matching’s CIA.”
“How is RD estimated with local linear regression? Explain the bandwidth bias–variance tradeoff and the case against high-order polynomials.”
“What is the manipulation threat, and how does the McCrary density test detect it? Why is RD’s external validity only local?”

title: Synthetic Control
text: Difference-in-differences needs a control group with parallel trends. But what if exactly one place is treated — California passes a tobacco tax, terrorism strikes the Basque Country — and no single other place is a credible stand-in? Synthetic control manufactures the missing counterpart: a weighted average of untreated units, with weights chosen so the blend tracks the treated unit’s history almost perfectly. The post-treatment gap between the real unit and its synthetic twin is the effect — a transparent, data-driven counterfactual where DiD has no clean comparison.

title: Synthetic Control
text:
Why it matters
The “Furious Five” all assume you have a usable comparison group. At the aggregate level — states, countries, regions — you often don’t: there’s one treated unit and a handful of imperfect controls, and picking one control is ad hoc. (Cunningham 2021) Synthetic control, due to Abadie and coauthors, solves exactly this, and Cunningham frames it as a generalisation of difference-in-differences for comparative case studies. (Cunningham 2021) It has become one of the most influential methods in modern policy evaluation precisely because it makes the counterfactual explicit and reproducible rather than a matter of the analyst’s judgment.

title: Synthetic Control
section: The idea: a weighted donor pool
text:
The idea: a weighted donor pool
Instead of choosing one control, build a synthetic one from many. Take a donor pool of untreated units and form a weighted average, with a weight w_j on each donor: (Cunningham 2021)

the weights are non-negative and sum to one;
they are chosen so the synthetic unit matches the treated unit’s pre-treatment outcomes and predictors as closely as possible.

That constraint — weights on the simplex — is the method’s quiet genius: it forces interpolation, not extrapolation. The counterfactual lives inside the convex hull of the donor pool, anchored to combinations of data that actually exist rather than a regression’s projection beyond the data’s support. (Cunningham 2021) (This is the discipline a linear regression lacks: OLS will happily extrapolate with negative or huge coefficients; synthetic control refuses.)

title: Synthetic Control
text:
The estimate: a gap
Once the weights w^* reproduce the treated unit’s pre-treatment path, the treatment effect is the post-treatment gap between the treated unit and its synthetic control: (Cunningham 2021) 
\hat\delta_{1t}=Y_{1t}-\sum_{j=2}^{J+1} w_j^*\,Y_{jt}.
 Before treatment the two lines should sit on top of each other (that’s how the weights were chosen); after, any divergence is attributed to the intervention. The figure shows the signature picture: a near-perfect pre-period match, then a widening gap.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(99)
years = np.arange(1970, 2001)
treat_year = 1988
pre = years < treat_year

# A common smooth trend both real and synthetic share pre-treatment.
trend = 130 - 0.6 * (years - 1970) + 6 * np.sin((years - 1970) / 4)
real = trend + rng.normal(0, 1.0, years.size)
synth = trend + rng.normal(0, 1.0, years.size)        # matches in the pre-period
# Treatment opens a growing gap after 1988 (program cuts real CA sales).
effect = np.where(years >= treat_year, -2.0 * (years - treat_year + 1), 0.0)
real = real + effect

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
ax.plot(years, real, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.2, label="California (treated)")
ax.plot(years, synth, color="#c0392b", lw=2.0, ls="--", label="synthetic California")
ax.fill_between(years[years >= treat_year], real[years >= treat_year],
                synth[years >= treat_year], color="#1f8a4c", alpha=0.18, label="estimated effect")
ax.axvline(treat_year, color="#9aa0a6", lw=1.2, ls=":")
ax.text(treat_year + 0.4, ax.get_ylim()[1]*0.96, "Prop 99 (1988)", fontsize=8, color="#6b6f76")
ax.set_xlabel("year"); ax.set_ylabel("cigarette sales (packs per capita)")
ax.set_title("Match the pre-period, read the effect off the gap")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="lower left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Synthetic control (California Proposition 99 style). The synthetic California — a weighted blend of donor states — tracks real California’s cigarette sales almost exactly before the 1988 tax (the weights are fit to the pre-period). After 1988 the two diverge: real California falls below its synthetic twin, and the shaded gap is the estimated effect of the tobacco-control program. Inference compares this gap to placebo gaps from pretending each donor state was treated.

title: Synthetic Control
text:
Inference: placebos, not standard errors
With a single treated unit, classical standard errors (which reflect sampling variability) miss the point — the real question is whether the synthetic control could reproduce the gap by chance. (Cunningham 2021) So inference is by placebo / permutation: (Cunningham 2021)

Pretend each donor was the treated unit, and run synthetic control on it — generating a distribution of placebo gaps for units that got no treatment.
Measure fit with the root-mean-squared prediction error (RMSPE), pre and post, and rank the treated unit’s post/pre RMSPE ratio against the placebos.
If the true treated unit’s gap is an extreme outlier — a high rank — the effect is unlikely to be chance. The rank gives an exact p-value. (Cunningham 2021)

The post/pre ratio is the clever part: it rewards a large post-period gap only if the pre-period fit was tight, automatically penalising placebos that never matched their own history.






NoteThe two founding studies




California Proposition 99 (Abadie, Diamond & Hainmueller 2010). California’s 1988 tobacco program raised cigarette taxes by 25¢. A synthetic California built from a 38-state donor pool tracked real California almost exactly before 1988 (in 1980, real =120.20 packs vs synthetic =120.43). After 1988 the lines split sharply, and placebo tests gave California the highest post/pre RMSPE ratio in the sample — an exact p=0.026. (Cunningham 2021)
Basque Country terrorism (Abadie & Gardeazabal 2003). The method’s debut: a synthetic Basque region, weighted from other Spanish regions, showed terrorism drove Basque per-capita GDP below its counterfactual. (Cunningham 2021)

title: Synthetic Control
text:
Requirements and limits
Synthetic control’s credibility comes with conditions: (Cunningham 2021)

Good pre-treatment fit. The synthetic unit must hug the treated unit’s pre-period path; a poor match means no credible counterfactual.
A long pre-period. Many pre-intervention periods are what let matching on past outcomes soak up unobserved factors — the more history matched, the harder it is to match by luck.
No anticipation. Units mustn’t change behaviour before treatment in expectation of it, or the pre-period is contaminated.
A clean donor pool. Exclude units hit by similar interventions or large idiosyncratic shocks — they’d poison the blend.
No-extrapolation honesty. If the treated unit is outside the donor pool’s convex hull (an extreme on some predictor), no weighted average can match it, and the method shouldn’t be forced.

title: Synthetic Control
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Poor pre-treatment fit. If the synthetic unit doesn’t reproduce the pre-period, the post-period gap is not a clean effect. Report the fit and the RMSPE. (Cunningham 2021)
A contaminated donor. A donor that also experienced the policy (or a big shock) drags the counterfactual; curate the pool. (Cunningham 2021)
Reading standard errors instead of placebos. Inference is by permutation/RMSPE ranking, not textbook SEs, when there’s one treated unit. (Cunningham 2021)
Overfitting the pre-period. Matching noise (too many predictors, too short a pre-period) can fake a good fit that doesn’t reflect a real counterfactual. (Cunningham 2021)
Forcing extrapolation. If no convex combination matches the treated unit, the design is telling you it can’t answer — don’t override it. (Cunningham 2021)

title: Synthetic Control
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Cunningham, The Mixtape — synthetic control as a generalisation of DiD for comparative case studies with one treated aggregate unit; the donor pool and non-negative weights summing to one (interpolation within the convex hull, no extrapolation); fitting weights to pre-treatment outcomes/predictors; the gap estimator \hat\delta_{1t}=Y_{1t}-\sum_j w_j^* Y_{jt}; placebo inference with RMSPE and the post/pre ratio rank giving an exact p-value; the requirements (pre-fit, long pre-period, no anticipation, clean donor pool); and the California Prop 99 (38 donors; 1980 120.20 vs 120.43; p=0.026) and Basque Country studies.

title: Synthetic Control
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The optimisation. Let X_1 be the treated unit’s vector of pre-treatment predictors and X_0 the donors’. Choose weights W=(w_2,\dots,w_{J+1}) to 
\min_{W}\ (X_1-X_0 W)'V(X_1-X_0 W)\quad\text{s.t.}\quad w_j\ge0,\ \sum_j w_j=1,
 where V weights predictors by importance (often chosen so the resulting W best fits pre-treatment outcomes). The simplex constraints w_j\ge0,\sum w_j=1 are what restrict the synthetic unit to the convex hull of the donors — no negative or >1 weights, hence no extrapolation.
Why it generalises DiD. DiD gives the single untreated comparison a weight of one and assumes parallel trends. Synthetic control instead chooses weights so the pre-trends match by construction — turning the parallel-trends assumption into a fitted property of the pre-period, at the cost of needing a rich donor pool and long history.
Placebo p-value. With J donors, run the method treating each as “treated,” compute the post/pre RMSPE ratio r_j for all units, and rank the true treated unit. The exact p-value is \,p=\dfrac{\#\{j: r_j\ge r_{\text{treated}}\}}{J+1}\, — the share of units (placebos plus the real one) whose gap is at least as extreme. California’s rank of 1 out of 39 gives 1/39\approx0.026.

title: Synthetic Control
text:
Connections

Builds on: difference-in-differences (synthetic control generalises it; pre-fit replaces assumed parallel trends), panel data (aggregate time series per unit), and resampling (placebo inference is a permutation test).
Leads to: causal machine learning — flexible, data-driven counterfactuals and heterogeneous effects, the modern frontier.
Reappears in: aggregate policy evaluation, where a single jurisdiction adopts a reform.

title: Synthetic Control
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“What problem does synthetic control solve that difference-in-differences cannot, and why is it called a generalisation of DiD?”
“How is the synthetic control constructed? Explain the donor pool, the non-negative weights summing to one, and why that prevents extrapolation (the convex hull).”
“How is the treatment effect estimated as a gap, and what makes a good pre-treatment fit essential?”
“Explain placebo/permutation inference with RMSPE and the post/pre ratio — how does it produce an exact p-value?”
“Summarise the California Proposition 99 and Basque Country studies, including the donor pool and the p = 0.026 result.”

title: From Evidence to Decision
text: The academy stops at the estimate; the world begins there. A coefficient with a confidence interval is an input to a decision, never the decision itself — because acting requires what the number leaves out: how big the effect is, how common the case, what each kind of mistake costs, who bears the risk, and what you should do before the evidence is complete. Evidence informs the choice; it cannot make it. The last competence this book teaches is wearing both hats — analyst and decider — at once.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: From Evidence to Decision
text:
Why it matters
This is the subtitle of the whole module: the client who needs an answer by Friday. Evidence-based practice says decisions should rest on the best available research — a genuine advance over hunch and authority. (Bryman 2016) But evidence is partial, contested, and value-laden, and a number never speaks for itself. The skill of translating an estimate into a defensible action — explicit about costs, risks, and uncertainty — is what separates a technician from someone who can be trusted with a question.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: From Evidence to Decision
text:
Evidence-based policy, and its limits
The ideal: ground decisions in rigorous research, synthesised through systematic reviews. (Bryman 2016) The limits are just as important to state: (Muller 2018; Bryman 2016)

Evidence is partial — and can deceive. What is measurable is not what is important; leaning only on metrics yields “distorted knowledge” that looks solid. (Muller 2018)
Context-dependence. “What works once may not work again” — David Kilcullen’s lesson from counterinsurgency, where human actors adapt to whatever you do (as students adapt to a tested curriculum). (Muller 2018) External validity is not guaranteed.
The is/ought gap. Evidence describes how the world is; it cannot dictate what a society ought to value. Isaiah Berlin’s point that judgment needs a “talent for synthesis” and a “feel for the whole” names exactly the part no metric supplies. (Muller 2018)

Evidence narrows the argument and disciplines it; it does not end it.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: From Evidence to Decision
section: Translating an estimate into a decision
text:
Translating an estimate into a decision
Four lenses turn a “best guess” into an action: (Reinhart 2015; Muller 2018)

Effect size, not just significance. Decision-makers need how big, not is it p<0.05. A trivial effect can be significant in a large sample and still not worth the investment. (Reinhart 2015)
Base rates. A low base rate flips the calculus: screen for a rare condition and even an accurate test yields more false alarms than true cases, inviting harmful over-intervention. (Reinhart 2015)
The asymmetry of errors. A Type I and a Type II error rarely cost the same. An administrator weighing a $900,000 reading curriculum fears a Type I error (paying for something no better than the status quo); a traffic-safety regulator fears a Type II error (missing a real hazard — measured in preventable deaths). (Reinhart 2015) The same p-value warrants different decisions depending on which error you can least afford.
Expected value. Weight each outcome by its probability and its payoff — the logic even of Pascal’s Wager, where a large enough stake dominates a small probability. (Reinhart 2015)

The figure puts these together: the right choice depends not just on how likely the intervention works but on the cost of being wrong — and the break-even probability moves with it.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

p = np.linspace(0, 1, 200)            # probability the intervention truly works
benefit = 100                          # value if it works

# Expected net value of adopting = p*benefit - (1-p)*cost_of_wrong_adoption
cost_cheap = 40                        # a cheap mistake
cost_costly = 400                      # a costly/harmful mistake
ev_cheap = p * benefit - (1 - p) * cost_cheap
ev_costly = p * benefit - (1 - p) * cost_costly

be_cheap = cost_cheap / (benefit + cost_cheap)
be_costly = cost_costly / (benefit + cost_costly)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
ax.axhline(0, color="#9aa0a6", lw=1.2)
ax.plot(p, ev_cheap, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.4, label="cheap to be wrong")
ax.plot(p, ev_costly, color="#c0392b", lw=2.4, label="costly/harmful to be wrong")
for be, c in [(be_cheap, "#1f3a5f"), (be_costly, "#c0392b")]:
    ax.axvline(be, color=c, ls=":", lw=1.4)
    ax.text(be + 0.01, -120, f"act if p > {be:.2f}", color=c, fontsize=8, rotation=90, va="bottom")
ax.fill_between(p, ev_cheap, 0, where=ev_cheap > 0, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.06)
ax.set_xlabel("probability the intervention truly works")
ax.set_ylabel("expected net value of adopting")
ax.set_title("The same evidence, different decisions — set by the cost of error")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: From estimate to decision: the cost of error sets the bar. Expected net value of adopting an intervention as a function of the probability it truly works, for two regimes. When a wrong adoption is cheap (blue), you should act even at modest confidence — the break-even probability is low. When a wrong adoption is costly or harmful (red), the bar is far higher. The same evidence (the same probability) implies opposite decisions depending on the asymmetry of costs — which is why an estimate alone never decides.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: From Evidence to Decision
section: Deciding under uncertainty and time pressure
text:
Deciding under uncertainty and time pressure
Decisions rarely wait for clean evidence. (Reinhart 2015)






NoteRight turn on red



The 1970s oil crisis pushed the U.S. to allow right turns on red to save fuel. Early studies said it was safe — but they were underpowered. Only years later, with more data, did the policy reveal a roughly 60% increase in pedestrian accidents at affected intersections. (Reinhart 2015) Acting on weak evidence was perhaps unavoidable under the deadline — but treating “not significant” as “safe” was the error.


Two principles for the deadline: (Reinhart 2015)

The precautionary principle. Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence: no proof of harm is not proof of safety. Where the downside is catastrophic or irreversible, the burden shifts toward caution.
“More research is needed” — wisdom or dodge? Usually scientific honesty; sometimes a way for organisations to defer a hard, unpopular action. (Reinhart 2015) Knowing which it is, in a given case, is itself a judgment.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: From Evidence to Decision
text:
Stakeholders and how organisations (mis)use numbers
A decision lands inside an institution with its own incentives — and the tyranny-of-metrics dynamics return in force. (Muller 2018) Organisations use numbers not only to learn but to control, and control invites gaming: surgeons avoid risky patients to protect mortality scores; police downgrade felonies to hit targets; Vietnam body counts were fabricated. (Muller 2018) The practical lesson for anyone handing an estimate to an organisation: anticipate how the number will be used and gamed, and prefer metrics that are internal, diagnostic, and built bottom-up with the people measured. (Muller 2018)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: From Evidence to Decision
text:
The researcher–decision-maker gap
Researchers and decision-makers inhabit different worlds, and the handoff between them is where good evidence goes to die or to work: (Bryman 2016; Muller 2018)

Incommensurate goals. Researchers chase novel, generalisable truths and journal publication; decision-makers need local, actionable solutions to a specific problem, now. (Bryman 2016)
Even transparency cuts both ways. Researchers prize openness; but Muller notes that in politics and diplomacy, total transparency can be the enemy of performance — it kills the backstage negotiation real decisions require. (Muller 2018)
Judgment over mechanical precision. Muller’s bottom line: measurement should inform judgment, not replace it — there is no silver bullet and no substitute for actually knowing your subject and your organisation. (Muller 2018)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: From Evidence to Decision
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Reporting significance and calling it a decision. Decisions need effect size, base rate, and the costs of each error — not a p-value. (Reinhart 2015)
Ignoring the asymmetry of errors. The cost of a false positive vs a false negative should set the evidentiary bar; the $900k curriculum and the traffic hazard warrant different thresholds. (Reinhart 2015)
Treating non-significant as safe. Absence of evidence isn’t proof of safety — the right-turn-on-red trap. (Reinhart 2015)
Forgetting the decision lands in an institution. Anticipate gaming and incentive distortion before your metric becomes someone’s target. (Muller 2018)
Letting evidence pose as values. Data informs the is; it can’t settle the ought. Name the value judgments explicitly. (Muller 2018)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: From Evidence to Decision
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — effect size vs significance for decisions; base rates and over-intervention; the asymmetry of Type I/II errors (the $900k reading curriculum vs traffic safety); expected value / Pascal’s Wager; deciding under uncertainty (right turn on red, ~60% more pedestrian accidents), the precautionary principle, and the “more research needed” dodge.
Muller, The Tyranny of Metrics — limits of evidence (measurable ≠ important; Kilcullen context-dependence; Berlin’s “feel for the whole”; the is/ought gap); organisational gaming; transparency as an enemy of performance; judgment over mechanical precision, “no silver bullet.”
Bryman, Social Research Methods — evidence-based policy and systematic reviews; the researcher–decision-maker gap (incommensurate goals).
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: From Evidence to Decision
text:
Appendix · the decision arithmetic

Expected value and the break-even probability. Let p=\Pr(\text{intervention works}), benefit B if it works, and cost C if you adopt and it doesn’t. Adopt when 
\mathbb E[\text{adopt}] = pB-(1-p)C > 0 \iff p > p^\ast=\frac{C}{B+C}.
 The break-even probability p^\ast rises with the cost of a wrong adoption C — formalising the asymmetry of errors. A cheap mistake (C\ll B) justifies acting on weak evidence; a catastrophic one (C\gg B) demands near-certainty. The same estimate p implies opposite decisions across regimes.
Base rates and the cost of screening. Screening a population with prevalence \rho using a test of sensitivity s and false-positive rate f yields, per N people, sN\rho true positives and fN(1-\rho) false positives. The ratio of false alarms to true cases is \dfrac{f(1-\rho)}{s\rho}, which explodes as \rho\to0 — so even an accurate test does net harm when the base rate is low and the follow-up intervention is risky. The decision must weigh that harm, not just the test’s accuracy.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: From Evidence to Decision

Connections

Leads to: the limits of quantification — the closing case for judgment as the master skill.
Realises: the module’s promise — holding academic rigour and the deadline-bound real world in the same hand.

title: The Limits of Quantification
text: This manual has spent six parts making you formidable with numbers. It ends by insisting that the numbers are not enough. Not everything that counts can be counted, and not everything that can be counted counts. The hardest thing a quantitative researcher learns is where measurement stops — where context, meaning, and tacit experience take over, and where a confident decimal is a fake veil of objectivity. The destination of the whole journey is not the model. It is judgment: rigour and humility, held in the same hand.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
text:
Why it matters
Everything before this taught the power of quantification. Muller and Bryman supply the boundary: measurement is the servant of experience, never its master. (Muller 2018; Bryman 2016) This is not a retreat from rigour — it is its completion. The analyst who can’t say where the numbers fail will over-trust them precisely where they mislead. The closing skill is knowing the edge of your instrument.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
text:
What numbers cannot capture
The fundamental limit: not everything important is measurable. What is countable is not always what is worth counting, and an over-reliance on metrics yields “distorted knowledge” that looks solid and is actually deceptive. (Muller 2018) Numbers struggle with the things that often matter most: (Muller 2018)

Intangibles — reputation, trust, morale, motivation, cooperation, the quality of a relationship.
The “feel for the whole.” Judgment needs a talent for synthesis — grasping the particularities of a situation and the way things hang together — which no indicator supplies. (Muller 2018)
Tacit knowledge. Drawing on Michael Oakeshott, Muller separates abstract “recipe knowledge” (conveyable in formulas) from practical knowledge earned through experience, which cannot be written as a general rule. (Muller 2018) The expert clinician, teacher, or detective knows more than they can say — and more than any dataset records.

The adage Muller cites is the whole entry in one line: “Not everything that can be counted counts, and not everything that counts can be counted.” (Muller 2018)


Show the figure code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.patches import Circle

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.4))
ax.add_patch(Circle((0.38, 0.5), 0.30, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.20))
ax.add_patch(Circle((0.62, 0.5), 0.30, color="#1f8a4c", alpha=0.20))
ax.text(0.38, 0.86, "MEASURABLE", ha="center", color="#1f3a5f", fontsize=11, fontweight="bold")
ax.text(0.62, 0.86, "IMPORTANT", ha="center", color="#1f7a44", fontsize=11, fontweight="bold")

ax.text(0.22, 0.5, "measurable\nbut trivial\n(metric fixation\nover-weights this)",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=8, color="#1f3a5f")
ax.text(0.78, 0.5, "important but\nunmeasurable\n(trust, meaning,\ntacit skill)",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=8, color="#1f7a44")
ax.text(0.50, 0.5, "good\nquantitative\nwork", ha="center", va="center",
        fontsize=9, color="#111", fontweight="bold")

ax.set_xlim(0, 1); ax.set_ylim(0.1, 1.0); ax.axis("off")
ax.set_title("Not everything that counts can be counted")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: What gets measured vs what matters. Good quantitative work lives in the overlap — things both important and measurable. But two regions sit outside it: the measurable-but-trivial (which metric fixation over-weights precisely because it is easy to count) and the important-but-unmeasurable (trust, meaning, tacit skill — which numbers silently drop). The discipline is to keep the whole picture in view, not just the part that fits in a spreadsheet.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
section: Mixed methods: numbers and meaning together
text:
Mixed methods: numbers and meaning together
If numbers miss meaning, the answer is not to abandon them but to complement them. Bryman argues the rigid quantitative/qualitative divide is unhelpful: mixed-methods research gives a fuller account by combining their strengths. (Bryman 2016)

Completeness. Qualitative work fills the gaps numbers leave — the reasons, rationalisations, and arguments behind observed behaviour. (Bryman 2016)
Meaning and process. Where quantitative research gives a static snapshot, qualitative work captures process — how events unfold and how participants interpret their world. (Bryman 2016)
Triangulation. Cross-checking findings across methods (a survey and focus groups) raises the credibility of a conclusion: agreement from independent angles is hard to fake. (Bryman 2016)

The regression coefficient tells you that education raises wages; the interview tells you how a particular person’s schooling changed their life. A mature study often needs both.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
section: The epistemology: how quantifying can distort
text:
The epistemology: how quantifying can distort
Quantification can warp reality through the very act of organising it: (Bryman 2016; Muller 2018)

Abstraction from the particular. Making things comparable through standardisation strips them of context, history, and meaning — the price of a clean variable. (Muller 2018)
Spurious precision. A numerical result wears an appearance of certainty and objectivity it may not have earned, peeling away the caveats and ambiguities that belong with it. (Muller 2018) A reported “3.7%” feels more authoritative than “roughly a thirtieth,” though they may say the same uncertain thing.
Reification. Treating a constructed measure as a real, fixed thing — a static view of social life, as if structures existed independently of the people who make them. (Bryman 2016)
False objectivity. Muller’s sharpest warning: metric enthusiasts seek mechanically universalizable procedures because they distrust judgment — not realising the metrics themselves are products of choices and observational bias. The objectivity is partly a pose. (Muller 2018)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
text:
Muller’s verdict: complement, not substitute
The “tyranny” of metrics is what happens when numbers replace judgment instead of informing it. Muller’s constructive conclusion gives measurement a proper, humble role: (Muller 2018)

Complement judgment — use metrics as internal, diagnostic tools to spot outliers and refine practice, not as high-stakes cudgels.
No silver bullet — there is no substitute for actually knowing your subject and your organisation, an unquantifiable skill earned by experience.
The beginning of wisdom is recognising the limits of the possible: not every problem yields to measurement, and “everything that can be measured can be improved” is a fallacy. (Muller 2018)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
section: The path of the practitioner: rigour and humility
text:
The path of the practitioner: rigour and humility
So we end where the causal summit pointed: the master walks toward knowledge with, in Angrist and Pischke’s phrase, “confidence as well as humility.” (Angrist and Pischke 2015)

Rigour — deploy the most powerful tools available to reach conclusions that can survive cross-examination. Everything in Parts I–V is in service of this.
Humility — acknowledge the messiness of real research and be transparent about mistakes, rather than hiding behind a fake veil of objectivity. (Reinhart 2015)

The master skill is judgment: knowing when the data gives a clear signal and when it is merely a quagmire of quantification. (Muller 2018) Isaiah Berlin said it with finality: “To demand or preach mechanical precision, even in principle, in a field incapable of it is to be blind and to mislead others.” (Muller 2018) That is the last thing this book has to teach — and the reason the quantitative social scientist must be more than a calculator.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Mistaking the measurable for the important. The easy-to-count crowds out the things that matter; keep the unmeasured in view. (Muller 2018)
Spurious precision. A decimal point is not certainty; carry the caveats the number was built on. (Muller 2018)
Reifying constructs. A standardised measure is a useful abstraction, not the thing itself; don’t forget the people behind the variable. (Bryman 2016)
Treating qualitative work as soft or optional. It captures meaning, process, and context numbers can’t; triangulate. (Bryman 2016)
Hiding behind objectivity. Judgment is unavoidable; name it openly rather than pretending the metric decided. (Muller 2018)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Muller, The Tyranny of Metrics — measurable ≠ important and “distorted knowledge”; the “feel for the whole” and tacit knowledge (Oakeshott, recipe vs practical); the “not everything that counts” dictum; spurious precision, false objectivity; metrics as complement not substitute, no silver bullet, the “improvement” fallacy; judgment as the master skill; Berlin on mechanical precision.
Bryman, Social Research Methods — mixed methods (completeness, meaning/process, triangulation); the quant/qual divide as unhelpful; reification and the “static view of social life.”
Angrist & Pischke; Reinhart — the practitioner’s stance of rigour and humility (“confidence as well as humility”; conclusions that survive cross-examination; transparency over a “fake veil of objectivity”).
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

title: The Limits of Quantification
text:
Appendix · why precision can mislead

Spurious precision, quantified. A report of “3.74\%” implies resolution to 0.01, yet if the estimate carries a standard error of, say, 1.5 points, the honest statement is “3.7\%\ \pm\ 3” — the last two digits are noise. Reporting more significant figures than the uncertainty supports manufactures false confidence; the number of meaningful digits is set by the confidence interval, not the software’s default. This is the formal core of “spurious precision”: the display claims information the data don’t contain.
Reification and construct validity. A latent construct (ability, well-being, “school quality”) is operationalised as a measured proxy M. Reification is forgetting the gap M\ne construct — treating M as the thing rather than a noisy, choice-laden indicator of it. The validity of every downstream inference is bounded by how well M stands in for what you actually care about — a question no amount of M-precision can answer.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World

Hands off to: the ML Track for those extending the predictive toolkit — carrying this humility with them.
