title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text: The replication crisis is not mostly a fraud problem. It is a problem of flexibility: every analysis involves dozens of defensible choices — which outliers to drop, which covariates to include, which outcome to feature, when to stop collecting — and if you make those choices after seeing the data, you can find significance in almost anything. This is Goodhart’s law turned on researchers: when a p-value below 0.05 becomes the target for publication, the analysis bends until it clears the bar. The result is an honest literature full of effects that aren’t there.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
Why it matters
Reinhart’s central, unsettling claim: most false findings come not from cheats but from honest researchers with poor statistical instincts. (Reinhart 2015) That makes this everyone’s problem — including yours. The defences (preregistration, replication, correction for multiple testing) only work if you can recognise the failure modes in your own workflow. This entry catalogues how a sincere analysis goes wrong, turning the replication crisis from a headline into a set of habits to guard against.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
Researcher degrees of freedom
Reinhart’s name for the root cause: the “enormous amount of freedom” a researcher has in analysing data. (Reinhart 2015) Each choice is individually defensible:

Which observations to exclude as outliers (drop the subjects who had diarrhea during the diet trial?).
How to define the groups (where exactly does “overweight” begin?).
Which covariates to control for.
Which outcome to treat as primary, and when to stop collecting data.

None is fraud. But made after seeing the data, they let you — usually unconsciously — pick the path most flattering to your hypothesis. Reinhart’s striking number: handling these choices differently can change the estimated effect size by a factor of two. (Reinhart 2015) The analysis is honest at every step and biased as a whole.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
section: P-hacking and the garden of forking paths
text:
P-hacking and the garden of forking paths
P-hacking is selectively analysing data until the p-value drops below 0.05. (Reinhart 2015) Its subtler cousin is the garden of forking paths: even a researcher who runs one analysis can be biased, because the analysis they chose was contingent on the data — at each fork they took the defensible branch that happened to lead to significance. (Reinhart 2015) No conscious cherry-picking is required; the flexibility alone does the damage.






NoteReinhart’s anti-aging Beatles



The demonstration that made the point unforgettable: researchers “proved” that listening to the Beatles’ When I’m Sixty-Four made undergraduates nearly a year and a half younger — a logical impossibility. They did it honestly, by deciding after seeing the data which covariates to control for (the subject’s father’s age, etc.) and which outcome to report. Simulation shows this kind of flexibility can push the false-positive rate as high as 50%. (Reinhart 2015) If you can “discover” reverse aging, you can discover anything.


The figure shows the mechanism directly: take pure noise, try a handful of defensible analyses, report the best — and “significance” appears far more than the advertised 5% of the time.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from statistics import NormalDist

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
studies, n, forks = 4000, 40, 10

def two_sided_p(z):
    return 2 * (1 - NormalDist().cdf(abs(z)))

single_p, hacked_p = [], []
for _ in range(studies):
    # honest single test on a null effect
    g1 = rng.normal(0, 1, n); g2 = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
    z = (g1.mean() - g2.mean()) / np.sqrt(g1.var(ddof=1)/n + g2.var(ddof=1)/n)
    single_p.append(two_sided_p(z))
    # garden of forking paths: try `forks` defensible analyses, keep the smallest p
    ps = []
    for _ in range(forks):
        a = rng.normal(0, 1, n); b = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
        zz = (a.mean() - b.mean()) / np.sqrt(a.var(ddof=1)/n + b.var(ddof=1)/n)
        ps.append(two_sided_p(zz))
    hacked_p.append(min(ps))

single_p, hacked_p = np.array(single_p), np.array(hacked_p)
fp_single = (single_p < 0.05).mean()
fp_hacked = (hacked_p < 0.05).mean()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.0))
bins = np.linspace(0, 1, 21)
ax.hist(single_p, bins=bins, color="#9aa0a6", alpha=0.7, density=True,
        label=f"single honest test — {fp_single*100:.0f}% significant")
ax.hist(hacked_p, bins=bins, color="#c0392b", alpha=0.55, density=True,
        label=f"best of {forks} analyses — {fp_hacked*100:.0f}% significant")
ax.axvline(0.05, color="#1f3a5f", ls="--", lw=1.6)
ax.text(0.06, ax.get_ylim()[1]*0.9, "p = 0.05", color="#1f3a5f", fontsize=8)
ax.set_xlabel("reported p-value (data with NO real effect)")
ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("Flexibility manufactures significance from noise")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The garden of forking paths. Each study here is pure noise — there is no real effect. An honest single test (grey) gives p-values that are uniform, crossing 0.05 exactly 5% of the time. But an analyst who tries 10 defensible analyses per study and reports the smallest p-value (red) finds ‘significance’ in roughly 40% of studies — eight times the nominal rate. The flexibility, not dishonesty, manufactures the false positives.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
Multiple comparisons
The most quantifiable QRP: run enough tests and false positives are guaranteed. (Reinhart 2015) Test 20 independent true-null hypotheses at \alpha=0.05, and the chance of at least one false positive is 1-(1-0.05)^{20}\approx \mathbf{64\%}. (Reinhart 2015) This is the jelly-bean problem: test 20 colours of jelly bean for a link to acne and you’ll “find” one — a famous comic, and a real research pathology. (Reinhart 2015) The corrections: (Reinhart 2015)

Bonferroni — divide the threshold by the number of tests (\alpha/n); controls the chance of any false positive (family-wise error), at the cost of power.
Benjamini–Hochberg — controls the false discovery rate (the expected fraction of significant results that are false), less conservative and often more sensible when many tests are genuinely exploratory.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
HARKing and circular analysis
HARKing — Hypothesising After Results are Known — is dressing up a pattern you found in the data as a hypothesis you predicted. (Reinhart 2015) It is a form of circular analysis or “double-dipping”: using the same data to both generate and test a hypothesis. The canonical case is neuroimaging — select a brain “region of interest” because it lit up to a stimulus, then run a detailed test on that same region, and you’ve guaranteed an inflated effect: you selected for the strongest random noise, then “confirmed” it. (Reinhart 2015) Discovery and confirmation must use different data.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
Optional stopping
Optional stopping is peeking at the data as it accumulates and stopping the moment p<0.05. (Reinhart 2015) Because the p-value random-walks as n grows, it will dip below 0.05 by chance eventually even under the null — and if you stop there, you bank a false positive. Testing after every subject and stopping at significance massively inflates the error rate. The clinical signature: medical trials stopped early for benefit exaggerate their effects by an average of 29% versus trials that ran to completion. (Reinhart 2015)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
section: Publication bias and the file drawer
text:
Publication bias and the file drawer
Even a literature of honest, correct studies can lie in aggregate, because of what gets published. Eye-catching significant results are far more publishable than null findings — the file-drawer problem — so the published record is a biased sample of all results. (Reinhart 2015) Two cases: (Reinhart 2015)

TP53 and cancer. A meta-analysis of 18 published studies showed a highly significant correlation; add the unpublished data and the effect vanished.
Reboxetine. Pfizer’s antidepressant looked effective in the published trials — while studies comprising three times more data, showing it didn’t work, went unmentioned.

This is why meta-analysis must explicitly probe for publication bias: pooling a biased sample faithfully reproduces the bias.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
section: Truth inflation and the winner’s curse
text:
Truth inflation and the winner’s curse
The final twist, and the link back to statistical power: truth inflation (a Type M, or magnitude, error). (Reinhart 2015) An underpowered study has high sampling variability, so it only reaches significance when it draws a lucky, large-effect sample. The published estimate is therefore systematically too big — the winner’s curse. (Reinhart 2015) Hence the Proteus phenomenon: in fast-moving fields the earliest published effects are the most extreme, and follow-ups shrink them — and higher-impact journals, chasing splashier findings, may overstate effects more. (Reinhart 2015) A significant result from a small study is not just possibly false; if true, it is probably exaggerated.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
Reading other people’s research
Every practice above is also a checklist for consuming research — you spend more time reading studies than producing them, and the same flaws hide in others’ work. (Reinhart 2015) Martin & Bridgmon offer a disciplined five-step way to evaluate a paper before trusting its headline: (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)

Gain a general overview — what is the study about, and what does it claim?
Ascertain the rationale — why was it done; what gap or question motivates it?
Identify the theoretical framework — what assumptions and prior theory underpin the design?
Identify the setting — who, where, when; the sample and context that bound the result.
Determine the nature of the research — design, methods, and analysis: is the inference licensed by the design? (Martin and Bridgmon 2012)

Two habits sharpen the read. First, return to the research question as your anchor — it is easy to get lost in charts and jargon, so keep asking whether each result actually bears on the question posed. (Reinhart 2015) Second, look for the mundane failure modes this Part catalogues: an uncorrected multiple-comparisons fishing expedition, a subgroup that smells of HARKing, a small “significant” study with an implausibly large effect, a non-reproducible analysis with no shared data or code, sloppy code errors like mislabelled columns. (Reinhart 2015) Critical consumption is not cynicism — it is applying to others’ work the same scrutiny you owe your own.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Deciding the analysis after seeing the data. Outlier rules, covariates, subgroups, and the primary outcome should be fixed in advance; otherwise you walk the garden of forking paths. (Reinhart 2015)
Running many tests and reporting the winners. Correct for multiplicity (Bonferroni / FDR) and report all the tests you ran, not just the significant ones. (Reinhart 2015)
Testing a hypothesis on the data that generated it. HARKing/double-dipping inflates effects; split discovery from confirmation. (Reinhart 2015)
Peeking and stopping at significance. Optional stopping inflates false positives and effect sizes; fix the sample size or use a proper sequential design. (Reinhart 2015)
Trusting a small significant study’s effect size. Truth inflation means it’s likely overstated; expect regression toward a smaller true effect. (Reinhart 2015)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Reinhart, Statistics Done Wrong — false results from honest researchers via researcher degrees of freedom (outliers/groups/covariates; effect sizes off by 2×); p-hacking and the garden of forking paths (the When I’m Sixty-Four study; false-positive rates to 50%); multiple comparisons (1-0.95^{20}\approx64\%; jelly beans; Bonferroni and Benjamini–Hochberg FDR); HARKing / circular analysis / double-dipping (neuroimaging ROI); optional stopping (early- stopped trials exaggerate ~29%); publication bias / file drawer (TP53; Reboxetine); truth inflation / Type M / winner’s curse and the Proteus phenomenon; the consumer’s habit of returning to the research question and spotting these failure modes in others’ work.
Martin & Bridgmon — the five-step process for critically evaluating a study (overview, rationale, theoretical framework, setting, nature of the research).
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Family-wise error. For m independent true-null tests at level \alpha, the probability of at least one false rejection is 
\text{FWER}=1-(1-\alpha)^m,
 \to1 as m grows (64\% at m=20,\alpha=0.05). Bonferroni uses \alpha/m per test, bounding FWER \le\alpha. Benjamini–Hochberg instead orders the p-values p_{(1)}\le\dots\le p_{(m)} and rejects the largest k with p_{(k)}\le \frac{k}{m}\alpha, controlling the expected false discovery proportion at \alpha — more powerful when many alternatives are true.
Optional stopping inflates \alpha. Under the null the test statistic behaves like a random walk; by the law of the iterated logarithm it crosses any fixed boundary infinitely often as n\to\infty, so \Pr(\exists\,n: p_n<0.05)\to1. Stopping at the first crossing therefore rejects with probability far above 0.05.
Truth inflation (Type M). Condition the estimate \hat\theta\sim\mathcal N(\theta,\sigma^2) on significance |\hat\theta|>1.96\,\sigma. When power is low (\theta/\sigma small), the conditional mean \mathbb E[\hat\theta\mid \text{significant}] greatly exceeds \theta — the published effect is inflated, by a factor that grows as power falls. This is the formal winner’s curse, and the reason power protects magnitude, not just detection.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
Connections

Builds on: the replication crisis (the symptom), hypothesis testing and power (the machinery being abused), and the tyranny of metrics (the p<0.05 target as a Goodhart trap).
Leads to: open & reproducible science — preregistration, replication, and sharing as the structural fixes for every practice here.
Reappears in: communicating findings (significant ≠ important) and from evidence to decision (discounting inflated literatures).
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“What are researcher degrees of freedom, and how can defensible choices made after seeing the data change an effect size by a factor of two?”
“Explain p-hacking and the garden of forking paths using the When I’m Sixty-Four study. Why is no conscious cheating required?”
“Why does running 20 tests give a ~64% chance of a false positive, and how do Bonferroni and Benjamini–Hochberg differ?”
“Define HARKing and circular analysis (‘double-dipping’) and explain the neuroimaging region-of- interest example.”
“Explain optional stopping, publication bias / the file drawer (TP53, Reboxetine), and truth inflation / the winner’s curse.”
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Questionable Research Practices & Researcher Degrees of Freedom

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
text: Every estimate in this book isolates one effect: what does X do to Y, all else equal? But the world you act on is a system — stocks, flows, and feedback loops that respond to your intervention and often push back. Donella Meadows’ Thinking in Systems is the lens for that: it explains why policies provoke resistance, why problems persist despite our efforts, and why the highest-leverage place to intervene is almost never the one our instincts reach for. It is the structural complement to everything you’ve learned about estimating effects.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
text:
Why it matters
A causal estimate is a statement about a nudge to one variable. A policy is a sustained shove against a living structure that adapts. (Meadows 2008) The reason good interventions disappoint — and the reason a metric gets gamed the moment it becomes a target — is that you are not acting on an inert quantity but on a network of feedback loops that responds. Meadows’ core warning is blunt: “the system causes its own behavior.” (Meadows 2008) The quantitative researcher who can estimate an effect and read the system it lives in is the one whose recommendations survive contact with reality.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
text:
What a system is
A system is not a heap of parts; it is “an interconnected set of elements coherently organized in a way that achieves something.” It has three components, in rising order of importance and falling order of visibility: (Meadows 2008)

Elements — the tangible parts you notice first (a university’s students, faculty, buildings).
Interconnections — the relationships that bind them, usually flows of information or signals (admission standards, grading rules, the chemical messages in a tree).
Purpose / function — the “most crucial but least obvious” part. (Meadows 2008)

The deep move is how you find the purpose: “purposes are deduced from behavior, not from rhetoric or stated goals.” (Meadows 2008) Swap every player on a team and the system is barely changed; change its purpose — a university shifting from “disseminating knowledge” to “making a profit” — and it transforms, even with the same people in the same buildings. Meadows’ compressed version, and a razor for cutting through any mission statement: “the purpose of a system is what it does.” (Meadows 2008)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
text:
Stocks and flows
The building blocks. A stock is an accumulation — water in a bathtub, a population, a bank balance, a “reserve of good will.” A flow is the action that fills or drains it (the tap and the plughole). (Meadows 2008) Two properties make stocks the heart of system behaviour:

A stock is “the memory of the history of changing flows within the system.” (Meadows 2008) It carries the past forward; you read history in the level, not the moment.
Stocks decouple inflow from outflow. Because the bathtub buffers, the two flows need not match instant to instant — an oil company keeps running even when refinery output doesn’t equal the rate cars burn fuel. (Meadows 2008) Stocks act as delays, buffers, and shock absorbers — and those delays are exactly what makes systems hard to steer.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
text:
Feedback loops
A feedback loop is “a closed chain of causal connections” from a stock, through a flow, back to the stock. (Meadows 2008) There are only two kinds, and almost all dynamics are their interplay:

Balancing (stabilizing) loops are goal-seeking: they oppose whatever direction of change is imposed, holding a stock near a target. A thermostat returning a room to its setpoint; a hot coffee cooling to room temperature. (Meadows 2008)
Reinforcing (amplifying) loops are self-multiplying: the more there is in the stock, the faster it grows — exponential growth or runaway collapse. An interest-bearing account; a breeding population of rabbits. (Meadows 2008)



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

T = 60
# Reinforcing loop: stock grows in proportion to itself (dS = r*S dt)
r = 0.06
reinforcing = np.empty(T); reinforcing[0] = 2.0
for t in range(1, T):
    reinforcing[t] = reinforcing[t-1] + r * reinforcing[t-1]

# Balancing loop: stock seeks a goal (dS = k*(goal - S) dt)
goal, k = 8.0, 0.12
balancing = np.empty(T); balancing[0] = 0.5
for t in range(1, T):
    balancing[t] = balancing[t-1] + k * (goal - balancing[t-1])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.0))
ax.plot(reinforcing, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.4, label="reinforcing loop (amplifies)")
ax.plot(balancing, color="#3f8f6f", lw=2.4, label="balancing loop (goal-seeking)")
ax.axhline(goal, color="#3f8f6f", ls="--", lw=1.2, alpha=0.7)
ax.text(T*0.62, goal + 0.4, "balancing loop's goal", color="#3f8f6f", fontsize=8.5)
ax.set_ylim(0, 16)
ax.set_xlabel("time"); ax.set_ylabel("stock level")
ax.set_title("Reinforcing vs balancing feedback")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, frameon=False, loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: The two feedback loops, simulated. A reinforcing loop (navy) feeds on itself — the fuller the stock, the faster it grows — producing exponential lift-off. A balancing loop (green) is goal-seeking: it closes the gap to a target (dashed) and settles, opposing any push away from it. Real systems are tugs-of-war between the two; which dominates, and the delays in between, set the behaviour you observe.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
section: The system causes its own behaviour
text:
The system causes its own behaviour
Here is the insight that reorders how you read evidence. We instinctively blame events — a single bad number, a single villain. Meadows insists we climb from events to behaviour (trends over time) to structure (the arrangement of stocks, flows, and loops). (Meadows 2008) “The system causes its own behavior.” A political leader doesn’t cause a recession; “ups and downs are inherent in the structure of the market economy.” (Meadows 2008) Persistent ills — poverty, war — endure because they are “intrinsically systems problems,” generated by structures that recreate them regardless of who occupies the roles. (Meadows 2008)
This is why “systems fool us”: they behave beautifully for a while, then surprise us with terrible results. (Meadows 2008) And the surprise is rarely anyone’s malice. Under bounded rationality, every actor behaves “dutifully and rationally” on the local information they can see, yet the aggregate is “a perfectly terrible result.” (Meadows 2008) No bad guys required — just a structure that makes sensible local choices add up to collective failure.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
section: Leverage points — and why we push them backwards
text:
Leverage points — and why we push them backwards
A leverage point is “a place in a system where a small shift… can produce big changes in everything.” (Meadows 2008) The famous, humbling observation: people are often good at intuiting where the leverage points are — and then push them in the wrong direction. (Meadows 2008) The hierarchy explains why our instincts mislead. The interventions that feel powerful — changing a parameter (swap the leader, tweak a number, adjust a tax rate) — are usually the weakest. (Meadows 2008) The strongest leverage sits where we rarely look: in the interconnections, the goals, and the paradigm — the system’s purpose. Change the rules and the purpose and you change everything; change the people at the top and the structure quietly reasserts itself.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
section: What this means for the quantitative researcher
text:
What this means for the quantitative researcher
Systems thinking does not contradict the causal toolkit — it frames it. Three consequences for applied work:

Policy resistance is predictable. A balancing loop will oppose your intervention; the system pushes back toward its prior state. An effect estimated in a trial can be neutralised at scale by feedback the trial never exercised — a structural reason external validity fails.
Goodhart’s law is a feedback loop. “When a measure becomes a target it ceases to be a good measure” is precisely a system responding to the stock you chose to optimise. Metric fixation is systems-blindness with a dashboard. (Meadows 2008)
Look for structure, not villains. When a result disappoints or an organisation misbehaves, the systems question — what structure makes this behaviour the natural output? — beats the search for someone to blame, and points at the high-leverage fix. This is the “feel for the whole” that no metric supplies. (Meadows 2008)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Treating a stock like a flow. A one-time payment (flow) doesn’t fix a depleted reserve (stock) that took years to drain; mind the difference, and the delays. (Meadows 2008)
Blaming the event, missing the structure. Reacting to each data point in isolation keeps you forever fire-fighting symptoms a feedback loop keeps regenerating. Climb to behaviour and structure. (Meadows 2008)
Pulling the obvious lever. Changing a parameter (the number, the person at the top) feels decisive and usually changes little; the goals and rules are where the leverage hides. (Meadows 2008)
Pushing leverage the wrong way. Even when you find the leverage point, intuition often has the sign backwards — e.g. relieving a pressure that was actually holding the system in check. Test the direction. (Meadows 2008)
Mistaking stated purpose for real purpose. Read behaviour, not rhetoric: “the purpose of a system is what it does.” A mission statement is not evidence. (Meadows 2008)
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Meadows, Thinking in Systems: A Primer — a system as elements + interconnections + purpose, with purpose “deduced from behavior, not rhetoric” and “the purpose of a system is what it does”; stocks (the “memory of the history of changing flows”) and flows, stocks as buffers/delays that decouple inflow and outflow; feedback loops — balancing (goal-seeking: thermostat, cooling coffee) vs reinforcing (amplifying: interest, rabbits); “the system causes its own behavior” and the events → behavior → structure ladder; bounded rationality (“dutifully and rationally” → “a perfectly terrible result”); leverage points, the claim that people push them in the wrong direction, and the hierarchy in which parameters are weak and goals/paradigms are strong; and “systems fool us” / “you can’t see the forest for the trees.”
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

The stock–flow integral. A stock is the accumulation of net flow over time: 
S(t)=S(0)+\int_0^t \big[\text{inflow}(\tau)-\text{outflow}(\tau)\big]\,d\tau,
 the continuous statement of “the level is the memory of past flows.” Its discrete cousin is S_{t}=S_{t-1}+(\text{in}-\text{out}) — the update rule in the figure.
Why the two loops give exponential vs goal-seeking curves. A reinforcing loop makes net flow proportional to the stock, \dot S = rS, whose solution S(t)=S_0 e^{rt} is exponential — runaway growth (or, with r<0, collapse). A balancing loop makes net flow proportional to the gap from a goal G, \dot S = k(G-S), whose solution S(t)=G-(G-S_0)e^{-kt} decays toward G and stays there — goal-seeking stability. Every system trajectory is a superposition of these two motifs, shaped by the delays that the stocks impose.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
text:
Connections

Builds on: the tyranny of metrics (Goodhart’s law as a feedback loop) and from evidence to decision (the “feel for the whole” a single estimate lacks).
Reframes: randomized experiments and external validity — why an effect can be absorbed by feedback the study never exercised (policy resistance).
Leads to: the limits of quantification — structure, purpose, and the whole are exactly what numbers struggle to capture.
crumbs: VI · Doing Research in the World
Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage
text:
Study with NotebookLM

Thinking in Systems: Feedback, Stocks & Leverage

title: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web
text: The CSV is the easy case. Real social-science data lives in Excel workbooks with merged cells, SQL databases too big to load, JSON APIs that return deeply nested lists, and web pages with no download button at all. R for Data Science gives a consistent escape hatch for each — and the same destination every time: a tidy data frame you can transform and model.
crumbs: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web

title: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web
text:
Why it matters
Importing a flat file is the start; the data you actually need is often locked in a spreadsheet, a database, or behind an API. Knowing how to liberate it — and where each source’s traps lie — is what separates an analyst who can only use pre-packaged datasets from one who can assemble the dataset a question requires. (Wickham et al. 2023)
crumbs: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web

title: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web
text:
Spreadsheets
Use the readxl package: read_excel() reads both .xls and .xlsx, and excel_sheets() lists the sheet names in a workbook so you can pick the one you want. (Wickham et al. 2023) For Google Sheets, the parallel package is googlesheets4. Spreadsheets carry spreadsheet-specific hazards — formatting as data, multiple header rows, merged cells — so expect to combine read_excel() with skip, explicit col_names, and a tidy-up pass.
crumbs: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web

title: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web
text:
Databases
For data too large to hold in memory, the authors recommend DBI: open a connection to a backend (RSQLite, RMySQL, Postgres…) and run SQL queries that return results as a data frame. (Wickham et al. 2023) The key idea — vital for large data — is that you don’t pull everything into memory; you query only what you need. (Wickham et al. 2023)
The tidyverse makes this seamless via dbplyr: write ordinary dplyr verbs against a database table and they are translated to SQL and run on the database server, with only the (small) result collected back into R.
library(dbplyr)
con <- DBI::dbConnect(RSQLite::SQLite(), "company.sqlite")

tbl(con, "transactions") |>           # a remote table, not in memory
  filter(year == 2024) |>             # these verbs become SQL...
  group_by(region) |>
  summarize(revenue = sum(amount)) |>
  collect()                            # ...and only the summary is pulled into R
crumbs: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web

title: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web
text:
Hierarchical / JSON data
APIs and config files return hierarchical data — deeply nested lists. Read it with jsonlite (JSON) or xml2 (XML), which lands in R as a nested list, and then “rectangle” it: tidyr::unnest() (and unnest_longer()/unnest_wider()) expands nested columns back into a regular data frame where each row is one observation. (Wickham et al. 2023) Rectangling is the JSON-era equivalent of tidying.
crumbs: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web

title: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web
text:
Web scraping
When there’s no file at all, rvest scrapes the page itself: read_html() loads it, CSS selectors in html_elements() pick out the parts you want, and html_table() turns an HTML <table> straight into a data frame. (Wickham et al. 2023)






WarningScrape responsibly



Scraping pulls data someone else published, often without an API. Before scraping at scale, check the site’s terms and robots.txt, identify yourself, rate-limit your requests, and prefer an official API or data download when one exists. Treat a website’s data as someone else’s property and server — the same ethics that govern research integrity and data handling apply here.
crumbs: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web

title: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Loading a giant table into memory. With databases, filter and summarise on the server (dbplyr → SQL); collect() only the result. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Trusting a spreadsheet’s layout. Merged cells, multi-row headers, and “formatting as data” need skip, explicit col_names, and cleanup. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Stopping at the nested list. JSON isn’t analysis-ready until you rectangle it with unnest_*() into one-row-per-observation. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Scraping without checking permission. Respect terms/robots.txt, rate-limit, and prefer an API; a scraper is a guest on someone’s server.
crumbs: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web

title: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — spreadsheets (readxl::read_excel, excel_sheets, googlesheets4); databases via DBI running SQL and returning data frames, the principle of querying only what you need (and dbplyr translating dplyr to SQL); hierarchical/JSON data (jsonlite/xml2 → nested list → tidyr::unnest() “rectangling”); and web scraping with rvest (read_html, html_elements + CSS selectors, html_table).
crumbs: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web

title: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web
text:
Connections

Builds on: importing data (the flat-file case) and tidy data (rectangling is tidying for nested data).
Pairs with: the grammar of manipulation (dbplyr runs the same verbs against SQL) and iteration (scrape/parse many pages).
Respects: research integrity & data ethics — scraped and queried data still carries provenance and consent obligations.
crumbs: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web

title: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“How do you read Excel and Google Sheets, and what spreadsheet-specific problems should you expect?”
“How does DBI/dbplyr let you work with a database, and why is querying-only-what-you-need vital for large data?”
“What does it mean to ‘rectangle’ hierarchical/JSON data, and which tidyr functions do it?”
“What are the rvest steps for scraping a web page, and what ethical checks come first?”
crumbs: Beyond Flat Files: Spreadsheets, Databases, JSON & the Web

title: Exploratory Visualization: Variation & Covariation
text: Before any model, you interrogate the data. R for Data Science frames exploratory data analysis (EDA) not as a fixed procedure but as an iterative cycle and a “state of mind”: ask a question, answer it with a plot or a summary, let the answer breed the next question. (Wickham et al. 2023) Two questions are always worth asking — about variation within a variable and covariation between variables — and a handful of plots answer them.

title: Exploratory Visualization: Variation & Covariation
text:
Why it matters
This is the visualize loop of the workflow, the step that can surprise you. The authors are explicit that EDA is “fundamentally a creative process”: at the start you don’t know what’s hidden, so the goal is to “generate a large quantity of questions” and investigate “every idea that occurs to you” before honing in. (Wickham et al. 2023) It is the dataframe-native version of Part I’s exploratory data analysis — and, as Tukey said (quoted in the book), “the simple graph has brought more information to the data analyst’s mind than any other device.” (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Exploratory Visualization: Variation & Covariation
text:
The EDA cycle
A three-step loop you spin many times: (Wickham et al. 2023)

Generate questions about your data.
Search for answers by visualizing, transforming, and modelling.
Refine the questions, or spawn new ones, from what you learn.

There are no rules for which questions — but two types are “always useful”: (Wickham et al. 2023)

What type of variation occurs within my variables? What type of covariation occurs between my variables?

title: Exploratory Visualization: Variation & Covariation
text:
Variation: looking within one variable
Variation is the tendency of a variable’s values to change from measurement to measurement; you see it by plotting the distribution. (Wickham et al. 2023)

Categorical → a bar chart (geom_bar()): bar height is the count per category.
Continuous → a histogram (geom_histogram()): counts within bins.

What to look for: (Wickham et al. 2023)

Typical values — the tall bars (common) vs short bars (rare). Why is each common or rare?
Clusters / subgroups — separated humps suggest distinct subpopulations. How are within-cluster cases alike, and how do clusters differ?
Outliers — points that don’t fit. In big data they hide; the authors zoom the y-axis with coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 50)) to expose the rare bins.



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
# Two subgroups + a few outliers — the kind of structure a histogram reveals
a = rng.normal(20, 3, 600)
b = rng.normal(38, 4, 400)
outliers = np.array([70, 72, 75, 78, 80])
x = np.concatenate([a, b, outliers])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.0))
ax.hist(x, bins=40, color="#1f3a5f", alpha=0.85, edgecolor="white")
ax.annotate("two clusters →\nlikely two subgroups", xy=(29, 40), fontsize=8.5, color="#3f8f6f")
ax.annotate("outliers", xy=(74, 8), xytext=(60, 35), fontsize=8.5, color="#c0392b",
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color="#c0392b"))
ax.set_xlabel("value"); ax.set_ylabel("count")
ax.set_title("What does the distribution look like?")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Variation within one continuous variable. A histogram exposes what a mean and SD hide: here, two clusters (a bimodal distribution — evidence of two subgroups) plus a handful of outliers far to the right. R for Data Science’s EDA habit is to SEE the distribution first and let its shape — typical values, clusters, outliers — generate the next question.





Handling unusual values: the authors don’t recommend silently dropping outlier rows. Instead, replace the suspect value with NA and keep the row: mutate(y = ifelse(y < 3 | y > 20, NA, y)). (Wickham et al. 2023) That preserves the rest of the observation and makes the decision explicit (see missing values).

title: Exploratory Visualization: Variation & Covariation
text:
Covariation: looking between variables
Covariation is the tendency of two variables to move together. (Wickham et al. 2023) The right plot depends on the pair of types:

Categorical × continuous. Overlaid frequency polygons (geom_freqpoly()), or — to compare groups of very different sizes — map y = after_stat(density) so shapes are comparable. A boxplot (geom_boxplot()) gives a compact median/spread summary; if the categories have no natural order, reorder() them by the value you’re plotting (aes(x = reorder(class, hwy, FUN = median), y = hwy)) to reveal the trend. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Two categorical. geom_count() (circle size = count per combination), or count() then geom_tile() with a fill aesthetic — a heatmap of the cross-tabulation. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Two continuous. The scatterplot (geom_point()). When points pile up (overplotting), bin into geom_bin2d() (rectangles) or geom_hex() (hexagons), or slice one variable with cut_width() and boxplot each slice. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Exploratory Visualization: Variation & Covariation
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Treating EDA as a checklist. It’s a creative, iterative search; mechanical plotting misses the surprising question. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Dropping outliers reflexively. Investigate first; if you must remove, set to NA (keep the row) and say so. A dropped outlier can be the most interesting finding. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Comparing raw counts across unequal groups. Use density so shape, not size, drives the comparison. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Overplotting hiding the pattern. Thousands of overlapping points look like a blob; bin (geom_bin2d/geom_hex) to recover the density. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Leaving categories in alphabetical order. reorder() (or fct_reorder) turns a jumbled boxplot into a readable ranking. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Exploratory Visualization: Variation & Covariation
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — EDA as an iterative cycle / “state of mind” / “creative process” (generate → answer → refine; “generate a large quantity of questions”; the Tukey quote); the two always-useful questions (variation within, covariation between); variation via bar charts (geom_bar) and histograms (geom_histogram), reading typical values, clusters/subgroups, and outliers (coord_cartesian zoom; replace with NA rather than drop); and covariation — categorical×continuous (geom_freqpoly, density, geom_boxplot, reorder), two categorical (geom_count, count + geom_tile), two continuous (geom_point, geom_bin2d/geom_hex, cut_width).

title: Exploratory Visualization: Variation & Covariation
text:
Connections

Builds on: the grammar of graphics (these are its geoms in action) and the data-science workflow (the visualize loop).
Mirrors: Part I · exploratory data analysis — the same Tukey-rooted discipline, here in tidyverse form.
Leads to: modelling — EDA’s covariation questions become the hypotheses a model tests.

title: Exploratory Visualization: Variation & Covariation

title: The Grammar of Data Manipulation
text: Once data is tidy, nearly everything you do to it is one of a few verbs: pick rows, pick columns, make new columns, sort, summarise — and, crucially, do any of these group-by-group. Chain them with the pipe and a transformation stops being a tangle of nested functions and becomes a readable sequence of actions. This is the grammar that turns “wrangling” from a fight into a sentence.

title: The Grammar of Data Manipulation
text:
Why it matters
The transform station of the workflow is where you spend much of your day, and without structure it degenerates into bespoke, unreadable code. Wickham & Grolemund’s dplyr imposes a grammar: a small, consistent set of verbs that each take a data frame and return a data frame, so they compose without friction. (Wickham et al. 2023) Learn five verbs plus one grouping operator and you can express the overwhelming majority of real data transformations.

title: The Grammar of Data Manipulation
text:
The verbs
Each verb does one thing, takes a data frame as its first argument, and returns a modified data frame: (Wickham et al. 2023)



Verb
What it does




filter()
Pick observations (rows) by their values


select()
Pick variables (columns) by their names


mutate()
Create new variables as functions of existing ones


arrange()
Reorder the rows


summarize()
Collapse many values down to a single summary



Because each verb’s input and output are the same kind of object — a data frame — they snap together like LEGO. The connective tissue is the pipe.

title: The Grammar of Data Manipulation
section: The pipe: code that reads as then
text:
The pipe: code that reads as then
The pipe, written |> and pronounced “then,” takes the result on its left and feeds it as the first argument on its right. (Wickham et al. 2023) It lets you write a transformation as the sequence of actions you’d say out loud:
library(tidyverse)

flights |>
  filter(dest == "IAH") |>            # keep flights to Houston, THEN
  group_by(year, month, day) |>       # for each day, THEN
  summarize(avg_delay = mean(arr_delay, na.rm = TRUE)) |>  # average the delay, THEN
  arrange(desc(avg_delay))            # sort worst-first
Read it top to bottom: take flights, then keep Houston-bound ones, then group by day, then average the delay, then sort. The authors stress that the pipe “focuses on verbs, not nouns,” making code “easier for a human to consume.” (Wickham et al. 2023)






NoteTwo analogies for why the pipe wins




The Dagwood-sandwich problem. Without the pipe, the same logic nests inside-out: arrange(summarize(group_by(filter(flights, ...), ...), ...), ...). You must read it from the innermost call outward — a towering, unreadable sandwich. The pipe flattens it into reading order. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Little Bunny Foo Foo. Wickham & Grolemund tell the children’s rhyme to make the point: a story of hopping, then scooping up the field mice, then bopping them on the head reads naturally as a sequence — but written as nested functions (bop(scoop(hop(foo_foo)))) or as a clutter of named intermediates (foo_foo_1, foo_foo_2, …) it becomes hard to follow. The pipe tells the story in order and spares you inventing “unimportant names” for intermediate objects. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: The Grammar of Data Manipulation
text:
Split–apply–combine
The most powerful move is summarize() paired with group_by(). Alone, group_by() changes nothing visible — it just changes the scope of the verbs that follow, from “the whole table” to “group-by-group.” (Wickham et al. 2023) That single switch implements the split–apply–combine strategy:


Show the figure code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(8.8, 3.2))
for ax in axes:
    ax.axis("off"); ax.set_xlim(0, 1); ax.set_ylim(0, 1)

def cell(ax, x, y, text, color):
    ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((x, y), 0.26, 0.13, fc=color, ec="#1f3a5f", lw=1.1))
    ax.text(x + 0.13, y + 0.065, text, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=8.5)

# SPLIT
axes[0].set_title("split", fontsize=11, fontweight="bold", color="#1f3a5f")
for i, (g, v, c) in enumerate([("A", 3, "#dce7f0"), ("A", 5, "#dce7f0"),
                               ("B", 8, "#e7f0e9"), ("B", 6, "#e7f0e9")]):
    cell(axes[0], 0.36, 0.78 - i*0.17, f"{g}   {v}", c)
axes[0].text(0.49, 0.97, "key  value", ha="center", fontsize=8, style="italic")

# APPLY
axes[1].set_title("apply (mean)", fontsize=11, fontweight="bold", color="#3f8f6f")
cell(axes[1], 0.36, 0.62, "A → mean", "#dce7f0")
cell(axes[1], 0.36, 0.30, "B → mean", "#e7f0e9")

# COMBINE
axes[2].set_title("combine", fontsize=11, fontweight="bold", color="#9a6a2f")
cell(axes[2], 0.36, 0.58, "A   4.0", "#dce7f0")
cell(axes[2], 0.36, 0.41, "B   7.0", "#e7f0e9")
axes[2].text(0.49, 0.78, "one row per group", ha="center", fontsize=8, style="italic")

# arrows between panels
for x in (0.0, 0.5):
    fig.add_artist(plt.Line2D([0.33 + x*0.0, 0.36 + x*0.0], [0.5, 0.5], lw=0))
fig.text(0.345, 0.5, "→", fontsize=20, ha="center", va="center", color="#5a6b7a")
fig.text(0.675, 0.5, "→", fontsize=20, ha="center", va="center", color="#5a6b7a")

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Split–apply–combine, the engine behind group_by() |> summarize(). SPLIT the tidy table into groups by a key (here, group A/B); APPLY a summary (the mean) within each group; COMBINE the per-group answers back into one tidy table — one row per group. group_by() sets the scope; summarize() does the collapsing.





Split the data into groups by a key, apply a function (a summary) to each group, and combine the results back into a single data frame — one row per group. (Wickham et al. 2023) This one pattern answers an enormous share of real questions: average delay per airline, total sales per region, recidivism per prior-conviction count. It is the dataframe-native expression of “compare groups,” and it reappears the moment you reach comparing groups in inference.

title: The Grammar of Data Manipulation
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Forgetting na.rm in a summary. A single missing value makes mean() return NA; summaries silently collapse to nothing. Decide what missing means before you aggregate. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Leaving data grouped. A group_by() persists; verbs downstream keep operating per-group when you meant the whole table. ungroup() when the grouped work is done. (Wickham et al. 2023)
mutate vs summarize confusion. mutate() keeps every row (adds a column); summarize() collapses rows (one per group). Reach for the wrong one and your table is the wrong length. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Nesting instead of piping. The Dagwood sandwich is still legal R; it’s just unreadable. If you’re counting parentheses, reach for the pipe. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: The Grammar of Data Manipulation
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham & Grolemund, R for Data Science — the dplyr grammar and its core verbs (filter, select, mutate, arrange, summarize); group_by() as a change of scope and the split–apply–combine strategy; the pipe (|>, “then”) as code that “focuses on verbs, not nouns”; the Dagwood-sandwich problem of nested calls and the Little Bunny Foo Foo analogy for avoiding “unimportant names” for intermediate objects.

title: The Grammar of Data Manipulation
text:
Connections

Builds on: tidy data (the verbs assume one row per observation) and the workflow (this is the transform station).
Leads to: the grammar of graphics (the same composable, layered thinking, applied to plots) and relational joins (verbs across tables).
Reappears as: comparing groups and every per-group summary in applied work — split–apply–combine is “compare groups” in dataframe form.

title: The Grammar of Data Manipulation

title: Thinking in Dataframes (R)
text: Every other Part of this book is about a method — a way of turning data into a claim. This track is about the step that comes before any method and never appears in the results section: getting the data into a shape you can think with, and then bending it to your questions. Experienced researchers spend the majority of their time here, and the fluent ones share a mental model — the dataframe as the unit of thought, and a small grammar of operations on it. This track teaches that craft end to end, following Hadley Wickham, Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel & Garrett Grolemund’s R for Data Science (2e). (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Thinking in Dataframes (R)
text:
Why a separate track
The dataframe craft is orthogonal to the statistical ascent. Probability, inference, regression, and causal inference are a rising arc of ideas; tidying and transforming data is a skill you apply at every rung of that arc. Folding it into the spine would interrupt the argument; leaving it out would pretend the data arrives analysis-ready. So it lives here — a companion you reach for the moment you face a real, messy table. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Thinking in Dataframes (R)
text:
The whole game
R for Data Science organises everything around one picture of the data-science workflow — import → tidy → (transform ⇄ visualise ⇄ model) → communicate. (Wickham et al. 2023) This track walks that whole picture, one craft at a time.

title: Thinking in Dataframes (R)
section: The bridge to the rest of the book
text:
The bridge to the rest of the book
Tidy data is what every model in this encyclopedia silently assumes: a regression expects one row per observation and one column per variable; an exploratory plot is a mapping from tidy columns to visual properties; a causal design lives or dies on whether treatment, outcome, and covariates are cleanly separated columns. Learn to think in dataframes and the rest of the book gets a great deal easier — and reproducible. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Logical Vectors, Numbers & Summaries
text: The dplyr verbs are only as good as the expressions you feed them. filter() needs a logical test; mutate() needs a numeric transformation; summarize() needs a summary. This entry is the toolbox of those small operations — comparisons and boolean logic, numeric helpers, and aggregation functions — plus the two traps that bite everyone: floating-point equality and the & / && distinction.

title: Logical Vectors, Numbers & Summaries
text:
Logical vectors
A logical vector holds only TRUE, FALSE, or NA. (Wickham et al. 2023) You make one with the comparison operators — >, >=, <, <=, == (equal), != (not equal) — and combine logicals with boolean algebra: & (and), | (or), ! (not). (Wickham et al. 2023) A handy shorthand for a chain of “or”s is x %in% y, which is TRUE wherever x is one of the values in y.






WarningThe floating-point trap



Never use == on numbers with decimals. Computers use finite precision, so “every number you see is an approximation” — and sqrt(2)^2 == 2 actually evaluates to FALSE. (Wickham et al. 2023) Use dplyr::near(), which ignores tiny differences. This bug is silent and ruins filters and joins.








Note& vs && — vectorised vs short-circuit



Use & and | for vectorised logic — element by element, the kind filter() needs. Use && and || only for a single TRUE/FALSE in conditional execution (an if statement); they short-circuit, stopping the moment the answer is settled (&& quits at the first FALSE). (Wickham et al. 2023) Putting && inside filter() is a classic mistake.


For conditional values, if_else() handles a two-way choice (a test, a “yes” value, a “no” value), and case_when() handles multi-way choices without a tangle of nested ifs: (Wickham et al. 2023)
flights |>
  mutate(status = case_when(
    arr_delay < 0  ~ "early",
    arr_delay == 0 ~ "on time",
    arr_delay > 0  ~ "late",
    .default       = "unknown"        # catches NA and anything unmatched
  ))

Logicals are numbers in disguise
The most useful trick: in a numeric context FALSE becomes 0 and TRUE becomes 1. (Wickham et al. 2023) So inside summarize(), sum() of a logical counts the TRUEs and mean() of a logical is the proportion of TRUEs — no extra recoding:
flights |>
  group_by(dest) |>
  summarize(
    n_late    = sum(arr_delay > 0, na.rm = TRUE),   # count of late flights
    prop_late = mean(arr_delay > 0, na.rm = TRUE)   # share that were late
  )

title: Logical Vectors, Numbers & Summaries
text:
Numeric transformations
The numeric helpers that show up constantly: (Wickham et al. 2023)

Modular arithmetic — %/% (integer division) and %% (remainder) split an integer apart (turn 1517 into 15 hours and 17 minutes).
Offsets — lead() and lag() refer to the next/previous value, essential for running differences and change-over-time.
Ranking — min_rank() (ties share a rank: 1, 2, 2, 4), with desc() to rank from the top; cousins row_number(), dense_rank(), percent_rank().
Binning — cut() “discretizes continuous variables” into labelled groups (turn income into brackets).
Cumulative aggregates — cumsum(), cumprod(), cummin(), cummax(), and dplyr’s cummean().

title: Logical Vectors, Numbers & Summaries
text:
Summary functions
Summaries collapse a column to one number per group: (Wickham et al. 2023)

Counts: n() (group size), n_distinct(x) (number of unique values).
Location & spread: median(), IQR() (interquartile range), min(), max() — the robust pair when data is skewed.

These are the building blocks that, with group_by(), implement split–apply–combine.

title: Logical Vectors, Numbers & Summaries
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




== on decimals. Floating-point approximation makes exact equality unreliable; use near(). (Wickham et al. 2023)
&&/|| in vectorised code. They return a single value and short-circuit; inside filter()/mutate() use &/|. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Forgetting na.rm = TRUE. A single NA makes sum/mean/median return NA (next entry); decide how missing is handled before aggregating. (Wickham et al. 2023)
case_when() with no default. Unmatched rows become NA; add .default (or an explicit catch-all) unless you mean that. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Logical Vectors, Numbers & Summaries
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — logical vectors (comparisons, the floating-point trap and near(), boolean &/|/!, %in%, & vs && vectorised vs short-circuit, if_else/case_when, and TRUE/FALSE as 1/0 so sum counts and mean is a proportion); numbers (%/%/%%, lead/lag, min_rank & ranking family, cut binning, cumulative aggregates); and summary functions (n, n_distinct, median, IQR, min/max).

title: Logical Vectors, Numbers & Summaries
text:
Connections

Builds on: the grammar of manipulation (these expressions are what the verbs consume).
Leads to: missing values (why na.rm matters) and exploratory visualization (summaries become plots).
Mirrors: Part I · location, spread & shape — the same statistics, in dataframe-native form.

title: Relational Data: Thinking Across Tables
text: Data almost never arrives as a single tidy table. It comes as several — survey responses in one, respondent demographics in another, region codes in a third — linked by shared identifiers. Thinking relationally means seeing those keys and knowing how to join on them. Get joins right and scattered tables become one analysis-ready frame; get them wrong and you silently drop or duplicate observations.

title: Relational Data: Thinking Across Tables
text:
Why it matters
The transform station usually begins by assembling the data, because the information you need is spread across tables — “data analysis rarely involves a single data frame.” (Wickham et al. 2023) A flight has a carrier code; the carrier’s name lives in a separate airlines table. To plot delay by airline name, you must join them. The relational mindset — keys connecting tables — is the difference between an analyst who can only use pre-merged data and one who can build the dataset the question actually requires.

title: Relational Data: Thinking Across Tables
text:
Keys: how tables connect
Tables are linked by keys — variables that identify observations: (Wickham et al. 2023)

A primary key uniquely identifies each observation in its own table (e.g. carrier code in the airlines table — one row per carrier).
A foreign key is that same variable appearing in another table, where it points back to the first (e.g. carrier in the flights table — many rows per carrier).

A primary key in one table matched to a foreign key in another is a relation. Verifying that your primary key really is unique — exactly one row per value — is the unglamorous check that prevents silent duplication later. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Relational Data: Thinking Across Tables
text:
Joins: combining on a key
A join uses the key to bring columns from one table into another, matching rows where the keys agree. R for Data Science pictures a match as a dot at the intersection of a row from each table. (Wickham et al. 2023) The mutating joins add variables from one table to another, and they differ only in which unmatched rows they keep: (Wickham et al. 2023)

inner_join() — keep only rows that match in both tables. Unmatched rows are dropped from both.
left_join() — keep all rows of the left table, filling NA where the right has no match.
right_join() — keep all rows of the right table.
full_join() — keep all rows of both, NA wherever either side is missing.



Show the figure code
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.4))

def draw_join(ax, title, rows, title_color):
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10.5, fontweight="bold", color=title_color)
    ax.axis("off"); ax.set_xlim(0, 1); ax.set_ylim(0, 1)
    ax.text(0.28, 0.92, "key  val_x  val_y", fontsize=8, style="italic", ha="center")
    for i, (k, vx, vy, present) in enumerate(rows):
        y = 0.76 - i * 0.18
        color = "#e7f0e9" if present else "#f0e0e0"
        ax.add_patch(plt.Rectangle((0.10, y), 0.55, 0.14, fc=color, ec="#1f3a5f", lw=1.1))
        ax.text(0.37, y + 0.07, f"{k}      {vx}        {vy}", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=9)

# inner: only keys 1,2
draw_join(axL, "inner_join (matches only)",
          [("1", "x1", "y1", True), ("2", "x2", "y2", True)], "#1f3a5f")
axL.text(0.37, 0.04, "rows 3 and 4 dropped", ha="center", fontsize=8, color="#9a6a2f", style="italic")

# left: keep all of x (1,2,3), NA for 3
draw_join(axR, "left_join (keep all of x)",
          [("1", "x1", "y1", True), ("2", "x2", "y2", True), ("3", "x3", "NA", False)], "#3f8f6f")
axR.text(0.37, 0.04, "key 3 kept, y filled NA", ha="center", fontsize=8, color="#3f8f6f", style="italic")

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Two joins on a shared key. Left table x (keys 1,2,3) and right table y (keys 1,2,4). An INNER join keeps only keys present in both (1,2) — row 3 and row 4 are dropped. A LEFT join keeps every row of x (1,2,3), filling NA where y has no match (key 3); it never loses an original observation, which is why it’s the recommended default.

title: Relational Data: Thinking Across Tables
text:
Why left_join is the default
Wickham & Grolemund give a clear rule of thumb: the left join should be your default. (Wickham et al. 2023) The reason is conservatism — it “preserves all the original observations” in your primary table even when the lookup table has no match. You started with a table of flights; a left join onto airline names keeps every flight, attaching the name where it exists and NA where it doesn’t. An inner join, by contrast, would silently delete any flight whose carrier code was missing from the airlines table — quietly changing your sample. When the left table is your analysis sample, left_join is what keeps that sample intact. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Relational Data: Thinking Across Tables
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Using inner_join as a habit. It drops unmatched rows from both sides; if the left table is your sample, that’s silent, non-random attrition. Default to left_join and check what didn’t match. (Wickham et al. 2023)
A non-unique “primary” key. If the key repeats in the table you thought was one-row-per-key, the join duplicates rows — your dataset quietly grows and every later count is wrong. Verify uniqueness first. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Mismatched key types or codings. "01" ≠ 1, "USA" ≠ "US"; keys must match exactly to join. Reconcile types and coding before joining. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Not inspecting NAs after a left join. The NAs a left join introduces are information — they tell you which rows had no match. Ignoring them hides a data problem. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Relational Data: Thinking Across Tables
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham & Grolemund, R for Data Science — relational data because “data analysis rarely involves a single data frame”; keys (primary uniquely identifies an observation in its own table; foreign points to it from another); a match pictured as a dot at the intersection; the mutating joins (inner_join keeps only matches; left_join/right_join/full_join keep unmatched rows from one or both sides); and the recommendation that left_join be the default because it preserves all original observations.

title: Relational Data: Thinking Across Tables
text:
Connections

Builds on: tidy data (each table tidy, linked by keys) and the grammar of manipulation (joins are verbs that act across tables).
Pairs with: the workflow — assembling tables is usually the first move of transform.
Underwrites: any analysis whose variables come from multiple sources — merging treatment assignment, outcomes, and covariates into the single tidy frame a model or causal design requires.

title: Relational Data: Thinking Across Tables
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Define primary key and foreign key, and explain what a ‘relation’ between two tables is.”
“Compare inner_join, left_join, right_join, and full_join in terms of which unmatched rows each keeps.”
“Why do Wickham & Grolemund recommend left_join as the default join?”
“What goes wrong when a ‘primary’ key isn’t actually unique, and why does it corrupt later counts?”

title: Strings & Regular Expressions
text: A surprising share of wrangling is text surgery: a column called new_sp_m014 that secretly holds three variables, a date trapped in a sentence, a category spelled four different ways. Two tools handle almost all of it — the stringr package for clean, consistent string operations, and regular expressions, a “terse language” for describing patterns. Master them and “unstructured” data stops being a wall.

title: Strings & Regular Expressions
text:
Why it matters
Strings are where messy reality leaks into the dataframe. Real datasets are full of “unstructured or semi-structured data” and “odd variable codes” that must be parsed before they mean anything. (Wickham et al. 2023) The tidy shape you need often lives inside a text column, and getting it out is a string problem. This is among the least glamorous and most decisive skills in the craft.

title: Strings & Regular Expressions
text:
String basics
A string is text in quotes — single ' or double ", with no behavioural difference, though the authors recommend double quotes as the default. (Wickham et al. 2023) The wrinkle is escapes: to put a literal quote inside a double-quoted string you escape it with a backslash, "\""; to put a literal backslash you double it, "\\". (Wickham et al. 2023)
library(tidyverse)

x <- "She said \"hi\" and left.\nThen \\ appeared."
x              # printed form SHOWS the escapes: "She said \"hi\"...
writeLines(x)  # writeLines() shows the ACTUAL content (the quotes, newline, backslash)
Because the printed representation shows the escapes, use writeLines() to see what a string really contains. (Wickham et al. 2023) Common special characters: \n (newline), \t (tab), and Unicode escapes like "µ" for µ. (Wickham et al. 2023)






NoteUnder the hood: the global string pool



R stores text efficiently through a “global string pool” — each unique string is held in memory once, and every later use is just a pointer to it. (Wickham et al. 2023) So a million-row column with five distinct labels costs about five strings of memory, not a million. (This is also why factors are cheap.)

title: Strings & Regular Expressions
text:
stringr: the str_ verbs
Base R has string functions, but stringr is preferred for consistent, guessable names — they all start with str_ and take the string as the first argument (so they pipe). (Wickham et al. 2023) The core set:



Function
What it does




str_length()
number of characters


str_c()
combine strings — vectorised, recycles shorter vectors; collapse= joins a vector into one


str_sub()
extract by position (inclusive); negative counts from the end (-3, -1 = last three)


str_detect()
logical: does the pattern match? (pairs with filter())


str_count()
how many matches — matches never overlap ("aba" hits "abababa" twice, not three times)


str_extract() / _all()
pull out the matched text (_all returns a list; simplify=TRUE → matrix)


str_replace() / _all()
substitute matches (a named vector does several replacements at once)


str_split()
break a string into pieces on a pattern



fruits <- c("apple", "banana", "pear")
str_detect(fruits, "an")          # FALSE  TRUE FALSE
str_replace_all("2024-01-31", c("-" = "/"))   # "2024/01/31"
str_c("id", 1:3, sep = "_")       # "id_1" "id_2" "id_3"  (vectorised + recycled)

title: Strings & Regular Expressions
text:
Regular expressions
A regular expression describes a pattern, not a literal string — the engine behind str_detect, str_extract, and friends. (Wickham et al. 2023) The pieces:

Literals match themselves; the . (dot) is a wildcard for any character except a newline. To match a literal dot you escape it: the regex is \., which in an R string is written "\\." (the double-backslash trips everyone up at first). (Wickham et al. 2023)
Anchors: ^ matches the start, $ the end. The book’s mnemonic: “if you begin with power (^), you end up with money ($).” (Wickham et al. 2023)
Character classes: \d any digit, \s whitespace, [abc] any of a/b/c, [^abc] anything but. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Quantifiers (repetition): ? 0-or-1, + 1-or-more, * 0-or-more, {n} exactly n, {n,m} between n and m. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Alternation & groups: | is “or” (red|blue); parentheses () make groups, referenced later by backreferences \1, \2. (Wickham et al. 2023)

str_detect(c("grey", "gray"), "gr(e|a)y")   # TRUE TRUE  — one pattern, both spellings
str_extract("Order #4821 shipped", "\\d+")  # "4821"     — one or more digits
str_detect(words, "^p")                      # words starting with p






WarningDon’t reach for one giant regex



The authors quote Jamie Zawinski’s famous warning: “Some people, when confronted with a problem, think ‘I know, I’ll use regular expressions.’ Now they have two problems.” (Wickham et al. 2023) The fix is to break it down — a “series of simpler regexps”, or several str_detect() calls combined with logical operators (!, &, |) — rather than one unreadable monster. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Strings & Regular Expressions
section: The payoff: parsing codes into tidy columns
text:
The payoff: parsing codes into tidy columns
Strings and tidying meet in the book’s WHO tuberculosis case study. A column named new_sp_m014 secretly encodes three variables: the diagnosis method (sp), the gender (m), and the age group (014). (Wickham et al. 2023) The cleaning recipe is pure string work: first str_replace() fixes an inconsistency ("newrel" → "new_rel") so the fields line up, then separate() splits the code into its constituent columns. (Wickham et al. 2023) tidyr::extract() does the same job straight from regex groups, pulling each () capture into its own column. This is the moment a string column becomes a tidy table.

title: Strings & Regular Expressions
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Forgetting the double escape. A literal dot is \. in regex but "\\." in an R string; one backslash isn’t enough. The same trap bites \d ("\\d"). (Wickham et al. 2023)
Expecting . to match a newline. The wildcard skips newlines by default — multi-line text needs care. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Assuming matches can overlap. str_count counts non-overlapping matches; "aba" in "abababa" is 2, not 3. (Wickham et al. 2023)
One regex to rule them all. Unreadable and unmaintainable. Compose simpler patterns and logical operators instead. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Reading a printed string literally. The console shows escapes; use writeLines() to see the true content before you debug a pattern. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Strings & Regular Expressions
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — strings (quotes, escapes, writeLines(), special characters, the global string pool); stringr’s str_ verbs (str_length/c/sub/detect/count/extract/replace/split, vectorisation & recycling, non-overlapping counts); regular expressions as a “terse language” (literals & ., anchors ^ $, character classes, quantifiers, alternation, groups & backreferences, the double-escape); the Zawinski “two problems” warning and “series of simpler regexps”; and the WHO new_sp_m014 parsing case study (str_replace → separate/extract).

title: Strings & Regular Expressions
text:
Connections

Builds on: the grammar of manipulation (str_detect inside filter, str_replace inside mutate) and tidy data (parsing codes into columns).
Leads to: factors (once text is clean, categoricals get explicit levels) and importing data (parsing is where raw text becomes typed columns).
Reappears in: the data-science workflow — text cleaning is a large, unglamorous slice of wrangling.

title: Strings & Regular Expressions
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Why do strings need escapes, and what does writeLines() show that printing a string doesn’t?”
“List the core stringr str_ verbs and what each does; why does str_count count non-overlapping matches?”
“Explain regular expressions: literals and ., anchors ^/$, character classes, quantifiers, alternation, and groups/backreferences. Why the double backslash in R?”
