“What is Zawinski’s ‘two problems’ warning, and what’s the better strategy than one giant regex?”

title: The Grammar of Graphics
text: Most people think of plots as a menu — bar chart, scatter, line — and pick one. R for Data Science teaches something deeper and more generative: every plot is a mapping from variables in a tidy table to visual properties (position, colour, size, shape), assembled from interchangeable layers. Learn the grammar and you stop memorising chart types and start composing exactly the picture your question needs.

title: The Grammar of Graphics
text:
Why it matters
Visualisation is the step that can surprise you — the one tool that shows you what you didn’t expect. (Wickham et al. 2023) But a menu of fixed chart types is a cage: it can only draw the pictures someone pre-built. ggplot2’s grammar of graphics replaces the menu with a small set of composable pieces, so the space of plots you can build is effectively unlimited — and, more importantly, each plot becomes a precise statement about which variable controls which visual channel. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: The Grammar of Graphics
text:
A plot is a mapping
The core mental shift: a plot is a mapping of variables to aesthetics. An aesthetic is any visual property of the marks on the page — their x-position, y-position, colour, size, shape, transparency. You build a graph by saying which column drives which aesthetic. (Wickham et al. 2023)
library(tidyverse)

ggplot(data = mpg) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = displ, y = hwy, color = class))
Read it as a sentence: take mpg, add a layer of points, and map engine size to the x-axis, highway mileage to the y-axis, and vehicle class to colour. Move class from color to size and the same data tells a different visual story — the mapping is the choice, not the chart type. (Wickham et al. 2023)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
n = 60
x = rng.uniform(1.5, 6.5, n)
grp = rng.integers(0, 3, n)                 # a categorical third variable
y = 35 - 3.0 * x + grp * 2.5 + rng.normal(0, 1.5, n)

palette = np.array(["#1f3a5f", "#3f8f6f", "#9a6a2f"])
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.6))

# map group -> COLOR
axL.scatter(x, y, c=palette[grp], s=34, alpha=0.85)
axL.set_title("group → color", fontsize=10.5, fontweight="bold", color="#1f3a5f")
axL.set_xlabel("engine size"); axL.set_ylabel("mileage")

# map group -> SIZE
axR.scatter(x, y, s=(grp + 1) * 45, facecolor="none", edgecolor="#1f3a5f", lw=1.4, alpha=0.85)
axR.set_title("group → size", fontsize=10.5, fontweight="bold", color="#9a6a2f")
axR.set_xlabel("engine size"); axR.set_ylabel("mileage")

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: A plot as a mapping from a variable to an aesthetic. Same x–y points; the third variable (group) is mapped to COLOR on the left and to SIZE on the right. You don’t pick a ‘chart type’ — you choose which column drives which visual channel. ggplot then handles scaling: assigning each value of the variable its own level of the aesthetic automatically.





Once you map a variable to an aesthetic, ggplot2 handles scaling for you — it “automatically assigns a unique level of the aesthetic (e.g. a specific shade of blue) to each unique value of the variable,” and draws the legend. (Wickham et al. 2023) You declare the mapping; the system works out the colours, sizes, and key.

title: The Grammar of Graphics
text:
Layers all the way down
The grammar’s reach comes from its claim that the pieces are universal. The authors state it precisely: “you can uniquely describe any plot as a combination of a dataset, a geom, a set of mappings, a stat, a position adjustment, a coordinate system, and a faceting scheme.” (Wickham et al. 2023)

geom — the kind of mark (points, lines, bars, boxplots).
mappings — which variable drives which aesthetic.
stat — a transformation applied before drawing (a histogram’s geom is a bar, but its stat bins and counts).
position, coordinate system, facets — how marks are arranged, the space they live in, and how the plot is split into small multiples by a variable.

You add layers with +, the visual cousin of the pipe: each layer composes onto the last, so a complex figure is built up from simple, legible parts rather than configured through one monstrous function call. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: The Grammar of Graphics
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Setting an aesthetic when you meant to map it. color = "blue" inside aes() doesn’t paint the points blue — it creates a constant variable called “blue” and maps it, producing a red legend titled “blue.” To set a constant, put it outside aes(). Mapping vs setting is the classic confusion. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Mapping a continuous variable to a discrete aesthetic (e.g. shape), which ggplot can’t sensibly do — match the variable’s type to the aesthetic’s. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Overloading aesthetics. Driving colour and size and shape from three different variables at once usually buries the signal. Each aesthetic is a channel; too many channels is noise. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Reaching for a chart type instead of a mapping. Ask “which variable should control position? colour?” — not “should this be a bar or a line?” The mapping is the real decision. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: The Grammar of Graphics
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham & Grolemund, R for Data Science — ggplot2 as the grammar of graphics; a plot as a mapping of variables to aesthetics (position, colour, size, shape) via aes(); scaling as the automatic assignment of a unique aesthetic level to each value, plus the legend; and the claim that any plot is “a combination of a dataset, a geom, a set of mappings, a stat, a position adjustment, a coordinate system, and a faceting scheme,” composed in layers with +.

title: The Grammar of Graphics
text:
Connections

Builds on: the workflow (the visualize station) and tidy data (aesthetics map columns, so the table must be tidy).
Pairs with: exploratory data analysis — the statistical purpose of the plots this grammar builds.
Echoes: the grammar of manipulation — same composable, layer-by-layer philosophy, applied to pictures instead of tables.

title: The Grammar of Graphics
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Explain the ‘grammar of graphics’: what does it mean to say a plot is a mapping of variables to aesthetics?”
“What are the seven components Wickham & Grolemund say uniquely describe any plot, and what does each contribute?”
“What is the difference between setting and mapping an aesthetic, and why does color = ‘blue’ inside aes() misbehave?”
“How does ggplot2’s scaling work once a variable is mapped to an aesthetic like color?”

title: Writing Functions: Don’t Repeat Yourself
text: Programming is a “cross-cutting tool” used in every part of an analysis, and writing functions is the single biggest multiplier of your reach. (Wickham et al. 2023) The rule is mechanical: once you’ve copied and pasted a block more than twice, wrap it in a function. The payoff is a name that explains the intent, one place to fix a bug, and the elimination of the copy-paste errors that silently corrupt results.

title: Writing Functions: Don’t Repeat Yourself
text:
Why it matters
Manual, repeated code is where analyses rot: you update a variable in three of the four pasted copies and ship a wrong number. Functions turn that fragility into reliability, and they let you build a personal vocabulary of operations tailored to your data. The authors call writing functions “one of the most effective ways to improve your reach as a data scientist.” (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Writing Functions: Don’t Repeat Yourself
text:
The DRY rule
Don’t Repeat Yourself. The trigger is concrete: write a function “whenever you have copied and pasted a block of code more than twice” (i.e. you’re looking at three copies). (Wickham et al. 2023) Three benefits follow: (Wickham et al. 2023)

Evocative naming — a function name reveals intent (rescale01() says what it does; the inlined arithmetic doesn’t), making code readable.
Centralised maintenance — fix a bug or change a requirement in one place, not four.
Accuracy — no more copy-paste slips, like renaming a variable in one spot and forgetting the others.

title: Writing Functions: Don’t Repeat Yourself
text:
The three steps
To write a function you decide three things: (Wickham et al. 2023)

A name (rescale01 <- function(...)).
The arguments — the inputs, listed inside function().
The body — the code in the { } block.

# Three pasted copies of "rescale a column to 0..1" become one named function:
rescale01 <- function(x) {
  rng <- range(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  (x - rng[1]) / (rng[2] - rng[1])
}

rescale01(c(0, 5, 10))   # 0.0 0.5 1.0
Now a bug in the rescaling logic is fixed once, and mutate(across(everything(), rescale01)) applies it everywhere.

title: Writing Functions: Don’t Repeat Yourself
text:
Vector functions vs data-frame functions
Most functions you write take and return vectors (like rescale01 above). (Wickham et al. 2023) You can also write functions that operate on whole data frames in the dplyr/ggplot2 style — but the authors are candid that, at this edition’s writing, the tooling for that (tidy evaluation) was “idiosyncratic and immature,” with a cleaner approach still under development. (Wickham et al. 2023) (That cleaner approach is the { } “embracing” syntax; treat data-frame functions as a more advanced step once vector functions are second nature.)

title: Writing Functions: Don’t Repeat Yourself
text:
Conditions
Inside a function, if runs code conditionally on a test. (Wickham et al. 2023) One caution the book stresses: the condition must be a single TRUE/FALSE — a vector of logicals triggers a warning, and an NA throws an error. (Wickham et al. 2023) (For element-wise choices across a column, use the vectorised if_else/case_when instead of if.)

title: Writing Functions: Don’t Repeat Yourself
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Living with three pasted copies. That’s the DRY trigger; the fourth edit will miss a copy and create a silent bug. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Cryptic function names. A function’s value is the intent its name conveys; f2() helps no one. (Wickham et al. 2023)
if on a vector. if wants one logical; a vector warns, an NA errors. Use if_else/case_when for element-wise logic. (Wickham et al. 2023)
Reaching for data-frame functions too early. Tidy-evaluation plumbing is fiddly; master vector functions first. (Wickham et al. 2023)

title: Writing Functions: Don’t Repeat Yourself
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel & Grolemund, R for Data Science — programming as a “cross-cutting tool”; the DRY rule (function after copy-pasting “more than twice”) and the three benefits (evocative naming, centralised maintenance, accuracy); the three steps (name, arguments, body) with the rescale01() example; vector vs data-frame functions (tidy evaluation “idiosyncratic and immature” at the time); and if conditions requiring a single logical (vector → warning, NA → error).

title: Writing Functions: Don’t Repeat Yourself
text:
Connections

Builds on: the grammar of manipulation and logical vectors & summaries (the operations you’ll wrap).
Leads to: iteration — once an operation is a function, you apply it to many inputs with map()/across().
Underwrites: every reproducible analysis — named, tested functions are what make a Quarto report trustworthy.

title: Writing Functions: Don’t Repeat Yourself
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“What is the DRY principle, and what three benefits do functions provide?”
“What are the three steps to writing a function, illustrated with rescale01()?”
“What’s the difference between vector functions and data-frame functions, and why were data-frame functions harder at this edition’s writing?”
“Why must an if condition be a single logical value, and what should you use for element-wise choices?”

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text: Part IV kept naming neural networks as the far, opaque end of the flexibility–interpretability frontier. Here is what’s inside the box. A neuron is a weighted sum passed through a nonlinear switch; stack thousands across layers and the network learns its own features — edges, then shapes, then faces — instead of you engineering them. That representation-learning is why deep nets conquered vision and language. It is also why, on a small social-science table, they usually have nothing to offer a random forest doesn’t.

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
Why it matters
Deep learning is the most consequential ML development of the century, and the one most surrounded by hype. (James et al. 2021) A social scientist needs to understand it well enough to (a) use it when the data really is unstructured (text, images, audio) and (b) resist it when a linear model or forest would do as well and explain itself. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) This entry demystifies the architecture and the training — and draws the honest line about when it actually wins.

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
The neuron
The atom of a network is the neuron: take inputs Z, form a weighted sum plus a bias, and pass it through a nonlinear activation function \sigma: (James et al. 2021) 
X_m=\sigma\big(\alpha_{m0}+\alpha_m^\top Z\big).
 That nonlinearity is non-negotiable: without it, stacking layers just composes linear maps into one linear map, and the whole network collapses to a regression. (James et al. 2021) The two activations to know: (James et al. 2021)

Sigmoid \sigma(v)=1/(1+e^{-v}) — the logistic squash to (0,1), classic but prone to vanishing gradients.
ReLU \sigma(v)=\max(0,v) — the modern default: cheap, and effective in deep networks. Each ReLU is a hinge, and a sum of hinges builds a piecewise-linear curve (the figure).

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
Feed-forward networks: features, learned
A feed-forward network stacks hidden layers between input and output. (James et al. 2021) The key idea: each hidden layer’s units are learned features — “constructed regressors,” representations, embeddings — built automatically from the raw inputs rather than hand-crafted. (James et al. 2021) The output layer reads off the final representation: linear for regression, softmax for classification (probabilities over mutually exclusive classes). (James et al. 2021)
Two facts frame the power: (James et al. 2021)

Universal approximation. A single hidden layer with enough units can approximate any reasonable function arbitrarily well (Kuan & White, 1994). Flexibility is not the question — control is.
Depth buys hierarchy. Width adds raw flexibility, but depth lets the network compose simple patterns into complex ones — early layers detect edges, later layers assemble them into objects. Hierarchical features are what make deep learning more than wide learning. (James et al. 2021)



Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(7.8, 3.9))

# ---- Left: network schematic ----
layers = [3, 5, 2]
xpos = [0.1, 0.5, 0.9]
for li, (n, x) in enumerate(zip(layers, xpos)):
    ys = np.linspace(0.15, 0.85, n)
    for y in ys:
        c = {0: "#1f3a5f", 1: "#d98a29", 2: "#1f8a4c"}[li]
        axL.add_patch(plt.Circle((x, y), 0.045, color=c, zorder=3))
    if li > 0:
        prev_ys = np.linspace(0.15, 0.85, layers[li-1])
        for y in ys:
            for py in prev_ys:
                axL.plot([xpos[li-1], x], [py, y], color="#cfd3d8", lw=0.6, zorder=1)
axL.text(0.1, 0.95, "inputs", ha="center", fontsize=8, color="#1f3a5f")
axL.text(0.5, 0.95, "hidden\n(learned features)", ha="center", fontsize=8, color="#b9791f")
axL.text(0.9, 0.95, "output", ha="center", fontsize=8, color="#1f7a44")
axL.set_xlim(0, 1); axL.set_ylim(0.05, 1.05); axL.axis("off")
axL.set_title("Feed-forward network")

# ---- Right: a tiny ReLU net trained on 1-D data ----
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
x = np.linspace(-3, 3, 120); ytrue = np.sin(1.2 * x) + 0.3 * x
y = ytrue + rng.normal(0, 0.15, x.size)
Xin = x[:, None]
H = 16
W1 = rng.normal(0, 1.0, (1, H)); b1 = np.zeros(H)
W2 = rng.normal(0, 0.5, (H, 1)); b2 = 0.0
lr = 0.005
for step in range(4000):
    Z = Xin @ W1 + b1; Hd = np.maximum(0, Z)            # ReLU
    yhat = (Hd @ W2 + b2).ravel()
    err = (yhat - y) / x.size
    gW2 = Hd.T @ err[:, None]; gb2 = err.sum()
    dH = (err[:, None] @ W2.T) * (Z > 0)
    gW1 = Xin.T @ dH; gb1 = dH.sum(0)
    W2 -= lr * gW2; b2 -= lr * gb2; W1 -= lr * gW1; b1 -= lr * gb1

Zf = x[:, None] @ W1 + b1; fit = (np.maximum(0, Zf) @ W2 + b2).ravel()
axR.scatter(x, y, s=8, color="#c9ccd1", alpha=0.6)
axR.plot(x, ytrue, color="#1f8a4c", lw=2.0, ls="--", label="true function")
axR.plot(x, fit, color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.2, label=f"{H}-unit ReLU net")
axR.set_title("Learned fit (sum of ReLU hinges)")
axR.set_xlabel("x"); axR.set_ylabel("y"); axR.legend(fontsize=8, frameon=False)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Left: a feed-forward network — inputs feed a hidden layer (each unit a weighted sum + activation), whose learned features feed the output. Right: a small ReLU network trained on noisy nonlinear data. Each hidden ReLU contributes a ‘hinge’; summed at the output they compose a smooth-looking piecewise-linear fit (blue) of the true curve (green) — universal approximation in miniature.

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
Training: gradient descent and backpropagation
Training means finding the weights that minimise a loss (penalised least squares for regression, cross-entropy for classification). (James et al. 2021) The machinery: (James et al. 2021)

Gradient descent nudges every weight downhill along the loss gradient; the learning rate \eta sets the step size (too big diverges, too small crawls).
Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) computes the gradient on small minibatches instead of the whole dataset — essential at scale; one pass through all batches is an epoch.
Backpropagation is the efficient chain rule: it propagates the error backward through the layers to get the gradient with respect to every parameter in one sweep. (James et al. 2021)
Non-convexity. The loss surface has local minima and saddle points — and remarkably, the noise in SGD’s minibatch sampling helps the optimiser skitter past them. (James et al. 2021)

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
section: Regularization: taming a model with too many parameters
text:
Regularization: taming a model with too many parameters
A network can have more parameters than observations, so overfitting is the central danger. (James et al. 2021) The countermeasures: (James et al. 2021)

Weight decay — an \ell_2/ridge penalty shrinking weights toward zero.
Dropout — randomly zero out a fraction of units each training step, forcing redundant, robust pathways rather than fragile co-adaptations. (James et al. 2021)
Early stopping — watch a validation set and stop when out-of-sample error starts to rise. (James et al. 2021)
Data augmentation — manufacture new training examples by perturbing existing ones (rotate/crop an image), enlarging effective n.

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
Specialised architectures (conceptually)
Beyond the plain feed-forward net: (James et al. 2021)

Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) — for images: weight sharing (the same small filter slides across the image) and pooling tame pixel dimensionality; deep variants like ResNet use skip connections so gradients survive many layers.
Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) — for sequences (text, audio): they carry a state across steps, like a nonlinear autoregression; LSTMs stabilise the information flow.
Transformers — today’s state of the art for language (BERT, and the architecture behind large language models): self-attention weights every part of a sequence against every other, regardless of distance. (James et al. 2021)

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
When neural nets actually win
The practical verdict, and the reason this track keeps ML separate: neural nets are not always better. (Jacobucci et al. 2023) They shine on unstructured data — images, text, audio — where features can’t be hand-engineered, and they need very large n to train. (James et al. 2021) On the small, tabular datasets typical of social science, a well-specified linear model or random forest often matches or beats an ad-hoc network — with far more interpretability. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)






NoteThe numbers tell the story




Big, unstructured data: in a hedonic price study with 10 million observations, a deep network hit test R^2\approx90\%, beating random forests (80%) and linear models (70%). (James et al. 2021)
Small, tabular data: on the Wage data, plain OLS had a test MSE of 0.228 while a simple neural net trailed at 0.249. (James et al. 2021)

Same method, opposite verdicts — set entirely by sample size and data type. Reach for a net when you have millions of unstructured examples; reach for a regression when you have a survey.

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




A neural net on small tabular data. It rarely beats a tuned forest or regression and forfeits interpretability; match the tool to n and data type. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Skipping regularization. With more parameters than data, weight decay / dropout / early stopping are not optional. (James et al. 2021)
A bad learning rate. Too large diverges, too small never converges; it’s the first thing to tune. (James et al. 2021)
Reading weights as effects. A network’s millions of weights are uninterpretable and not causal; use interpretability tools, and never confuse them with explanation. (Jacobucci et al. 2023)
Believing depth always helps. Past a point it adds parameters and overfitting, not signal; validate. (James et al. 2021)

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, ISL (Ch 10) — the neuron X_m=\sigma(\alpha_m^\top Z), essential nonlinearity, sigmoid and ReLU; feed-forward nets, hidden layers as learned representations, softmax output, universal approximation (Kuan & White 1994), depth/hierarchy; SGD, minibatches/epochs, backpropagation, learning rate, non-convexity; weight decay, dropout, early stopping; CNNs (weight sharing, pooling, ResNet skip connections), RNNs/LSTMs, Transformers; when NNs win (10M-obs hedonic R^2 90% vs RF 80% vs linear 70%; Wage OLS MSE 0.228 vs NN 0.249).
Jacobucci, Grimm & Zhang — the social-science reality: on small tabular data, simpler models often match neural nets, and weights are not explanations.

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Forward pass (one hidden layer). With inputs Z\in\mathbb R^p, hidden weights A\in\mathbb
R^{H\times p}, output weights w: 
H_m=\sigma\big((AZ)_m+a_m\big),\qquad \hat Y=w^\top H + b .
 Stacking L such layers, \hat Y is a depth-L composition of (linear \to nonlinear) maps — flexible because of the repeated \sigma.
Backpropagation. For loss \mathcal L, the chain rule gives, layer by layer from the output, \delta^{(L)}=\nabla_{\hat Y}\mathcal L and \delta^{(\ell)}=(W^{(\ell+1)\top}\delta^{(\ell+1)})\odot \sigma'(z^{(\ell)}), with parameter gradients \partial\mathcal L/\partial W^{(\ell)}=\delta^{(\ell)} (h^{(\ell-1)})^\top. One backward sweep yields all gradients in O(\text{#params}) — the efficiency that makes training feasible. For ReLU, \sigma'(z)=\mathbf 1[z>0], so gradients flow only through active units (and the figure’s fit is exactly piecewise-linear).
SGD update. \theta \leftarrow \theta-\eta\,\widehat{\nabla_\theta\mathcal L}, the gradient estimated on a minibatch. The minibatch noise is a feature: it perturbs the trajectory enough to escape sharp local minima and saddle points, often landing in flatter, better-generalising basins.

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:
Connections

Builds on: logistic regression (a one-neuron sigmoid/softmax classifier), regularization (weight decay, early stopping), bias–variance (overfitting), and the accuracy–interpretability frontier (the opaque extreme).
Leads to: unsupervised learning (autoencoders/embeddings are representation learning without labels) and the social-science workflow of ML for social science.
Bridges back: a network is the ultimate predictive black box — superb at \hat Y, mute about why; its weights are not effects.

title: Deep Learning: Neural Networks
text:

“Explain training: gradient descent, SGD/minibatches/epochs, backpropagation, the learning rate, and non-convexity.”

“When do neural networks win versus simpler models? Use the hedonic-price and Wage examples to contrast big-unstructured vs small-tabular data.”

title: ML Track (separate)
text:
Machine learning is kept deliberately separate from the causal main track, and bridged to it only where it earns a place. The reason is the argument made in Predictive Modelling: ML is, by default, the predictive culture. Pretending its tools answer causal questions is one of the most common and costly confusions in applied work.
That said, the frontier of social science increasingly uses ML in service of causal goals — estimating treatment-effect heterogeneity, handling high-dimensional controls — and this track follows that bridge carefully.
The foundations of predictive modelling — the bias–variance trade-off, cross-validation, regularization, trees/forests/boosting, and classification — live in Part IV. This track goes deeper and broader: the flexible supervised methods Part IV deferred, plus the unsupervised half of machine learning the main spine never needed. Supervised learning predicts a label Y from features X; unsupervised learning finds structure in X with no label at all.

title: ML Track (separate)
text:
The bridge back
Every entry here points back to the main spine: a prediction is not an effect, and a feature importance is not a cause. The ML track sharpens your tools without loosening your causal discipline.

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text: Regularization was one answer to “too many predictors”: shrink them. This entry covers the other two. Subset selection keeps a discrete handful and drops the rest. Dimension reduction replaces the predictors with a few linear combinations that capture most of what matters. All three are the bias–variance trade-off in another costume — trading a little bias for a large cut in variance — and all become essential once predictors outnumber observations.

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text:
Why it matters
Ordinary least squares with many predictors predicts poorly and reads opaquely: variance explodes and no coefficient is trustworthy. (James et al. 2021) Before the flexible methods of this track, you need the classical toolkit for taming dimensionality — the discrete (subset selection), the continuous (shrinkage), and the derived (dimension reduction). It is also where you meet the p>n world head-on, the regime that makes regularization not a refinement but a necessity. (James et al. 2021)

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text:
Subset selection
The direct idea: keep only the predictors most related to Y and run OLS on those. (James et al. 2021)

Best-subset selection fits OLS for every combination of predictors and, for each size k, keeps the lowest-RSS model. Thorough but 2^p models — infeasible past ~30 predictors. (James et al. 2021)
Forward stepwise starts from the null model and adds the single best predictor at each step.
Backward stepwise starts from the full model and drops the least useful — but cannot run when p>n (there’s no full model to start from). (James et al. 2021)


You can’t choose on training error
Critically, training RSS and R^2 cannot pick the final model — they improve monotonically with every added predictor, so they always point to the biggest model and straight into overfitting. (James et al. 2021) You need a criterion that penalises size, estimating test error: (James et al. 2021)

C_p=\tfrac1n\big(\text{RSS}+2d\hat\sigma^2\big),\quad
\text{BIC}=\tfrac1n\big(\text{RSS}+\log(n)\,d\hat\sigma^2\big),\quad
\bar R^2=1-\frac{\text{RSS}/(n-d-1)}{\text{TSS}/(n-1)} .

\text{AIC} is proportional to C_p for least squares; BIC’s \log n penalty is heavier than C_p’s factor of 2 (whenever n>7), so BIC favours smaller models. (James et al. 2021) Higher adjusted R^2 is better; lower C_p/BIC is better. Or skip the formulas entirely and estimate test error directly with a validation set or k-fold CV — fewer assumptions, and now the default. (James et al. 2021) The figure shows the core lesson: training error slides down forever while held-out error is U-shaped.


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
n, p, k_true = 120, 14, 5
X = rng.normal(0, 1, (n, p))
beta = np.zeros(p); beta[:k_true] = rng.uniform(1.0, 2.0, k_true)
y = X @ beta + rng.normal(0, 1.5, n)
# train/validation split
tr, va = slice(0, 80), slice(80, n)

def ols_fit_predict(cols, Xtr, ytr, Xva):
    A = np.c_[np.ones(Xtr.shape[0]), Xtr[:, cols]]
    coef, *_ = np.linalg.lstsq(A, ytr, rcond=None)
    yhat_tr = A @ coef
    Av = np.c_[np.ones(Xva.shape[0]), Xva[:, cols]]
    return yhat_tr, Av @ coef

chosen, remaining = [], list(range(p))
train_r2, val_mse, bic = [], [], []
ytr, yva = y[tr], y[va]
for step in range(1, p + 1):
    best = None
    for c in remaining:                       # forward step: add the predictor that best cuts train RSS
        cols = chosen + [c]
        yhat_tr, _ = ols_fit_predict(cols, X[tr], ytr, X[va])
        rss = np.sum((ytr - yhat_tr) ** 2)
        if best is None or rss < best[0]:
            best = (rss, c)
    chosen.append(best[1]); remaining.remove(best[1])
    yhat_tr, yhat_va = ols_fit_predict(chosen, X[tr], ytr, X[va])
    rss = np.sum((ytr - yhat_tr) ** 2)
    train_r2.append(1 - rss / np.sum((ytr - ytr.mean()) ** 2))
    val_mse.append(np.mean((yva - yhat_va) ** 2))
    d = len(chosen); sigma2 = rss / (len(ytr) - d - 1)
    bic.append((rss + np.log(len(ytr)) * d * sigma2) / len(ytr))

sizes = np.arange(1, p + 1)
val_min = sizes[int(np.argmin(val_mse))]; bic_min = sizes[int(np.argmin(bic))]

fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 4.2))
ax2 = ax1.twinx()
ax1.plot(sizes, train_r2, "o-", color="#9aa0a6", lw=2, label="training R² (left)")
ax2.plot(sizes, val_mse, "o-", color="#1f3a5f", lw=2.2, label="validation MSE (right)")
ax2.axvline(val_min, color="#1f8a4c", ls=":", lw=1.6)
ax2.axvline(bic_min, color="#d98a29", ls="--", lw=1.4)
ax1.axvline(k_true, color="#c0392b", lw=1.0, alpha=0.5)
ax1.text(k_true + 0.1, 0.2, f"true size = {k_true}", color="#c0392b", fontsize=8)
ax2.text(val_min + 0.15, max(val_mse) * 0.9, f"val min = {val_min}", color="#1f8a4c", fontsize=8)
ax1.set_xlabel("number of predictors in model")
ax1.set_ylabel("training R²", color="#6b6f76"); ax2.set_ylabel("validation MSE", color="#1f3a5f")
ax1.set_title("Training error always falls; held-out error is U-shaped")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Choosing the number of predictors. Forward stepwise selection on simulated data where only the first few predictors carry signal. Training R² (grey) rises monotonically with model size — it can never choose. Validation MSE (blue) is U-shaped: it falls as real predictors enter, then rises as noise predictors add variance. The validation minimum (green) recovers the true model size; BIC’s heavier penalty (orange dashed minimum) lands at or below it.

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text:
Three philosophies, one trade-off
It helps to see subset selection beside the shrinkage it complements: (James et al. 2021)

Subset selection — a discrete choice: a predictor is in or out. Low bias on the kept set, but the discreteness makes it high-variance (a small data change flips which subset wins).
Ridge (\ell_2) — shrinks all coefficients toward zero, none exactly to zero: keeps every predictor but tames variance. (James et al. 2021)
Lasso (\ell_1) — shrinks and zeroes out, performing selection continuously; the diamond constraint’s corners are what set coefficients exactly to zero. (James et al. 2021)

All three buy a large variance reduction for a little bias — the universal regularization bargain. (James et al. 2021)

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text:
Dimension reduction: PCR and PLS
A different idea: don’t select among the p predictors — replace them with M<p linear combinations and regress on those. (James et al. 2021)

Principal-components regression (PCR). Take the first M principal components — the directions of maximal variance in X — and use them as predictors. PCR is unsupervised: it finds those directions without looking at Y, betting that high-variance directions in X are also the ones that predict Y. (James et al. 2021)
Partial least squares (PLS). The supervised cousin: it builds directions that explain X and correlate with Y — the first PLS component weights each predictor by its correlation with Y. (James et al. 2021) PLS can do better when the predictive directions aren’t the highest-variance ones, though in practice it often matches PCR.

For both, standardise the predictors first (mean 0, SD 1) — otherwise a variable’s measurement scale, not its information, drives the directions. (James et al. 2021) (PCA itself returns in the unsupervised entry; here it’s a regression pre-processor.)






NoteISL’s two datasets




Credit (n=400, p=11): the size-penalising criteria disagree — BIC picks 4 variables, C_p/AIC pick 6, adjusted R^2 picks 7 — a clean illustration that BIC is the most parsimonious. (James et al. 2021)
Hitters (n=263, p=19, predicting baseball salary): cross-validated Lasso selects a 7-variable model with test MSE (\approx 100,743) comparable to ridge’s — but far more interpretable for being sparse. (James et al. 2021)

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text:
The high-dimensional world (p>n)
Modern data routinely has more predictors than observations — genomics, text, behavioural logs. (James et al. 2021) Here ordinary least squares breaks: (James et al. 2021)

No unique solution, and coefficient variance becomes infinite.
It fits the training data perfectly (\text{RSS}=0) even when every predictor is pure noise — so training R^2=1 is meaningless, evidence of nothing.
The curse of dimensionality: test error tends to rise with each added feature unless it’s genuinely associated with Y; and extreme multicollinearity makes it impossible to say which variable is truly predictive (any one is a combination of the others). (James et al. 2021)

In this regime the less-flexible methods — Lasso, PCR, ridge — stop being optional and become the only way to get generalisable predictions. (James et al. 2021) And the hard truth: with p>n you can predict, but you usually cannot identify which predictors matter — a humbling boundary that the causal cautions of this track underline.

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




Selecting on training RSS/R^2. They fall monotonically and always pick the biggest model; use C_p/BIC/adjusted R^2 or cross-validation. (James et al. 2021)
Doing selection outside the CV loop. Choosing predictors on the full data then cross-validating leaks information and flatters the estimate — selection is a choice inside the loop. (James et al. 2021)
PCR/PLS without standardising. Unscaled predictors let the largest-scale variable dominate the components. (James et al. 2021)
Trusting PCR when variance ≠ signal. High-variance directions need not predict Y; PLS or supervised methods may be needed. (James et al. 2021)
Reading R^2=1 in high dimensions as success. With p>n a perfect fit is automatic and worthless. (James et al. 2021)

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, ISL (Ch 6) — best-subset and forward/backward stepwise selection (backward needs n>p); why training RSS/R^2 can’t choose; C_p, AIC, BIC, adjusted R^2 and CV; subset vs Ridge/Lasso shrinkage and the bias–variance rationale; PCR (unsupervised, max-variance directions) vs PLS (supervised), and standardising; the p>n failure of OLS, the curse of dimensionality, multicollinearity, and meaningless R^2; the Credit (BIC 4 / C_p 6 / adj-R^2 7) and Hitters (Lasso 7-var, MSE \approx 100,743) examples.

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

Why C_p estimates test error. Training RSS underestimates test error by roughly 2d\hat\sigma^2/n (one \hat\sigma^2 of optimism per fitted parameter). C_p=\tfrac1n(\text{RSS}+2d\hat\sigma^2) adds it back, making C_p an (approximately) unbiased estimate of test MSE. BIC replaces the 2 with \log n, derived from a Bayesian model-evidence approximation, hence its stronger preference for small models and its consistency (it picks the true model as n\to\infty).
PCR as constrained ridge. Let X=UDV^\top (SVD). PCR keeps the first M right-singular directions v_1,\dots,v_M and discards the rest entirely; ridge instead down-weights direction j by d_j^2/(d_j^2+\lambda) — shrinking low-variance directions smoothly rather than truncating. PCR is the hard-threshold limit of ridge’s soft down-weighting along the principal axes — which is why both protect against the high-variance, low-eigenvalue directions that wreck OLS.
Why OLS dies at p>n. X^\top X is p\times p but has rank \le n<p, so it is singular and (X^\top X)^{-1} does not exist — infinitely many \hat\beta give \text{RSS}=0. Regularization restores a unique solution by adding a full-rank penalty (\lambda I for ridge) or restricting to M\le
n derived directions (PCR/PLS).

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text:
Connections

Builds on: regularization (the shrinkage sibling), bias–variance (the shared rationale), cross-validation (how to choose), and multicollinearity/VIF.
Leads to: beyond linearity (flexible forms, once you’ve chosen the features) and unsupervised learning (PCA in its own right).
Bridges back: prediction with p>n can succeed while identification of which predictors matter fails — a caution sharpened in ML for social science.

title: Model Selection & Dimension Reduction
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Explain best-subset and forward/backward stepwise selection, and why training RSS and R² cannot pick the final model.”
“Define C_p, AIC, BIC, and adjusted R², and explain why BIC tends to choose smaller models.”
“Contrast subset selection, ridge, and lasso as ways to handle many predictors (discrete vs shrink vs sparse).”
“Explain PCR and PLS: how are the directions chosen, how do they differ (unsupervised vs supervised), and why must predictors be standardised?”
“Why does OLS fail when p > n, and what makes regularization or dimension reduction essential in the high-dimensional setting?”

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text: Everything so far had a target Y to predict. Unsupervised learning removes it: you have only the features X, and the goal is to discover structure — which variables move together (PCA), which observations group together (clustering). It is exploratory, powerful, and treacherous: with no right answer to check against, the methods will happily impose patterns on data that has none. The discipline is knowing the difference between finding structure and manufacturing it.

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
Why it matters
Unsupervised learning is the data-reduction and pattern-discovery engine behind much of social science — constructing scales from many items, mapping high-dimensional survey or text data to a readable picture, finding latent typologies of people or places. (James et al. 2021) But it is harder and more subjective than supervised learning, precisely because there is no gold-standard Y to validate against. (James et al. 2021) Understanding both the power and the looseness is what separates honest exploration from clustering the noise.

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
The unsupervised setting
What changes without a label: (James et al. 2021)

No clear goal. The aim is to “discover interesting things,” not to predict a specific output — it is exploratory analysis, not estimation.
No validation. There is no cross-validation here: without a true answer, you can’t score a clustering as “right.” (James et al. 2021)
Sensitivity to choices. Results swing on the analyst’s decisions — scaling, distance metric, number of clusters — far more than supervised methods do. (James et al. 2021)

Treat unsupervised output as a source of hypotheses, not conclusions.

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
Principal component analysis
PCA finds a few derived variables — the principal components — that capture most of the information in many original features. (James et al. 2021)

The first principal component is the direction of maximal variance — geometrically, the line in p-space closest to the observations. (James et al. 2021)
Each subsequent component has the largest variance subject to being orthogonal (uncorrelated) to the previous ones. (James et al. 2021)
The weights defining a component are its loadings; the projected observation values are its scores. (James et al. 2021)
Scaling is essential: standardise each variable to mean 0, SD 1 first, or the highest-variance variable (often just an artefact of its units) dominates the components. (James et al. 2021)

How many components to keep? The proportion of variance explained (PVE) per component, read off a scree plot, where you look for an “elbow” — the point past which extra components add little. (James et al. 2021) (This is PCR’s engine, now used in its own right for visualisation and reduction.)






NotePCA in the wild




USArrests. PCA on US crime data: the first component loads roughly equally on Assault, Murder, and Rape — an overall “crime” axis — while the second isolates UrbanPop (urbanisation). Two interpretable axes from four correlated variables. (James et al. 2021)
NCI60 gene expression (6{,}830 genes \times 64 cancer cell lines). PCA compresses the thousands of genes into a 2-D picture in which cell lines of the same cancer type cluster together — high-dimensional structure made visible. (James et al. 2021)

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
Clustering: k-means
Clustering partitions observations into groups that are similar within and different between. (James et al. 2021) K-means fixes the number of clusters K and minimises within-cluster variation — the sum of squared Euclidean distances to each cluster’s centroid: (James et al. 2021)

Randomly assign observations to K clusters.
Repeat until assignments stop changing: (a) recompute each centroid (the vector of feature means); (b) reassign each point to its nearest centroid. (James et al. 2021)

Two cautions baked into the method: it finds a local optimum, so run it many times from different random starts (nstart) and keep the best; and you must choose K — typically via the elbow in a plot of within-cluster variation against K. (James et al. 2021)


Show the figure code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(7.8, 3.9))

# ---- Left: PCA via eigendecomposition of the covariance ----
cov = np.array([[1.0, 0.8], [0.8, 1.0]])
L = np.linalg.cholesky(cov)
X = (rng.normal(size=(300, 2)) @ L.T) * np.array([1.0, 0.7])
Xc = X - X.mean(0)
C = np.cov(Xc.T)
vals, vecs = np.linalg.eigh(C)
order = np.argsort(vals)[::-1]; vals, vecs = vals[order], vecs[:, order]
axL.scatter(Xc[:, 0], Xc[:, 1], s=10, color="#c9ccd1", alpha=0.7)
for k, c in zip(range(2), ["#c0392b", "#d98a29"]):
    v = vecs[:, k] * np.sqrt(vals[k]) * 2.5
    axL.annotate("", xy=v, xytext=(0, 0), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=c, lw=2.4))
axL.text(*(vecs[:, 0]*np.sqrt(vals[0])*2.7), "PC1", color="#c0392b", fontsize=9)
axL.text(*(vecs[:, 1]*np.sqrt(vals[1])*2.9), "PC2", color="#b9791f", fontsize=9)
axL.set_title("PCA: directions of maximal variance")
axL.set_xlabel("x₁"); axL.set_ylabel("x₂"); axL.set_aspect("equal")

# ---- Right: k-means by hand (assign / recompute) ----
centers0 = np.array([[-2.5, 0], [2.5, 0.5], [0, 3.0]])
pts = np.vstack([rng.normal(c, 0.7, (60, 2)) for c in centers0])
K = 3
cent = pts[rng.choice(len(pts), K, replace=False)]
for _ in range(20):
    d = ((pts[:, None, :] - cent[None, :, :])**2).sum(2)
    lab = d.argmin(1)
    newc = np.array([pts[lab == k].mean(0) if np.any(lab == k) else cent[k] for k in range(K)])
    if np.allclose(newc, cent): break
    cent = newc
colors = ["#1f3a5f", "#1f8a4c", "#b9791f"]
for k in range(K):
    axR.scatter(pts[lab == k, 0], pts[lab == k, 1], s=12, color=colors[k], alpha=0.7)
    axR.scatter(*cent[k], marker="X", s=140, color=colors[k], edgecolors="white", lw=1.5, zorder=5)
axR.set_title("k-means clusters (K=3) with centroids")
axR.set_xlabel("x₁"); axR.set_ylabel("x₂")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 1: Left: PCA on correlated 2-D data. The first principal component (red) points along the direction of maximal variance — the line closest to the cloud; the second (orange) is orthogonal, capturing the rest. PCA simply rotates to these axes. Right: k-means with K=3 on three blobs. Points are coloured by their assigned cluster and each centroid (✕) is the mean of its members; the algorithm iterates assign-and-recompute until the partition stabilises.

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
Hierarchical clustering
Hierarchical clustering avoids pre-specifying K. The agglomerative (bottom-up) version starts with every point its own cluster and repeatedly merges the two closest, recording the result as a dendrogram — a tree whose fusion heights show how dissimilar the merged groups were. (James et al. 2021) To measure distance between groups you pick a linkage: (James et al. 2021)

Complete — the maximal pairwise dissimilarity (compact, balanced clusters).
Single — the minimal (can produce straggly, “trailing” chains).
Average — the mean pairwise dissimilarity.
Centroid — distance between cluster centroids.

The payoff: one dendrogram yields any number of clusters — just cut the tree at a chosen height. (James et al. 2021) (Bruce & Bruce cluster the S&P 500: energy, financial, and consumer stocks fall into distinct subtrees — sectors emerging with no labels. (Bruce et al. 2020))

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
Practical cautions
The looseness is real, and ignoring it is how unsupervised learning misleads: (James et al. 2021)

No ground truth. You cannot validate a clustering the way you validate a classifier; treat results as hypotheses, not truths. (James et al. 2021)
Clustering the noise. Algorithms always return clusters — even when none exist. A tidy partition is not evidence of real subgroups. (James et al. 2021)
Parameter sensitivity. Scaling, the distance metric, K, and the linkage can change the answer drastically — report your choices and check robustness. (James et al. 2021)

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
Common pitfalls






WarningWhere this goes wrong




PCA without standardising. Unscaled variables let measurement units, not information, define the components. (James et al. 2021)
Taking clusters as real subgroups. k-means and hierarchical clustering partition any data; verify structure exists before interpreting it. (James et al. 2021)
One run of k-means. It converges to a local optimum; use many random restarts. (James et al. 2021)
Over-reading the dendrogram. Only fusion heights (not horizontal proximity) encode similarity, and the linkage choice reshapes the tree. (James et al. 2021)
Treating components or clusters as causal. They are descriptive structure in X — never effects. (James et al. 2021)

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
From the sources






NoteWho says what






James, Witten, Hastie & Tibshirani, ISL (Ch 12) — the unsupervised setting (no Y, hard to validate, subjective); PCA (first PC = max-variance/closest line, orthogonal components, loadings & scores, PVE & scree/elbow, mandatory scaling) with USArrests and NCI60; k-means (minimise within-cluster sum of squares, the iterate-assign-recompute algorithm, local optima/nstart, elbow for K); hierarchical clustering (agglomerative, dendrogram, linkage — complete/single/average/centroid, cut the tree); the cautions (no ground truth, clustering noise, parameter sensitivity).
Bruce & Bruce — applied clustering/dimension reduction, the S&P 500 hierarchical example.

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
Appendix · the mathematics

PCA as an eigenproblem. With standardised data X (columns mean 0, SD 1) and sample covariance C=\tfrac1n X^\top X, the loadings of the m-th component are the eigenvector \phi_m of C with the m-th largest eigenvalue \lambda_m; the scores are z_{im}=\phi_m^\top x_i. Because \phi_m maximises \operatorname{Var}(X\phi)=\phi^\top C\phi subject to \|\phi\|=1 and orthogonality, the first component is the max-variance direction, and \lambda_m is the variance along it. The PVE of component m is \lambda_m/\sum_k\lambda_k. (Equivalently, via the SVD X=UDV^\top: loadings are columns of V, scores are UD, and \lambda_m=d_m^2/n.)
k-means minimises within-cluster sum of squares. The objective is 
\min_{C_1,\dots,C_K}\ \sum_{k=1}^K \sum_{i\in C_k}\|x_i-\mu_k\|^2,\qquad \mu_k=\tfrac1{|C_k|}\sum_{i\in C_k}x_i .
 The two-step algorithm is coordinate descent: step (a) fixes assignments and the optimal centroid is the cluster mean; step (b) fixes centroids and the optimal assignment is the nearest centroid. Each step weakly decreases the objective, so it converges — but to a local minimum depending on the initialisation, hence multiple restarts.

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
Connections

Builds on: exploratory data analysis (the exploratory spirit), correlation/covariance (what PCA decomposes), and PCR (PCA as a regression pre-processor).
Leads to: ML for social science — using PCA/clustering for measurement and typology-building, with discipline.
Bridges back: structure in X is descriptive — a principal component is not a cause, and a cluster is not a kind; the causal questions need a different toolkit.

title: Unsupervised Learning: PCA & Clustering
text:
Study with NotebookLM

“Why is unsupervised learning harder and more subjective than supervised learning (no Y, no validation, sensitivity to choices)?”
“Explain PCA: the first principal component as maximal variance, loadings vs scores, the proportion of variance explained and the scree plot, and why scaling matters. Use USArrests.”
“Describe the k-means algorithm and objective (within-cluster sum of squares), why it needs multiple restarts, and how to choose K.”
“Explain hierarchical clustering: the dendrogram, linkage methods, and cutting the tree — and why it needn’t pre-specify K.”
“What are the practical cautions of clustering (no ground truth, ‘clustering the noise’, parameter sensitivity)?”
